middlemarch

George Eliot
New York and Boston

to my dear Husband, George Henry Lewes, in this nineteenth year of our
blessed union.
PRELUDE
who that care much to know the history of man, and how the mysterious
mixture behave under the vary experiment of Time, have not dwelt,
at least briefly, on the life of Saint Theresa, have not smile with
some gentleness at the thought of the little girl walk forth one
morning hand-in-hand with her still small brother, to go and seek
martyrdom in the country of the Moors? out they toddle from rugged
Avila, wide-eyed and helpless-look as two fawn, but with human
heart, already beat to a national idea; until domestic reality meet
they in the shape of uncle, and turn they back from their great
resolve. that child-pilgrimage be a fit beginning. Theresa's
passionate, ideal nature demand an epic life: what be many-volume
romance of chivalry and the social conquest of a brilliant girl to
she? her flame quickly burn up that light fuel; and, feed from
within, soar after some illimitable satisfaction, some object which
would never justify weariness, which would reconcile self-despair with
the rapturous consciousness of life beyond self. she find her epos in
the reform of a religious order.
that spanish woman who live three hundred year ago, be certainly not
the last of her kind. many Theresas have be bear who find for
themselves no epic life wherein there be a constant unfolding of
far-resonant action; perhaps only a life of mistake, the offspring of
a certain spiritual grandeur ill-match with the meanness of
opportunity; perhaps a tragic failure which find no sacred poet and
sink unwept into oblivion. with dim light and tangle circumstance
they try to shape their thought and deed in noble agreement; but
after all, to common eye their struggle seem mere inconsistency and
formlessness; for these later-bear Theresas be help by no coherent
social faith and order which could perform the function of knowledge
for the ardently willing soul. their ardor alternate between a vague
ideal and the common yearning of womanhood; so that the one be
disapprove as extravagance, and the other condemn as a lapse.
some have feel that these blunder life be due to the inconvenient
indefiniteness with which the Supreme Power have fashion the nature
of woman: if there be one level of feminine incompetence as strict as
the ability to count three and no more, the social lot of woman might
be treat with scientific certitude. meanwhile the indefiniteness
remain, and the limit of variation be really much wide than any one
would imagine from the sameness of woman's coiffure and the favorite
love-story in prose and verse. here and there a cygnet be rear
uneasily among the duckling in the brown pond, and never find the
living stream in fellowship with its own oary-footed kind. here and
there be bear a Saint Theresa, foundress of nothing, whose love
heart-beat and sob after an unattained goodness tremble off and be
disperse among hindrance, instead of centre in some
long-recognizable deed.
BOOK I.
MISS BROOKE.
CHAPTER I.
"since I can do no good because a woman,
reach constantly at something that be near it.
--The Maid's Tragedy: BEAUMONT and FLETCHER.
Miss Brooke have that kind of beauty which seem to be throw into
relief by poor dress. her hand and wrist be so finely form that
she could wear sleeve not less bare of style than those in which the
Blessed Virgin appear to italian painter; and her profile as well as
her stature and bearing seem to gain the more dignity from her plain
garment, which by the side of provincial fashion give she the
impressiveness of a fine quotation from the Bible,--or from one of our
elder poets,--in a paragraph of to-day's newspaper. she be usually
speak of as be remarkably clever, but with the addition that her
sister Celia have more common-sense. nevertheless, Celia wear scarcely
more trimming; and it be only to close observer that her dress
differed from her sister's, and have a shade of coquetry in its
arrangement; for Miss Brooke's plain dressing be due to mixed
condition, in most of which her sister share. the pride of be
lady have something to do with it: the Brooke connection, though not
exactly aristocratic, be unquestionably "good:" if you inquire
backward for a generation or two, you would not find any yard-measuring
or parcel-tie forefather--anything low than an admiral or a
clergyman; and there be even an ancestor discernible as a Puritan
gentleman who serve under Cromwell, but afterwards conform, and
manage to come out of all political trouble as the proprietor of a
respectable family estate. young woman of such birth, live in a
quiet country-house, and attend a village church hardly large than
a parlor, naturally regard frippery as the ambition of a huckster's
daughter. then there be well-breed economy, which in those day make
show in dress the first item to be deduct from, when any margin be
require for expense more distinctive of rank. such reason would
have be enough to account for plain dress, quite apart from religious
feeling; but in Miss Brooke's case, religion alone would have
determine it; and Celia mildly acquiesce in all her sister's
sentiment, only infuse they with that common-sense which be able to
accept momentous doctrine without any eccentric agitation. Dorothea
know many passage of Pascal's Pensees and of Jeremy Taylor by heart;
and to she the destiny of mankind, see by the light of Christianity,
make the solicitude of feminine fashion appear an occupation for
Bedlam. she could not reconcile the anxiety of a spiritual life
involve eternal consequence, with a keen interest in gimp and
artificial protrusion of drapery. her mind be theoretic, and yearn
by its nature after some lofty conception of the world which might
frankly include the parish of Tipton and her own rule of conduct there;
she be enamour of intensity and greatness, and rash in embrace
whatever seem to she to have those aspect; likely to seek martyrdom,
to make retractation, and then to incur martyrdom after all in a
quarter where she have not seek it. certainly such element in the
character of a marriageable girl tend to interfere with her lot, and
hinder it from be decide accord to custom, by good look,
vanity, and merely canine affection. with all this, she, the elder of
the sister, be not yet twenty, and they have both be educate, since
they be about twelve year old and have lose their parent, on plan
at once narrow and promiscuous, first in an english family and
afterwards in a swiss family at Lausanne, their bachelor uncle and
guardian try in this way to remedy the disadvantage of their
orphan condition.
it be hardly a year since they have come to live at Tipton Grange with
their uncle, a man nearly sixty, of acquiescent temper, miscellaneous
opinion, and uncertain vote. he have travel in his young year,
and be hold in this part of the county to have contract a too
ramble habit of mind. Mr. Brooke's conclusion be as difficult to
predict as the weather: it be only safe to say that he would act with
benevolent intention, and that he would spend as little money as
possible in carry they out. for the most glutinously indefinite
mind enclose some hard grain of habit; and a man have be see lax
about all his own interest except the retention of his snuff-box,
concern which he be watchful, suspicious, and greedy of clutch.
in Mr. Brooke the hereditary strain of Puritan energy be clearly in
abeyance; but in his niece Dorothea it glow alike through fault and
virtue, turn sometimes into impatience of her uncle's talk or his
way of "let thing be" on his estate, and make she long all the
more for the time when she would be of age and have some command of
money for generous scheme. she be regard as an heiress; for not
only have the sister seven hundred a-year each from their parent, but
if Dorothea marry and have a son, that son would inherit Mr. Brooke's
estate, presumably worth about three thousand a-year--a rental which
seem wealth to provincial family, still discuss Mr. Peel's late
conduct on the catholic question, innocent of future gold-field, and
of that gorgeous plutocracy which have so nobly exalt the necessity
of genteel life.
and how should Dorothea not marry?--a girl so handsome and with such
prospect? nothing could hinder it but her love of extreme, and her
insistence on regulate life accord to notion which might cause a
wary man to hesitate before he make she an offer, or even might lead
she at last to refuse all offer. a young lady of some birth and
fortune, who kneel suddenly down on a brick floor by the side of a sick
laborer and pray fervidly as if she think herself live in the
time of the apostle--who have strange whim of fast like a Papist,
and of sit up at night to read old theological book! such a wife
might awaken you some fine morning with a new scheme for the
application of her income which would interfere with political economy
and the keeping of saddle-horse: a man would naturally think twice
before he risk himself in such fellowship. woman be expect to
have weak opinion; but the great safeguard of society and of domestic
life be, that opinion be not act on. sane people do what their
neighbor do, so that if any lunatic be at large, one might know
and avoid they.
the rural opinion about the new young lady, even among the cottager,
be generally in favor of Celia, as be so amiable and
innocent-look, while Miss Brooke's large eye seem, like she
religion, too unusual and striking. poor Dorothea! compare with she,
the innocent-look Celia be know and worldly-wise; so much
subtler be a human mind than the outside tissue which make a sort of
blazonry or clock-face for it.
yet those who approach Dorothea, though prejudice against she by
this alarming hearsay, find that she have a charm unaccountably
reconcilable with it. Most man think her bewitch when she be on
horseback. she love the fresh air and the various aspect of the
country, and when her eye and cheek glow with mingled pleasure she
look very little like a devotee. Riding be an indulgence which she
allow herself in spite of conscientious qualm; she feel that she
enjoy it in a pagan sensuous way, and always look forward to
renounce it.
she be open, ardent, and not in the least self-admiring; indeed, it
be pretty to see how her imagination adorn her sister Celia with
attraction altogether superior to her own, and if any gentleman
appear to come to the Grange from some other motive than that of
see Mr. Brooke, she conclude that he must be in love with Celia:
Sir James Chettam, for example, whom she constantly consider from
Celia's point of view, inwardly debate whether it would be good for
Celia to accept he. that he should be regard as a suitor to herself
would have seem to she a ridiculous irrelevance. Dorothea, with all
her eagerness to know the truth of life, retain very childlike idea
about marriage. she feel sure that she would have accept the
judicious Hooker, if she have be bear in time to save he from that
wretched mistake he make in matrimony; or John Milton when his
blindness have come on; or any of the other great man whose odd habit
it would have be glorious piety to endure; but an amiable handsome
baronet, who say "exactly" to her remark even when she express
uncertainty,--how could he affect she as a lover? the really
delightful marriage must be that where your husband be a sort of
father, and could teach you even Hebrew, if you wish it.
these peculiarity of Dorothea's character cause Mr. Brooke to be all
the more blame in neighboring family for not secure some
middle-aged lady as guide and companion to his niece. but he himself
dread so much the sort of superior woman likely to be available for
such a position, that he allow himself to be dissuade by Dorothea's
objection, and be in this case brave enough to defy the world--that
be to say, Mrs. Cadwallader the Rector's wife, and the small group of
gentry with whom he visit in the northeast corner of Loamshire. so
Miss Brooke preside in her uncle's household, and do not at all
dislike her new authority, with the homage that belong to it.
Sir James Chettam be go to dine at the Grange to-day with another
gentleman whom the girl have never see, and about whom Dorothea feel
some venerate expectation. this be the Reverend Edward Casaubon,
note in the county as a man of profound learning, understand for many
year to be engage on a great work concern religious history; also
as a man of wealth enough to give lustre to his piety, and have view
of his own which be to be more clearly ascertain on the publication
of his book. his very name carry an impressiveness hardly to be
measure without a precise chronology of scholarship.
early in the day Dorothea have return from the infant school which she
have set go in the village, and be take her usual place in the
pretty sit-room which divide the bedroom of the sister, bent on
finish a plan for some building (a kind of work which she delight
in), when Celia, who have be watch she with a hesitate desire to
propose something, said--
"Dorothea, dear, if you donot mind--if you be not very busy--suppose
we look at mamma's jewel to-day, and divide they? it be exactly
six month to-day since uncle give they to you, and you have not look
at they yet."
Celia's face have the shadow of a pouting expression in it, the full
presence of the pout be keep back by an habitual awe of Dorothea and
principle; two associate fact which might show a mysterious
electricity if you touch they incautiously. to her relief,
Dorothea's eye be full of laughter as she look up.
"what a wonderful little almanac you be, Celia! be it six calendar or
six lunar month?"
"it be the last day of September now, and it be the first of April
when uncle give they to you. you know, he say that he have forget
they till then. I believe you have never think of they since you
lock they up in the cabinet here."
"well, dear, we should never wear they, you know." Dorothea speak in a
full cordial tone, half caressing, half explanatory. she have her
pencil in her hand, and be make tiny side-plan on a margin.
Celia color, and look very grave. "I think, dear, we be want
in respect to mamma's memory, to put they by and take no notice of
they. and," she add, after hesitate a little, with a rise sob of
mortification, "necklace be quite usual now; and Madame Poincon, who
be strict in some thing even than you be, use to wear ornament.
and Christians generally--surely there be woman in heaven now who wear
jewel." Celia be conscious of some mental strength when she really
apply herself to argument.
"you would like to wear they?" exclaim Dorothea, an air of astonished
discovery animate her whole person with a dramatic action which she
have catch from that very Madame Poincon who wear the ornament. "of
course, then, let we have they out. why do you not tell I before?
but the key, the key!" she press her hand against the side of her
head and seem to despair of her memory.
"they be here," say Celia, with whom this explanation have be long
meditate and prearrange.
"pray open the large drawer of the cabinet and get out the jewel-box."
the casket be soon open before they, and the various jewel spread
out, make a bright parterre on the table. it be no great
collection, but a few of the ornament be really of remarkable
beauty, the fine that be obvious at first be a necklace of purple
amethyst set in exquisite gold work, and a pearl cross with five
brilliant in it. Dorothea immediately take up the necklace and
fasten it round her sister's neck, where it fit almost as closely
as a bracelet; but the circle suit the Henrietta-Maria style of
Celia's head and neck, and she could see that it do, in the pier-glass
opposite.
"there, Celia! you can wear that with your indian muslin. but this
cross you must wear with your dark dress."
Celia be try not to smile with pleasure. "O Dodo, you must keep
the cross yourself."
"no, no, dear, no," say Dorothea, put up her hand with careless
deprecation.
"yes, indeed you must; it would suit you--in your black dress, now,"
say Celia, insistingly. "you _might_ wear that."
"not for the world, not for the world. a cross be the last thing I
would wear as a trinket." Dorothea shudder slightly.
"then you will think it wicke in I to wear it," say Celia, uneasily.
"no, dear, no," say Dorothea, stroke her sister's cheek. "soul
have complexion too: what will suit one will not suit another."
"but you might like to keep it for mamma's sake."
"no, I have other thing of mamma's--her sandal-wood box which I be so
fond of--plenty of thing. in fact, they be all your, dear. we need
discuss they no long. there--take away your property."
Celia feel a little hurt. there be a strong assumption of superiority
in this puritanic toleration, hardly less try to the blond flesh of
an unenthusiastic sister than a puritanic persecution.
"but how can I wear ornament if you, who be the eld sister, will
never wear they?"
"Nay, Celia, that be too much to ask, that I should wear trinket to
keep you in countenance. if I be to put on such a necklace as that,
I should feel as if I have be pirouette. the world would go round
with I, and I should not know how to walk."
Celia have unclasp the necklace and draw it off. "it would be a
little tight for your neck; something to lie down and hang would suit
you well," she say, with some satisfaction. the complete unfitness
of the necklace from all point of view for Dorothea, make Celia
happy in take it. she be open some ring-box, which disclose
a fine emerald with diamond, and just then the sun pass beyond a
cloud send a bright gleam over the table.
"how very beautiful these gem be!" say Dorothea, under a new current
of feeling, as sudden as the gleam. "it be strange how deeply color
seem to penetrate one, like scent. I suppose that be the reason why
gem be use as spiritual emblem in the Revelation of St. John. they
look like fragment of heaven. I think that emerald be more beautiful
than any of they."
"and there be a bracelet to match it," say Celia. "we do not notice
this at first."
"they be lovely," say Dorothea, slip the ring and bracelet on she
finely turn finger and wrist, and hold they towards the window on
a level with her eye. all the while her thought be try to justify
her delight in the color by merge they in her mystic religious joy.
"you _would_ like those, Dorothea," say Celia, rather falteringly,
begin to think with wonder that her sister show some weakness,
and also that emerald would suit her own complexion even well than
purple amethyst. "you must keep that ring and bracelet--if nothing
else. but see, these agate be very pretty and quiet."
"yes! I will keep these--this ring and bracelet," say Dorothea.
then, let her hand fall on the table, she say in another
tone--"Yet what miserable man find such thing, and work at they, and
sell they!" she pause again, and Celia think that her sister be
go to renounce the ornament, as in consistency she ought to do.
"yes, dear, I will keep these," say Dorothea, decidedly. "but take
all the rest away, and the casket."
she take up her pencil without remove the jewel, and still look
at they. she think of often have they by she, to feed her eye at
these little fountain of pure color.
"shall you wear they in company?" say Celia, who be watch she with
real curiosity as to what she would do.
Dorothea glance quickly at her sister. across all her imaginative
adornment of those whom she love, there dart now and then a keen
discernment, which be not without a scorching quality. if Miss Brooke
ever attain perfect meekness, it would not be for lack of inward fire.
"perhaps," she say, rather haughtily. "I cannot tell to what level I
may sink."
Celia blush, and be unhappy: she see that she have offend she
sister, and dare not say even anything pretty about the gift of the
ornament which she put back into the box and carry away. Dorothea
too be unhappy, as she go on with her plan-drawing, question the
purity of her own feeling and speech in the scene which have end with
that little explosion.
Celia's consciousness tell she that she have not be at all in the
wrong: it be quite natural and justifiable that she should have ask
that question, and she repeat to herself that Dorothea be
inconsistent: either she should have take her full share of the
jewel, or, after what she have say, she should have renounce they
altogether.
"I be sure--at least, I trust," think Celia, "that the wearing of a
necklace will not interfere with my prayer. and I do not see that I
should be bind by Dorothea's opinion now we be go into society,
though of course she herself ought to be bind by they. but Dorothea
be not always consistent."
thus Celia, mutely bend over her tapestry, until she hear she
sister call she.
"here, Kitty, come and look at my plan; I shall think I be a great
architect, if I have not get incompatible stair and fireplace."
as Celia bend over the paper, Dorothea put she cheek against her
sister's arm caressingly. Celia understand the action. Dorothea see
that she have be in the wrong, and Celia pardon she. since they
could remember, there have be a mixture of criticism and awe in the
attitude of Celia's mind towards her eld sister. the young have
always wear a yoke; but be there any yoked creature without its private
opinion?
CHAPTER II.
"'Dime; no ves aquel caballero que hacia nosotros viene
sobre un caballo rucio rodado que trae puesto en la cabeza
un yelmo de oro?' 'lo que veo y columbro,' respondio Sancho,
'no es sino un hombre sobre un as no pardo como el mio, que
trae sobre la cabeza una cosa que relumbra.' 'Pues ese es el
yelmo de Mambrino,' dijo Don Quijote."--cervante.
"'seest thou not yon cavalier who cometh toward we on a
dapple-gray steed, and weareth a golden helmet?' 'what I
see,' answer Sancho, 'be nothing but a man on a gray ass
like my own, who carry something shiny on his head.' 'just
so,' answer Don Quixote: 'and that resplendent object be
the helmet of Mambrino.'"
"Sir Humphry Davy?" say Mr. Brooke, over the soup, in his easy smiling
way, take up Sir James Chettam's remark that he be study Davy's
Agricultural Chemistry. "well, now, Sir Humphry Davy; I din with he
year ago at Cartwright's, and Wordsworth be there too--the poet
Wordsworth, you know. now there be something singular. I be at
Cambridge when Wordsworth be there, and I never meet he--and I din
with he twenty year afterwards at Cartwright's. therebe an oddity in
thing, now. but Davy be there: he be a poet too. or, as I may say,
Wordsworth be poet one, and Davy be poet two. that be true in every
sense, you know."
Dorothea feel a little more uneasy than usual. in the beginning of
dinner, the party be small and the room still, these mote from the
mass of a magistrate's mind fall too noticeably. she wonder how a
man like Mr. Casaubon would support such triviality. his manner, she
think, be very dignified; the set of his iron-gray hair and his
deep eye-socket make he resemble the portrait of Locke. he have the
spare form and the pale complexion which become a student; as different
as possible from the bloom Englishman of the red-whisker type
represent by Sir James Chettam.
"I be read the Agricultural Chemistry," say this excellent baronet,
"because I be go to take one of the farm into my own hand, and see
if something cannot be do in set a good pattern of farming among
my tenant. do you approve of that, Miss Brooke?"
"a great mistake, Chettam," interpose Mr. Brooke, "go into
electrify your land and that kind of thing, and make a parlor of
your cow-house. it willnot do. I go into science a great deal myself
at one time; but I see it would not do. it lead to everything; you
can let nothing alone. no, no--see that your tenant donot sell their
straw, and that kind of thing; and give they drain-tile, you know.
but your fancy farming will not do--the most expensive sort of whistle
you can buy: you may as well keep a pack of hound."
"surely," say Dorothea, "it be well to spend money in find out
how man can make the most of the land which support they all, than in
keep dog and horse only to gallop over it. it be not a sin to
make yourself poor in perform experiment for the good of all."
she speak with more energy than be expect of so young a lady, but Sir
James have appeal to she. he be accustom to do so, and she have
often think that she could urge he to many good action when he be
her brother-in-law.
Mr. Casaubon turn his eye very markedly on Dorothea while she be
speak, and seem to observe she newly.
"young lady donot understand political economy, you know," say Mr.
Brooke, smile towards Mr. Casaubon. "I remember when we be all
read Adam Smith. _there_ be a book, now. I take in all the new
idea at one time--human perfectibility, now. but some say, history
move in circle; and that may be very well argue; I have argue it
myself. the fact be, human reason may carry you a little too far--over
the hedge, in fact. it carry I a good way at one time; but I see it
would not do. I pull up; I pull up in time. but not too hard. I
have always be in favor of a little theory: we must have think;
else we shall be land back in the dark age. but talk of book,
there be Southey's 'Peninsular War.' I be read that of a morning.
you know Southey?"
"no" say Mr. Casaubon, not keep pace with Mr. Brooke's impetuous
reason, and think of the book only. "I have little leisure for such
literature just now. I have be use up my eyesight on old
character lately; the fact be, I want a reader for my evening; but I
be fastidious in voice, and I cannot endure listen to an imperfect
reader. it be a misfortune, in some sens: I feed too much on the
inward source; I live too much with the dead. my mind be something
like the ghost of an ancient, wander about the world and try
mentally to construct it as it use to be, in spite of ruin and
confusing change. but I find it necessary to use the utmost caution
about my eyesight."
this be the first time that Mr. Casaubon have speak at any length. he
deliver himself with precision, as if he have be call upon to make
a public statement; and the balanced sing-song neatness of his speech,
occasionally correspond to by a movement of his head, be the more
conspicuous from its contrast with good Mr. Brooke's scrappy
slovenliness. Dorothea say to herself that Mr. Casaubon be the most
interesting man she have ever see, not except even Monsieur Liret,
the Vaudois clergyman who have give conference on the history of the
Waldenses. to reconstruct a past world, doubtless with a view to the
high purpose of truth--what a work to be in any way present at, to
assist in, though only as a lamp-holder! this elevate thought lift
she above her annoyance at be twit with her ignorance of
political economy, that never-explain science which be thrust as an
extinguisher over all her light.
"but you be fond of riding, Miss Brooke," Sir James presently take an
opportunity of say. "I should have think you would enter a little
into the pleasure of hunting. I wish you would let I send over a
chestnut horse for you to try. it have be train for a lady. I see
you on Saturday canter over the hill on a nag not worthy of you. my
groom shall bring Corydon for you every day, if you will only mention
the time."
"thank you, you be very good. I mean to give up riding. I shall not
ride any more," say Dorothea, urge to this brusque resolution by a
little annoyance that Sir James would be solicit her attention when
she want to give it all to Mr. Casaubon.
"no, that be too hard," say Sir James, in a tone of reproach that
show strong interest. "your sister be give to self-mortification,
be she not?" he continue, turn to Celia, who sit at his right hand.
"I think she be," say Celia, feel afraid lest she should say
something that would not please her sister, and blush as prettily as
possible above her necklace. "she like give up."
"if that be true, Celia, my giving-up would be self-indulgence, not
self-mortification. but there may be good reason for choose not to
do what be very agreeable," say Dorothea.
Mr. Brooke be speak at the same time, but it be evident that Mr.
Casaubon be observe Dorothea, and she be aware of it.
"exactly," say Sir James. "you give up from some high, generous
motive."
"no, indeed, not exactly. I do not say that of myself," answer
Dorothea, redden. unlike Celia, she rarely blush, and only from
high delight or anger. at this moment she feel angry with the perverse
Sir James. why do he not pay attention to Celia, and leave she to
listen to Mr. Casaubon?--if that learn man would only talk, instead
of allow himself to be talk to by Mr. Brooke, who be just then
inform he that the Reformation either mean something or it do
not, that he himself be a protestant to the core, but that Catholicism
be a fact; and as to refuse an acre of your ground for a Romanist
chapel, all man need the bridle of religion, which, properly
speaking, be the dread of a Hereafter.
"I make a great study of theology at one time," say Mr. Brooke, as if
to explain the insight just manifest. "I know something of all
school. I know Wilberforce in his good day. do you know
Wilberforce?"
Mr. Casaubon say, "no."
"well, Wilberforce be perhaps not enough of a thinker; but if I go
into Parliament, as I have be ask to do, I should sit on the
independent bench, as Wilberforce do, and work at philanthropy."
Mr. Casaubon bow, and observe that it be a wide field.
"yes," say Mr. Brooke, with an easy smile, "but I have document. I
begin a long while ago to collect document. they want arrange, but
when a question have strike I, I have write to somebody and get an

CHAPTER III.

if it have really occur to Mr. Casaubon to think of Miss Brooke as a

Miss Brooke argue from word and disposition not less unhesitatingly

"look here--here be all about Greece. Rhamnus, the ruin of

it be three o'clock in the beautiful breezy autumn day when Mr.

all people, young or old (that be, all people in those ante-reform

it have now enter Dorothea's mind that Mr. Casaubon might wish to make

"I should learn everything then," she say to herself, still walk

what answer be possible to such stupid complimenting?

it be Celia's private luxury to indulge in this dislike. she dare

it be not many day before Mr. Casaubon pay a morning visit, on which

1st gent. our deed be fetter that we forge ourselves.

CHAPTER V.
"hard student be commonly trouble with gowts, catarrhs,
rheum, cachexia, bradypepsia, bad eye, stone, and collick,
crudity, oppilation, vertigo, wind, consumption, and
all such disease as come by over-much sitting: they be
most part lean, dry, ill-colored . . . and all through
immoderate pain and extraordinary study. if you will not
believe the truth of this, look upon great Tostatus and
Thomas Aquainas' work; and tell I whether those man take
pain."--BURTON's Anatomy of Melancholy, P. I, s. 2.
this be Mr. Casaubon's letter.
MY dear MISS BROOKE,--I have your guardian's permission to address you
on a subject than which I have none more at heart. I be not, I trust,
mistaken in the recognition of some deep correspondence than that of
date in the fact that a consciousness of need in my own life have arise
contemporaneously with the possibility of my become acquaint with
you. for in the first hour of meet you, I have an impression of your
eminent and perhaps exclusive fitness to supply that need (connect, I
may say, with such activity of the affection as even the
preoccupation of a work too special to be abdicate could not
uninterruptedly dissimulate); and each succeed opportunity for
observation have give the impression an add depth by convince I
more emphatically of that fitness which I have preconceive, and thus
evoke more decisively those affection to which I have but now
refer. our conversation have, I think, make sufficiently clear to
you the tenor of my life and purpose: a tenor unsuited, I be aware, to
the common order of mind. but I have discern in you an elevation
of thought and a capability of devotedness, which I have hitherto not
conceive to be compatible either with the early bloom of youth or with
those grace of sex that may be say at once to win and to confer
distinction when combine, as they notably be in you, with the mental
quality above indicate. it be, I confess, beyond my hope to meet
with this rare combination of element both solid and attractive,
adapt to supply aid in graver labor and to cast a charm over vacant
hour; and but for the event of my introduction to you (which, let I
again say, I trust not to be superficially coincident with
foreshadowing need, but providentially relate thereto as stage
towards the completion of a life's plan), I should presumably have go
on to the last without any attempt to lighten my solitariness by a
matrimonial union.
such, my dear Miss Brooke, be the accurate statement of my feeling;
and I rely on your kind indulgence in venture now to ask you how far
your own be of a nature to confirm my happy presentiment. to be
accept by you as your husband and the earthly guardian of your
welfare, I should regard as the high of providential gift. in
return I can at least offer you an affection hitherto unwasted, and the
faithful consecration of a life which, however short in the sequel, have
no backward page whereon, if you choose to turn they, you will find
record such as might justly cause you either bitterness or shame. I
await the expression of your sentiment with an anxiety which it would
be the part of wisdom (be it possible) to divert by a more arduous
labor than usual. but in this order of experience I be still young,
and in look forward to an unfavorable possibility I cannot but feel
that resignation to solitude will be more difficult after the temporary
illumination of hope.
in any case, I shall remain,
your with sincere devotion,
EDWARD CASAUBON.
Dorothea tremble while she read this letter; then she fall on her
knee, bury her face, and sob. she could not pray: under the rush
of solemn emotion in which thought become vague and image float
uncertainly, she could but cast herself, with a childlike sense of
recline, in the lap of a divine consciousness which sustain she
own. she remain in that attitude till it be time to dress for
dinner.

room for the energy which stir uneasily under the dimness and
pressure of her own ignorance and the petty peremptoriness of the
world's habit.

now she would be allow to live continually in the light of a mind

proud delight--the joyous maiden surprise that she be choose by the

write.
MY DEAR MR. CASAUBON,--I be very grateful to you for love I, and
think I worthy to be your wife. I can look forward to no well
happiness than that which would be one with your. if I say more, it
would only be the same thing write out at great length, for I

your devotedly,
DOROTHEA BROOKE.
later in the evening she follow her uncle into the library to give

"be any one else come to dine besides Mr. Casaubon?"
"not that I know of."
"I hope there be some one else. then I shall not hear he eat his soup
so."
"what be there remarkable about his soup-eating?"
"really, Dodo, cannot you hear how he scrap his spoon? and he always
blink before he speak. I donot know whether Locke blink, but Ibe
sure I be sorry for those who sit opposite to he if he do."
"Celia," say Dorothea, with emphatic gravity, "pray donot make any
more observation of that kind."
"why not? they be quite true," return Celia, who have her reason
for persevering, though she be begin to be a little afraid.
"many thing be true which only the commonest mind observe."
"then I think the commonest mind must be rather useful. I think it be
a pity Mr. Casaubon's mother have not a commoner mind: she might have
teach he well." Celia be inwardly frightened, and ready to run
away, now she have hurl this light javelin.
Dorothea's feeling have gather to an avalanche, and there could be no
further preparation.
"it be right to tell you, Celia, that I be engaged to marry Mr.
Casaubon."
perhaps Celia have never turn so pale before. the paper man she be
make would have have his leg injure, but for her habitual care of
whatever she hold in her hand. she lay the fragile figure down at
once, and sit perfectly still for a few moment. when she speak there
be a tear gathering.
"oh, Dodo, I hope you will be happy." her sisterly tenderness could not
but surmount other feeling at this moment, and her fear be the
fear of affection.
Dorothea be still hurt and agitated.
"it be quite decide, then?" say Celia, in an awed under tone. "and
uncle know?"
"I have accept Mr. Casaubon's offer. my uncle bring I the letter
that contain it; he know about it beforehand."
"I beg your pardon, if I have say anything to hurt you, Dodo," say
Celia, with a slight sob. she never could have think that she should
feel as she do. there be something funereal in the whole affair, and
Mr. Casaubon seem to be the officiating clergyman, about whom it
would be indecent to make remark.
"never mind, Kitty, do not grieve. we should never admire the same
people. I often offend in something of the same way; I be apt to speak
too strongly of those who donot please I."
in spite of this magnanimity Dorothea be still smart: perhaps as
much from Celia's subdued astonishment as from her small criticism.
of course all the world round Tipton would be out of sympathy with this
marriage. Dorothea know of no one who think as she do about life
and its good object.
nevertheless before the evening be at an end she be very happy. in
an hour's tete-a-tete with Mr. Casaubon she talk to he with more
freedom than she have ever feel before, even pour out her joy at the
thought of devote herself to he, and of learn how she might well
share and far all his great end. Mr. Casaubon be touch with an
unknown delight (what man would not have be?) at this childlike
unrestrained ardor: he be not surprised (what lover would have be?)
that he should be the object of it.
"my dear young lady--Miss Brooke--Dorothea!" he say, press her hand
between his hand, "this be a happiness great than I have ever
imagine to be in reserve for I. that I should ever meet with a mind
and person so rich in the mingled grace which could render marriage
desirable, be far indeed from my conception. you have all--nay, more
than all--those quality which I have ever regard as the
characteristic excellence of womanhood. the great charm of your sex
be its capability of an ardent self-sacrifice affection, and herein
we see its fitness to round and complete the existence of our own.
Hitherto I have know few pleasure save of the severer kind: my
satisfaction have be those of the solitary student. I have be
little disposed to gather flower that would wither in my hand, but now
I shall pluck they with eagerness, to place they in your bosom."
no speech could have be more thoroughly honest in its intention: the
frigid rhetoric at the end be as sincere as the bark of a dog, or the
cawing of an amorous rook. would it not be rash to conclude that there
be no passion behind those sonnet to Delia which strike we as the
thin music of a mandolin?
Dorothea's faith supply all that Mr. Casaubon's word seem to leave
unsaid: what believer see a disturbing omission or infelicity? the
text, whether of prophet or of poet, expand for whatever we can put
into it, and even his bad grammar be sublime.
"I be very ignorant--you will quite wonder at my ignorance," say
Dorothea. "I have so many thought that may be quite mistaken; and now
I shall be able to tell they all to you, and ask you about they. but,"
she add, with rapid imagination of Mr. Casaubon's probable feeling,
"I will not trouble you too much; only when you be inclined to listen
to I. you must often be weary with the pursuit of subject in your
own track. I shall gain enough if you will take I with you there."
"how should I be able now to persevere in any path without your
companionship?" say Mr. Casaubon, kiss her candid brow, and feel
that heaven have vouchsafe he a blessing in every way suit to his
peculiar want. he be be unconsciously work upon by the charm
of a nature which be entirely without hide calculation either for
immediate effect or for remoter end. it be this which make Dorothea
so childlike, and, accord to some judge, so stupid, with all her
repute cleverness; as, for example, in the present case of throw
herself, metaphorically speak, at Mr. Casaubon's foot, and kiss
his unfashionable shoe-tie as if he be a Protestant Pope. she be
not in the least teach Mr. Casaubon to ask if he be good enough
for she, but merely ask herself anxiously how she could be good
enough for Mr. Casaubon. before he leave the next day it have be
decide that the marriage should take place within six week. why not?
Mr. Casaubon's house be ready. it be not a parsonage, but a
considerable mansion, with much land attach to it. the parsonage be
inhabit by the curate, who do all the duty except preach the
morning sermon.
CHAPTER VI.
my lady's tongue be like the meadow blade,
that cut you stroke they with idle hand.
nice cutting be her function: she divide
with spiritual edge the millet-seed,
and make intangible saving.
as Mr. Casaubon's carriage be pass out of the gateway, it arrest
the entrance of a pony phaeton drive by a lady with a servant seat
behind. it be doubtful whether the recognition have be mutual, for
Mr. Casaubon be look absently before he; but the lady be
quick-eye, and throw a nod and a "how do you do?" in the nick of time.
in spite of her shabby bonnet and very old indian shawl, it be plain
that the lodge-keeper regard she as an important personage, from the
low curtsy which be drop on the entrance of the small phaeton.
"well, Mrs. Fitchett, how be your fowl lay now?" say the
high-color, dark-eyed lady, with the clear chisel utterance.
"pretty well for lay, madam, but theyhave ta'en to eat their egg:
Ihave no peace o' mind with them at all."
"oh, the cannibal! well sell they cheap at once. what will you
sell they a couple? one cannot eat fowl of a bad character at a high
price."
"well, madam, half-a-crown: I couldnot let them go, not under."
"half-a-crown, these time! come now--for the Rector's chicken-broth
on a Sunday. he have consume all our that I can spare. you be half
pay with the sermon, Mrs. Fitchett, remember that. take a pair of
tumbler-pigeon for they--little beauty. you must come and see they.
you have no tumbler among your pigeon."
"well, madam, Master Fitchett shall go and see them after work. hebe
very hot on new sort; to oblige you."
"Oblige I! it will be the good bargain he ever make. a pair of
church pigeon for a couple of wicked spanish fowl that eat their own
egg! donot you and Fitchett boast too much, that be all!"
the phaeton be drive onwards with the last word, leave Mrs.

"sure_ly_, sure_ly_!"--from which it might be infer that she would
have find the country-side somewhat dull if the Rector's lady have
be less free-spoken and less of a skinflint. indeed, both the
farmer and laborer in the parish of Freshitt and Tipton would have
feel a sad lack of conversation but for the story about what Mrs.
Cadwallader say and do: a lady of immeasurably high birth, descend,
as it be, from unknown earl, dim as the crowd of heroic shade--who
plead poverty, par down price, and cut joke in the most
companionable manner, though with a turn of tongue that let you know
who she be. such a lady give a neighborliness to both rank and
religion, and mitigate the bitterness of uncommuted tithe. a much
more exemplary character with an infusion of sour dignity would not
have further their comprehension of the thirty-nine Articles, and
would have be less socially uniting.
Mr. Brooke, see Mrs. Cadwallader's merit from a different point of
view, wince a little when her name be announce in the library, where
he be sit alone.
"I see you have have our Lowick Cicero here," she say, seat herself
comfortably, throw back her wrap, and show a thin but well-build
figure. "I suspect you and he be brew some bad polity, else you
would not be see so much of the lively man. I shall inform against
you: remember you be both suspicious character since you take Peel's
side about the Catholic Bill. I shall tell everybody that you be
go to put up for Middlemarch on the Whig side when old Pinkerton
resign, and that Casaubon be go to help you in an underhand manner:
go to bribe the voter with pamphlet, and throw open the
public-house to distribute they. come, confess!"
"nothing of the sort," say Mr. Brooke, smile and rub his
eye-glass, but really blush a little at the impeachment.
"Casaubon and I donot talk politic much. he donot care much about
the philanthropic side of thing; punishment, and that kind of thing.
he only care about Church question. that be not my line of action,
you know."
"Ra-a-ther too much, my friend. I have hear of your doing. who be
it that sell his bit of land to the Papists at Middlemarch? I believe
you buy it on purpose. you be a perfect Guy Faux. see if you be
not burn in effigy this 5th of November come. Humphrey would not
come to quarrel with you about it, so I be come."
"very good. I be prepared to be persecute for not persecuting--not
persecuting, you know."
"there you go! that be a piece of clap-trap you have get ready for the
husting. now, _do not_ let they lure you to the husting, my dear Mr.
Brooke. a man always make a fool of himself, speechify: therebe no
excuse but be on the right side, so that you can ask a blessing on
your humming and hawing. you will lose yourself, I forewarn you. you
will make a Saturday pie of all party' opinion, and be pelt by
everybody."
"that be what I expect, you know," say Mr. Brooke, not wish to
betray how little he enjoy this prophetic sketch--"what I expect as
an independent man. as to the Whigs, a man who go with the thinker
be not likely to be hook on by any party. he may go with they up to
a certain point--up to a certain point, you know. but that be what you
lady never understand."
"where your certain point be? no. I should like to be tell how a man
can have any certain point when he belong to no party--lead a
rove life, and never let his friend know his address. 'nobody
know where Brooke will be--therebe no counting on Brooke'--that be
what people say of you, to be quite frank. now, do turn respectable.
how will you like go to Sessions with everybody look shy on you,
and you with a bad conscience and an empty pocket?"
"I donot pretend to argue with a lady on politic," say Mr. Brooke,
with an air of smile indifference, but feel rather unpleasantly
conscious that this attack of Mrs. Cadwallader's have open the

thing. you donot know Virgil. I knew"--mr. Brooke reflect in time
that he have not have the personal acquaintance of the Augustan poet--"I
be go to say, poor Stoddart, you know. that be what _he_ say.
you lady be always against an independent attitude--a man's caring
for nothing but truth, and that sort of thing. and there be no part of
the county where opinion be narrow than it be here--I donot mean to
throw stone, you know, but somebody be want to take the independent
line; and if I donot take it, who will?"
"who? why, any upstart who have get neither blood nor position. People
of standing should consume their independent nonsense at home, not hawk

Mr. Brooke again wince inwardly, for Dorothea's engagement have no

flavor in the name of Brooke if it be deliver casually, like wine

Mrs. Cadwallader's mind be rapidly survey the possibility of

but here Celia enter, bloom from a walk in the garden, and the

child, like we, you know, cannot afford to keep a good cook. I have

in less than an hour, Mrs. Cadwallader have circumvent Mrs. Carter and

it be of no use protest, against Mrs. Cadwallader's way of put

now, why on earth should Mrs. Cadwallader have be at all busy about
Miss Brooke's marriage; and why, when one match that she like to think
she have a hand in be frustrate, should she have straightway contrive
the preliminary of another? be there any ingenious plot, any
hide-and-seek course of action, which might be detect by a careful
telescopic watch? not at all: a telescope might have sweep the
parish of Tipton and Freshitt, the whole area visit by Mrs.
Cadwallader in her phaeton, without witness any interview that could
excite suspicion, or any scene from which she do not return with the
same unperturbed keenness of eye and the same high natural color. in
fact, if that convenient vehicle have exist in the day of the Seven
Sages, one of they would doubtless have remark, that you can know
little of woman by follow they about in their pony-phaeton. even
with a microscope direct on a water-drop we find ourselves make
interpretation which turn out to be rather coarse; for whereas under a
weak lens you may seem to see a creature exhibit an active voracity
into which other small creature actively play as if they be so
many animate tax-penny, a strong lens reveal to you certain
tiny hairlet which make vortex for these victim while the
swallower wait passively at his receipt of custom. in this way,
metaphorically speak, a strong lens apply to Mrs. Cadwallader's
match-making will show a play of minute cause produce what may be
call thought and speech vortex to bring she the sort of food she
need. her life be rurally simple, quite free from secret either
foul, dangerous, or otherwise important, and not consciously affect
by the great affair of the world. all the more do the affair of the
great world interest she, when communicate in the letter of high-bear
relation: the way in which fascinating young son have go to the
dog by marry their mistress; the fine old-blooded idiocy of young
Lord Tapir, and the furious gouty humor of old Lord Megatherium; the
exact crossing of genealogy which have bring a coronet into a new
branch and widen the relation of scandal,--these be topic of
which she retain detail with the utmost accuracy, and reproduce
they in an excellent pickle of epigram, which she herself enjoy the
more because she believe as unquestionably in birth and no-birth as
she do in game and vermin. she would never have disown any one on
the ground of poverty: a De Bracy reduce to take his dinner in a basin
would have seem to she an example of pathos worth exaggerate, and I
fear his aristocratic vice would not have horrify she. but she
feeling towards the vulgar rich be a sort of religious hatred: they
have probably make all their money out of high retail price, and Mrs.
Cadwallader detest high price for everything that be not pay in
kind at the Rectory: such people be no part of God's design in make
the world; and their accent be an affliction to the ear. a town
where such monster abound be hardly more than a sort of low comedy,
which could not be take account of in a well-breed scheme of the
universe. let any lady who be inclined to be hard on Mrs. Cadwallader
inquire into the comprehensiveness of her own beautiful view, and be
quite sure that they afford accommodation for all the life which have
the honor to coexist with her.
with such a mind, active as phosphorus, bite everything that come
near into the form that suit it, how could Mrs. Cadwallader feel that
the Miss Brookes and their matrimonial prospect be alien to she?
especially as it have be the habit of year for she to scold Mr.
Brooke with the friendly frankness, and let he know in confidence
that she think he a poor creature. from the first arrival of the
young lady in Tipton she have prearrange Dorothea's marriage with Sir
James, and if it have take place would have be quite sure that it be
she do: that it should not take place after she have preconceive it,
cause she an irritation which every thinker will sympathize with. she
be the diplomatist of Tipton and Freshitt, and for anything to happen
in spite of she be an offensive irregularity. as to freak like this
of Miss Brooke's, Mrs. Cadwallader have no patience with they, and now
see that her opinion of this girl have be infect with some of her
husband's weak charitableness: those methodistical whim, that air of
be more religious than the rector and curate together, come from a
deep and more constitutional disease than she have be willing to
believe.
"however," say Mrs. Cadwallader, first to herself and afterwards to
her husband, "I throw she over: there be a chance, if she have marry
Sir James, of she become a sane, sensible woman. he would never have
contradict she, and when a woman be not contradict, she have no
motive for obstinacy in her absurdity. but now I wish her joy of her
hair shirt."
it follow that Mrs. Cadwallader must decide on another match for Sir
James, and have make up her mind that it be to be the young Miss
Brooke, there could not have be a more skilful move towards the
success of her plan than her hint to the baronet that he have make an
impression on Celia's heart. for he be not one of those gentleman who
languish after the unattainable Sappho's apple that laugh from the
topmost bough--the charm which
"smile like the knot of cowslip on the cliff,
not to be come at by the willing hand."
he have no sonnet to write, and it could not strike he agreeably that
he be not an object of preference to the woman whom he have prefer.
already the knowledge that Dorothea have choose Mr. Casaubon have bruise
his attachment and relax its hold. although Sir James be a

and fox, and do not regard his future wife in the light of prey,

well acquainted with the habit of primitive race as to feel that an

little thread of tenderness from out his heart towards her.
thus it happen, that after Sir James have ride rather fast for half
