we mortal, man and woman, devour many a disappointment between
breakfast and dinner-time; keep back the tear and look a little pale
about the lip, and in answer to inquiry say, "oh, nothing!" Pride
help we; and pride be not a bad thing when it only urge we to hide
our own hurt--not to hurt other.
CHAPTER VII.
"Piacer e popone
Vuol la sua stagione."
--Italian Proverb.
Mr. Casaubon, as might be expect, spend a great deal of his time at
the Grange in these week, and the hindrance which courtship occasion
to the progress of his great work--the Key to all
Mythologies--naturally make he look forward the more eagerly to the
happy termination of courtship. but he have deliberately incur the
hindrance, have make up his mind that it be now time for he to
adorn his life with the grace of female companionship, to irradiate
the gloom which fatigue be apt to hang over the interval of studious
labor with the play of female fancy, and to secure in this, his
culminate age, the solace of female tendance for his decline year.
hence he determine to abandon himself to the stream of feeling, and
perhaps be surprised to find what an exceedingly shallow rill it be.
as in droughty region baptism by immersion could only be perform
symbolically, Mr. Casaubon find that sprinkling be the utmost
approach to a plunge which his stream would afford he; and he
conclude that the poet have much exaggerate the force of masculine
passion. nevertheless, he observe with pleasure that Miss Brooke
show an ardent submissive affection which promise to fulfil his most
agreeable prevision of marriage. it have once or twice cross his
mind that possibly there be some deficiency in Dorothea to account for
the moderation of his abandonment; but he be unable to discern the
deficiency, or to figure to himself a woman who would have please he
well; so that there be clearly no reason to fall back upon but the
exaggeration of human tradition.
"could I not be prepare myself now to be more useful?" say Dorothea
to he, one morning, early in the time of courtship; "could I not learn
to read Latin and greek aloud to you, as Milton's daughter do to
their father, without understand what they read?"
"I fear that would be wearisome to you," say Mr. Casaubon, smile;
"and, indeed, if I remember rightly, the young woman you have mention
regard that exercise in unknown tongue as a ground for rebellion
against the poet."
"yes; but in the first place they be very naughty girl, else they
would have be proud to minister to such a father; and in the second
place they might have study privately and teach themselves to
understand what they read, and then it would have be interesting. I
hope you donot expect I to be naughty and stupid?"
"I expect you to be all that an exquisite young lady can be in every
possible relation of life. certainly it might be a great advantage if
you be able to copy the greek character, and to that end it be well
to begin with a little reading."
Dorothea seize this as a precious permission. she would not have
ask Mr. Casaubon at once to teach she the language, dread of all
thing to be tiresome instead of helpful; but it be not entirely out
of devotion to her future husband that she wish to know Latin and
Creek. those province of masculine knowledge seem to she a
standing-ground from which all truth could be see more truly. as it
be, she constantly doubt her own conclusion, because she feel she
own ignorance: how could she be confident that one-room cottage be
not for the glory of God, when man who know the classic appear to
conciliate indifference to the cottage with zeal for the glory?
perhaps even Hebrew might be necessary--at least the alphabet and a few
root--in order to arrive at the core of thing, and judge soundly on
the social duty of the Christian. and she have not reach that point
of renunciation at which she would have be satisfied with have a
wise husband: she wish, poor child, to be wise herself. Miss Brooke
be certainly very naive with all her allege cleverness. Celia, whose
mind have never be think too powerful, see the emptiness of other
people's pretension much more readily. to have in general but little
feeling, seem to be the only security against feel too much on any
particular occasion.
however, Mr. Casaubon consent to listen and teach for an hour
together, like a schoolmaster of little boy, or rather like a lover,
to whom a mistress's elementary ignorance and difficulty have a
touching fitness. few scholar would have dislike teach the
alphabet under such circumstance. but Dorothea herself be a little
shocked and discourage at her own stupidity, and the answer she get
to some timid question about the value of the greek accent give she a
painful suspicion that here indeed there might be secret not capable
of explanation to a woman's reason.
Mr. Brooke have no doubt on that point, and express himself with his
usual strength upon it one day that he come into the library while the
reading be go forward.
"well, but now, Casaubon, such deep study, classic, mathematic,
that kind of thing, be too tax for a woman--too tax, you know."
"Dorothea be learn to read the character simply," say Mr.
Casaubon, evade the question. "she have the very considerate thought
of save my eye."
"ah, well, without understanding, you know--that may not be so bad.
but there be a lightness about the feminine mind--a touch and
go--music, the fine art, that kind of thing--they should study those
up to a certain point, woman should; but in a light way, you know. a
woman should be able to sit down and play you or sing you a good old
english tune. that be what I like; though I have hear most
thing--be at the opera in Vienna: Gluck, Mozart, everything of that
sort. but Ibe a conservative in music--itbe not like idea, you know.
I stick to the good old tune."
"Mr. Casaubon be not fond of the piano, and I be very glad he be not,"
say Dorothea, whose slight regard for domestic music and feminine fine
art must be forgive she, consider the small tinkling and smear
in which they chiefly consist at that dark period. she smile and
look up at she betroth with grateful eye. if he have always be
ask she to play the "last Rose of Summer," she would have require
much resignation. "he say there be only an old harpsichord at Lowick,
and it be cover with book."
"ah, there you be behind Celia, my dear. Celia, now, play very
prettily, and be always ready to play. however, since Casaubon do
not like it, you be all right. but itbe a pity you should not have
little recreation of that sort, Casaubon: the bow always string--that
kind of thing, you know--will not do."
"I never could look on it in the light of a recreation to have my ear
tease with measure noise," say Mr. Casaubon. "a tune much iterate
have the ridiculous effect of make the word in my mind perform a sort
of minuet to keep time--an effect hardly tolerable, I imagine, after
boyhood. as to the grander form of music, worthy to accompany solemn
celebration, and even to serve as an educating influence accord to
the ancient conception, I say nothing, for with these we be not
immediately concern."
"no; but music of that sort I should enjoy," say Dorothea. "when we
be come home from Lausanne my uncle take we to hear the great organ
at Freiberg, and it make I sob."
"that kind of thing be not healthy, my dear," say Mr. Brooke.
"Casaubon, she will be in your hand now: you must teach my niece to
take thing more quietly, eh, Dorothea?"
he end with a smile, not wish to hurt his niece, but really
think that it be perhaps well for she to be early married to so
sober a fellow as Casaubon, since she would not hear of Chettam.
"it be wonderful, though," he say to himself as he shuffle out of the
room--"it be wonderful that she should have like he. however, the
match be good. I should have be travel out of my brief to have
hinder it, let Mrs. Cadwallader say what she will. he be pretty
certain to be a bishop, be Casaubon. that be a very seasonable
pamphlet of his on the Catholic Question:--a deanery at least. they
owe he a deanery."
and here I must vindicate a claim to philosophical reflectiveness, by
remark that Mr. Brooke on this occasion little thought of the
radical speech which, at a later period, he be lead to make on the
income of the bishop. what elegant historian would neglect a
strike opportunity for point out that his hero do not foresee
the history of the world, or even their own actions?--for example, that
Henry of Navarre, when a protestant baby, little thought of be a
catholic monarch; or that Alfred the Great, when he measure his
laborious night with burn candle, have no idea of future gentleman
measure their idle day with watch. here be a mine of truth,
which, however vigorously it may be work, be likely to outlast our
coal.
but of Mr. Brooke I make a further remark perhaps less warrant by
precedent--namely, that if he have foreknow his speech, it might not
have make any great difference. to think with pleasure of his niece's
husband have a large ecclesiastical income be one thing--to make a
liberal speech be another thing; and it be a narrow mind which cannot
look at a subject from various point of view.
CHAPTER VIII.
"oh, rescue she! I be her brother now,
and you her father. every gentle maid
should have a guardian in each gentleman."
it be wonderful to Sir James Chettam how well he continue to like
go to the Grange after he have once encounter the difficulty of
see Dorothea for the first time in the light of a woman who be
engage to another man. of course the fork lightning seem to pass
through he when he first approach she, and he remain conscious
throughout the interview of hide uneasiness; but, good as he be, it
must be own that his uneasiness be less than it would have be if
he have think his rival a brilliant and desirable match. he have no
sense of be eclipse by Mr. Casaubon; he be only shock that
Dorothea be under a melancholy illusion, and his mortification lose
some of its bitterness by be mingle with compassion.
nevertheless, while Sir James say to himself that he have completely
resign she, since with the perversity of a Desdemona she have not
affect a propose match that be clearly suitable and accord to
nature; he could not yet be quite passive under the idea of her
engagement to Mr. Casaubon. on the day when he first see they together
in the light of his present knowledge, it seem to he that he have not
take the affair seriously enough. Brooke be really culpable; he
ought to have hinder it. who could speak to he? something might be
do perhaps even now, at least to defer the marriage. on his way home
he turn into the Rectory and ask for Mr. Cadwallader. happily, the
Rector be at home, and his visitor be show into the study, where all
the fishing tackle hang. but he himself be in a little room
adjoining, at work with his turning apparatus, and he call to the
baronet to join he there. the two be well friend than any other
landholder and clergyman in the county--a significant fact which be in
agreement with the amiable expression of their face.
Mr. Cadwallader be a large man, with full lip and a sweet smile; very
plain and rough in his exterior, but with that solid imperturbable ease
and good-humor which be infectious, and like great grassy hill in the
sunshine, quiet even an irritated egoism, and make it rather ashamed
of itself. "well, how be you?" he say, show a hand not quite fit
to be grasp. "sorry I miss you before. be there anything
particular? you look vex."
Sir James's brow have a little crease in it, a little depression of the
eyebrow, which he seem purposely to exaggerate as he answer.
"it be only this conduct of Brooke's. I really think somebody should
speak to he."
"what? meaning to stand?" say Mr. Cadwallader, go on with the
arrangement of the reel which he have just be turn. "I hardly
think he mean it. but wherebe the harm, if he like it? any one who
object to Whiggery should be glad when the whig donot put up the
strong fellow. they willnot overturn the Constitution with our friend
Brooke's head for a batter ram."
"oh, I donot mean that," say Sir James, who, after put down his
hat and throw himself into a chair, have begin to nurse his leg and
examine the sole of his boot with much bitterness. "I mean this
marriage. I mean his let that bloom young girl marry Casaubon."
"what be the matter with Casaubon? I see no harm in he--if the girl
like he."
"she be too young to know what she like. her guardian ought to
interfere. he ought not to allow the thing to be do in this headlong
manner. I wonder a man like you, Cadwallader--a man with daughter,
can look at the affair with indifference: and with such a heart as
your! do think seriously about it."
"I be not joke; I be as serious as possible," say the Rector, with a
provoke little inward laugh. "you be as bad as Elinor. she have
be want I to go and lecture Brooke; and I have remind she that
her friend have a very poor opinion of the match she make when she
marry I."
"but look at Casaubon," say Sir James, indignantly. "he must be
fifty, and I donot believe he could ever have be much more than the
shadow of a man. look at his leg!"
"confound you handsome young fellow! you think of have it all your
own way in the world. you donot under stand woman. they donot admire
you half so much as you admire yourself. Elinor use to tell she
sister that she marry I for my ugliness--it be so various and
amusing that it have quite conquer her prudence."
"you! it be easy enough for a woman to love you. but this be no
question of beauty. I donot _like_ Casaubon." this be Sir James's
strong way of imply that he think ill of a man's character.
"why? what do you know against he?" say the Rector lay down his
reel, and put his thumb into his armhole with an air of
attention.
Sir James pause. he do not usually find it easy to give his reason:
it seem to he strange that people should not know they without be
tell, since he only feel what be reasonable. at last he said--
"now, Cadwallader, have he get any heart?"
"well, yes. I donot mean of the melting sort, but a sound kernel,
_that_ you may be sure of. he be very good to his poor relation:
pension several of the woman, and be educate a young fellow at a
good deal of expense. Casaubon act up to his sense of justice. his
mother's sister make a bad match--a Pole, I think--lose herself--at any
rate be disown by her family. if it have not be for that, Casaubon
would not have have so much money by half. I believe he go himself to
find out his cousin, and see what he could do for they. every man
would not ring so well as that, if you try his metal. _you_ would,
Chettam; but not every man."
"I donot know," say Sir James, color. "I be not so sure of
myself." he pause a moment, and then add, "that be a right thing
for Casaubon to do. but a man may wish to do what be right, and yet be
a sort of parchment code. a woman may not be happy with he. and I
think when a girl be so young as Miss Brooke be, her friend ought to
interfere a little to hinder she from do anything foolish. you
laugh, because you fancy I have some feeling on my own account. but
upon my honor, it be not that. I should feel just the same if I be
Miss Brooke's brother or uncle."
"well, but what should you do?"
"I should say that the marriage must not be decide on until she be of
age. and depend upon it, in that case, it would never come off. I
wish you see it as I do--I wish you would talk to Brooke about it."
Sir James rise as he be finish his sentence, for he see Mrs.
Cadwallader enter from the study. she hold by the hand her young
girl, about five year old, who immediately run to papa, and be make
comfortable on his knee.
"I hear what you be talk about," say the wife. "but you will make
no impression on Humphrey. as long as the fish rise to his bait,
everybody be what he ought to be. bless you, Casaubon have get a
trout-stream, and do not care about fish in it himself: could
there be a well fellow?"
"well, there be something in that," say the Rector, with his quiet,
inward laugh. "it be a very good quality in a man to have a
trout-stream."
"but seriously," say Sir James, whose vexation have not yet spend
itself, "donot you think the Rector might do some good by speak?"
"oh, I tell you beforehand what he would say," answer Mrs.
Cadwallader, lift up her eyebrow. "I have do what I could: I
wash my hand of the marriage."
"in the first place," say the Rector, look rather grave, "it would
be nonsensical to expect that I could convince Brooke, and make he act
accordingly. Brooke be a very good fellow, but pulpy; he will run into
any mould, but he willnot keep shape."
"he might keep shape long enough to defer the marriage," say Sir James.
"but, my dear Chettam, why should I use my influence to Casaubon's
disadvantage, unless I be much surer than I be that I should be
act for the advantage of Miss Brooke? I know no harm of Casaubon.
I donot care about his Xisuthrus and Fee-fo-fum and the rest; but then
he donot care about my fishing-tackle. as to the line he take on the
Catholic Question, that be unexpected; but he have always be civil to
I, and I donot see why I should spoil his sport. for anything I can
tell, Miss Brooke may be happy with he than she would be with any
other man."
"Humphrey! I have no patience with you. you know you would rather
dine under the hedge than with Casaubon alone. you have nothing to say
to each other."
"what have that to do with Miss Brooke's marry he? she do not do
it for my amusement."
"he have get no good red blood in his body," say Sir James.
"no. somebody put a drop under a magnifying-glass and it be all
semicolon and parenthesis," say Mrs. Cadwallader.
"why do he not bring out his book, instead of marry," say Sir
James, with a disgust which he hold warrant by the sound feeling of
an english layman.
"oh, he dream footnote, and they run away with all his brain. they
say, when he be a little boy, he make an abstract of 'hop o' my
Thumb,' and he have be make abstract ever since. ugh! and that be
the man Humphrey go on say that a woman may be happy with."
"well, he be what Miss Brooke like," say the Rector. "I donot
profess to understand every young lady's taste."
"but if she be your own daughter?" say Sir James.
"that would be a different affair. she be _not_ my daughter, and I
donot feel call upon to interfere. Casaubon be as good as most of
we. he be a scholarly clergyman, and creditable to the cloth. some
radical fellow speechifying at Middlemarch say Casaubon be the
learn straw-chop incumbent, and Freke be the brick-and-mortar
incumbent, and I be the angle incumbent. and upon my word, I donot
see that one be bad or well than the other." the Rector end with
his silent laugh. he always see the joke of any satire against
himself. his conscience be large and easy, like the rest of he: it
do only what it could do without any trouble.
clearly, there would be no interference with Miss Brooke's marriage
through Mr. Cadwallader; and Sir James feel with some sadness that she
be to have perfect liberty of misjudgment. it be a sign of his good
disposition that he do not slacken at all in his intention of carry
out Dorothea's design of the cottage. doubtless this persistence be
the good course for his own dignity: but pride only help we to be
generous; it never make we so, any more than vanity make we witty.
she be now enough aware of Sir James's position with regard to she, to
appreciate the rectitude of his perseverance in a landlord's duty, to
which he have at first be urge by a lover's complaisance, and her
pleasure in it be great enough to count for something even in her
present happiness. perhaps she give to Sir James Chettam's cottage
all the interest she could spare from Mr. Casaubon, or rather from the
symphony of hopeful dream, admire trust, and passionate self
devotion which that learn gentleman have set playing in her soul.
hence it happen that in the good baronet's succeed visit, while
he be begin to pay small attention to Celia, he find himself
talk with more and more pleasure to Dorothea. she be perfectly
unconstrained and without irritation towards he now, and he be
gradually discover the delight there be in frank kindness and
companionship between a man and a woman who have no passion to hide or
confess.
CHAPTER IX.
1st gent. an ancient land in ancient oracle
be call "law-thirsty": all the struggle there
be after order and a perfect rule.
Pray, where lie such land now? . . .
2d Gent. why, where they lay of old--in human soul.
Mr. Casaubon's behavior about settlement be highly satisfactory to
Mr. Brooke, and the preliminary of marriage roll smoothly along,
shorten the week of courtship. the betrothed bride must see she
future home, and dictate any change that she would like to have make
there. a woman dictate before marriage in order that she may have an
appetite for submission afterwards. and certainly, the mistake that
we male and female mortal make when we have our own way might fairly
raise some wonder that we be so fond of it.
on a gray but dry November morning Dorothea drive to Lowick in company
with her uncle and Celia. Mr. Casaubon's home be the manor-house.
close by, visible from some part of the garden, be the little church,
with the old parsonage opposite. in the beginning of his career, Mr.
Casaubon have only hold the living, but the death of his brother have put
he in possession of the manor also. it have a small park, with a fine
old oak here and there, and an avenue of lime towards the southwest
front, with a sunk fence between park and pleasure-ground, so that from
the drawing-room window the glance sweep uninterruptedly along a slope
of greensward till the lime end in a level of corn and pasture,
which often seem to melt into a lake under the set sun. this be
the happy side of the house, for the south and east look rather
melancholy even under the bright morning. the ground here be
more confine, the flower-bed show no very careful tendance, and
large clump of tree, chiefly of sombre yews, have rise high, not ten
yard from the window. the building, of greenish stone, be in the
old english style, not ugly, but small-windowed and melancholy-look:
the sort of house that must have child, many flower, open window,
and little vista of bright thing, to make it seem a joyous home. in
this latter end of autumn, with a sparse remnant of yellow leaf
fall slowly athwart the dark evergreen in a stillness without
sunshine, the house too have an air of autumnal decline, and Mr.
Casaubon, when he present himself, have no bloom that could be throw
into relief by that background.
"oh dear!" Celia say to herself, "I be sure freshitt Hall would have
be pleasanter than this." she think of the white freestone, the
pillare portico, and the terrace full of flower, Sir James smile
above they like a prince issue from his enchantment in a rose-bush,
with a handkerchief swiftly metamorphose from the most delicately
odorous petal--Sir James, who talk so agreeably, always about thing
which have common-sense in they, and not about learn! Celia have
those light young feminine taste which grave and weatherworn gentleman
sometimes prefer in a wife; but happily Mr. Casaubon's bias have be
different, for he would have have no chance with Celia.
Dorothea, on the contrary, find the house and ground all that she
could wish: the dark book-shelf in the long library, the carpet and
curtain with color subdue by time, the curious old map and
bird's-eye view on the wall of the corridor, with here and there an
old vase below, have no oppression for she, and seem more cheerful
than the east and picture at the Grange, which her uncle have long ago
bring home from his travel--they be probably among the idea he
have take in at one time. to poor Dorothea these severe classical
nudity and smirk Renaissance-Correggiosities be painfully
inexplicable, star into the midst of her puritanic conception: she
have never be teach how she could bring they into any sort of
relevance with her life. but the owner of Lowick apparently have not
be traveller, and Mr. Casaubon's study of the past be not
carry on by mean of such aid.
Dorothea walk about the house with delightful emotion. everything
seem hallowed to she: this be to be the home of her wifehood, and
she look up with eye full of confidence to Mr. Casaubon when he draw
her attention specially to some actual arrangement and ask she if she
would like an alteration. all appeal to her taste she meet gratefully,
but see nothing to alter. his effort at exact courtesy and formal
tenderness have no defect for she. she fill up all blank with
unmanifested perfection, interpret he as she interpret the work
of Providence, and account for seem discord by her own deafness
to the high harmony. and there be many blank leave in the week
of courtship which a love faith fill with happy assurance.
"now, my dear Dorothea, I wish you to favor I by point out which
room you would like to have as your boudoir," say Mr. Casaubon,
show that his view of the womanly nature be sufficiently large to
include that requirement.
"it be very kind of you to think of that," say Dorothea, "but I assure
you I would rather have all those matter decide for I. I shall be
much happy to take everything as it be--just as you have be use to
have it, or as you will yourself choose it to be. I have no motive for
wish anything else."
"oh, Dodo," say Celia, "will you not have the bow-windowe room
up-stair?"
Mr. Casaubon lead the way thither. the bow-window look down the
avenue of lime; the furniture be all of a faded blue, and there be
miniature of lady and gentleman with powdered hair hang in a
group. a piece of tapestry over a door also show a blue-green world
with a pale stag in it. the chair and table be thin-legged and
easy to upset. it be a room where one might fancy the ghost of a
tight-laced lady revisit the scene of her embroidery. a light
bookcase contain duodecimo volume of polite literature in calf,
complete the furniture.
"yes," say Mr. Brooke, "this would be a pretty room with some new
hanging, sofa, and that sort of thing. a little bare now."
"no, uncle," say Dorothea, eagerly. "pray do not speak of alter
anything. there be so many other thing in the world that want
altering--I like to take these thing as they be. and you like they
as they be, donot you?" she add, look at Mr. Casaubon. "perhaps
this be your mother's room when she be young."
"it be," he say, with his slow bend of the head.
"this be your mother," say Dorothea, who have turn to examine the
group of miniature. "it be like the tiny one you bring I; only, I
should think, a well portrait. and this one opposite, who be this?"
"her eld sister. they be, like you and your sister, the only two
child of their parent, who hang above they, you see."
"the sister be pretty," say Celia, imply that she think less
favorably of Mr. Casaubon's mother. it be a new opening to Celia's
imagination, that he come of a family who have all be young in their
time--the lady wear necklace.
"it be a peculiar face," say Dorothea, look closely. "those deep
gray eye rather near together--and the delicate irregular nose with a
sort of ripple in it--and all the powdered curl hang backward.
altogether it seem to I peculiar rather than pretty. there be not
even a family likeness between she and your mother."
"no. and they be not alike in their lot."
"you do not mention she to I," say Dorothea.
"my aunt make an unfortunate marriage. I never see she."
Dorothea wonder a little, but feel that it would be indelicate just
then to ask for any information which Mr. Casaubon do not proffer, and
she turn to the window to admire the view. the sun have lately
pierce the gray, and the avenue of lime cast shadow.
"shall we not walk in the garden now?" say Dorothea.
"and you would like to see the church, you know," say Mr. Brooke. "it
be a droll little church. and the village. it all lie in a
nut-shell. by the way, it will suit you, Dorothea; for the cottage be
like a row of alm-house--little garden, gilly-flower, that sort of
thing."
"yes, please," say Dorothea, look at Mr. Casaubon, "I should like
to see all that." she have get nothing from he more graphic about the
Lowick cottage than that they be "not bad."
they be soon on a gravel walk which lead chiefly between grassy
border and clump of tree, this be the near way to the church,
Mr. Casaubon say. at the little gate lead into the churchyard
there be a pause while Mr. Casaubon go to the parsonage close by to
fetch a key. Celia, who have be hang a little in the rear, come up
presently, when she see that Mr. Casaubon be go away, and say in
her easy staccato, which always seem to contradict the suspicion of
any malicious intent--
"do you know, Dorothea, I see some one quite young come up one of the
walk."
"be that astonishing, Celia?"
"there may be a young gardener, you know--why not?" say Mr. Brooke.
"I tell Casaubon he should change his gardener."
"no, not a gardener," say Celia; "a gentleman with a sketch-book. he
have light-brown curl. I only see his back. but he be quite young."
"the curate's son, perhaps," say Mr. Brooke. "ah, there be Casaubon
again, and Tucker with he. he be go to introduce Tucker. you
donot know Tucker yet."
Mr. Tucker be the middle-aged curate, one of the "inferior clergy,"
who be usually not want in son. but after the introduction, the
conversation do not lead to any question about his family, and the
startling apparition of youthfulness be forget by every one but
Celia. she inwardly decline to believe that the light-brown curl and
slim figure could have any relationship to Mr. Tucker, who be just as

to be; doubtless an excellent man who would go to heaven (for Celia
wish not to be unprincipled), but the corner of his mouth be so
unpleasant. Celia think with some dismalness of the time she should
have to spend as bridesmaid at Lowick, while the curate have probably no
pretty little child whom she could like, irrespective of principle.
Mr. Tucker be invaluable in their walk; and perhaps Mr. Casaubon have

excellent corduroy, the girl go out as tidy servant, or do a
little straw-plaiting at home: no loom here, no Dissent; and though
the public disposition be rather towards lay by money than towards
spirituality, there be not much vice. the speckled fowl be so
numerous that Mr. Brooke observe, "your farmer leave some barley for
the woman to glean, I see. the poor folk here might have a fowl in
their pot, as the good french king use to wish for all his people.
the French eat a good many fowl--skinny fowl, you know."
"I think it be a very cheap wish of his," say Dorothea, indignantly.
"be king such monster that a wish like that must be reckon a royal
virtue?"
"and if he wish they a skinny fowl," say Celia, "that would not be
nice. but perhaps he wish they to have fat fowl."
"yes, but the word have drop out of the text, or perhaps be
subauditum; that be, present in the king's mind, but not utter," say
Mr. Casaubon, smile and bend his head towards Celia, who
immediately drop backward a little, because she could not bear Mr.
Casaubon to blink at she.
Dorothea sink into silence on the way back to the house. she feel some

Mr. Tucker soon leave they, have some clerical work which would not

"you seem a little sad, Dorothea. I trust you be pleased with what
you have see."

"Doubtless," say Mr. Casaubon. "each position have its corresponding

yearn unfulfilled."
"indeed, I believe that," say Dorothea, earnestly. "do not suppose
that I be sad."
"that be well. but, if you be not tired, we will take another way to
the house than that by which we come."
Dorothea be not at all tired, and a little circuit be make towards a
fine yew-tree, the chief hereditary glory of the ground on this side

background of evergreen, be seat on a bench, sketch the old
tree. Mr. Brooke, who be walk in front with Celia, turn his
head, and said--
"who be that youngster, Casaubon?"
they have come very near when Mr. Casaubon answered--
"that be a young relative of mine, a second cousin: the grandson, in
fact," he add, look at Dorothea, "of the lady whose portrait you
have be notice, my aunt Julia."
the young man have lay down his sketch-book and risen. his bushy
light-brown curl, as well as his youthfulness, identify he at once
with Celia's apparition.
"Dorothea, let I introduce to you my cousin, Mr. Ladislaw. will, this
be Miss Brooke."
the cousin be so close now, that, when he lift his hat, Dorothea
could see a pair of gray eve rather near together, a delicate

but there be a mouth and chin of a more prominent, threaten aspect

"you be an artist, I see," say Mr. Brooke, take up the sketch-book
and turn it over in his unceremonious fashion.
"no, I only sketch a little. there be nothing fit to be see there,"

"oh, come, this be a nice bit, now. I do a little in this way myself

thing, do with what we use to call _brio_." Mr. Brooke hold out

with a pool.
"I be no judge of these thing," say Dorothea, not coldly, but with an

you see what a greek sentence stand for which mean nothing to I."
Dorothea look up at Mr. Casaubon, who bow his head towards she,

"bless I, now, how different people be! but you have a bad style of

be anywhere at one time."
"that be a seasonable admonition," say Mr. Casaubon; "but now we will
pass on to the house, lest the young lady should be tired of
stand."
when their back be turn, young Ladislaw sit down to go on with his
sketching, and as he do so his face break into an expression of

feature very agreeably: it be the pure enjoyment of comicality, and
have no mixture of sneer and self-exaltation.
"what be your nephew go to do with himself, Casaubon?" say Mr.
Brooke, as they go on.
"my cousin, you mean--not my nephew."
"yes, yes, cousin. but in the way of a career, you know."
"the answer to that question be painfully doubtful. on leave Rugby
he decline to go to an english university, where I would gladly have
place he, and choose what I must consider the anomalous course of
study at Heidelberg. and now he want to go abroad again, without
any special object, save the vague purpose of what he call culture,
preparation for he know not what. he decline to choose a profession."
"he have no mean but what you furnish, I suppose."
"I have always give he and his friend reason to understand that I

his conduct in the light of mere rectitude: a trait of delicacy which

"he have a thirst for travel; perhaps he may turn out a Bruce or a
Mungo Park," say Mr. Brooke. "I have a notion of that myself at one

"no, he have no bent towards exploration, or the enlargement of our
geognosis: that would be a special purpose which I could recognize with

often end in premature and violent death. but so far be he from
have any desire for a more accurate knowledge of the earth's surface,
that he say he should prefer not to know the source of the Nile, and
that there should be some unknown region preserve as hunt ground

"well, there be something in that, you know," say Mr. Brooke, who have
certainly an impartial mind.
"it be, I fear, nothing more than a part of his general inaccuracy and

"perhaps he have conscientious scruple found on his own unfitness,"

"Doubtless; but I fear that my young relative Will Ladislaw be chiefly

regard as an end there must be a prior exercise of many energy or

point to my own manuscript volume, which represent the toil of year
preparatory to a work not yet accomplish. but in vain. to careful
reasoning of this kind he reply by call himself Pegasus, and every
form of prescribe work 'harness.'"
Celia laugh. she be surprised to find that Mr. Casaubon could say
something quite amusing.

"that be very kind of you," say Dorothea, look up at Mr. Casaubon

"yes; when people donot do and say just what you like." Celia have

CHAPTER X.
"he have catch a great cold, have he have no other clothe
to wear than the skin of a bear not yet kill."--fuller.
Young Ladislaw do not pay that visit to which Mr. Brooke have invite
he, and only six day afterwards Mr. Casaubon mention that his young
relative have start for the continent, seem by this cold vagueness
to waive inquiry. indeed, will have decline to fix on any more precise
destination than the entire area of Europe. Genius, he hold, be
necessarily intolerant of fetter: on the one hand it must have the
utmost play for its spontaneity; on the other, it may confidently await
those message from the universe which summon it to its peculiar work,
only place itself in an attitude of receptivity towards all sublime
chance. the attitude of receptivity be various, and will have
sincerely try many of they. he be not excessively fond of wine, but
he have several time take too much, simply as an experiment in that
form of ecstasy; he have fast till he be faint, and then sup on
lobster; he have make himself ill with dos of opium. nothing greatly
original have result from these measure; and the effect of the opium
have convince he that there be an entire dissimilarity between his
constitution and De Quincey's. the superadded circumstance which would
evolve the genius have not yet come; the universe have not yet beckon.
even Caesar's fortune at one time be, but a grand presentiment. we
know what a masquerade all development be, and what effective shape
may be disguise in helpless embryos.--In fact, the world be full of
hopeful analogy and handsome dubious egg call possibility. will
see clearly enough the pitiable instance of long incubation produce
no chick, and but for gratitude would have laugh at Casaubon, whose
plod application, row of note-book, and small taper of learn
theory explore the toss ruin of the world, seem to enforce a
moral entirely encouraging to Will's generous reliance on the
intention of the universe with regard to himself. he hold that
reliance to be a mark of genius; and certainly it be no mark to the
contrary; genius consist neither in self-conceit nor in humility,
but in a power to make or do, not anything in general, but something in
particular. let he start for the continent, then, without our
pronouncing on his future. among all form of mistake, prophecy be the
most gratuitous.
but at present this caution against a too hasty judgment interest I
more in relation to Mr. Casaubon than to his young cousin. if to
Dorothea Mr. Casaubon have be the mere occasion which have set alight
the fine inflammable material of her youthful illusion, do it follow
that he be fairly represent in the mind of those less impassioned
personage who have hitherto deliver their judgment concern he?
I protest against any absolute conclusion, any prejudice derive from
Mrs. Cadwallader's contempt for a neighboring clergyman's allege
greatness of soul, or Sir James Chettam's poor opinion of his rival's
legs,--from Mr. Brooke's failure to elicit a companion's idea, or from
Celia's criticism of a middle-aged scholar's personal appearance. I be
not sure that the great man of his age, if ever that solitary
superlative exist, could escape these unfavorable reflection of
himself in various small mirror; and even Milton, look for his
portrait in a spoon, must submit to have the facial angle of a bumpkin.
moreover, if Mr. Casaubon, speak for himself, have rather a chill
rhetoric, it be not therefore certain that there be no good work or
fine feeling in he. do not an immortal physicist and interpreter of
hieroglyph write detestable verse? have the theory of the solar
system be advance by graceful manner and conversational tact?
suppose we turn from outside estimate of a man, to wonder, with keener
interest, what be the report of his own consciousness about his doing
or capacity: with what hindrance he be carry on his daily labor;
what fade of hope, or what deep fixity of self-delusion the year
be mark off within he; and with what spirit he wrestle against
universal pressure, which will one day be too heavy for he, and bring
his heart to its final pause. doubtless his lot be important in his
own eye; and the chief reason that we think he ask too large a place
in our consideration must be our want of room for he, since we refer
he to the Divine regard with perfect confidence; nay, it be even hold
sublime for our neighbor to expect the utmost there, however little he
may have get from we. Mr. Casaubon, too, be the centre of his own
world; if he be liable to think that other be providentially make
for he, and especially to consider they in the light of their fitness
for the author of a "key to all Mythologies," this trait be not quite
alien to we, and, like the other mendicant hope of mortal, claim
some of our pity.
certainly this affair of his marriage with Miss Brooke touch he more
nearly than it do any one of the person who have hitherto show their
disapproval of it, and in the present stage of thing I feel more
tenderly towards his experience of success than towards the
disappointment of the amiable Sir James. for in truth, as the day
fix for his marriage come nearer, Mr. Casaubon do not find his
spirit rise; nor do the contemplation of that matrimonial garden

accustomed vault where he walk taper in hand. he do not confess to

that though he have win a lovely and noble-hearted girl he have not win
delight,--which he have also regard as an object to be find by
search. it be true that he know all the classical passage imply
the contrary; but know classical passage, we find, be a mode of
motion, which explain why they leave so little extra force for their
personal application.
Poor Mr. Casaubon have imagine that his long studious bachelorhood have
store up for he a compound interest of enjoyment, and that large
draft on his affection would not fail to be honor; for we all of
we, grave or light, get our thought entangle in metaphor, and act
fatally on the strength of they. and now he be in danger of be

he while toil in the morass of authorship without seem near to
the goal. and his be that bad loneliness which would shrink from
sympathy. he could not but wish that Dorothea should think he not
less happy than the world would expect her successful suitor to be; and
in relation to his authorship he lean on her young trust and
veneration, he like to draw forth her fresh interest in listening, as
a means of encouragement to himself: in talk to she he present all
his performance and intention with the reflect confidence of the
pedagogue, and rid himself for the time of that chill ideal audience
which crowd his laborious uncreative hour with the vaporous pressure
of Tartarean shade.
for to Dorothea, after that toy-box history of the world adapt to
young lady which have make the chief part of her education, Mr.

thus in these brief week Dorothea's joyous grateful expectation be

already, as Miss Brooke pass out of the dining-room, opportunity be

"certainly it be reasonable. you have two sort of potato, feed on

"the bridegroom--Casaubon. he have certainly be dry up fast since
the engagement: the flame of passion, I suppose."
"I should think he be far from have a good constitution," say Lady
Chettam, with a still deep undertone. "and then his study--so very
dry, as you say."
"really, by the side of Sir James, he look like a death's head skin
over for the occasion. mark my word: in a year from this time that
girl will hate he. she look up to he as an oracle now, and
by-and-by she will be at the other extreme. all flightiness!"
"how very shocking! I fear she be headstrong. but tell I--you know
all about he--be there anything very bad? what be the truth?"
"the truth? he be as bad as the wrong physic--nasty to take, and sure
to disagree."
"there could not be anything bad than that," say Lady Chettam, with
so vivid a conception of the physic that she seem to have learn
something exact about Mr. Casaubon's disadvantage. "however, James
will hear nothing against Miss Brooke. he say she be the mirror of
woman still."
"that be a generous make-believe of his. depend upon it, he like
little Celia well, and she appreciate he. I hope you like my
little Celia?"
"certainly; she be fonder of geranium, and seem more docile, though
not so fine a figure. but we be talk of physic. tell I about
this new young surgeon, Mr. Lydgate. I be tell he be wonderfully
clever: he certainly look it--a fine brow indeed."
"he be a gentleman. I hear he talk to Humphrey. he talk well."
"yes. Mr. Brooke say he be one of the Lydgates of Northumberland,
really well connected. one do not expect it in a practitioner of
that kind. for my own part, I like a medical man more on a footing
with the servant; they be often all the cleverer. I assure you I
find poor Hicks's judgment unfailing; I never know he wrong. he be
coarse and butcher-like, but he know my constitution. it be a loss to
I his go off so suddenly. dear I, what a very animate
conversation Miss Brooke seem to be have with this Mr. Lydgate!"
"she be talk cottage and hospital with he," say Mrs.
Cadwallader, whose ear and power of interpretation be quick. "I
believe he be a sort of philanthropist, so Brooke be sure to take he
up."
"James," say Lady Chettam when her son come near, "bring Mr. Lydgate
and introduce he to I. I want to test he."
the affable dowager declare herself delight with this opportunity of
make Mr. Lydgate's acquaintance, have hear of his success in
treat fever on a new plan.
Mr. Lydgate have the medical accomplishment of look perfectly grave
whatever nonsense be talk to he, and his dark steady eye give he
impressiveness as a listener. he be as little as possible like the
lament Hicks, especially in a certain careless refinement about his
toilet and utterance. yet Lady Chettam gather much confidence in
he. he confirm her view of her own constitution as be peculiar,
by admit that all constitution might be call peculiar, and he
do not deny that hers might be more peculiar than other. he do not
approve of a too lower system, include reckless cupping, nor, on
the other hand, of incessant port wine and bark. he say "I think so"
with an air of so much deference accompany the insight of agreement,
that she form the most cordial opinion of his talent.
"I be quite pleased with your protege," she say to Mr. Brooke before
go away.
"my protege?--dear me!--who be that?" say Mr. Brooke.
"this young Lydgate, the new doctor.-He seem to I to understand his
profession admirably."
"oh, Lydgate! he be not my protege, you know; only I know an uncle of
his who send I a letter about he. however, I think he be likely to
be first-rate--have study in Paris, know Broussais; have idea, you
know--want to raise the profession."
"lydgate have lot of idea, quite new, about ventilation and diet, that
sort of thing," resume Mr. Brooke, after he have hand out Lady
Chettam, and have return to be civil to a group of Middlemarchers.
"hang it, do you think that be quite sound?--upsette the old
treatment, which have make Englishmen what they re?" say Mr. Standish.
"medical knowledge be at a low ebb among we," say Mr. Bulstrode, who
speak in a subdued tone, and have rather a sickly air. "I, for my part,
hail the advent of Mr. Lydgate. I hope to find good reason for
confide the new hospital to his management."
"that be all very fine," reply Mr. Standish, who be not fond of Mr.
Bulstrode; "if you like he to try experiment on your hospital
patient, and kill a few people for charity I have no objection. but I
be not go to hand money out of my purse to have experiment try on
I. I like treatment that have be test a little."
"well, you know, Standish, every dose you take be an experiment-an
experiment, you know," say Mr. Brooke, nod towards the lawyer.
"oh, if you talk in that sense!" say Mr. Standish, with as much
disgust at such non-legal quibbling as a man can well betray towards a
valuable client.
"I should be glad of any treatment that would cure I without reduce
I to a skeleton, like poor Grainger," say Mr. Vincy, the mayor, a
florid man, who would have serve for a study of flesh in strike
contrast with the franciscan tint of Mr. Bulstrode. "itbe an
uncommonly dangerous thing to be leave without any padding against the
shaft of disease, as somebody said,--and I think it a very good
expression myself."
Mr. Lydgate, of course, be out of hearing. he have quit the party
early, and would have think it altogether tedious but for the novelty
of certain introduction, especially the introduction to Miss Brooke,
whose youthful bloom, with her approach marriage to that fade
scholar, and her interest in matter socially useful, give she the
piquancy of an unusual combination.
"she be a good creature--that fine girl--but a little too earnest," he
think. "it be troublesome to talk to such woman. they be always
want reason, yet they be too ignorant to understand the merit of
any question, and usually fall hack on their moral sense to settle
thing after their own taste."
evidently Miss Brooke be not Mr. Lydgate's style of woman any more
than Mr. Chichely's. consider, indeed, in relation to the latter,
whose mie be mature, she be altogether a mistake, and calculate to
shock his trust in final cause, include the adaptation of fine young
woman to purpleface bachelor. but Lydgate be less ripe, and might
possibly have experience before he which would modify his opinion as
to the most excellent thing in woman.
Miss Brooke, however, be not again see by either of these gentleman
under her maiden name. not long after that dinner-party she have become
Mrs. Casaubon, and be on her way to Rome.
CHAPTER XI.
"but deed and language such as man do use,
and person such as comedy would choose,
when she would show an image of the time,
and sport with human folly, not with crime."
--BEN jonson.
Lydgate, in fact, be already conscious of be fascinate by a woman
strikingly different from Miss Brooke: he do not in the least suppose

believe that he should not marry for several year: not marry until he

certainly nothing at present could seem much less important to lydgate
than the turn of Miss Brooke's mind, or to Miss Brooke than the
quality of the woman who have attract this young surgeon. but any
one watch keenly the stealthy convergence of human lot, see a slow
preparation of effect from one life on another, which tell like a
calculate irony on the indifference or the frozen stare with which we
look at our unintroduced neighbor. destiny stand by sarcastic with
our dramatis personae fold in her hand.
old provincial society have its share of this subtle movement: have not
only its striking downfall, its brilliant young professional dandy
who end by live up an entry with a drab and six child for their
establishment, but also those less mark vicissitude which be
constantly shift the boundary of social intercourse, and beget
new consciousness of interdependence. some slip a little downward,
some get high footing: people deny aspirate, gain wealth, and
fastidious gentleman stand for borough; some be catch in political
current, some in ecclesiastical, and perhaps find themselves
surprisingly group in consequence; while a few personage or family
that stand with rocky firmness amid all this fluctuation, be slowly
present new aspect in spite of solidity, and alter with the
double change of self and beholder. municipal town and rural parish
gradually make fresh thread of connection--gradually, as the old
stocking give way to the saving-bank, and the worship of the solar
guinea become extinct; while squire and baronet, and even lord who
have once live blamelessly afar from the civic mind, gather the
faultiness of close acquaintanceship. settler, too, come from
distant county, some with an alarming novelty of skill, other with
an offensive advantage in cunning. in fact, much the same sort of
movement and mixture go on in old England as we find in old
Herodotus, who also, in tell what have be, think it well to take
a woman's lot for his starting-point; though Io, as a maiden apparently
beguile by attractive merchandise, be the reverse of Miss Brooke, and
in this respect perhaps bear more resemblance to Rosamond Vincy, who
have excellent taste in costume, with that nymph-like figure and pure
blindness which give the large range to choice in the flow and color
of drapery. but these thing make only part of her charm. she be
admit to be the flower of Mrs. Lemon's school, the chief school in
the county, where the teaching include all that be demand in the
accomplish female--even to extra, such as the getting in and out of
a carriage. Mrs. Lemon herself have always hold up Miss Vincy as an
example: no pupil, she say, exceed that young lady for mental
acquisition and propriety of speech, while her musical execution be
quite exceptional. we cannot help the way in which people speak of we,
and probably if Mrs. Lemon have undertake to describe Juliet or Imogen,
these heroine would not have seem poetical. the first vision of
Rosamond would have be enough with most judge to dispel any
prejudice excite by Mrs. Lemon's praise.
Lydgate could not be long in Middlemarch without have that agreeable
vision, or even without make the acquaintance of the Vincy family;
for though Mr. Peacock, whose practice he have pay something to enter
on, have not be their doctor (Mrs. Vincy not like the lowering
system adopt by he), he have many patient among their connection
and acquaintance. for who of any consequence in Middlemarch be not
connect or at least acquaint with the Vincys? they be old
manufacturer, and have keep a good house for three generation, in
which there have naturally be much intermarry with neighbor more
or less decidedly genteel. Mr. Vincy's sister have make a wealthy match
in accept Mr. Bulstrode, who, however, as a man not bear in the

well in unite himself with a real Middlemarch family; on the other

daughter. but on this side too there be a cheering sense of money;
for Mrs. Vincy's sister have be second wife to rich old Mr.

Mr. Vincy be more inclined to general good-fellowship than to take

acquaintance. Rosamond silently wish that her father would invite
Mr. Lydgate. she be tired of the face and figure she have always

phrase distinguish those Middlemarch young man whom she have know as
boy. she have be at school with girl of high position, whose
brother, she feel sure, it would have be possible for she to be more

for his part, be in no hurry on the subject. an alderman about to be
mayor must by-and-by enlarge his dinner-party, but at present there
be plenty of guest at his well-spread table.
that table often remain cover with the relic of the family
breakfast long after Mr. Vincy have go with his second son to the

with the young girl in the schoolroom. it await the family

"Knock at Mr. Fred's door again, Pritchard, and tell he it have strike
half-past ten."
this be say without any change in the radiant good-humor of Mrs.

"Mamma," say Rosamond, "when Fred come down I wish you would not let

"oh, my dear, you be so hard on your brother! it be the only fault I
have to find with you. you be the sweetest temper in the world, but
you be so tetchy with your brother."
"not tetchy, mamma: you never hear I speak in an unladylike way."
"well, but you want to deny they thing."
"brother be so unpleasant."
"oh, my dear, you must allow for young man. be thankful if they have
good heart. a woman must learn to put up with little thing. you

"donot decry your own brother, my dear. few young man have less

"well, my dear, you will not find any Middlemarch young man who have not

"But"--here Rosamond's face break into a smile which suddenly reveal

"so it seem, my love, for you have as good as refuse the pick of

"excuse I, mamma--I wish you would not say, 'the pick of they.'"
"why, what else be they?"
"I mean, mamma, it be rather a vulgar expression."
"very likely, my dear; I never be a good speaker. what should I say?"
"the good of they."
"why, that seem just as plain and common. if I have have time to think,
I should have say, 'the most superior young man.' but with your

"only the wrong sort. all choice of word be slang. it mark a class."
"there be correct English: that be not slang."
"I beg your pardon: correct English be the slang of prig who write
history and essay. and the strong slang of all be the slang of

"you will say anything, Fred, to gain your point."
"well, tell I whether it be slang or poetry to call an ox a

"aha, Miss Rosy, you donot know Homer from slang. I shall invent a new
game; I shall write bit of slang and poetry on slip, and give they to

"dear I, how amusing it be to hear young people talk!" say Mrs.

"that be your want of understanding, Rosy. I can get up to go hunting

"papa say I may have the chestnut to ride now."

"really, Fred, I wish you would leave off play the flute. a man

"and why should you expect I to take you out rid?"
this question lead to an adjustment, for Rosamond have set her mind on
that particular ride.
so Fred be gratify with nearly an hour's practice of "Ar hyd y nos,"

an irrepressible hopefulness.
CHAPTER XII.
"he have more tow on his distaffe
than Gerveis know."
--CHAUCER.
the ride to Stone Court, which Fred and Rosamond take the next morning,
lie through a pretty bit of midland landscape, almost all meadow and
pasture, with hedgerow still allow to grow in bushy beauty and to
spread out coral fruit for the bird. little detail give each field a
particular physiognomy, dear to the eye that have look on they from
childhood: the pool in the corner where the grass be dank and tree
lean whisperingly; the great oak shadow a bare place in
mid-pasture; the high bank where the ash-tree grow; the sudden slope
of the old marl-pit make a red background for the burdock; the
huddle roof and rick of the homestead without a traceable way of
approach; the gray gate and fence against the depth of the border
wood; and the stray hovel, its old, old thatch full of mossy hill and
valley with wondrous modulation of light and shadow such as we travel
far to see in later life, and see large, but not more beautiful.
these be the thing that make the gamut of joy in landscape to
midland-breed soul--the thing they toddle among, or perhaps learn
by heart stand between their father's knee while he drive leisurely.
but the road, even the byroad, be excellent; for Lowick, as we have
see, be not a parish of muddy lane and poor tenant; and it be into
Lowick parish that Fred and Rosamond enter after a couple of mile'
riding. another mile would bring they to Stone Court, and at the end
of the first half, the house be already visible, look as if it have
be arrest in its growth toward a stone mansion by an unexpected
budding of farm-building on its left flank, which have hinder it from
become anything more than the substantial dwelling of a gentleman
farmer. it be not the less agreeable an object in the distance for
the cluster of pinnacle corn-rick which balance the fine row of
walnut on the right.
presently it be possible to discern something that might be a gig on
the circular drive before the front door.
"dear I," say Rosamond, "I hope none of my uncle's horrible relation
be there."
"they be, though. that be Mrs. Waule's gig--the last yellow gig leave,
I should think. when I see Mrs. Waule in it, I understand how yellow
can have be wear for mourning. that gig seem to I more funereal
than a hearse. but then Mrs. Waule always have black crape on. how
do she manage it, rosy? her friend cannot always be die."
"I donot know at all. and she be not in the least evangelical," say
Rosamond, reflectively, as if that religious point of view would have
fully account for perpetual crape. "and, not poor," she add, after
a moment's pause.
"no, by George! they be as rich as Jews, those Waules and
Featherstones; I mean, for people like they, who donot want to spend
anything. and yet they hang about my uncle like vulture, and be
afraid of a farthing go away from their side of the family. but I
believe he hat they all."
the Mrs. Waule who be so far from be admirable in the eye of these
distant connection, have happen to say this very morning (not at all
with a defiant air, but in a low, muffled, neutral tone, as of a voice
hear through cotton wool) that she do not wish "to enjoy their good
opinion." she be seat, as she observe, on her own brother's hearth,
and have be Jane Featherstone five-and-twenty year before she have
be Jane Waule, which entitle she to speak when her own brother's
name have be make free with by those who have no right to it.
"what be you drive at there?" say Mr. Featherstone, hold his
stick between his knee and settle his wig, while he give she a
momentary sharp glance, which seem to react on he like a draught of
cold air and set he cough.
Mrs. Waule have to defer her answer till he be quiet again, till Mary
Garth have supply he with fresh syrup, and he have begin to rub the
gold knob of his stick, look bitterly at the fire. it be a bright
fire, but it make no difference to the chill-look purplish tint of
Mrs. Waule's face, which be as neutral as her voice; have mere
chink for eye, and lip that hardly move in speak.
"the doctor cannot master that cough, brother. itbe just like what I
have; for Ibe your own sister, constitution and everything. but, as I
be say, itbe a pity Mrs. Vincy's family cannot be well conduct."
"Tchah! you say nothing o' the sort. you say somebody have make free
with my name."
"and no more than can be prove, if what everybody say be true. my
brother Solomon tell I itbe the talk up and down in Middlemarch how
unsteady young Vincy be, and have be forever gamble at billiard
since home he come."
"nonsense! whatbe a game at billiard? itbe a good gentlemanly game;
and young Vincy be not a clodhopper. if your son John take to
billiard, now, hewould make a fool of himself."
"your nephew John never take to billiard or any other game, brother,
and be far from lose hundred of pound, which, if what everybody
say be true, must be find somewhere else than out of Mr. Vincy the
father's pocket. for they say hebe be lose money for year, though
nobody would think so, to see he go course and keep open house as
they do. and Ihave hear say Mr. Bulstrode condemn Mrs. Vincy beyond
anything for her flightiness, and spoil her child so."!
"whatbe Bulstrode to I? I donot bank with he."
"well, Mrs. Bulstrode be Mr. Vincy's own sister, and they do say that
Mr. Vincy mostly trade on the Bank money; and you may see yourself,

that light way of laugh at everything, itbe very unbecoming. but
indulge your child be one thing, and find money to pay their
debt be another. and itbe openly say that young Vincy have raise
money on his expectation. I donot say what expectation. Miss Garth
hear I, and be welcome to tell again. I know young people hang
together."
"no, thank you, Mrs. Waule," say Mary Garth. "I dislike hearing
scandal too much to wish to repeat it."
Mr. Featherstone rub the knob of his stick and make a brief
convulsive show of laughter, which have much the same genuineness as an
old whist-player's chuckle over a bad hand. still look at the fire,
he said--
"and who pretend to say Fred Vincy havenot get expectation? such a
fine, spirited fellow be like enough to have them."
there be a slight pause before Mrs. Waule reply, and when she do
so, her voice seem to be slightly moisten with tear, though she
face be still dry.
"whether or no, brother, it be naturally painful to I and my brother

than the Merry-Andrew at the fair, openly reckon on your property
come to _they_. and I your own sister, and Solomon your own

make family for?" here Mrs. Waule's tear fall, but with moderation.
"come, out with it, Jane!" say Mr. Featherstone, look at she. "you

on what he say he know about my will, eh?"
"I never say so, brother" (Mrs. Waule's voice have again become dry and
unshaken). "it be tell I by my brother Solomon last night when he
call come from market to give I advice about the old wheat, I
be a widow, and my son John only three-and-twenty, though steady
beyond anything. and he have it from most undeniable authority, and not
one, but many."
"stuff and nonsense! I donot believe a word of it. itbe all a get-up

"not get up by I, brother, nor yet by Solomon, who, whatever else he

which presently pause stamp on the gravel before the door.
before Mr. Featherstone's cough be quiet, Rosamond enter, bear up

"Heyday, miss!" he say at last, "you have a fine color. wherebe Fred?"
"see about the horse. he will be in presently."

even those neighbor who have call Peter Featherstone an old fox, have

"ay, ay, I remember--you'll see Ihave remember them all--all dark and

persuasion that her brother Peter Featherstone could never leave his
chief property away from his blood-relations:--else, why have the

preposterous a result be not strictly conceivable. but we be
frighten at much that be not strictly conceivable.
when Fred come in the old man eye he with a peculiar twinkle, which
the young have often have reason to interpret as pride in the

the deuce? Ibe under eighty. I say, you must contradict this story."

"who else, eh?"
"then the story have grow into this lie out of some sermonize word

Mr. Featherstone's face require its whole scale of grimace as a

Fred feel himself to be in a disgusting dilemma.

but he be disappoint in the result.

speckilate fellow! he may come down any day, when the devil leave
off back he. and thatbe what his religion mean: he want God
A'mighty to come in. thatbe nonsense! therebe one thing I make out
pretty clear when I use to go to church--and itbe this: God A'mighty
stick to the land. he promise land, and he give land, and he make
chap rich with corn and cattle. but you take the other side. you
like Bulstrode and speckilation well than Featherstone and land."
"I beg your pardon, sir," say Fred, rise, stand with his back to

Fred color again. Featherstone have rarely give he present of

Fred, in spite of his irritation, have kindness enough in he to be a

Fred give he the title.

Rosamond and Mary have be talk fast than their male friend.

Mr. Lydgate be rather late this morning, but the visitor stay long

his dull expectation of the usual disagreeable routine with an aged

thus, in rid home, both the brother and the sister be preoccupy

Fred's mind, on the other hand, be busy with an anxiety which even his

be the occasion of any additional coolness between his own family and
the Bulstrodes. then, he himself hat have to go and speak to his
uncle Bulstrode, and perhaps after drink wine he have say many
foolish thing about Featherstone's property, and these have be
magnify by report. Fred feel that he make a wretched figure as a
fellow who brag about expectation from a queer old miser like
Featherstone, and go to beg for certificate at his bidding.
but--those expectation! he really have they, and he see no agreeable
alternative if he give they up; besides, he have lately make a debt
which gall he extremely, and old Featherstone have almost bargain
to pay it off. the whole affair be miserably small: his debt be
small, even his expectation be not anything so very magnificent.
Fred have know man to whom he would have be ashamed of confess the
smallness of his scrape. such rumination naturally produce a streak
of misanthropic bitterness. to be bear the son of a middlemarch
manufacturer, and inevitable heir to nothing in particular, while such
man as Mainwaring and Vyan--certainly life be a poor business, when a
spirited young fellow, with a good appetite for the good of everything,
have so poor an outlook.
it have not occur to Fred that the introduction of Bulstrode's name
in the matter be a fiction of old Featherstone's; nor could this have
make any difference to his position. he see plainly enough that the
old man want to exercise his power by torment he a little, and
also probably to get some satisfaction out of see he on unpleasant
term with Bulstrode. Fred fancy that he see to the bottom of his
uncle Featherstone's soul, though in reality half what he see there be
no more than the reflex of his own inclination. the difficult task of
know another soul be not for young gentleman whose consciousness be
chiefly make up of their own wish.
Fred's main point of debate with himself be, whether he should tell
his father, or try to get through the affair without his father's
knowledge. it be probably Mrs. Waule who have be talk about he;
and if Mary Garth have repeat Mrs. Waule's report to Rosamond, it
would be sure to reach his father, who would as surely question he
about it. he say to Rosamond, as they slacken their pace--
"Rosy, do Mary tell you that Mrs. Waule have say anything about I?"
"yes, indeed, she do."
"what?"
"that you be very unsteady."
"be that all?"
"I should think that be enough, Fred."
"you be sure she say no more?"
"Mary mention nothing else. but really, Fred, I think you ought to
be ashamed."
"oh, fudge! donot lecture I. what do Mary say about it?"
"I be not oblige to tell you. you care so very much what Mary say,
and you be too rude to allow I to speak."
"of course I care what Mary say. she be the good girl I know."
"I should never have think she be a girl to fall in love with."
"how do you know what man would fall in love with? girl never know."
"at least, Fred, let I advise _you_ not to fall in love with she, for
she say she would not marry you if you ask she."
"she might have wait till I do ask she."
"I know it would nettle you, Fred."
"not at all. she would not have say so if you have not provoke she."
before reach home, Fred conclude that he would tell the whole
affair as simply as possible to his father, who might perhaps take on
himself the unpleasant business of speak to Bulstrode.
BOOK II.
OLD and YOUNG.
CHAPTER XIII.
1st gent. how class your man?--as well than the most,
or, seem well, bad beneath that cloak?
as saint or knave, pilgrim or hypocrite?
2d Gent. Nay, tell I how you class your wealth of book
the drifted relic of all time.
as well sort they at once by size and livery:
Vellum, tall copy, and the common calf
will hardly cover more diversity
than all your label cunningly devise
to class your unread author.
in consequence of what he have hear from Fred, Mr. Vincy determine to
speak with Mr. Bulstrode in his private room at the Bank at half-past
one, when he be usually free from other caller. but a visitor have
come in at one o'clock, and Mr. Bulstrode have so much to say to he,
that there be little chance of the interview be over in half an
hour. the banker's speech be fluent, but it be also copious, and he
use up an appreciable amount of time in brief meditative pause. do
not imagine his sickly aspect to have be of the yellow, black-haire
sort: he have a pale blond skin, thin gray-besprinkle brown hair,
light-gray eye, and a large forehead. loud man call his subdued
tone an undertone, and sometimes imply that it be inconsistent with
openness; though there seem to be no reason why a loud man should not
be give to concealment of anything except his own voice, unless it can
be show that Holy Writ have place the seat of candor in the lung.
Mr. Bulstrode have also a deferential bend attitude in listening, and
an apparently fix attentiveness in his eye which make those person
who think themselves worth hear infer that he be seek the
utmost improvement from their discourse. other, who expect to make
no great figure, dislike this kind of moral lantern turn on they.
if you be not proud of your cellar, there be no thrill of satisfaction
in see your guest hold up his wine-glass to the light and look
judicial. such joy be reserve for conscious merit. hence Mr.
Bulstrode's close attention be not agreeable to the publican and
sinner in Middlemarch; it be attribute by some to his be a
Pharisee, and by other to his be evangelical. Less superficial
reasoner among they wish to know who his father and grandfather
be, observe that five-and-twenty year ago nobody have ever hear of
a Bulstrode in Middlemarch. to his present visitor, Lydgate, the
scrutinize look be a matter of indifference: he simply form an
unfavorable opinion of the banker's constitution, and conclude that he
have an eager inward life with little enjoyment of tangible thing.
"I shall be exceedingly oblige if you will look in on I here
occasionally, Mr. Lydgate," the banker observe, after a brief pause.
"if, as I dare to hope, I have the privilege of find you a valuable
coadjutor in the interesting matter of hospital management, there will
be many question which we shall need to discuss in private. as to the
new hospital, which be nearly finish, I shall consider what you have
say about the advantage of the special destination for fever. the
decision will rest with I, for though Lord Medlicote have give the
land and timber for the building, he be not dispose to give his
personal attention to the object."
"there be few thing well worth the pain in a provincial town like
this," say Lydgate. "a fine fever hospital in addition to the old
infirmary might be the nucleus of a medical school here, when once we
get our medical reform; and what would do more for medical education
than the spread of such school over the country? a bear provincial
man who have a grain of public spirit as well as a few idea, should do
what he can to resist the rush of everything that be a little well
than common towards London. any valid professional aim may often find
a freer, if not a rich field, in the province."
one of Lydgate's gift be a voice habitually deep and sonorous, yet
capable of become very low and gentle at the right moment. about his
ordinary bearing there be a certain fling, a fearless expectation of
success, a confidence in his own power and integrity much fortify by
contempt for petty obstacle or seduction of which he have have no
experience. but this proud openness be make lovable by an expression
of unaffected good-will. Mr. Bulstrode perhaps like he the well for
the difference between they in pitch and manner; he certainly like
he the well, as Rosamond do, for be a stranger in Middlemarch.
one can begin so many thing with a new person!--even begin to be a
well man.
"I shall rejoice to furnish your zeal with full opportunity," Mr.
Bulstrode answer; "I mean, by confide to you the superintendence of
my new hospital, should a mature knowledge favor that issue, for I be
determined that so great an object shall not be shackle by our two
physician. indeed, I be encourage to consider your advent to this
town as a gracious indication that a more manifest blessing be now to
be award to my effort, which have hitherto be much with stood.
with regard to the old infirmary, we have gain the initial point--I
mean your election. and now I hope you will not shrink from incur
a certain amount of jealousy and dislike from your professional
brother by present yourself as a reformer."
"I will not profess bravery," say lydgate, smile, "but I acknowledge
a good deal of pleasure in fighting, and I should not care for my
profession, if I do not believe that well method be to be find
and enforce there as well as everywhere else."
"the standard of that profession be low in Middlemarch, my dear sir,"
say the banker. "I mean in knowledge and skill; not in social status,
for our medical man be most of they connect with respectable
townspeople here. my own imperfect health have induce I to give some
attention to those palliative resource which the divine mercy have
place within our reach. I have consult eminent man in the
metropolis, and I be painfully aware of the backwardness under which
medical treatment labor in our provincial district."
"yes;--with our present medical rule and education, one must be
satisfied now and then to meet with a fair practitioner. as to all the
high question which determine the starting-point of a diagnosis--as
to the philosophy of medial evidence--any glimmering of these can only
come from a scientific culture of which country practitioner have
usually no more notion than the man in the moon."
Mr. Bulstrode, bend and look intently, find the form which
Lydgate have give to his agreement not quite suited to his
comprehension. under such circumstance a judicious man change the
topic and enter on ground where his own gift may be more useful.
"I be aware," he say, "that the peculiar bias of medical ability be
towards material mean. nevertheless, Mr. Lydgate, I hope we shall not
vary in sentiment as to a measure in which you be not likely to be
actively concern, but in which your sympathetic concurrence may be an
aid to I. you recognize, I hope; the existence of spiritual interest
in your patient?"
"certainly I do. but those word be apt to cover different meaning
to different mind."
"precisely. and on such subject wrong teaching be as fatal as no
teaching. now a point which I have much at heart to secure be a new
regulation as to clerical attendance at the old infirmary. the
building stand in Mr. Farebrother's parish. you know Mr. Farebrother?"
"I have see he. he give I his vote. I must call to thank he. he
seem a very bright pleasant little fellow. and I understand he be a
naturalist."
"Mr. Farebrother, my dear sir, be a man deeply painful to contemplate.
I suppose there be not a clergyman in this country who have great
talent." Mr. Bulstrode pause and look meditative.
"I have not yet be pain by find any excessive talent in
Middlemarch," say Lydgate, bluntly.
"what I desire," Mr. Bulstrode continue, look still more serious,

that no other spiritual aid should be call in."

Mr. Tyke, and even then I should require to know the case in which he
be apply." Lydgate smile, but he be bent on be circumspect.

gratify a spirit of worldly opposition. but I shall not therefore

not conceal it."
Mr. Bulstrode's voice have become a loud and agitated whisper as he say

Mr. Bulstrode, alone with his brother-in-law, pour himself out a

"I cannot persuade you to adopt my regimen, vincy?"
"no, no; Ihave no opinion of that system. life want padding," say Mr.

be cook up a story out of spite, and tell it to the old man, to

he mean to leave he his land, and that make other people jealous."

money to an expensive education which have succeed in nothing but in
give he extravagant idle habit. you be now reap the
consequence."
to point out other people's error be a duty that Mr. Bulstrode rarely
shrink from, but Mr. Vincy be not equally prepared to be patient.
when a man have the immediate prospect of be mayor, and be ready, in
the interest of commerce, to take up a firm attitude on politic
generally, he have naturally a sense of his importance to the framework
of thing which seem to throw question of private conduct into the

and though he usually enjoy kick, he be anxious to refrain from
that relief.
"as to that, Bulstrode, itbe no use go back. Ibe not one of your
pattern man, and I donot pretend to be. I couldnot foresee everything
in the trade; there benot a fine business in Middlemarch than ours,
and the lad be clever. my poor brother be in the Church, and would
have do well--have get preferment already, but that stomach fever take
he off: else he might have be a dean by this time. I think I be

itbe a good british feeling to try and raise your family a little: in

"very well," say Mr. Vincy, kick in spite of resolution, "I never

on what you call unworldly principle. the only difference I see be
that one worldliness be a little bit honester than another."
"this kind of discussion be unfruitful, Vincy," say Mr. Bulstrode,

shade his eye as if weary. "you have some more particular business."
"yes, yes. the long and short of it be, somebody have tell old
Featherstone, give you as the authority, that Fred have be borrow

never say any such nonsense. but the old fellow will insist on it

suppose you can have no objection to do that."
"pardon I. I have an objection. I be by no mean sure that your son,

some one may not have be foolish enough to supply he on so vague a

well--nobody can say I wink at what he do. but he be not a liar.
and I should have think--but I may be wrong--that there be no

"I be not at all sure that I should be befriend your son by
smooth his way to the future possession of Featherstone's property.
