motive for further such a disposition of property as that which you
refer to. I do not shrink from say that it will not tend to your
son's eternal welfare or to the glory of God. why then should you
expect I to pen this kind of affidavit, which have no object but to
keep up a foolish partiality and secure a foolish bequest?"
"if you mean to hinder everybody from have money but saint and
evangelist, you must give up some profitable partnership, thatbe all
I can say," Mr. Vincy burst out very bluntly. "it may be for the glory
of God, but it be not for the glory of the Middlemarch trade, that
Plymdale's house use those blue and green dye it get from the
Brassing manufactory; they rot the silk, thatbe all I know about it.
perhaps if other people know so much of the profit go to the glory of
God, they might like it well. but I donot mind so much about that--I
could get up a pretty row, if I choose."
Mr. Bulstrode pause a little before he answer. "you pain I very
much by speak in this way, Vincy. I do not expect you to understand
my ground of action--it be not an easy thing even to thread a path for
principle in the intricacy of the world--still less to make the
thread clear for the careless and the scoffing. you must remember, if
you please, that I stretch my tolerance towards you as my wife's
brother, and that it little become you to complain of I as
withholding material help towards the worldly position of your family.
I must remind you that it be not your own prudence or judgment that have
enable you to keep your place in the trade."

Vincy, thoroughly nettle (a result which be seldom much retarded by
previous resolution). "and when you marry Harriet, I donot see how
you could expect that our family should not hang by the same nail.
if youhave change your mind, and want my family to come down in the
world, youwould well say so. Ihave never change; Ibe a plain churchman
now, just as I use to be before doctrine come up. I take the world
as I find it, in trade and everything else. Ibe content to be no
bad than my neighbor. but if you want we to come down in the world,
say so. I shall know well what to do then."

this letter about your son?"
"well, whether or not, I consider it very unhandsome of you to refuse
it. such doing may be line with religion, but outside they have a
nasty, dog-in-the-manger look. you might as well slander Fred: it
come pretty near to it when you refuse to say you donot set a slander
go. itbe this sort of thing--this tyrannical spirit, want to
play bishop and banker everywhere--itbe this sort of thing make a
man's name stink."
"Vincy, if you insist on quarrel with I, it will be exceedingly
painful to Harriet as well as myself," say Mr. Bulstrode, with a
trifle more eagerness and paleness than usual.
"I donot want to quarrel. itbe for my interest--and perhaps for your
too--that we should be friend. I bear you no grudge; I think no bad
of you than I do of other people. a man who half starve himself, and
go the length in family prayer, and so on, that you do, believe in
his religion whatever it may be: you could turn over your capital just
as fast with cursing and swearing:--plenty of fellow do. you like to
be master, therebe no deny that; you must be first chop in heaven,
else you willnot like it much. but yoube my sister's husband, and we
ought to stick together; and if I know Harriet, shewill consider it your
fault if we quarrel because you strain at a gnat in this way, and
refuse to do Fred a good turn. and I donot mean to say I shall bear it
well. I consider it unhandsome."
Mr. Vincy rise, begin to button his great-coat, and look steadily at

this be not the first time that Mr. Bulstrode have begin by admonish
Mr. Vincy, and have end by see a very unsatisfactory reflection of
himself in the coarse unflattering mirror which that manufacturer's
mind present to the subtler light and shadow of his fellow-man; and
perhaps his experience ought to have warn he how the scene would
end. but a full-fed fountain will be generous with its water even in
the rain, when they be bad than useless; and a fine fount of
admonition be apt to be equally irrepressible.
it be not in Mr. Bulstrode's nature to comply directly in consequence
of uncomfortable suggestion. before change his course, he always
need to shape his motif and bring they into accordance with his
habitual standard. he say, at last--
"I will reflect a little, Vincy. I will mention the subject to

"very well. as soon as you can, please. I hope it will all be settle
before I see you to-morrow."

Mr. Bulstrode's consultation of Harriet seem to have have the effect

the old gentleman be stay in bed on account of the cold weather,

prop up comfortably on a bed-rest, be not less able than usual to
enjoy his consciousness of wisdom in distrust and frustrating

"under the circumstance I will not decline to state my
conviction--tchah! what fine word the fellow put! hebe as fine as an
auctioneer--that your son Frederic have not obtain any advance of

Fred color. "you wish to have the letter, sir. I should think it
very likely that Mr. Bulstrode's denial be as good as the authority
which tell you what he deny."
"every bit. I never say I believe either one or the other. and now
what d' you expect?" say Mr. Featherstone, curtly, keep on his
spectacle, but withdraw his hand under his wrap.
"I expect nothing, sir." Fred with difficulty restrain himself from
vent his irritation. "I come to bring you the letter. if you like
I will bid you good morning."
"not yet, not yet. ring the bell; I want missy to come."
it be a servant who come in answer to the bell.
"tell missy to come!" say Mr. Featherstone, impatiently. "what
business have she to go away?" he speak in the same tone when Mary come.
"why couldnot you sit still here till I tell you to go? want my

Mary's eye look rather red, as if she have be cry. it be clear

now that the providential occurrence be apparently close at hand, it

of the need: as absurd as a faith that believe in half a miracle for
want of strength to believe in a whole one.
the deep-veined hand finger many bank-note-one after the other,
lay they down flat again, while Fred lean back in his chair,

might mean fifty pound. he take they, saying--
"I be very much oblige to you, sir," and be go to roll they up

"come, donot you think it worth your while to count them? you take
money like a lord; I suppose you lose it like one."
"I think I be not to look a gift-horse in the mouth, sir. but I
shall be very happy to count they."
Fred be not so happy, however, after he have count they. for they
actually present the absurdity of be less than his hopefulness have
decide that they must be. what can the fitness of thing mean, if not
their fitness to a man's expectation? fail this, absurdity and
atheism gape behind he. the collapse for Fred be severe when he
find that he hold no more than five twenty, and his share in the
high education of this country do not seem to help he.
nevertheless he say, with rapid change in his fair complexion--
"it be very handsome of you, sir."
"I should think it be," say Mr. Featherstone, lock his box and
replace it, then take off his spectacle deliberately, and at
length, as if his inward meditation have more deeply convince he,
repeat, "I should think it handsome."
"I assure you, sir, I be very grateful," say Fred, who have have time to
recover his cheerful air.
"so you ought to be. you want to cut a figure in the world, and I
reckon Peter Featherstone be the only one youhave get to trust to." here
the old man's eye gleam with a curiously mingle satisfaction in the
consciousness that this smart young fellow rely upon he, and that
the smart young fellow be rather a fool for do so.
"yes, indeed: I be not bear to very splendid chance. few man have
be more cramp than I have be," say Fred, with some sense of
surprise at his own virtue, consider how hardly he be deal with.
"it really seem a little too bad to have to ride a break-wind
hunter, and see man, who, be not half such good judge as yourself,
able to throw away any amount of money on buy bad bargain."
"well, you can buy yourself a fine hunter now. eighty pound be enough
for that, I reckon--and youwill have twenty pound over to get yourself
out of any little scrape," say Mr. Featherstone, chuckle slightly.
"you be very good, sir," say Fred, with a fine sense of contrast
between the word and his feeling.
"ay, rather a well uncle than your fine uncle Bulstrode. you willnot
get much out of his spekilation, I think. hebe get a pretty strong
string round your father's leg, by what I hear, eh?"
"my father never tell I anything about his affair, sir."
"well, he show some sense there. but other people find them out
without his telling. _hewill_ never have much to leave you: hewill
most-like die without a will--hebe the sort of man to do it--let them
make he mayor of Middlemarch as much as they like. but you willnot get
much by his dying without a will, though you _be_ the eld son."
Fred think that Mr. Featherstone have never be so disagreeable
before. true, he have never before give he quite so much money at
once.
"shall I destroy this letter of Mr. Bulstrode's, sir?" say Fred,
rise with the letter as if he would put it in the fire.
"ay, ay, I donot want it. itbe worth no money to I."
Fred carry the letter to the fire, and thrust the poker through it
with much z. he long to get out of the room, but he be a little
ashamed before his inner self, as well as before his uncle, to run away
immediately after pocket the money. presently, the farm-bailiff
come up to give his master a report, and Fred, to his unspeakable
relief, be dismiss with the injunction to come again soon.
he have long not only to be set free from his uncle, but also to find
Mary Garth. she be now in her usual place by the fire, with sew in
her hand and a book open on the little table by her side. her eyelid
have lose some of their redness now, and she have her usual air of
self-command.
"be I want up-stair?" she say, half rise as Fred enter.
"no; I be only dismiss, because Simmons be go up."
Mary sit down again, and resume her work. she be certainly treat
he with more indifference than usual: she do not know how
affectionately indignant he have feel on her behalf up-stair.
"may I stay here a little, Mary, or shall I bore you?"
"pray sit down," say Mary; "you will not be so heavy a bore as Mr.
John Waule, who be here yesterday, and he sit down without ask my
leave."
"poor fellow! I think he be in love with you."
"I be not aware of it. and to I it be one of the most odious thing
in a girl's life, that there must always be some supposition of fall
in love come between she and any man who be kind to she, and to whom
she be grateful. I should have think that I, at least, might have
be safe from all that. I have no ground for the nonsensical vanity
of fancy everybody who come near I be in love with I."
Mary do not mean to betray any feeling, but in spite of herself she
end in a tremulous tone of vexation.
"Confound John Waule! I do not mean to make you angry. I donot know
you have any reason for be grateful to I. I forget what a great
service you think it if any one snuff a candle for you. Fred also have
his pride, and be not go to show that he know what have call forth
this outburst of Mary's.
"oh, I be not angry, except with the way of the world. I do like to
be speak to as if I have common-sense. I really often feel as if I
could understand a little more than I ever hear even from young
gentleman who have be to college." Mary have recover, and she speak
with a suppress rippling under-current of laughter pleasant to hear.
"I donot care how merry you be at my expense this morning," say Fred,
"I think you look so sad when you come up-stair. it be a shame you
should stay here to be bully in that way."
"oh, I have an easy life--by comparison. I have try be a teacher,
and I be not fit for that: my mind be too fond of wander on its own
way. I think any hardship be well than pretend to do what one be
pay for, and never really do it. everything here I can do as well
as any one else could; perhaps well than some--rosy, for example.
though she be just the sort of beautiful creature that be imprison
with ogre in fairy tale."
"_rosy!_" cry Fred, in a tone of profound brotherly scepticism.
"Come, Fred!" say Mary, emphatically; "you have no right to be so
critical."
"do you mean anything particular--just now?"
"no, I mean something general--always."
"oh, that I be idle and extravagant. well, I be not fit to be a poor
man. I should not have make a bad fellow if I have be rich."
"you would have do your duty in that state of life to which it have
not pleased God to call you," say Mary, laugh.
"well, I couldnot do my duty as a clergyman, any more than you could do
your as a governess. you ought to have a little fellow-feeling there,
Mary."
"I never say you ought to be a clergyman. there be other sort of
work. it seem to I very miserable not to resolve on some course and
act accordingly."
"so I could, if--" Fred break off, and stand up, lean against the
mantel-piece.
"if you be sure you should not have a fortune?"
"I do not say that. you want to quarrel with I. it be too bad of
you to be guide by what other people say about I."
"how can I want to quarrel with you? I should be quarrel with all
my new book," say Mary, lift the volume on the table. "however
naughty you may be to other people, you be good to I."
"because I like you well than any one else. but I know you despise
I."
"yes, I do--a little," say Mary, nodding, with a smile.
"you would admire a stupendous fellow, who would have wise opinion
about everything."
"yes, I should." Mary be sew swiftly, and seem provokingly
mistress of the situation. when a conversation have take a wrong turn
for we, we only get far and far into the swamp of awkwardness.
this be what Fred Vincy feel.
"I suppose a woman be never in love with any one she have always
know--ever since she can remember; as a man often be. it be always
some new fellow who strike a girl."
"let I see," say Mary, the corner of her mouth curl archly; "I
must go back on my experience. there be Juliet--she seem an example
of what you say. but then Ophelia have probably know Hamlet a long
while; and Brenda Troil--she have know Mordaunt Merton ever since they
be child; but then he seem to have be an estimable young man;
and Minna be still more deeply in love with Cleveland, who be a
stranger. Waverley be new to Flora MacIvor; but then she do not fall
in love with he. and there be Olivia and Sophia Primrose, and
Corinne--they may be say to have fall in love with new man.
altogether, my experience be rather mixed."
Mary look up with some roguishness at Fred, and that look of hers be
very dear to he, though the eye be nothing more than clear window
where observation sit laughingly. he be certainly an affectionate
fellow, and as he have grow from boy to man, he have grow in love with
his old playmate, notwithstanding that share in the high education of
the country which have exalt his view of rank and income.
"when a man be not love, it be no use for he to say that he could be
a well fellow--could do anything--I mean, if he be sure of be
love in return."
"not of the least use in the world for he to say he _could_ be well.
might, could, would--they be contemptible auxiliary."
"I donot see how a man be to be good for much unless he have some one
woman to love he dearly."
"I think the goodness should come before he expect that."
"you know well, Mary. woman donot love man for their goodness."
"perhaps not. but if they love they, they never think they bad."
"it be hardly fair to say I be bad."
"I say nothing at all about you."
"I never shall be good for anything, Mary, if you will not say that you
love I--if you will not promise to marry I--I mean, when I be able to
marry."
"if I do love you, I would not marry you: I would certainly not
promise ever to marry you."
"I think that be quite wicked, Mary. if you love I, you ought to
promise to marry I."
"on the contrary, I think it would be wicked in I to marry you even if
I do love you."
"you mean, just as I be, without any mean of maintain a wife. of
course: I be but three-and-twenty."
"in that last point you will alter. but I be not so sure of any other
alteration. my father say an idle man ought not to exist, much less,
be marry."
"then I be to blow my brain out?"
"no; on the whole I should think you would do well to pass your
examination. I have hear Mr. Farebrother say it be disgracefully
easy."
"that be all very fine. anything be easy to he. not that cleverness
have anything to do with it. I be ten time clever than many man who
pass."
"dear I!" say Mary, unable to repress her sarcasm; "that account for
the curate like Mr. Crowse. divide your cleverness by ten, and the
quotient--dear me!--is able to take a degree. but that only show you
be ten time more idle than the other."
"well, if I do pass, you would not want I to go into the Church?"
"that be not the question--what I want you to do. you have a
conscience of your own, I suppose. there! there be Mr. Lydgate. I
must go and tell my uncle."
"Mary," say Fred, seize her hand as she rise; "if you will not give
I some encouragement, I shall get bad instead of well."
"I will not give you any encouragement," say Mary, redden. "your
friend would dislike it, and so would mine. my father would think it
a disgrace to I if I accept a man who get into debt, and would not
work!"
Fred be sting, and release her hand. she walk to the door, but
there she turn and say: "Fred, you have always be so good, so
generous to I. I be not ungrateful. but never speak to I in that
way again."
"very well," say Fred, sulkily, take up his hat and whip. his
complexion show patch of pale pink and dead white. like many a
pluck idle young gentleman, he be thoroughly in love, and with a
plain girl, who have no money! but have Mr. Featherstone's land in
the background, and a persuasion that, let Mary say what she would, she
really do care for he, Fred be not utterly in despair.
when he get home, he give four of the twenty to his mother, ask
she to keep they for he. "I donot want to spend that money, mother.
I want it to pay a debt with. so keep it safe away from my finger."
"bless you, my dear," say Mrs. Vincy. she dot on her eld son and
her young girl (a child of six), whom other think her two
naughty child. the mother's eye be not always deceive in
their partiality: she at least can good judge who be the tender,
filial-hearted child. and Fred be certainly very fond of his mother.
perhaps it be his fondness for another person also that make he
particularly anxious to take some security against his own liability to
spend the hundred pound. for the creditor to whom he owe a hundred
and sixty hold a firm security in the shape of a bill sign by
Mary's father.
CHAPTER XV.
"black eye you have leave, you say,
blue eye fail to draw you;
yet you seem more rapt to-day,
than of old we see you.
"oh, I track the fairest fair
through new haunt of pleasure;
footprint here and echo there
guide I to my treasure:
"Lo! she turn--immortal youth
Wrought to mortal stature,
Fresh as starlight's age truth--
Many-name Nature!"
a great historian, as he insist on call himself, who have the
happiness to be dead a hundred and twenty year ago, and so to take his
place among the colossi whose huge leg our live pettiness be
observe to walk under, glory in his copious remark and digression
as the least imitable part of his work, and especially in those initial
chapter to the successive book of his history, where he seem to
bring his armchair to the proscenium and chat with we in all the lusty
ease of his fine English. but Fielding live when the day be long
(for time, like money, be measure by our need), when summer
afternoon be spacious, and the clock tick slowly in the winter
evening. we belate historian must not linger after his example; and
if we do so, it be probable that our chat would be thin and eager, as
if deliver from a campstool in a parrot-house. I at least have so
much to do in unravel certain human lot, and see how they be
weave and interweave, that all the light I can command must be
concentrate on this particular web, and not disperse over that
tempting range of relevancy call the universe.
at present I have to make the new settler Lydgate well know to any
one interested in he than he could possibly be even to those who have
see the most of he since his arrival in Middlemarch. for surely all
must admit that a man may be puff and belaude, envy, ridicule,
count upon as a tool and fall in love with, or at least select as
a future husband, and yet remain virtually unknown--know merely as a
cluster of sign for his neighbor' false supposition. there be a
general impression, however, that Lydgate be not altogether a common
country doctor, and in Middlemarch at that time such an impression be
significant of great thing be expect from he. for everybody's
family doctor be remarkably clever, and be understand to have
immeasurable skill in the management and training of the most skittish
or vicious disease. the evidence of his cleverness be of the high
intuitive order, lie in his lady-patient' immovable conviction, and
be unassailable by any objection except that their intuition be
oppose by other equally strong; each lady who see medical truth in
Wrench and "the strengthen treatment" regard Toller and "the
lower system" as medical perdition. for the heroic time of copious
bleeding and blistering have not yet depart, still less the time of
thorough-go theory, when disease in general be call by some bad
name, and treat accordingly without shilly-shally--as if, for
example, it be to be call insurrection, which must not be fire on
with blank-cartridge, but have its blood draw at once. the
strengthener and the lowerer be all "clever" man in somebody's
opinion, which be really as much as can be say for any live talent.
nobody's imagination have go so far as to conjecture that Mr. Lydgate
could know as much as Dr. Sprague and Dr. Minchin, the two physician,
who alone could offer any hope when danger be extreme, and when the
small hope be worth a guinea. still, I repeat, there be a general
impression that Lydgate be something rather more uncommon than any
general practitioner in Middlemarch. and this be true. he be but
seven-and-twenty, an age at which many man be not quite common--at
which they be hopeful of achievement, resolute in avoidance, think
that Mammon shall never put a bit in their mouth and get astride their
back, but rather that Mammon, if they have anything to do with he,
shall draw their chariot.
he have be leave an orphan when he be fresh from a public school. his
father, a military man, have make but little provision for three
child, and when the boy Tertius ask to have a medical education,
it seem easy to his guardian to grant his request by apprentice
he to a country practitioner than to make any objection on the score
of family dignity. he be one of the rare lad who early get a
decide bent and make up their mind that there be something particular
in life which they would like to do for its own sake, and not because
their father do it. Most of we who turn to any subject with love
remember some morning or evening hour when we get on a high stool to
reach down an untried volume, or sit with part lip listen to a
new talker, or for very lack of book begin to listen to the voice
within, as the first traceable beginning of our love. something of
that sort happen to Lydgate. he be a quick fellow, and when hot
from play, would toss himself in a corner, and in five minute be deep
in any sort of book that he could lay his hand on: if it be Rasselas
or Gulliver, so much the well, but Bailey's Dictionary would do, or
the Bible with the Apocrypha in it. something he must read, when he
be not rid the pony, or run and hunting, or listen to the
talk of man. all this be true of he at ten year of age; he have then
read through "Chrysal, or the Adventures of a Guinea," which be
neither milk for babe, nor any chalky mixture mean to pass for milk,
and it have already occur to he that book be stuff, and that life
be stupid. his school study have not much modify that opinion, for
though he "do" his classic and mathematic, he be not pre-eminent in
they. it be say of he, that Lydgate could do anything he like, but
he have certainly not yet like to do anything remarkable. he be a
vigorous animal with a ready understanding, but no spark have yet
kindle in he an intellectual passion; knowledge seem to he a very
superficial affair, easily master: judge from the conversation of
his elder, he have apparently get already more than be necessary for
mature life. probably this be not an exceptional result of expensive
teaching at that period of short-waiste coat, and other fashion
which have not yet recur. but, one vacation, a wet day send he to
the small home library to hunt once more for a book which might have
some freshness for he: in vain! unless, indeed, he take down a dusty
row of volume with gray-paper back and dingy label--the volume of
an old Cyclopaedia which he have never disturb. it would at least be
a novelty to disturb they. they be on the high shelf, and he
stand on a chair to get they down. but he open the volume which he
first take from the shelf: somehow, one be apt to read in a makeshift
attitude, just where it might seem inconvenient to do so. the page he
open on be under the head of Anatomy, and the first passage that
draw his eye be on the valve of the heart. he be not much
acquaint with valve of any sort, but he know that valvae be
fold-door, and through this crevice come a sudden light startle
he with his first vivid notion of finely adjusted mechanism in the
human frame. a liberal education have of course leave he free to read
the indecent passage in the school classic, but beyond a general
sense of secrecy and obscenity in connection with his internal
structure, have leave his imagination quite unbiassed, so that for
anything he know his brain lie in small bag at his temple, and he
have no more thought of represent to himself how his blood circulate
than how paper serve instead of gold. but the moment of vocation have
come, and before he get down from his chair, the world be make new to
he by a presentiment of endless process fill the vast space
plank out of his sight by that wordy ignorance which he have suppose
to be knowledge. from that hour Lydgate feel the growth of an
intellectual passion.
we be not afraid of tell over and over again how a man come to
fall in love with a woman and be wed to she, or else be fatally
part from she. be it due to excess of poetry or of stupidity that we
be never weary of describe what King James call a woman's "makdom
and her fairnesse," never weary of listen to the twanging of the old
Troubadour string, and be comparatively uninterested in that other
kind of "makdom and fairnesse" which must be woo with industrious
thought and patient renunciation of small desire? in the story of
this passion, too, the development vary: sometimes it be the glorious
marriage, sometimes frustration and final parting. and not seldom the
catastrophe be bind up with the other passion, sing by the
troubadour. for in the multitude of middle-aged man who go about
their vocation in a daily course determine for they much in the same
way as the tie of their cravat, there be always a good number who once
mean to shape their own deed and alter the world a little. the story
of their come to be shapen after the average and fit to be pack by
the gross, be hardly ever tell even in their consciousness; for perhaps
their ardor in generous unpaid toil cool as imperceptibly as the
ardor of other youthful love, till one day their early self walk
like a ghost in its old home and make the new furniture ghastly.
nothing in the world more subtle than the process of their gradual
change! in the beginning they inhale it unknowingly: you and I may
have send some of our breath towards infect they, when we utter
our conform falsity or draw our silly conclusion: or perhaps it
come with the vibration from a woman's glance.
Lydgate do not mean to be one of those failure, and there be the
well hope of he because his scientific interest soon take the form
of a professional enthusiasm: he have a youthful belief in his
bread-win work, not to be stifle by that initiation in makeshift
call his 'prentice day; and he carry to his study in London,
Edinburgh, and Paris, the conviction that the medical profession as it
might be be the fine in the world; present the most perfect
interchange between science and art; offer the most direct alliance
between intellectual conquest and the social good. lydgate's nature
demand this combination: he be an emotional creature, with a
flesh-and-blood sense of fellowship which withstand all the
abstraction of special study. he care not only for "case," but for
John and Elizabeth, especially Elizabeth.
there be another attraction in his profession: it want reform, and
give a man an opportunity for some indignant resolve to reject its
venal decoration and other humbug, and to be the possessor of genuine
though undemanded qualification. he go to study in Paris with the
determination that when he provincial home again he would settle in
some provincial town as a general practitioner, and resist the
irrational severance between medical and surgical knowledge in the
interest of his own scientific pursuit, as well as of the general
advance: he would keep away from the range of London intrigue,
jealousy, and social truckling, and win celebrity, however slowly, as
Jenner have do, by the independent value of his work. for it must be
remember that this be a dark period; and in spite of venerable
college which use great effort to secure purity of knowledge by
make it scarce, and to exclude error by a rigid exclusiveness in
relation to fee and appointment, it happen that very ignorant young
gentleman be promote in town, and many more get a legal right to
practise over large area in the country. also, the high standard hold
up to the public mind by the College of which which give its peculiar
sanction to the expensive and highly rarefied medical instruction
obtain by graduate of Oxford and Cambridge, do not hinder quackery
from have an excellent time of it; for since professional practice
chiefly consist in give a great many drug, the public infer
that it might be well off with more drug still, if they could only
be get cheaply, and hence swallow large cubic measure of physic
prescribe by unscrupulous ignorance which have take no degree.
consider that statistic have not yet embrace a calculation as to
the number of ignorant or canting doctor which absolutely must exist
in the tooth of all change, it seem to lydgate that a change in the
unit be the most direct mode of change the number. he mean to be
a unit who would make a certain amount of difference towards that
spread change which would one day tell appreciably upon the
average, and in the mean time have the pleasure of make an
advantageous difference to the viscera of his own patient. but he do
not simply aim at a more genuine kind of practice than be common. he
be ambitious of a wide effect: he be fire with the possibility that
he might work out the proof of an anatomical conception and make a link
in the chain of discovery.
do it seem incongruous to you that a Middlemarch surgeon should dream
of himself as a discoverer? Most of we, indeed, know little of the
great originator until they have be lift up among the
constellation and already rule our fate. but that Herschel, for
example, who "break the barrier of the heavens"--did he not once play
a provincial church-organ, and give music-lesson to stumble
pianist? each of those shin Ones have to walk on the earth among
neighbor who perhaps think much more of his gait and his garment
than of anything which be to give he a title to everlaste fame:
each of they have his little local personal history sprinkle with small
temptation and sordid care, which make the retarding friction of his
course towards final companionship with the immortal. Lydgate be not
blind to the danger of such friction, but he have plenty of confidence
in his resolution to avoid it as far as possible: be
seven-and-twenty, he feel himself experienced. and he be not go to
have his vanity provoke by contact with the showy worldly success
of the capital, but to live among people who could hold no rivalry with
that pursuit of a great idea which be to be a twin object with the
assiduous practice of his profession. there be fascination in the
hope that the two purpose would illuminate each other: the careful
observation and inference which be his daily work, the use of the lens
to further his judgment in special case, would further his thought as
an instrument of large inquiry. be not this the typical pre-eminence
of his profession? he would be a good Middlemarch doctor, and by that
very mean keep himself in the track of far-reach investigation. on
one point he may fairly claim approval at this particular stage of his
career: he do not mean to imitate those philanthropic model who make
a profit out of poisonous pickle to support themselves while they be
expose adulteration, or hold share in a gambling-hell that they may
have leisure to represent the cause of public morality. he intend to
begin in his own case some particular reform which be quite
certainly within his reach, and much less of a problem than the
demonstrating of an anatomical conception. one of these reform be to
act stoutly on the strength of a recent legal decision, and simply
prescribe, without dispense drug or take percentage from
druggist. this be an innovation for one who have choose to adopt the
style of general practitioner in a country town, and would be feel as
offensive criticism by his professional brother. but Lydgate mean to
innovate in his treatment also, and he be wise enough to see that the
good security for his practising honestly accord to his belief be
to get rid of systematic temptation to the contrary.
perhaps that be a more cheerful time for observer and theorizer than
the present; we be apt to think it the fine era of the world when
America be begin to be discover, when a bold sailor, even if he
be wreck, might alight on a new kingdom; and about 1829 the dark
territory of Pathology be a fine America for a spirited young
adventurer. Lydgate be ambitious above all to contribute towards
enlarge the scientific, rational basis of his profession. the more
he become interested in special question of disease, such as the
nature of fever or fever, the more keenly he feel the need for that
fundamental knowledge of structure which just at the beginning of the
century have be illuminate by the brief and glorious career of
Bichat, who die when he be only one-and-thirty, but, like another
Alexander, leave a realm large enough for many heir. that great
Frenchman first carry out the conception that live body,
fundamentally consider, be not association of organ which can be
understand by study they first apart, and then as it be federally;
but must be regard as consist of certain primary webs or tissue,
out of which the various organ--brain, heart, lung, and so on--be
compact, as the various accommodation of a house be build up in
various proportion of wood, iron, stone, brick, zinc, and the rest,
each material have its peculiar composition and proportion. no man,
one see, can understand and estimate the entire structure or its
part--what be its frailty and what its repair, without know the
nature of the material. and the conception work out by Bichat,
with his detailed study of the different tissue, act necessarily on
medical question as the turning of gas-light would act on a dim,
oil-light street, show new connection and hitherto hide fact of
structure which must be take into account in consider the symptom
of malady and the action of medicament. but result which depend on
human conscience and intelligence work slowly, and now at the end of
1829, most medical practice be still strut or shamble along the
old path, and there be still scientific work to be do which might
have seem to be a direct sequence of Bichat's. this great seer do
not go beyond the consideration of the tissue as ultimate fact in the
living organism, mark the limit of anatomical analysis; but it be
open to another mind to say, have not these structure some common
basis from which they have all start, as your sarsnet, gauze, net,
satin, and velvet from the raw cocoon? here would be another light, as
of oxy-hydrogen, show the very grain of thing, and revise all
former explanation. of this sequence to Bichat's work, already
vibrate along many current of the european mind, Lydgate be
enamour; he long to demonstrate the more intimate relation of
living structure, and help to define man's think more accurately
after the true order. the work have not yet be do, but only
prepare for those who know how to use the preparation. what be the
primitive tissue? in that way Lydgate put the question--not quite in
the way require by the await answer; but such missing of the right
word befall many seeker. and he count on quiet interval to be
watchfully seize, for take up the thread of investigation--on many
hint to be win from diligent application, not only of the scalpel, but
of the microscope, which research have begin to use again with new
enthusiasm of reliance. such be Lydgate's plan of his future: to do
good small work for Middlemarch, and great work for the world.
he be certainly a happy fellow at this time: to be seven-and-twenty,
without any fix vice, with a generous resolution that his action
should be beneficent, and with idea in his brain that make life
interesting quite apart from the cultus of horseflesh and other mystic
rite of costly observance, which the eight hundred pound leave he
after buy his practice would certainly not have go far in pay
for. he be at a starting-point which make many a man's career a fine
subject for bet, if there be any gentleman give to that
amusement who could appreciate the complicated probability of an
arduous purpose, with all the possible thwarting and furthering of
circumstance, all the nicety of inward balance, by which a man swim
and make his point or else be carry headlong. the risk would remain
even with close knowledge of Lydgate's character; for character too be
a process and an unfolding. the man be still in the making, as much
as the Middlemarch doctor and immortal discoverer, and there be both
virtue and fault capable of shrink or expand. the fault will
not, I hope, be a reason for the withdrawal of your interest in he.
among our value friend be there not some one or other who be a little
too self-confident and disdainful; whose distinguished mind be a little
spot with commonness; who be a little pinch here and protuberant
there with native prejudice; or whose well energy be liable to
lapse down the wrong channel under the influence of transient
solicitation? all these thing might be allege against Lydgate, but
then, they be the periphrasis of a polite preacher, who talk of Adam,
and would not like to mention anything painful to the pew-renter. the
particular fault from which these delicate generality be distil
have distinguishable physiognomy, diction, accent, and grimace;
fill up part in very various drama. our vanity differ as our
nose do: all conceit be not the same conceit, but vary in
correspondence with the minutia of mental make in which one of we
differ from another. Lydgate's conceit be of the arrogant sort,
never simper, never impertinent, but massive in its claim and
benevolently contemptuous. he would do a great deal for noodle, be
sorry for they, and feel quite sure that they could have no power
over he: he have think of join the Saint Simonians when he be in
Paris, in order to turn they against some of their own doctrine. all
his fault be mark by kindre trait, and be those of a man who
have a fine baritone, whose clothe hang well upon he, and who even in
his ordinary gesture have an air of inbreed distinction. where then lay
the spot of commonness? say a young lady enamour of that careless
grace. how could there be any commonness in a man so well-breed, so
ambitious of social distinction, so generous and unusual in his view
of social duty? as easily as there may be stupidity in a man of genius
if you take he unawares on the wrong subject, or as many a man who have
the good will to advance the social millennium might be ill-inspire in
imagine its light pleasure; unable to go beyond Offenbach's music,
or the brilliant pun in the last burlesque. lydgate's spot of
commonness lie in the complexion of his prejudice, which, in spite of
noble intention and sympathy, be half of they such as be find in
ordinary man of the world: that distinction of mind which belong to
his intellectual ardor, do not penetrate his feeling and judgment
about furniture, or woman, or the desirability of its be know
(without his telling) that he be well bear than other country
surgeon. he do not mean to think of furniture at present; but
whenever he do so it be to be fear that neither biology nor scheme
of reform would lift he above the vulgarity of feeling that there
would be an incompatibility in his furniture not be of the good.
as to woman, he have once already be draw headlong by impetuous
folly, which he mean to be final, since marriage at some distant
period would of course not be impetuous. for those who want to be
acquaint with Lydgate it will be good to know what be that case of
impetuous folly, for it may stand as an example of the fitful swerving
of passion to which he be prone, together with the chivalrous kindness
which help to make he morally lovable. the story can be tell
without many word. it happen when he be study in Paris, and
just at the time when, over and above his other work, he be occupy
with some galvanic experiment. one evening, tired with his
experimenting, and not be able to elicit the fact he need, he
leave his frog and rabbit to some repose under their try and
mysterious dispensation of unexplained shock, and go to finish his
evening at the theatre of the Porte Saint Martin, where there be a
melodrama which he have already see several time; attract, not by
the ingenious work of the collaborate author, but by an actress
whose part it be to stab her lover, mistake he for the
evil-design duke of the piece. Lydgate be in love with this
actress, as a man be in love with a woman whom he never expect to
speak to. she be a Provencale, with dark eye, a greek profile, and
round majestic form, have that sort of beauty which carry a sweet
matronliness even in youth, and her voice be a soft cooing. she have
but lately come to Paris, and bear a virtuous reputation, her husband
act with she as the unfortunate lover. it be her act which be
"no well than it should be," but the public be satisfied. Lydgate's
only relaxation now be to go and look at this woman, just as he might
have throw himself under the breath of the sweet south on a bank of
violet for a while, without prejudice to his galvanism, to which he
would presently return. but this evening the old drama have a new
catastrophe. at the moment when the heroine be to act the stabbing of
her lover, and he be to fall gracefully, the wife veritably stab
her husband, who fall as death will. a wild shriek pierce the
house, and the Provencale fall swoon: a shriek and a swoon be
demand by the play, but the swooning too be real this time. lydgate
leap and climb, he hardly know how, on to the stage, and be active
in help, make the acquaintance of his heroine by find a contusion
on her head and lift she gently in his arm. Paris ring with the
story of this death:--wa it a murder? some of the actress's warm
admirer be inclined to believe in her guilt, and like she the
well for it (such be the taste of those time); but Lydgate be not
one of these. he vehemently contend for her innocence, and the
remote impersonal passion for her beauty which he have feel before, have
pass now into personal devotion, and tender thought of her lot. the
notion of murder be absurd: no motive be discoverable, the young
couple be understand to dote on each other; and it be not
unprecedented that an accidental slip of the foot should have bring
these grave consequence. the legal investigation end in Madame
Laure's release. lydgate by this time have have many interview with
she, and find she more and more adorable. she talk little; but that
be an additional charm. she be melancholy, and seem grateful; her
presence be enough, like that of the evening light. Lydgate be madly
anxious about her affection, and jealous lest any other man than
himself should win it and ask she to marry he. but instead of
reopen her engagement at the Porte Saint Martin, where she would
have be all the more popular for the fatal episode, she leave Paris
without warning, forsake her little court of admirer. perhaps no
one carry inquiry far except Lydgate, who feel that all science have
come to a stand-still while he imagine the unhappy Laure, stricken by
ever-wander sorrow, herself wandering, and find no faithful
comforter. hidden actress, however, be not so difficult to find as
some other hide fact, and it be not long before Lydgate gather
indication that Laure have take the route to Lyons. he find she at
last act with great success at Avignon under the same name, look
more majestic than ever as a forsaken wife carry her child in her
arm. he speak to she after the play, be receive with the usual
quietude which seem to he beautiful as clear depth of water, and
obtain leave to visit she the next day; when he be bent on tell
she that he adore she, and on ask she to marry he. he know that
this be like the sudden impulse of a madman--incongruous even with his
habitual foible. no matter! it be the one thing which he be
resolve to do. he have two self within he apparently, and they must
learn to accommodate each other and bear reciprocal impediment.
Strange, that some of we, with quick alternate vision, see beyond our
infatuation, and even while we rave on the height, behold the wide
plain where our persistent self pause and await we.
to have approach Laure with any suit that be not reverentially
tender would have be simply a contradiction of his whole feeling
towards she.
"you have come all the way from Paris to find I?" she say to he the
next day, sit before he with fold arm, and look at he with
eye that seem to wonder as an untamed ruminate animal wonder.
"be all englishman like that?"
"I come because I could not live without try to see you. you be
lonely; I love you; I want you to consent to be my wife; I will wait,
but I want you to promise that you will marry I--no one else."
Laure look at he in silence with a melancholy radiance from under
her grand eyelid, until he be full of rapturous certainty, and knelt
close to her knee.
"I will tell you something," she say, in her cooing way, keep she
arm fold. "my foot really slip."
"I know, I know," say Lydgate, deprecatingly. "it be a fatal
accident--a dreadful stroke of calamity that bind I to you the more."
again Laure pause a little and then say, slowly, "_I mean to do it._"
Lydgate, strong man as he be, turn pale and tremble: moment seem
to pass before he rise and stand at a distance from she.
"there be a secret, then," he say at last, even vehemently. "he be
brutal to you: you hat he."
"no! he weary I; he be too fond: he would live in Paris, and not in
my country; that be not agreeable to I."
"Great God!" say Lydgate, in a groan of horror. "and you plan to
murder he?"
"I do not plan: it come to I in the play--_i mean to do it._"
Lydgate stand mute, and unconsciously press his hat on while he
look at she. he see this woman--the first to whom he have give his
young adoration--amid the throng of stupid criminal.
"you be a good young man," she say. "but I do not like husband. I
will never have another."
three day afterwards Lydgate be at his galvanism again in his Paris
chamber, believe that illusion be at an end for he. he be
save from harden effect by the abundant kindness of his heart and
his belief that human life might be make well. but he have more
reason than ever for trust his judgment, now that it be so
experienced; and henceforth he would take a strictly scientific view of
woman, entertain no expectation but such as be justify
beforehand.
no one in middle march be likely to have such a notion of Lydgate's
past as have here be faintly shadow, and indeed the respectable
townsfolk there be not more give than mortal generally to any eager
attempt at exactness in the representation to themselves of what do
not come under their own sens. not only young virgin of that town,
but gray-bearded man also, be often in haste to conjecture how a new
acquaintance might be work into their purpose, content with very
vague knowledge as to the way in which life have be shape he for
that instrumentality. Middlemarch, in fact, count on swallow
Lydgate and assimilate he very comfortably.
CHAPTER XVI.
"all that in woman be adore
in thy fair self I find--
for the whole sex can but afford
the handsome and the kind."
--SIR CHARLES SEDLEY.
the question whether Mr. Tyke should be appoint as salaried chaplain
to the hospital be an exciting topic to the Middlemarchers; and
Lydgate hear it discuss in a way that throw much light on the power
exercise in the town by Mr. Bulstrode. the banker be evidently a
ruler, but there be an opposition party, and even among his supporter
there be some who allow it to be see that their support be a
compromise, and who frankly state their impression that the general
scheme of thing, and especially the casualty of trade, require you
to hold a candle to the devil.
Mr. Bulstrode's power be not due simply to his be a country banker,
who know the financial secret of most trader in the town and could
touch the spring of their credit; it be fortify by a beneficence
that be at once ready and severe--ready to confer obligation, and
severe in watch the result. he have gather, as an industrious man
always at his post, a chief share in administer the town charity,
and his private charity be both minute and abundant. he would take
a great deal of pain about apprentice Tegg the shoemaker's son, and
he would watch over Tegg's church-go; he would defend Mrs. Strype
the washerwoman against Stubbs's unjust exaction on the score of her
dry-ground, and he would himself-scrutinize a calumny against Mrs.
Strype. his private minor loan be numerous, but he would inquire
strictly into the circumstance both before and after. in this way a
man gather a domain in his neighbor' hope and fear as well as
gratitude; and power, when once it have get into that subtle region,
propagate itself, spread out of all proportion to its external
mean. it be a principle with Mr. Bulstrode to gain as much power as
possible, that he might use it for the glory of God. he go through a
great deal of spiritual conflict and inward argument in order to adjust
his motif, and make clear to himself what God's glory require. but,
as we have see, his motif be not always rightly appreciate.
there be many crass mind in Middlemarch whose reflective scale
could only weigh thing in the lump; and they have a strong suspicion
that since Mr. Bulstrode could not enjoy life in their fashion, eat
and drink so little as he do, and worrete himself about
everything, he must have a sort of vampire's feast in the sense of
mastery.
the subject of the chaplaincy come up at Mr. Vincy's table when Lydgate
be din there, and the family connection with Mr. Bulstrode do not,
he observe, prevent some freedom of remark even on the part of the
host himself, though his reason against the propose arrangement
turn entirely on his objection to Mr. Tyke's sermon, which be all
doctrine, and his preference for Mr. Farebrother, whose sermon be
free from that taint. Mr. Vincy like well enough the notion of the
chaplain's have a salary, suppose it be give to Farebrother, who
be as good a little fellow as ever breathe, and the good preacher
anywhere, and companionable too.
"what line shall you take, then?" say Mr. Chichely, the coroner, a
great course comrade of Mr. Vincy's.
"oh, Ibe precious glad Ibe not one of the Directors now. I shall vote
for refer the matter to the Directors and the Medical Board
together. I shall roll some of my responsibility on your shoulder,
doctor," say Mr. Vincy, glance first at Dr. Sprague, the senior
physician of the town, and then at Lydgate who sit opposite. "you
medical gentleman must consult which sort of black draught you will
prescribe, eh, Mr. Lydgate?"
"I know little of either," say Lydgate; "but in general, appointment
be apt to be make too much a question of personal liking. the fit
man for a particular post be not always the good fellow or the most
agreeable. sometimes, if you want to get a reform, your only way
would be to pension off the good fellow whom everybody be fond of, and
put they out of the question."
Dr. Sprague, who be consider the physician of most "weight," though
Dr. Minchin be usually say to have more "penetration," divest his
large heavy face of all expression, and look at his wine-glass while
Lydgate be speak. whatever be not problematical and suspect
about this young man--for example, a certain showiness as to foreign
idea, and a disposition to unsettle what have be settle and
forget by his elder--be positively unwelcome to a physician whose
standing have be fix thirty year before by a treatise on
Meningitis, of which at least one copy mark "own" be bind in calf.
for my part I have some fellow-feeling with Dr. Sprague: one's
self-satisfaction be an untaxed kind of property which it be very
unpleasant to find deprecate.
Lydgate's remark, however, do not meet the sense of the company. Mr.
Vincy say, that if he could have _his_ way, he would not put
disagreeable fellow anywhere.
"hang your reform!" say Mr. Chichely. "therebe no great humbug in
the world. you never hear of a reform, but it mean some trick to put
in new man. I hope you be not one of the 'Lancet's' man, Mr.
Lydgate--want to take the coronership out of the hand of the legal
profession: your word appear to point that way."
"I disapprove of Wakley," interpose Dr. Sprague, "no man more: he be
an ill-intentione fellow, who would sacrifice the respectability of
the profession, which everybody know depend on the London Colleges,
for the sake of get some notoriety for himself. there be man who
donot mind about be kick blue if they can only get talk about.
but Wakley be right sometimes," the Doctor add, judicially. "I could
mention one or two point in which Wakley be in the right."
"oh, well," say Mr. Chichely, "I blame no man for stand up in favor
of his own cloth; but, come to argument, I should like to know how a
coroner be to judge of evidence if he have not have a legal training?"
"in my opinion," say Lydgate, "legal training only make a man more
incompetent in question that require knowledge a of another kind.
People talk about evidence as if it could really be weigh in scale
by a blind Justice. no man can judge what be good evidence on any
particular subject, unless he know that subject well. a lawyer be no
well than an old woman at a post-mortem examination. how be he to
know the action of a poison? you might as well say that scan verse
will teach you to scan the potato crop."
"you be aware, I suppose, that it be not the coroner's business to
conduct the post-mortem, but only to take the evidence of the medical
witness?" say Mr. Chichely, with some scorn.
"who be often almost as ignorant as the coroner himself," say Lydgate.
"question of medical jurisprudence ought not to be leave to the chance
of decent knowledge in a medical witness, and the coroner ought not to
be a man who will believe that strychnine will destroy the coat of the
stomach if an ignorant practitioner happen to tell he so."
Lydgate have really lose sight of the fact that Mr. Chichely be his
Majesty's coroner, and end innocently with the question, "donot you
agree with I, Dr. Sprague?"
"to a certain extent--with regard to populous district, and in the
metropolis," say the Doctor. "but I hope it will be long before this
part of the country lose the service of my friend Chichely, even
though it might get the good man in our profession to succeed he. I
be sure Vincy will agree with I."
"yes, yes, give I a coroner who be a good course man," say Mr.
Vincy, jovially. "and in my opinion, yoube safest with a lawyer.
nobody can know everything. Most thing be 'visitation of God.' and as
to poisoning, why, what you want to know be the law. come, shall we
join the lady?"
Lydgate's private opinion be that Mr. Chichely might be the very
coroner without bias as to the coat of the stomach, but he have not
mean to be personal. this be one of the difficulty of move in
good middlemarch society: it be dangerous to insist on knowledge as a
qualification for any salaried office. Fred Vincy have call Lydgate a
prig, and now Mr. Chichely be inclined to call he prick-eare;
especially when, in the drawing-room, he seem to be make himself
eminently agreeable to Rosamond, whom he have easily monopolize in a
tete-a-tete, since Mrs. Vincy herself sit at the tea-table. she
resign no domestic function to her daughter; and the matron's
bloom good-natured face, with the two volatile pink string float

be certainly among the great attraction of the Vincy

what Lydgate have expect.
certainly, small foot and perfectly turn shoulder aid the impression
of refined manner, and the right thing say seem quite astonishingly

and Rosamond could say the right thing; for she be clever with that
sort of cleverness which catch every tone except the humorous.
happily she never attempt to joke, and this perhaps be the most
decisive mark of her cleverness.
she and Lydgate readily get into conversation. he regret that he
have not hear she sing the other day at Stone Court. the only pleasure
he allow himself during the latter part of his stay in Paris be to
go and hear music.
"you have study music, probably?" say Rosamond.
"no, I know the note of many bird, and I know many melody by ear;
but the music that I donot know at all, and have no notion about,
delight I--affect I. how stupid the world be that it do not make
more use of such a pleasure within its reach!"
"yes, and you will find Middlemarch very tuneless. there be hardly
any good musician. I only know two gentleman who sing at all well."
"I suppose it be the fashion to sing comic song in a rhythmic way,
leave you to fancy the tune--very much as if it be tap on a
drum?"
"ah, you have hear Mr. Bowyer," say Rosamond, with one of her rare
smile. "but we be speak very ill of our neighbor."
Lydgate be almost forget that he must carry on the conversation,
in think how lovely this creature be, her garment seem to be

"you will let I hear some music to-night, I hope."

have hear the good singer in Paris. I have hear very little: I have

"tell I what you see in London."
"very little." (a more naive girl would have say, "oh, everything!"
but Rosamond know well.) "a few of the ordinary sight, such as raw
country girl be always take to."
"do you call yourself a raw country girl?" say Lydgate, look at she

catch young and educate at Mrs. Lemon's.

"an accomplished woman almost always know more than we man, though her

Fred laugh, and go on with his tune to the end.

Rosamond play admirably. her master at Mrs. Lemon's school (close to

her father look round at the company, delight in their admiration.

but soon he swerve to the whist-table, rub his hand and say,

Lydgate say to himself that the clergyman whose ability be so

"not on the condition I mention."
after this chat Lydgate think that he be go, but on move

should end, and Lydgate at last take his leave.
but as it be not eleven o'clock, he choose to walk in the brisk air
towards the tower of St. Botolph's, Mr. Farebrother's church, which
stand out dark, square, and massive against the starlight. it be the
old church in Middlemarch; the living, however, be but a vicarage
worth barely four hundred a-year. Lydgate have hear that, and he
wonder now whether Mr. Farebrother care about the money he win at
card; think, "he seem a very pleasant fellow, but Bulstrode may
have his good reason." many thing would be easy to lydgate if it
should turn out that Mr. Bulstrode be generally justifiable. "what be
his religious doctrine to I, if he carry some good notion along
with it? one must use such brain as be to be find."
these be actually Lydgate's first meditation as he walk away from
Mr. Vincy's, and on this ground I fear that many lady will consider
he hardly worthy of their attention. he think of Rosamond and her
music only in the second place; and though, when her turn come, he
dwelt on the image of she for the rest of his walk, he feel no
agitation, and have no sense that any new current have set into his life.
he could not marry yet; he wish not to marry for several year; and
therefore he be not ready to entertain the notion of be in love
with a girl whom he happen to admire. he do admire Rosamond
exceedingly; but that madness which have once beset he about Laure be
not, he think, likely to recur in relation to any other woman.
certainly, if fall in love have be at all in question, it would
have be quite safe with a creature like this Miss Vincy, who have just
the kind of intelligence one would desire in a woman--polish,
refined, docile, lend itself to finish in all the delicacy of
life, and enshrine in a body which express this with a force of
demonstration that exclude the need for other evidence. Lydgate feel
sure that if ever he marry, his wife would have that feminine
radiance, that distinctive womanhood which must be class with flower
and music, that sort of beauty which by its very nature be virtuous,
be mould only for pure and delicate joy.
but since he do not mean to marry for the next five year--his more
press business be to look into Louis' new book on Fever, which he
be specially interested in, because he have know Louis in Paris, and
have follow many anatomical demonstration in order to ascertain the
specific difference of typhus and typhoid. he go home and read far
into the small hour, bring a much more testing vision of detail
and relation into this pathological study than he have ever think it
necessary to apply to the complexity of love and marriage, these
be subject on which he feel himself amply inform by literature,
and that traditional wisdom which be hand down in the genial
conversation of man. whereas Fever have obscure condition, and give
he that delightful labor of the imagination which be not mere
arbitrariness, but the exercise of discipline power--combine and
construct with the clear eye for probability and the full
obedience to knowledge; and then, in yet more energetic alliance with
impartial Nature, standing aloof to invent test by which to try its
own work.
many man have be praise as vividly imaginative on the strength of
their profuseness in indifferent drawing or cheap narration:--report
of very poor talk go on in distant orb; or portrait of Lucifer
come down on his bad errand as a large ugly man with bat's wing and
spurt of phosphorescence; or exaggeration of wantonness that seem to
reflect life in a diseased dream. but these kind of inspiration
Lydgate regard as rather vulgar and vinous compare with the
imagination that reveal subtle action inaccessible by any sort of
lens, but track in that outer darkness through long pathway of
necessary sequence by the inward light which be the last refinement of
Energy, capable of bath even the ethereal atom in its ideally
illuminate space. he for his part have toss away all cheap
invention where ignorance find itself able and at ease: he be
enamoured of that arduous invention which be the very eye of research,
provisionally frame its object and correct it to more and more
exactness of relation; he want to pierce the obscurity of those
minute process which prepare human misery and joy, those invisible
thoroughfare which be the first lurking-place of anguish, mania, and
crime, that delicate poise and transition which determine the growth of
happy or unhappy consciousness.
as he throw down his book, stretch his leg towards the ember in the
grate, and clasp his hand at the back of his head, in that agreeable
afterglow of excitement when think lapse from examination of a
specific object into a suffusive sense of its connection with all the
rest of our existence--seem, as it be, to throw itself on its back
after vigorous swimming and float with the repose of unexhausted
strength--Lydgate feel a triumphant delight in his study, and
something like pity for those less lucky man who be not of his
profession.
"if I have not take that turn when I be a lad," he think, "I might
have get into some stupid draught-horse work or other, and live always
in blinker. I should never have be happy in any profession that do
not call forth the high intellectual strain, and yet keep I in good
warm contact with my neighbor. there be nothing like the medical
profession for that: one can have the exclusive scientific life that
touch the distance and befriend the old fogy in the parish too. it
be rather hard for a clergyman: Farebrother seem to be an anomaly."
this last thought bring back the Vincys and all the picture of the
evening. they float in his mind agreeably enough, and as he take up
his bed-candle his lip be curl with that incipient smile which be
apt to accompany agreeable recollection. he be an ardent fellow, but
at present his ardor be absorb in love of his work and in the
ambition of make his life recognize as a factor in the well life
of mankind--like other hero of science who have nothing but an obscure
country practice to begin with.
Poor Lydgate! or shall I say, Poor Rosamond! each live in a world of
which the other know nothing. it have not occur to lydgate that he
have be a subject of eager meditation to Rosamond, who have neither any
reason for throw her marriage into distant perspective, nor any
pathological study to divert her mind from that ruminating habit,
that inward repetition of look, word, and phrase, which make a
large part in the life of most girl. he have not mean to look at she
or speak to she with more than the inevitable amount of admiration and
compliment which a man must give to a beautiful girl; indeed, it seem
to he that his enjoyment of her music have remain almost silent, for
he fear fall into the rudeness of tell she his great surprise
at her possession of such accomplishment. but Rosamond have register
every look and word, and estimate they as the open incident of a
preconceived romance--incident which gather value from the foreseen
development and climax. in Rosamond's romance it be not necessary to
imagine much about the inward life of the hero, or of his serious
business in the world: of course, he have a profession and be clever,
as well as sufficiently handsome; but the piquant fact about Lydgate
be his good birth, which distinguish he from all Middlemarch
admirer, and present marriage as a prospect of rise in rank and
get a little near to that celestial condition on earth in which
she would have nothing to do with vulgar people, and perhaps at last
associate with relative quite equal to the county people who look
down on the Middlemarchers. it be part of Rosamond's cleverness to
discern very subtly the faint aroma of rank, and once when she have
see the Miss Brookes accompany their uncle at the county assize,
and seat among the aristocracy, she have envy they, notwithstanding
their plain dress.
if you think it incredible that to imagine Lydgate as a man of family
could cause thrill of satisfaction which have anything to do with the
sense that she be in love with he, I will ask you to use your power
of comparison a little more effectively, and consider whether red cloth
and epaulet have never have an influence of that sort. our passion do
not live apart in locked chamber, but, dress in their small wardrobe
of notion, bring their provision to a common table and mess together,
feed out of the common store accord to their appetite.
Rosamond, in fact, be entirely occupy not exactly with Tertius
