excusable in a girl who be accustomed to hear that all young man
might, could, would be, or actually be in love with she, to believe
at once that Lydgate could be no exception. his look and word mean
more to she than other man's, because she care more for they: she
think of they diligently, and diligently attend to that perfection
of appearance, behavior, sentiment, and all other elegancie, which
would find in Lydgate a more adequate admirer than she have yet be
conscious of.
for Rosamond, though she would never do anything that be disagreeable
to she, be industrious; and now more than ever she be active in
sketch her landscape and market-cart and portrait of friend, in
practise her music, and in be from morning till night her own
standard of a perfect lady, have always an audience in her own
consciousness, with sometimes the not unwelcome addition of a more
variable external audience in the numerous visitor of the house. she
find time also to read the good novel, and even the second good, and
she know much poetry by heart. her favorite poem be "Lalla Rookh."
"the good girl in the world! he will be a happy fellow who get she!"
be the sentiment of the elderly gentleman who visit the Vincys; and
the reject young man think of try again, as be the fashion in
country town where the horizon be not thick with come rival. but
Mrs. Plymdale think that Rosamond have be educate to a ridiculous
pitch, for what be the use of accomplishment which would be all lay
aside as soon as she be marry? while her aunt Bulstrode, who have a
sisterly faithfulness towards her brother's family, have two sincere
wish for Rosamond--that she might show a more serious turn of mind,
and that she might meet with a husband whose wealth correspond to her
habit.
CHAPTER XVII.
"the clerkly person smile and say
promise be a pretty maid,
but be poor she die unwed."
the Rev. Camden Farebrother, whom Lydgate go to see the next evening,
live in an old parsonage, build of stone, venerable enough to match
the church which it look out upon. all the furniture too in the
house be old, but with another grade of age--that of Mr. Farebrother's
father and grandfather. there be paint white chair, with gilding
and wreath on they, and some linger red silk damask with slit in
it. there be engrave portrait of Lord Chancellors and other
celebrate lawyer of the last century; and there be old pier-glass
to reflect they, as well as the little satin-wood table and the sofas
resemble a prolongation of uneasy chair, all stand in relief
against the dark wainscot this be the physiognomy of the drawing-room
into which Lydgate be show; and there be three lady to receive
he, who be also old-fashioned, and of a faded but genuine
respectability: Mrs. Farebrother, the Vicar's white-haired mother,
befrille and kerchiefe with dainty cleanliness, up right, quick-eye,
and still under seventy; Miss Noble, her sister, a tiny old lady of
meeker aspect, with frill and kerchief decidedly more worn and mend;
and Miss Winifred Farebrother, the Vicar's eld sister, well-look
like himself, but nip and subdue as single woman be apt to be who
spend their life in uninterrupted subjection to their elder. lydgate
have not expect to see so quaint a group: know simply that Mr.
Farebrother be a bachelor, he have think of be usher into a
snuggery where the chief furniture would probably be book and
collection of natural object. the Vicar himself seem to wear
rather a change aspect, as most man do when acquaintance make
elsewhere see they for the first time in their own home; some indeed
show like an actor of genial part disadvantageously cast for the
curmudgeon in a new piece. this be not the case with Mr. Farebrother:
he seem a trifle milder and more silent, the chief talker be his
mother, while he only put in a good-humor moderate remark here and
there. the old lady be evidently accustomed to tell her company what
they ought to think, and to regard no subject as quite safe without her
steering. she be afford leisure for this function by have all her
little want attend to by Miss Winifred. meanwhile tiny Miss Noble
carry on her arm a small basket, into which she divert a bit of
sugar, which she have first drop in her saucer as if by mistake;
look round furtively afterwards, and revert to her teacup with a
small innocent noise as of a tiny timid quadruped. pray think no ill
of Miss Noble. that basket hold small saving from her more portable
food, destine for the child of her poor friend among whom she
trot on fine morning; foster and pet all needy creature
be so spontaneous a delight to she, that she regard it much as if
it have be a pleasant vice that she be addict to. perhaps she be
conscious of be tempt to steal from those who have much that she
might give to those who have nothing, and carry in her conscience the
guilt of that repressed desire. one must be poor to know the luxury of
give!
Mrs. Farebrother welcome the guest with a lively formality and
precision. she presently inform he that they be not often in want
of medical aid in that house. she have bring up her child to wear
flannel and not to over-eat themselves, which last habit she consider
the chief reason why people need doctor. Lydgate plead for those
whose father and mother have over-eat themselves, but Mrs.
Farebrother hold that view of thing dangerous: nature be more just
than that; it would be easy for any felon to say that his ancestor
ought to have be hang instead of he. if those he have bad father
and mother be bad themselves, they be hang for that. there be
no need to go back on what you couldnot see.
"my mother be like old George the Third," say the Vicar, "she object
to metaphysic."
"I object to what be wrong, Camden. I say, keep hold of a few plain
truth, and make everything square with they. when I be young, Mr.
Lydgate, there never be any question about right and wrong. we know
our catechism, and that be enough; we learn our creed and our duty.
every respectable Church person have the same opinion. but now, if you
speak out of the Prayer-book itself, you be liable to be contradict."
"that make rather a pleasant time of it for those who like to maintain
their own point," say Lydgate.
"but my mother always give way," say the Vicar, slyly.
"no, no, Camden, you must not lead Mr. Lydgate into a mistake about
_I_. I shall never show that disrespect to my parent, to give up what
they teach I. any one may see what come of turn. if you change
once, why not twenty time?"
"a man might see good argument for change once, and not see they for
change again," say Lydgate, amuse with the decisive old lady.
"excuse I there. if you go upon argument, they be never want,
when a man have no constancy of mind. my father never change, and he
preach plain moral sermon without argument, and be a good man--few
well. when you get I a good man make out of argument, I will get
you a good dinner with read you the cookery-book. thatbe my opinion,
and I think anybody's stomach will bear I out."
"about the dinner certainly, mother," say Mr. Farebrother.
"it be the same thing, the dinner or the man. I be nearly seventy, Mr.
Lydgate, and I go upon experience. I be not likely to follow new
light, though there be plenty of they here as elsewhere. I say, they
come in with the mixed stuff that will neither wash nor wear. it be
not so in my youth: a Churchman be a Churchman, and a clergyman, you
might be pretty sure, be a gentleman, if nothing else. but now he may
be no well than a Dissenter, and want to push aside my son on
pretence of doctrine. but whoever may wish to push he aside, I be
proud to say, Mr. Lydgate, that he will compare with any preacher in
this kingdom, not to speak of this town, which be but a low standard to
go by; at least, to my thinking, for I be bear and breed at Exeter."
"a mother be never partial," say Mr. Farebrother, smile. "what do
you think Tyke's mother say about he?"
"ah, poor creature! what indeed?" say Mrs. Farebrother, her sharpness
blunt for the moment by her confidence in maternal judgment. "she
say the truth to herself, depend upon it."
"and what be the truth?" say Lydgate. "I be curious to know."
"oh, nothing bad at all," say Mr. Farebrother. "he be a zealous
fellow: not very learn, and not very wise, I think--because I donot
agree with he."
"why, Camden!" say Miss Winifred, "Griffin and his wife tell I only
to-day, that Mr. Tyke say they should have no more coal if they come
to hear you preach."
Mrs. Farebrother lay down her knitting, which she have resume after
her small allowance of tea and toast, and look at her son as if to
say "you hear that?" Miss Noble say, "oh poor thing! poor thing!"
in reference, probably, to the double loss of preaching and coal. but
the Vicar answer quietly--
"that be because they be not my parishioner. and I donot think my
sermon be worth a load of coal to they."
"Mr. Lydgate," say Mrs. Farebrother, who could not let this pass, "you
donot know my son: he always undervalue himself. I tell he he be
undervalue the God who make he, and make he a most excellent
preacher."
"that must be a hint for I to take Mr. Lydgate away to my study,
mother," say the Vicar, laugh. "I promise to show you my
collection," he add, turn to lydgate; "shall we go?"
all three lady remonstrate. Mr. Lydgate ought not to be hurry
away without be allow to accept another cup of tea: Miss Winifred
have abundance of good tea in the pot. why be Camden in such haste to
take a visitor to his den? there be nothing but pickled vermin, and
drawer full of blue-bottle and moth, with no carpet on the floor.
Mr. Lydgate must excuse it. a game at cribbage would be far well.
in short, it be plain that a vicar might be adore by his womankind as
the king of man and preacher, and yet be hold by they to stand in much
need of their direction. lydgate, with the usual shallowness of a
young bachelor, wonder that Mr. Farebrother have not teach they
well.
"my mother be not use to my have visitor who can take any interest
in my hobby," say the Vicar, as he open the door of his study,
which be indeed as bare of luxury for the body as the lady have
imply, unless a short porcelain pipe and a tobacco-box be to be
excepted.
"man of your profession donot generally smoke," he say. lydgate
smile and shake his head. "nor of mine either, properly, I suppose.
you will hear that pipe allege against I by Bulstrode and Company.
they donot know how pleased the devil would be if I give it up."
"I understand. you be of an excitable temper and want a sedative. I
be heavy, and should get idle with it. I should rush into idleness,
and stagnate there with all my might."
"and you mean to give it all to your work. I be some ten or twelve
year old than you, and have come to a compromise. I feed a weakness
or two lest they should get clamorous. see," continue the Vicar,
open several small drawer, "I fancy I have make an exhaustive study
of the entomology of this district. I be go on both with the fauna
and flora; but I have at least do my insect well. we be singularly
rich in orthoptera: I donot know whether--ah! you have get hold of that
glass jar--you be look into that instead of my drawer. you donot
really care about these thing?"
"not by the side of this lovely anencephalous monster. I have never
have time to give myself much to natural history. I be early bite
with an interest in structure, and it be what lie most directly in my
profession. I have no hobby besides. I have the sea to swim in there."
"ah! you be a happy fellow," say Mr. Farebrother, turn on his heel
and begin to fill his pipe. "you donot know what it be to want
spiritual tobacco--bad emendation of old text, or small item about a
variety of Aphis Brassicae, with the well-know signature of
Philomicron, for the 'Twaddler's magazine;' or a learn treatise on
the entomology of the Pentateuch, include all the insect not
mention, but probably meet with by the Israelites in their passage
through the desert; with a monograph on the Ant, as treat by Solomon,
show the harmony of the Book of Proverbs with the result of modern
research. you donot mind my fumigating you?"
Lydgate be more surprised at the openness of this talk than at its
imply meaning--that the Vicar feel himself not altogether in the
right vocation. the neat fitting-up of drawer and shelf, and the
bookcase fill with expensive illustrate book on Natural history,
make he think again of the winning at card and their destination.
but he be begin to wish that the very good construction of
everything that Mr. Farebrother do should be the true one. the
Vicar's frankness seem not of the repulsive sort that come from an
uneasy consciousness seek to forestall the judgment of other, but
simply the relief of a desire to do with as little pretence as
possible. apparently he be not without a sense that his freedom of
speech might seem premature, for he presently said--
"I have not yet tell you that I have the advantage of you, Mr. Lydgate,
and know you well than you know I. you remember Trawley who share
your apartment at Paris for some time? I be a correspondent of his,
and he tell I a good deal about you. I be not quite sure when you
first come that you be the same man. I be very glad when I find
that you be. only I donot forget that you have not have the like
prologue about I."
Lydgate divine some delicacy of feeling here, but do not half
understand it. "by the way," he say, "what have become of Trawley? I
have quite lose sight of he. he be hot on the french social system,
and talk of go to the backwood to found a sort of pythagorean
community. be he go?"
"not at all. he be practise at a german bath, and have marry a rich
patient."
"then my notion wear the good, so far," say Lydgate, with a short
scornful laugh. "he would have it, the medical profession be an
inevitable system of humbug. I say, the fault be in the man--man who
truckle to lie and folly. instead of preach against humbug outside
the wall, it might be well to set up a disinfect apparatus
within. in short--I be report my own conversation--you may be sure
I have all the good sense on my side."
"your scheme be a good deal more difficult to carry out than the
pythagorean community, though. you have not only get the old Adam in
yourself against you, but you have get all those descendant of the
original Adam who form the society around you. you see, I have pay
twelve or thirteen year more than you for my knowledge of
difficulty. But"--Mr. Farebrother break off a moment, and then
add, "you be eye that glass vase again. do you want to make an
exchange? you shall not have it without a fair barter."
"I have some sea-mice--fine specimen--in spirit. and I will throw in
Robert Brown's new thing--'microscopic Observations on the Pollen of
plants'--if you donot happen to have it already."
"why, see how you long for the monster, I might ask a high price.
suppose I ask you to look through my drawer and agree with I about
all my new specie?" the Vicar, while he talk in this way,
alternately move about with his pipe in his mouth, and return to
hang rather fondly over his drawer. "that would be good discipline,
you know, for a young doctor who have to please his patient in
Middlemarch. you must learn to be bore, remember. however, you shall
have the monster on your own term."
"donot you think man overrate the necessity for humor everybody's
nonsense, till they get despise by the very fool they humor?" say
Lydgate, move to Mr. Farebrother's side, and look rather absently
at the insect range in fine gradation, with name subscribe in
exquisite writing. "the short way be to make your value feel, so
that people must put up with you whether you flatter they or not."
"with all my heart. but then you must be sure of have the value, and
you must keep yourself independent. very few man can do that. either
you slip out of service altogether, and become good for nothing, or you
wear the harness and draw a good deal where your yoke-fellow pull you.
but do look at these delicate orthoptera!"
Lydgate have after all to give some scrutiny to each drawer, the Vicar
laugh at himself, and yet persist in the exhibition.
"apropos of what you say about wear harness," Lydgate begin, after
they have sit down, "I make up my mind some time ago to do with as
little of it as possible. that be why I determine not to try anything
in London, for a good many year at least. I donot like what I see
when I be study there--so much empty bigwiggism, and obstructive
trickery. in the country, people have less pretension to knowledge,
and be less of companion, but for that reason they affect one's
amour-propre less: one make less bad blood, and can follow one's own
course more quietly."
"yes--well--you have get a good start; you be in the right profession,
the work you feel yourself most fit for. some people miss that, and
repent too late. but you must not be too sure of keep your
independence."
"you mean of family tie?" say Lydgate, conceive that these might
press rather tightly on Mr. Farebrother.
"not altogether. of course they make many thing more difficult. but
a good wife--a good unworldly woman--may really help a man, and keep
he more independent. therebe a parishioner of mine--a fine fellow,
but who would hardly have pull through as he have do without his
wife. do you know the Garths? I think they be not Peacock's
patient."
"no; but there be a Miss Garth at old Featherstone's, at Lowick."
"their daughter: an excellent girl."
"she be very quiet--I have hardly notice she."
"she have take notice of you, though, depend upon it."
"I donot understand," say lydgate; he could hardly say "of course."
"oh, she gauge everybody. I prepare she for confirmation--she be a
favorite of mine."
Mr. Farebrother puff a few moment in silence, Lydgate not care to
know more about the Garths. at last the Vicar lay down his pipe,
stretch out his leg, and turn his bright eye with a smile towards
Lydgate, saying--
"but we Middlemarchers be not so tame as you take we to be. we have
our intrigue and our party. I be a party man, for example, and
Bulstrode be another. if you vote for I you will offend Bulstrode."
"what be there against Bulstrode?" say Lydgate, emphatically.
"I do not say there be anything against he except that. if you vote
against he you will make he your enemy."
"I donot know that I need mind about that," say Lydgate, rather
proudly; "but he seem to have good idea about hospital, and he
spend large sum on useful public object. he might help I a good
deal in carry out my idea. as to his religious notion--why, as
Voltaire say, incantation will destroy a flock of sheep if
administer with a certain quantity of arsenic. I look for the man
who will bring the arsenic, and donot mind about his incantation."
"very good. but then you must not offend your arsenic-man. you will
not offend I, you know," say Mr. Farebrother, quite unaffectedly. "I
donot translate my own convenience into other people's duty. I be
oppose to Bulstrode in many way. I donot like the set he belong to:
they be a narrow ignorant set, and do more to make their neighbor
uncomfortable than to make they well. their system be a sort of
worldly-spiritual cliqueism: they really look on the rest of mankind as
a doomed carcass which be to nourish they for heaven. but," he add,
smilingly, "I donot say that Bulstrode's new hospital be a bad thing;
and as to his wanting to oust I from the old one--why, if he think I
a mischievous fellow, he be only return a compliment. and I be not
a model clergyman--only a decent makeshift."
Lydgate be not at all sure that the Vicar malign himself. a model
clergyman, like a model doctor, ought to think his own profession the
fine in the world, and take all knowledge as mere nourishment to his
moral pathology and therapeutic. he only say, "what reason do
Bulstrode give for supersede you?"
"that I donot teach his opinion--which he call spiritual religion;
and that I have no time to spare. both statement be true. but then
I could make time, and I should be glad of the forty pound. that be
the plain fact of the case. but let we dismiss it. I only want to
tell you that if you vote for your arsenic-man, you be not to cut I
in consequence. I cannot spare you. you be a sort of circumnavigator
come to settle among we, and will keep up my belief in the antipode.
now tell I all about they in Paris."
CHAPTER XVIII.
"oh, sir, the lofty hope on earth
draw lot with mean hope: heroic breast,
breathe bad air, run risk of pestilence;
or, lack lime-juice when they cross the line,
may languish with the scurvy."
some week pass after this conversation before the question of the
chaplaincy gather any practical import for Lydgate, and without
tell himself the reason, he defer the predetermination on which
side he should give his vote. it would really have be a matter of
total indifference to he--that be to say, he would have take the more
convenient side, and give his vote for the appointment of Tyke without
any hesitation--if he have not care personally for Mr. Farebrother.
but his liking for the Vicar of St. Botolph's grow with grow
acquaintanceship. that, enter into Lydgate's position as a
new-comer who have his own professional object to secure, Mr.
Farebrother should have take pain rather to warn off than to obtain
his interest, show an unusual delicacy and generosity, which
Lydgate's nature be keenly alive to. it go along with other point
of conduct in Mr. Farebrother which be exceptionally fine, and make
his character resemble those southern landscape which seem divide
between natural grandeur and social slovenliness. very few man could
have be as filial and chivalrous as he be to the mother, aunt, and
sister, whose dependence on he have in many way shape his life rather
uneasily for himself; few man who feel the pressure of small need be
so nobly resolute not to dress up their inevitably self-interest
desire in a pretext of well motif. in these matter he be
conscious that his life would bear the close scrutiny; and perhaps
the consciousness encourage a little defiance towards the critical
strictness of person whose celestial intimacy seem not to improve
their domestic manner, and whose lofty aim be not need to account
for their action. then, his preaching be ingenious and pithy, like
the preaching of the English Church in its robust age, and his sermon
be deliver without book. People outside his parish go to hear
he; and, since to fill the church be always the most difficult part
of a clergyman's function, here be another ground for a careless sense
of superiority. besides, he be a likable man: sweet-temper,
ready-witted, frank, without grin of suppress bitterness or other
conversational flavor which make half of we an affliction to our
friend. Lydgate like he heartily, and wish for his friendship.
with this feeling uppermost, he continue to waive the question of the
chaplaincy, and to persuade himself that it be not only no proper
business of his, but likely enough never to vex he with a demand for
his vote. lydgate, at Mr. Bulstrode's request, be lay down plan
for the internal arrangement of the new hospital, and the two be
often in consultation. the banker be always presuppose that he
could count in general on Lydgate as a coadjutor, but make no special
recurrence to the come decision between Tyke and Farebrother. when
the General Board of the Infirmary have meet, however, and Lydgate have
notice that the question of the chaplaincy be throw on a council of
the director and medical man, to meet on the following Friday, he have
a vexed sense that he must make up his mind on this trivial Middlemarch
business. he could not help hear within he the distinct
declaration that Bulstrode be prime minister, and that the Tyke affair
be a question of office or no office; and he could not help an equally
pronounce dislike to give up the prospect of office. for his
observation be constantly confirm Mr. Farebrother's assurance that
the banker would not overlook opposition. "confound their petty
politic!" be one of his thought for three morning in the meditative
process of shaving, when he have begin to feel that he must really hold
a court of conscience on this matter. certainly there be valid
thing to be say against the election of Mr. Farebrother: he have too
much on his hand already, especially consider how much time he
spend on non-clerical occupation. then again it be a continually
repeat shock, disturb Lydgate's esteem, that the Vicar should
obviously play for the sake of money, like the play indeed, but
evidently like some end which it serve. Mr. Farebrother contend
on theory for the desirability of all game, and say that Englishmen's
wit be stagnant for want of they; but Lydgate feel certain that he
would have play very much less but for the money. there be a
billiard-room at the Green Dragon, which some anxious mother and wife
regard as the chief temptation in Middlemarch. the Vicar be a
first-rate billiard-player, and though he do not frequent the Green
Dragon, there be report that he have sometimes be there in the
daytime and have win money. and as to the chaplaincy, he do not
pretend that he care for it, except for the sake of the forty pound.
Lydgate be no Puritan, but he do not care for play, and win money
at it have always seem a meanness to he; besides, he have an ideal of
life which make this subservience of conduct to the gaining of small
sum thoroughly hateful to he. Hitherto in his own life his want have
be supply without any trouble to himself, and his first impulse be
always to be liberal with half-crown as matter of no importance to a
gentleman; it have never occur to he to devise a plan for get
half-crown. he have always know in a general way that he be not
rich, but he have never feel poor, and he have no power of imagine the
part which the want of money play in determine the action of man.
Money have never be a motive to he. hence he be not ready to frame
excuse for this deliberate pursuit of small gain. it be altogether
repulsive to he, and he never enter into any calculation of the
ratio between the Vicar's income and his more or less necessary
expenditure. it be possible that he would not have make such a
calculation in his own case.
and now, when the question of voting have come, this repulsive fact tell
more strongly against Mr. Farebrother than it have do before. one
would know much well what to do if man's character be more
consistent, and especially if one's friend be invariably fit for any
function they desire to undertake! Lydgate be convince that if
there have be no valid objection to Mr. Farebrother, he would have
vote for he, whatever Bulstrode might have feel on the subject: he
do not intend to be a vassal of Bulstrode's. on the other hand, there
be Tyke, a man entirely give to his clerical office, who be simply
curate at a chapel of ease in St. Peter's parish, and have time for
extra duty. nobody have anything to say against Mr. Tyke, except that
they could not bear he, and suspect he of cannot. really, from his
point of view, Bulstrode be thoroughly justified.
but whichever way Lydgate begin to incline, there be something to make
he wince; and be a proud man, he be a little exasperate at be
oblige to wince. he do not like frustrate his own good purpose by
get on bad term with Bulstrode; he do not like vote against
Farebrother, and help to deprive he of function and salary; and the
question occur whether the additional forty pound might not leave
the Vicar free from that ignoble care about win at card.
moreover, Lydgate do not like the consciousness that in vote for
tyke he should be vote on the side obviously convenient for himself.
but would the end really be his own convenience? other people would
say so, and would allege that he be curry favor with Bulstrode for
the sake of make himself important and get on in the world. what
then? he for his own part know that if his personal prospect simply
have be concern, he would not have care a rotten nut for the
banker's friendship or enmity. what he really care for be a medium
for his work, a vehicle for his idea; and after all, be he not bind
to prefer the object of get a good hospital, where he could
demonstrate the specific distinction of fever and test therapeutic
result, before anything else connect with this chaplaincy? for the
first time Lydgate be feel the hampering threadlike pressure of
small social condition, and their frustrating complexity. at the end
of his inward debate, when he set out for the hospital, his hope be
really in the chance that discussion might somehow give a new aspect to
the question, and make the scale dip so as to exclude the necessity for
voting. I think he trust a little also to the energy which be
beget by circumstance--some feeling rush warmly and make
resolve easy, while debate in cool blood have only make it more
difficult. however it be, he do not distinctly say to himself on
which side he would vote; and all the while he be inwardly resent
the subjection which have be force upon he. it would have seem
beforehand like a ridiculous piece of bad logic that he, with his
unmixe resolution of independence and his select purpose, would find
himself at the very outset in the grasp of petty alternative, each of
which be repugnant to he. in his student's chamber, he have
prearrange his social action quite differently.
Lydgate be late in set out, but Dr. Sprague, the two other
surgeon, and several of the director have arrive early; Mr.
Bulstrode, treasurer and chairman, be among those who be still
absent. the conversation seem to imply that the issue be
problematical, and that a majority for Tyke be not so certain as have
be generally suppose. the two physician, for a wonder, turn out
to be unanimous, or rather, though of different mind, they concur
in action. Dr. Sprague, the rugged and weighty, be, as every one have
foresee, an adherent of Mr. Farebrother. the doctor be more than
suspect of have no religion, but somehow Middlemarch tolerate this
deficiency in he as if he have be a Lord Chancellor; indeed it be
probable that his professional weight be the more believe in, the
world-old association of cleverness with the evil principle be still
potent in the mind even of lady-patient who have the strict idea
of frilling and sentiment. it be perhaps this negation in the doctor
which make his neighbor call he hard-head and dry-witted;
condition of texture which be also hold favorable to the storing of
judgment connect with drug. at all event, it be certain that if
any medical man have come to Middlemarch with the reputation of have
very definite religious view, of be give to prayer, and of
otherwise show an active piety, there would have be a general
presumption against his medical skill.
on this ground it be (professionally speak) fortunate for Dr.
Minchin that his religious sympathy be of a general kind, and such
as give a distant medical sanction to all serious sentiment, whether of
Church or Dissent, rather than any adhesion to particular tenet. if
Mr. Bulstrode insist, as he be apt to do, on the Lutheran doctrine
of justification, as that by which a Church must stand or fall, Dr.
Minchin in return be quite sure that man be not a mere machine or a
fortuitous conjunction of atom; if Mrs. Wimple insist on a
particular providence in relation to her stomach complaint, Dr. Minchin
for his part like to keep the mental window open and object to
fix limit; if the unitarian brewer jest about the Athanasian
Creed, Dr. Minchin quote Pope's "Essay on Man." he object to the
rather free style of anecdote in which Dr. Sprague indulge, prefer
well-sanction quotation, and like refinement of all kind: it be
generally know that he have some kinship to a bishop, and sometimes
spend his holiday at "the palace."
Dr. Minchin be soft-handed, pale-complexion, and of rounded outline,
not to be distinguish from a mild clergyman in appearance: whereas
Dr. Sprague be superfluously tall; his trouser get crease at the
knee, and show an excess of boot at a time when strap seem
necessary to any dignity of bear; you hear he go in and out, and
up and down, as if he have come to see after the roofing. in short, he
have weight, and might be expect to grapple with a disease and throw
it; while Dr. Minchin might be well able to detect it lurk and to
circumvent it. they enjoy about equally the mysterious privilege of
medical reputation, and conceal with much etiquette their contempt
for each other's skill. regard themselves as Middlemarch
institution, they be ready to combine against all innovator, and
against non-professional give to interference. on this ground they
be both in their heart equally averse to Mr. Bulstrode, though Dr.
Minchin have never be in open hostility with he, and never differ
from he without elaborate explanation to Mrs. Bulstrode, who have find
that Dr. Minchin alone understand her constitution. a layman who pry
into the professional conduct of medical man, and be always obtrude
his reforms,--though he be less directly embarrassing to the two
physician than to the surgeon-apothecary who attend pauper by
contract, be nevertheless offensive to the professional nostril as
such; and Dr. Minchin share fully in the new pique against Bulstrode,
excite by his apparent determination to patronize Lydgate. the
long-establish practitioner, Mr. Wrench and Mr. Toller; be just
now stand apart and have a friendly colloquy, in which they agree
that Lydgate be a jackanape, just make to serve Bulstrode's purpose.
to non-medical friend they have already concur in praise the other
young practitioner, who have come into the town on Mr. Peacock's
retirement without further recommendation than his own merit and such
argument for solid professional acquirement as might be gather from
his have apparently waste no time on other branch of knowledge.
it be clear that lydgate, by not dispense drug, intend to cast
imputation on his equal, and also to obscure the limit between his
own rank as a general practitioner and that of the physician, who, in
the interest of the profession, feel bind to maintain its various
grades,--especially against a man who have not be to either of the
english university and enjoy the absence of anatomical and bedside
study there, but come with a libellous pretension to experience in
Edinburgh and Paris, where observation might be abundant indeed, but
hardly sound.
thus it happen that on this occasion Bulstrode become identify with
Lydgate, and Lydgate with Tyke; and owe to this variety of
interchangeable name for the chaplaincy question, diverse mind be
enable to form the same judgment concern it.
Dr. Sprague say at once bluntly to the group assemble when he
enter, "I go for Farebrother. a salary, with all my heart. but why
take it from the Vicar? he have none too much--have to insure his life,
besides keep house, and do a vicar's charity. put forty pound
in his pocket and youwill do no harm. hebe a good fellow, be
Farebrother, with as little of the parson about he as will serve to
carry order."
"Ho, ho! Doctor," say old Mr. Powderell, a retire iron-monger of
some standing--his interjection be something between a laugh and a
parliamentary disapproval; "we must let you have your say. but what we
have to consider be not anybody's income--itbe the soul of the poor
sick people"--here Mr. Powderell's voice and face have a sincere pathos
in they. "he be a real Gospel preacher, be Mr. Tyke. I should vote
against my conscience if I vote against Mr. Tyke--I should indeed."
"Mr. Tyke's opponent have not ask any one to vote against his
conscience, I believe," say Mr. Hackbutt, a rich tanner of fluent
speech, whose glitter spectacle and erect hair be turn with
some severity towards innocent Mr. Powderell. "but in my judgment it
behove we, as director, to consider whether we will regard it as our
whole business to carry out proposition emanate from a single
quarter. will any member of the committee aver that he would have
entertain the idea of displace the gentleman who have always
discharge the function of chaplain here, if it have not be suggest
to he by party whose disposition it be to regard every institution
of this town as a machinery for carry out their own view? I tax no
man's motif: let they lie between himself and a high power; but I
do say, that there be influence at work here which be incompatible
with genuine independence, and that a crawl servility be usually
dictate by circumstance which gentleman so conduct themselves
could not afford either morally or financially to avow. I myself be a
layman, but I have give no inconsiderable attention to the division
in the Church and--"
"oh, damn the division!" burst in Mr. Frank Hawley, lawyer and
town-clerk, who rarely present himself at the board, but now look
in hurriedly, whip in hand. "we have nothing to do with they here.
Farebrother have be do the work--what there be--without pay, and
if pay be to be give, it should be give to he. I call it a
confound job to take the thing away from Farebrother."
"I think it would be as well for gentleman not to give their remark a
personal bearing," say Mr. Plymdale. "I shall vote for the
appointment of Mr. Tyke, but I should not have know, if Mr. Hackbutt
havenot hint it, that I be a Servile Crawler."
"I disclaim any personality. I expressly say, if I may be allow
to repeat, or even to conclude what I be about to say--"
"ah, here's Minchin!" say Mr. Frank Hawley; at which everybody turn
away from Mr. Hackbutt, leave he to feel the uselessness of superior
gift in Middlemarch. "Come, Doctor, I must have you on the right
side, eh?"
"I hope so," say Dr. Minchin, nod and shake hand here and
there; "at whatever cost to my feeling."
"if therebe any feeling here, it should be feel for the man who be
turn out, I think," say Mr. Frank Hawley.
"I confess I have feeling on the other side also. I have a divided
esteem," say Dr. Minchin, rub his hand. "I consider Mr. Tyke an
exemplary man--none more so--and I believe he to be propose from
unimpeachable motif. I, for my part, wish that I could give he my
vote. but I be constrain to take a view of the case which give the
preponderance to Mr. Farebrother's claim. he be an amiable man, an
able preacher, and have be long among we."
Old Mr. Powderell look on, sad and silent. Mr. Plymdale settle his
cravat, uneasily.
"you donot set up Farebrother as a pattern of what a clergyman ought to
be, I hope," say Mr. Larcher, the eminent carrier, who have just come
in. "I have no ill-will towards he, but I think we owe something to
the public, not to speak of anything high, in these appointment. in
my opinion Farebrother be too lax for a clergyman. I donot wish to
bring up particular against he; but he will make a little attendance
here go as far as he can."
"and a devilish deal well than too much," say Mr. Hawley, whose bad
language be notorious in that part of the county. "sick people cannot
bear so much pray and preach. and that methodistical sort of
religion be bad for the spirit--bad for the inside, eh?" he add,
turn quickly round to the four medical man who be assemble.
but any answer be dispense with by the entrance of three gentleman,
with whom there be greeting more or less cordial. these be the
Reverend Edward Thesiger, Rector of St. Peter's, Mr. Bulstrode, and our
friend Mr. Brooke of Tipton, who have lately allow himself to be put
on the board of director in his turn, but have never before attend,
his attendance now be due to Mr. Bulstrode's exertion. Lydgate be
the only person still expect.
every one now sit down, Mr. Bulstrode presiding, pale and
self-restrain as usual. Mr. Thesiger, a moderate evangelical, wish
for the appointment of his friend Mr. Tyke, a zealous able man, who,
officiate at a chapel of ease, have not a cure of soul too extensive
to leave he ample time for the new duty. it be desirable that
chaplaincy of this kind should be enter on with a fervent
intention: they be peculiar opportunity for spiritual influence;
and while it be good that a salary should be allot, there be the
more need for scrupulous watching lest the office should be pervert
into a mere question of salary. Mr. Thesiger's manner have so much
quiet propriety that objector could only simmer in silence.
Mr. Brooke believe that everybody mean well in the matter. he have
not himself attend to the affair of the Infirmary, though he have a
strong interest in whatever be for the benefit of Middlemarch, and be
most happy to meet the gentleman present on any public question--"any
public question, you know," Mr. Brooke repeat, with his nod of
perfect understanding. "I be a good deal occupy as a magistrate, and
in the collection of documentary evidence, but I regard my time as
be at the disposal of the public--and, in short, my friend have
convince I that a chaplain with a salary--a salary, you know--be a
very good thing, and I be happy to be able to come here and vote for
the appointment of Mr. Tyke, who, I understand, be an unexceptionable
man, apostolic and eloquent and everything of that kind--and I be the
last man to withhold my vote--under the circumstance, you know."
"it seem to I that you have be cram with one side of the
question, Mr. Brooke," say Mr. Frank Hawley, who be afraid of nobody,
and be a Tory suspicious of electioneer intention. "you donot
seem to know that one of the worthy man we have have be do duty
as chaplain here for year without pay, and that Mr. Tyke be propose
to supersede he."
"excuse I, Mr. Hawley," say Mr. Bulstrode. "Mr. Brooke have be
fully informed of Mr. Farebrother's character and position."
"by his enemy," flash out Mr. Hawley.
"I trust there be no personal hostility concern here," say Mr.
Thesiger.
"Iwill swear there be, though," retort Mr. Hawley.
"gentleman," say Mr. Bulstrode, in a subdue tone, "the merit of the
question may be very briefly state, and if any one present doubt that
every gentleman who be about to give his vote have not be fully
inform, I can now recapitulate the consideration that should weigh
on either side."
"I donot see the good of that," say Mr. Hawley. "I suppose we all
know whom we mean to vote for. any man who want to do justice do
not wait till the last minute to hear both side of the question. I
have no time to lose, and I propose that the matter be put to the vote
at once."
a brief but still hot discussion follow before each person write
"tyke" or "Farebrother" on a piece of paper and slip it into a glass
tumbler; and in the mean time Mr. Bulstrode see Lydgate enter.
"I perceive that the vote be equally divide at present," say Mr.
Bulstrode, in a clear biting voice. then, look up at lydgate--
"there be a casting-vote still to be give. it be your, Mr. Lydgate:
will you be good enough to write?"
"the thing be settle now," say Mr. Wrench, rise. "we all know how
Mr. Lydgate will vote."
"you seem to speak with some peculiar meaning, sir," say Lydgate,
rather defiantly, and keep his pencil suspend.
"I merely mean that you be expect to vote with Mr. Bulstrode. do
you regard that meaning as offensive?"
"it may be offensive to other. but I shall not desist from voting
with he on that account." Lydgate immediately write down "Tyke."
so the Rev. Walter Tyke become chaplain to the Infirmary, and Lydgate
continue to work with Mr. Bulstrode. he be really uncertain whether
tyke be not the more suitable candidate, and yet his consciousness
tell he that if he have be quite free from indirect bias he should
have vote for Mr. Farebrother. the affair of the chaplaincy remain
a sore point in his memory as a case in which this petty medium of
Middlemarch have be too strong for he. how could a man be satisfy
with a decision between such alternative and under such circumstance?
no more than he can be satisfied with his hat, which he have choose from
among such shape as the resource of the age offer he, wear it at
well with a resignation which be chiefly support by comparison.
but Mr. Farebrother meet he with the same friendliness as before. the
character of the publican and sinner be not always practically
incompatible with that of the modern Pharisee, for the majority of we
scarcely see more distinctly the faultiness of our own conduct than the
faultiness of our own argument, or the dulness of our own joke. but
the Vicar of St. Botolph's have certainly escape the slight tincture
of the Pharisee, and by dint of admit to himself that he be too
much as other man be, he have become remarkably unlike they in
this--that he could excuse other for think slightly of he, and
could judge impartially of their conduct even when it tell against he.
"the world have be to strong for _I_, I know," he say one day to
lydgate. "but then I be not a mighty man--I shall never be a man of
renown. the choice of Hercules be a pretty fable; but Prodicus make
it easy work for the hero, as if the first resolve be enough.
another story say that he come to hold the distaff, and at last wear
the Nessus shirt. I suppose one good resolve might keep a man right if
everybody else's resolve help he."
the Vicar's talk be not always inspirit: he have escape be a
Pharisee, but he have not escape that low estimate of possibility
which we rather hastily arrive at as an inference from our own failure.
Lydgate think that there be a pitiable infirmity of will in Mr.
Farebrother.
CHAPTER XIX.
"L' altra vedete ch'ha fatto alla guancia
Della sua palma, sospirando, letto."
--Purgatorio, vii.
when George the Fourth be still reign over the privacy of
Windsor, when the Duke of Wellington be Prime Minister, and Mr. Vincy
be mayor of the old corporation in Middlemarch, Mrs. Casaubon, bear
Dorothea Brooke, have take her wedding journey to Rome. in those day
the world in general be more ignorant of good and evil by forty year
than it be at present. traveller do not often carry full information
on christian art either in their head or their pocket; and even the
most brilliant english critic of the day mistook the flower-flush
tomb of the ascended Virgin for an ornamental vase due to the painter's
fancy. romanticism, which have help to fill some dull blank with
love and knowledge, have not yet penetrate the time with its leaven
and enter into everybody's food; it be ferment still as a
distinguishable vigorous enthusiasm in certain long-haired german
artist at Rome, and the youth of other nation who work or idle
near they be sometimes catch in the spread movement.
one fine morning a young man whose hair be not immoderately long, but
abundant and curly, and who be otherwise english in his equipment, have
just turn his back on the Belvedere Torso in the Vatican and be
look out on the magnificent view of the mountain from the adjoining
round vestibule. he be sufficiently absorb not to notice the
approach of a dark-eye, animate German who come up to he and place
a hand on his shoulder, say with a strong accent, "come here, quick!
else she will have change her pose."
Quickness be ready at the call, and the two figure pass lightly
along by the Meleager, towards the hall where the recline Ariadne,
then call the Cleopatra, lie in the marble voluptuousness of her
beauty, the drapery fold around she with a petal-like ease and
tenderness. they be just in time to see another figure stand
against a pedestal near the reclining marble: a breathing bloom
girl, whose form, not sham by the Ariadne, be clothe in Quakerish gray
drapery; her long cloak, fasten at the neck, be throw backward from
her arm, and one beautiful ungloved hand pillow her cheek, push
somewhat backward the white beaver bonnet which make a sort of halo to
her face around the simply braid dark-brown hair. she be not
look at the sculpture, probably not think of it: her large eye
be fix dreamily on a streak of sunlight which fall across the
floor. but she become conscious of the two stranger who suddenly
pause as if to contemplate the Cleopatra, and, without look at
they, immediately turn away to join a maid-servant and courier who
be loiter along the hall at a little distance off.
"what do you think of that for a fine bit of antithesis?" say the
German, search in his friend's face for respond admiration, but
go on volubly without wait for any other answer. "there lie
antique beauty, not corpse-like even in death, but arrest in the
complete contentment of its sensuous perfection: and here stand beauty
in its breathing life, with the consciousness of christian century in
its bosom. but she should be dress as a nun; I think she look
almost what you call a Quaker; I would dress she as a nun in my
picture. however, she be marry; I see her wedding-ring on that
wonderful left hand, otherwise I should have think the sallow
Geistlicher be her father. I see he part from she a good while
ago, and just now I find she in that magnificent pose. only think! he
be perhaps rich, and would like to have her portrait take. ah! it be
no use look after she--there she go! let we follow her home!"
"no, no," say his companion, with a little frown.
"you be singular, Ladislaw. you look strike together. do you know
she?"
"I know that she be married to my cousin," say will Ladislaw,
saunter down the hall with a preoccupied air, while his german
friend keep at his side and watch he eagerly.
"what! the Geistlicher? he look more like an uncle--a more useful sort
of relation."
"he be not my uncle. I tell you he be my second cousin," say
Ladislaw, with some irritation.
"Schon, schon. donot be snappish. you be not angry with I for
think Mrs. Second-Cousin the most perfect young Madonna I ever see?"
"angry? nonsense. I have only see she once before, for a couple of
minute, when my cousin introduce she to I, just before I leave
England. they be not married then. I donot know they be come
to Rome."
"but you will go to see they now--you will find out what they have for
an address--since you know the name. shall we go to the post? and you
could speak about the portrait."
"confound you, Naumann! I donot know what I shall do. I be not so
brazen as you."
"Bah! that be because you be dilettantish and amateurish. if you be
an artist, you would think of Mistress second-Cousin as antique form
animate by christian sentiment--a sort of Christian Antigone--sensuous
force control by spiritual passion."
"yes, and that your paint she be the chief outcome of her
existence--the divinity pass into high completeness and all but
exhaust in the act of cover your bit of canvas. I be amateurish
if you like: I do _not_ think that all the universe be strain
towards the obscure significance of your picture."
"but it be, my dear!--so far as it be strain through I, Adolf
Naumann: that stand firm," say the good-natured painter, put a
hand on Ladislaw's shoulder, and not in the least disturb by the
unaccountable touch of ill-humor in his tone. "see now! my existence
presuppose the existence of the whole universe--do it _not?_ and my
function be to paint--and as a painter I have a conception which be
altogether genialisch, of your great-aunt or second grandmother as a
subject for a picture; therefore, the universe be strain towards
that picture through that particular hook or claw which it put forth
in the shape of I--not true?"
"but how if another claw in the shape of I be strain to thwart
it?--the case be a little less simple then."
"not at all: the result of the struggle be the same thing--picture or
no picture--logically."
will could not resist this imperturbable temper, and the cloud in his
face break into sunshiny laughter.
"come now, my friend--you will help?" say Naumann, in a hopeful tone.
"no; nonsense, Naumann! english lady be not at everybody's service
as model. and you want to express too much with your painting. you
would only have make a well or bad portrait with a background which
every connoisseur would give a different reason for or against. and
what be a portrait of a woman? your painting and Plastik be poor
stuff after all. they perturb and dull conception instead of raise
they. language be a fine medium."
"yes, for those who cannot paint," say Naumann. "there you have
perfect right. I do not recommend you to paint, my friend."

"of course there be always a great deal of poor work: the rare thing
want that soil to grow in."

Fred Vincy, we have see, have a debt on his mind, and though no such
immaterial burthen could depress that buoyant-hearted young gentleman
for many hour together, there be circumstance connect with this
debt which make the thought of it unusually importunate. the creditor
be Mr. Bambridge a horse-dealer of the neighborhood, whose company be
much seek in Middlemarch by young man understand to be "addict to
pleasure." during the vacation Fred have naturally require more
amusement than he have ready money for, and Mr. Bambridge have be
accommodate enough not only to trust he for the hire of horse and
the accidental expense of ruin a fine hunter, but also to make a
small advance by which he might be able to meet some loss at
billiard. the total debt be a hundred and sixty pound. Bambridge
be in no alarm about his money, be sure that young Vincy have
backer; but he have require something to show for it, and Fred have at
first give a bill with his own signature. three month later he have
renew this bill with the signature of Caleb Garth. on both occasion
Fred have feel confident that he should meet the bill himself, have
ample fund at disposal in his own hopefulness. you will hardly demand
that his confidence should have a basis in external fact; such
confidence, we know, be something less coarse and materialistic: it be
a comfortable disposition lead we to expect that the wisdom of
providence or the folly of our friend, the mystery of luck or the
still great mystery of our high individual value in the universe,
will bring about agreeable issue, such as be consistent with our good
taste in costume, and our general preference for the good style of
thing. Fred feel sure that he should have a present from his uncle,
that he should have a run of luck, that by dint of "swap" he should
gradually metamorphose a horse worth forty pound into a horse that
would fetch a hundred at any moment--"judgment" be always equivalent
to an unspecified sum in hard cash. and in any case, even suppose
negation which only a morbid distrust could imagine, Fred have always
(at that time) his father's pocket as a last resource, so that his
asset of hopefulness have a sort of gorgeous superfluity about they.
of what might be the capacity of his father's pocket, Fred have only a
vague notion: be not trade elastic? and would not the deficiency of
one year be make up for by the surplus of another? the Vincys live in
an easy profuse way, not with any new ostentation, but accord to the
family habit and tradition, so that the child have no standard of
economy, and the elder one retain some of their infantine notion
that their father might pay for anything if he would. Mr. Vincy
himself have expensive middlemarch habit--spend money on course, on
his cellar, and on dinner-giving, while mamma have those run
account with tradespeople, which give a cheerful sense of get
everything one want without any question of payment. but it be in
the nature of father, Fred know, to bully one about expense: there
be always a little storm over his extravagance if he have to disclose a

certainty that it be transient; but in the mean time it be
disagreeable to see his mother cry, and also to be oblige to look
sulky instead of have fun; for Fred be so good-temper that if he
look glum under scold, it be chiefly for propriety's sake. the
easy course plainly, be to renew the bill with a friend's signature.
why not? with the superfluous security of hope at his command, there
be no reason why he should not have increase other people's

the universal order of thing would necessarily be agreeable to an
agreeable young gentleman.
with a favor to ask we review our list of friend, do justice to their

when Fred state the circumstance of his debt, his wish to meet it

Fred be not a gambler: he have not that specific disease in which the

Most of those who see Fred riding out of Middlemarch in company with

dirt-enamel map of the county, a bad portrait of an anonymous horse
in a stable, his Majesty George the Fourth with leg and cravat, and
various leaden spittoon, might have seem a hard business, but for
the sustain power of nomenclature which determine that the pursuit
of these thing be "gay."
in Mr. Horrock there be certainly an apparent unfathomableness which
offer play to the imagination. Costume, at a glance, give he a
thrilling association with horse (enough to specify the hat-brim which
take the slight upward angle just to escape the suspicion of bend
downward), and nature have give he a face which by dint of mongolian
eye, and a nose, mouth, and chin seem to follow his hat-brim in a
moderate inclination upwards, give the effect of a subdue unchangeable
sceptical smile, of all expression the most tyrannous over a
susceptible mind, and, when accompany by adequate silence, likely to
create the reputation of an invincible understanding, an infinite fund
of humor--too dry to flow, and probably in a state of immovable
crust,--and a critical judgment which, if you could ever be fortunate
enough to know it, would be _the_ thing and no other. it be a
physiognomy see in all vocation, but perhaps it have never be more
powerful over the youth of England than in a judge of horse.
Mr. Horrock, at a question from Fred about his horse's fetlock, turn
sideway in his saddle, and watch the horse's action for the space of
three minute, then turn forward, twitch his own bridle, and
remain silent with a profile neither more nor less sceptical than it
have be.
the part thus play in dialogue by Mr. Horrock be terribly effective.
a mixture of passion be excited in Fred--a mad desire to thrash
Horrock's opinion into utterance, restrain by anxiety to retain the
advantage of his friendship. there be always the chance that horrock
might say something quite invaluable at the right moment.
Mr. Bambridge have more open manner, and appear to give forth his
idea without economy. he be loud, robust, and be sometimes speak
of as be "give to indulgence"--chiefly in swearing, drinking, and
beat his wife. some people who have lose by he call he a vicious
man; but he regard horse-dealing as the fine of the art, and might
have argue plausibly that it have nothing to do with morality. he be
undeniably a prosperous man, bear his drinking well than other bear
their moderation, and, on the whole, flourish like the green
bay-tree. but his range of conversation be limited, and like the fine
old tune, "drop of brandy," give you after a while a sense of
return upon itself in a way that might make weak head dizzy. but a
slight infusion of Mr. Bambridge be feel to give tone and character to
several circle in Middlemarch; and he be a distinguished figure in
the bar and billiard-room at the Green Dragon. he know some anecdote
about the hero of the turf, and various clever trick of Marquesses
and viscount which seem to prove that blood assert its
pre-eminence even among black-leg; but the minute retentiveness of his
memory be chiefly show about the horse he have himself buy and
sell; the number of mile they would trot you in no time without
turn a hair be, after the lapse of year, still a subject of
passionate asseveration, in which he would assist the imagination of
his hearer by solemnly swear that they never see anything like it.
in short, Mr. Bambridge be a man of pleasure and a gay companion.
Fred be subtle, and do not tell his friend that he be go to
Houndsley bent on sell his horse: he wish to get indirectly at
their genuine opinion of its value, not be aware that a genuine
opinion be the last thing likely to be extract from such eminent
critic. it be not Mr. Bambridge's weakness to be a gratuitous
flatterer. he have never before be so much strike with the fact that
this unfortunate bay be a roarer to a degree which require the
roundest word for perdition to give you any idea of it.
"you make a bad hand at swap when you go to anybody but I,
Vincy! why, you never throw your leg across a fine horse than that
chestnut, and you give he for this brute. if you set he canter,
he go on like twenty sawyer. I never hear but one bad roarer in
my life, and that be a roan: it belong to Pegwell, the corn-factor;
he use to drive he in his gig seven year ago, and he want I to
take he, but I say, 'thank you, Peg, I donot deal in
wind-instrument.' that be what I say. it go the round of the
country, that joke do. but, what the hell! the horse be a penny
trumpet to that roarer of your."
"why, you say just now his be bad than mine," say Fred, more
irritable than usual.
"I say a lie, then," say Mr. Bambridge, emphatically. "there benot
a penny to choose between them."
Fred spur his horse, and they trot on a little way. when they
slacken again, Mr. Bambridge said--
"not but what the roan be a well trotter than your."
"Ibe quite satisfied with his pace, I know," say Fred, who require
all the consciousness of be in gay company to support he; "I say
his trot be an uncommonly clean one, eh, Horrock?"
Mr. Horrock look before he with as complete a neutrality as if he
have be a portrait by a great master.
Fred give up the fallacious hope of get a genuine opinion; but on
reflection he see that Bambridge's depreciation and Horrock's silence
be both virtually encouraging, and indicate that they think well
of the horse than they choose to say.
that very evening, indeed, before the fair have set in, Fred think he
see a favorable opening for dispose advantageously of his horse, but
an opening which make he congratulate himself on his foresight in
bring with he his eighty pound. a young farmer, acquaint with
Mr. Bambridge, come into the Red Lion, and enter into conversation
about part with a hunter, which he introduce at once as Diamond,
imply that it be a public character. for himself he only want a
useful hack, which would draw upon occasion; be about to marry and
to give up hunting. the hunter be in a friend's stable at some little
distance; there be still time for gentleman to see it before dark.
the friend's stable have to be reach through a back street where you
might as easily have be poison without expense of drug as in any
grim street of that unsanitary period. Fred be not fortify against
disgust by brandy, as his companion be, but the hope of have at
last see the horse that would enable he to make money be
exhilarate enough to lead he over the same ground again the first
thing in the morning. he feel sure that if he do not come to a
bargain with the farmer, Bambridge would; for the stress of
circumstance, Fred feel, be sharpen his acuteness and endow he
with all the constructive power of suspicion. Bambridge have run down
Diamond in a way that he never would have do (the horse be a
friend's) if he have not think of buy it; every one who look at
the animal--even Horrock--be evidently impressed with its merit. to
get all the advantage of be with man of this sort, you must know how
to draw your inference, and not be a spoon who take thing literally.
the color of the horse be a dapple gray, and Fred happen to know
that Lord Medlicote's man be on the look-out for just such a horse.
after all his run down, Bambridge let it out in the course of the
evening, when the farmer be absent, that he have see bad horse go
for eighty pound. of course he contradict himself twenty time
over, but when you know what be likely to be true you can test a man's
admission. and Fred could not but reckon his own judgment of a horse
as worth something. the farmer have pause over Fred's respectable
though break-wind steed long enough to show that he think it worth
consideration, and it seem probable that he would take it, with
five-and-twenty pound in addition, as the equivalent of Diamond. in
that case Fred, when he have part with his new horse for at least
eighty pound, would be fifty-five pound in pocket by the transaction,
and would have a hundred and thirty-five pound towards meet the
bill; so that the deficit temporarily throw on Mr. Garth would at the
utmost be twenty-five pound. by the time he be hurry on his

this rare chance, that if Bambridge and Horrock have both dissuade he,
he would not have be delude into a direct interpretation of their
purpose: he would have be aware that those deep hand hold something
else than a young fellow's interest. with regard to horse, distrust
be your only clew. but scepticism, as we know, can never be
thoroughly apply, else life would come to a standstill: something we
must believe in and do, and whatever that something may be call, it
be virtually our own judgment, even when it seem like the most slavish
reliance on another. Fred believe in the excellence of his bargain,
and even before the fair have well set in, have get possession of the
dapple gray, at the price of his old horse and thirty pound in
addition--only five pound more than he have expect to give.
but he feel a little worried and weary, perhaps with mental debate,
and without wait for the further gayetie of the horse-fair, he set
out alone on his fourteen mile' journey, mean to take it very
quietly and keep his horse fresh.
CHAPTER XXIV.
"the offender's sorrow bring but small relief
to he who wear the strong offence's cross."
--SHAKESPEARE: sonnet.
I be sorry to say that only the third day after the propitious event
at Houndsley Fred Vincy have fall into bad spirit than he have know
in his life before. not that he have be disappoint as to the
possible market for his horse, but that before the bargain could be

amount of eighty pound have be invest, have without the slight
warning exhibit in the stable a most vicious energy in kicking, have
just miss kill the groom, and have end in lam himself severely
by catch his leg in a rope that overhang the stable-board. there be
no more redress for this than for the discovery of bad temper after
marriage--which of course old companion be aware of before the
ceremony. for some reason or other, Fred have none of his usual
elasticity under this stroke of ill-fortune: he be simply aware that
he have only fifty pound, that there be no chance of his get any
more at present, and that the bill for a hundred and sixty would be
present in five day. even if he have apply to his father on the
plea that Mr. Garth should be save from loss, Fred feel smartingly
that his father would angrily refuse to rescue Mr. Garth from the
consequence of what he would call encourage extravagance and deceit.
he be so utterly downcast that he could frame no other project than to

accident: when he do, he would storm about the vicious brute be
bring into his stable; and before meet that less annoyance Fred
want to get away with all his courage to face the great. he take

Garth, he would ride to Stone Court and confess all to Mary. in fact,

his conscience would have be much less active both in previously
urge the debt on his thought and impel he not to spare himself
after his usual fashion by defer an unpleasant task, but to act as
directly and simply as he could. even much strong mortal than Fred
Vincy hold half their rectitude in the mind of the being they love
well. "the theatre of all my action be fall," say an antique
personage when his chief friend be dead; and they be fortunate who
get a theatre where the audience demand their good. certainly it

Garth have have no decide notion as to what be admirable in character.
Mr. Garth be not at the office, and Fred ride on to his house, which
be a little way outside the town--a homely place with an orchard in

he be rather more in awe than of her husband. not that she be
inclined to sarcasm and to impulsive sally, as Mary be. in her
present matronly age at least, Mrs. Garth never commit herself by
over-hasty speech; have, as she say, bear the yoke in her youth,
and learn self-control. she have that rare sense which discern what
be unalterable, and submit to it without murmur. adore her
husband's virtue, she have very early make up her mind to his
incapacity of mind his own interest, and have meet the consequence

teapot or child's frilling, and have never pour any pathetic
confidence into the ear of her feminine neighbor concern Mr.
Garth's want of prudence and the sum he might have have if he have be
like other man. hence these fair neighbor think she either proud or
eccentric, and sometimes speak of she to their husband as "your fine
Mrs. Garth." she be not without her criticism of they in return, be
more accurately instruct than most matron in Middlemarch, and--where
be the blameless woman?--apt to be a little severe towards her own sex,
which in her opinion be frame to be entirely subordinate. on the
other hand, she be disproportionately indulgent towards the failing
of man, and be often hear to say that these be natural. also, it
must be admit that Mrs. Garth be a trifle too emphatic in her
resistance to what she hold to be folly: the passage from governess
into housewife have work itself a little too strongly into her
consciousness, and she rarely forget that while her grammar and accent
be above the town standard, she wear a plain cap, cook the family
dinner, and darn all the stocking. she have sometimes take pupil
in a peripatetic fashion, make they follow she about in the kitchen
with their book or slate. she think it good for they to see that she
could make an excellent lather while she correct their blunder
"without looking,"--that a woman with her sleeve tuck up above her
elbow might know all about the Subjunctive Mood or the Torrid
Zone--that, in short, she might possess "education" and other good
thing end in "tion," and worthy to be pronounce emphatically,
without be a useless doll. when she make remark to this edifying
effect, she have a firm little frown on her brow, which yet do not
hinder her face from look benevolent, and her word which come forth
like a procession be utter in a fervid agreeable contralto.
certainly, the exemplary Mrs. Garth have her droll aspect, but her
character sustain her oddity, as a very fine wine sustain a flavor
of skin.
towards Fred Vincy she have a motherly feeling, and have always be
dispose to excuse his error, though she would probably not have

hard to Fred that he must now inevitably sink in her opinion. and
the circumstance of his visit turn out to be still more unpleasant

and girl, who be stand opposite to she at the table with their

end of the kitchen indicate an intermittent wash of small thing also
go on.
Mrs. Garth, with her sleeve turn above her elbow, deftly handle
her pastry--apply her roll-pin and give ornamental pinch,

about the concord of verb and pronoun with "noun of multitude or
signify many," be a sight agreeably amusing. she be of the same
curly-haire, square-face type as Mary, but handsomer, with more
delicacy of feature, a pale skin, a solid matronly figure, and a
remarkable firmness of glance. in her snowy-frille cap she remind

would become like she, which be a prospective advantage equal to a
dowry--the mother too often stand behind the daughter like a
malignant prophecy--"Such as I be, she will shortly be."
"now let we go through that once more," say Mrs. Garth, pinch an

heavy brow, from due attention to the lesson. "'not without regard to
the import of the word as convey unity or plurality of idea'--tell
I again what that mean, Ben."
(Mrs. Garth, like more celebrated educator, have her favorite ancient
path, and in a general wreck of society would have try to hold she
"Lindley Murray" above the wave.)
"oh--it mean--you must think what you mean," say Ben, rather

"to teach you to speak and write correctly, so that you can be
understand," say Mrs. Garth, with severe precision. "should you like
to speak as old Job do?"
"yes," say Ben, stoutly; "itbe funny. he say, 'Yo goo'--thatbe
just as good as 'you go.'"
"but he say, 'a ship's in the garden,' instead of 'a sheep,'" say
Letty, with an air of superiority. "you might think he mean a ship
off the sea."
"no, you mightnot, if you benot silly," say Ben. "how could a ship
off the sea come there?"
"these thing belong only to pronunciation, which be the least part of

pig, Ben; if you eat it, I must give they your piece of pasty. Job
have only to speak about very plain thing. how do you think you would
write or speak about anything more difficult, if you know no more of
grammar than he do? you would use wrong word, and put word in the
wrong place, and instead of make people understand you, they would
turn away from you as a tiresome person. what would you do then?"
"I shouldnot care, I should leave off," say Ben, with a sense that
this be an agreeable issue where grammar be concern.
"I see you be get tired and stupid, Ben," say Mrs. Garth,
accustom to these obstructive argument from her male offspring.
have finish her pie, she move towards the clothe-horse, and

Cincinnatus."
"I know! he be a farmer," say Ben.
"now, Ben, he be a Roman--let _I_ tell," say Letty, use her elbow
contentiously.
"you silly thing, he be a roman farmer, and he be plough."
"yes, but before that--that donot come first--people want he," say
Letty.
"well, but you must say what sort of a man he be first," insist Ben.
"he be a wise man, like my father, and that make the people want his
advice. and he be a brave man, and could fight. and so could my

that of the Romans inclusive, life be already a painful affair.) "now,
Ben."

"very well, dictator!" say Ben, contemptuously. "but that benot a

the knock be Fred's; and when Letty say that her father be not in

this do not lead well towards the news that Mr. Garth be on the brink

Caleb have push his chair back, and be lean forward, shake his
head slowly, and fit his finger-tip together with much nicety.
"Poor Mary!" he say. "Susan," he go on in a lowered tone, "Ibe
afraid she may be fond of Fred."
"oh no! she always laugh at he; and he be not likely to think of she
in any other than a brotherly way."
Caleb make no rejoinder, but presently lower his spectacle, draw up
his chair to the desk, and say, "deuce take the bill--I wish it be
at Hanover! these thing be a sad interruption to business!"
the first part of this speech comprise his whole store of maledictory
expression, and be utter with a slight snarl easy to imagine. but
it would be difficult to convey to those who never hear he utter the
word "business," the peculiar tone of fervid veneration, of religious
regard, in which he wrap it, as a consecrated symbol be wrap in
its gold-fringe linen.
Caleb Garth often shake his head in meditation on the value, the
indispensable might of that myriad-head, myriad-hand labor by which
the social body be feed, clothe, and house. it have lay hold of his
imagination in boyhood. the echo of the great hammer where roof or
keel be a-making, the signal-shout of the workman, the roar of the
furnace, the thunder and plash of the engine, be a sublime music to
he; the felling and lading of timber, and the huge trunk vibrate
star-like in the distance along the highway, the crane at work on the
wharf, the pile-up produce in warehouse, the precision and variety of
muscular effort wherever exact work have to be turn out,--all these
sight of his youth have act on he as poetry without the aid of the
poet, have make a philosophy for he without the aid of philosopher,
a religion without the aid of theology. his early ambition have be to
have as effective a share as possible in this sublime labor, which be
peculiarly dignified by he with the name of "business;" and though he
have only be a short time under a surveyor, and have be chiefly his
own teacher, he know more of land, building, and mining than most of
the special man in the county.
his classification of human employment be rather crude, and, like the
category of more celebrated man, would not be acceptable in these
advanced time. he divide they into "business, politic, preach,
learning, and amusement." he have nothing to say against the last four;
but he regard they as a reverential pagan regard other god than
his own. in the same way, he think very well of all rank, but he
would not himself have like to be of any rank in which he have not such
close contact with "business" as to get often honorably decorate with
mark of dust and mortar, the damp of the engine, or the sweet soil of
the wood and field. though he have never regard himself as other
than an orthodox Christian, and would argue on prevenient grace if the
subject be propose to he, I think his virtual divinity be good
practical scheme, accurate work, and the faithful completion of
undertaking: his prince of darkness be a slack workman. but there
be no spirit of denial in Caleb, and the world seem so wondrous to
he that he be ready to accept any number of system, like any number
of firmament, if they do not obviously interfere with the good
land-drainage, solid building, correct measuring, and judicious boring
(for coal). in fact, he have a reverential soul with a strong practical
intelligence. but he could not manage finance: he know value well,
but he have no keenness of imagination for monetary result in the shape
of profit and loss: and have ascertain this to his cost, he
determine to give up all form of his beloved "business" which
require that talent. he give himself up entirely to the many kind of
work which he could do without handle capital, and be one of those
precious man within his own district whom everybody would choose to
work for they, because he do his work well, charge very little, and
often decline to charge at all. it be no wonder, then, that the
Garths be poor, and "live in a small way." however, they do not
mind it.
CHAPTER XXV.
"love seeketh not itself to please,
nor for itself hath any care
but for another give its ease
and build a heaven in hell's despair.
. . . . . . .
love seeketh only self to please,
to bind another to its delight,
joy in another's loss of ease,
and build a hell in heaven's despite."
--W. blake: song of experience
Fred Vincy want to arrive at Stone Court when Mary could not expect
he, and when his uncle be not down-stair in that case she might be
sit alone in the wainscote parlor. he leave his horse in the yard
to avoid make a noise on the gravel in front, and enter the parlor
without other notice than the noise of the door-handle. Mary be in her
usual corner, laugh over Mrs. Piozzi's recollection of Johnson, and
look up with the fun still in her face. it gradually fade as she
see Fred approach she without speak, and stand before she with his
elbow on the mantel-piece, look ill. she too be silent, only
raise her eye to he inquiringly.
"Mary," he begin, "I be a good-for-nothing blackguard."
"I should think one of those epithet would do at a time," say Mary,
try to smile, but feel alarmed.
"I know you will never think well of I any more. you will think I a
liar. you will think I dishonest. you will think I donot care for
you, or your father and mother. you always do make the bad of I, I
know."
"I cannot deny that I shall think all that of you, Fred, if you give I
good reason. but please to tell I at once what you have do. I
would rather know the painful truth than imagine it."
"I owe money--a hundred and sixty pound. I ask your father to put
his name to a bill. I think it would not signify to he. I make
sure of pay the money myself, and I have try as hard as I could.
and now, I have be so unlucky--a horse have turn out badly--I can
only pay fifty pound. and I cannot ask my father for the money: he
would not give I a farthing. and my uncle give I a hundred a little
while ago. so what can I do? and now your father have no ready money
to spare, and your mother will have to pay away her ninety-two pound
that she have save, and she say your saving must go too. you see
what a--"
"oh, poor mother, poor father!" say Mary, her eye fill with tear,
and a little sob rise which she try to repress. she look
