home become present to she. he too remain silent for some moment,
feel more miserable than ever. "I wouldnot have hurt you for the
world, Mary," he say at last. "you can never forgive I."
"what do it matter whether I forgive you?" say Mary, passionately.
"would that make it any well for my mother to lose the money she have
be earn by lesson for four year, that she might send Alfred to
Mr. Hanmer's? should you think all that pleasant enough if I forgave
you?"
"say what you like, Mary. I deserve it all."
"I donot want to say anything," say Mary, more quietly, "and my anger
be of no use." she dry her eye, throw aside her book, rise and
fetch her sewing.
Fred follow she with his eye, hop that they would meet her, and
in that way find access for his imploring penitence. but no! Mary
could easily avoid look upward.
"I do care about your mother's money go," he say, when she be
seat again and sew quickly. "I want to ask you, Mary--don't
you think that Mr. Featherstone--if you be to tell he--tell he, I
mean, about apprentice Alfred--would advance the money?"
"my family be not fond of beg, Fred. we would rather work for our
money. besides, you say that Mr. Featherstone have lately give you a
hundred pound. he rarely make present; he have never make present
to we. I be sure my father will not ask he for anything; and even if
I choose to beg of he, it would be of no use."
"I be so miserable, Mary--if you know how miserable I be, you would be
sorry for I."
"there be other thing to be more sorry for than that. but selfish
people always think their own discomfort of more importance than
anything else in the world. I see enough of that every day."
"it be hardly fair to call I selfish. if you know what thing other
young man do, you would think I a good way off the bad."
"I know that people who spend a great deal of money on themselves
without know how they shall pay, must be selfish. they be always
think of what they can get for themselves, and not of what other
people may lose."
"any man may be unfortunate, Mary, and find himself unable to pay when
he mean it. there be not a well man in the world than your father,
and yet he get into trouble."
"how dare you make any comparison between my father and you, Fred?"
say Mary, in a deep tone of indignation. "he never get into trouble
by think of his own idle pleasure, but because he be always
think of the work he be do for other people. and he have fare
hard, and work hard to make good everybody's loss."
"and you think that I shall never try to make good anything, Mary. it
be not generous to believe the bad of a man. when you have get any
power over he, I think you might try and use it to make he well;
but that be what you never do. however, Ibe go," Fred end,
languidly. "I shall never speak to you about anything again. Ibe very
sorry for all the trouble Ihave cause--thatbe all."
Mary have drop her work out of her hand and look up. there be
often something maternal even in a girlish love, and Mary's hard
experience have work her nature to an impressibility very different
from that hard slight thing which we call girlishness. at Fred's last
word she feel an instantaneous pang, something like what a mother
feel at the imagine sob or cry of her naughty truant child, which
may lose itself and get harm. and when, look up, her eye meet his
dull despairing glance, her pity for he surmount her anger and all

"oh, Fred, how ill you look! sit down a moment. donot go yet. let I

see you for a whole week." Mary speak hurriedly, say the word

the bad of I--will not give I up altogether."
"as if it be any pleasure to I to think ill of you," say Mary, in a
mournful tone. "as if it be not very painful to I to see you an
idle frivolous creature. how can you bear to be so contemptible, when
other be work and strive, and there be so many thing to be
do--how can you bear to be fit for nothing in the world that be
useful? and with so much good in your disposition, fred,--you might
be worth a great deal."
"I will try to be anything you like, Mary, if you will say that you
love I."
"I should be ashamed to say that I love a man who must always be
hang on other, and reckon on what they would do for he. what
will you be when you be forty? like Mr. Bowyer, I suppose--just as
idle, live in Mrs. Beck's front parlor--fat and shabby, hop
somebody will invite you to dinner--spend your morning in learn a
comic song--oh no! learn a tune on the flute."
Mary's lip have begin to curl with a smile as soon as she have ask
that question about Fred's future (young soul be mobile), and before
she end, her face have its full illumination of fun. to he it be
like the cessation of an ache that Mary could laugh at he, and with a
passive sort of smile he try to reach her hand; but she slip away

he for a moment or two."
Fred secretly feel that his future be guarantee against the

when Caleb Garth arrive at Stone Court soon after dusk, Mary be not

after discuss price during tea with Mr. Featherstone Caleb rise to

she take a candle into another large parlor, where there be no fire,

"ay? how can that be? you see, Ihave be a bit of a fool again, and

"oh yes; I have more than four-and-twenty pound. I think you would

Mary take out the fold money from her reticule and put it into her

but Fred do not go to Stone Court the next day, for reason that be

an eminent philosopher among my friend, who can dignify even your ugly

Mr. and Mrs. Casaubon, return from their wedding journey, arrive at

Key to all Mythologies. for my part I be very sorry for he. it be an
uneasy lot at good, to be what we call highly teach and yet not to
enjoy: to be present at this great spectacle of life and never to be
liberate from a small hungry shivering self--never to be fully
possess by the glory we behold, never to have our consciousness

passion, the energy of an action, but always to be scholarly and
uninspired, ambitious and timid, scrupulous and dim-sighted. become a
dean or even a bishop would make little difference, I fear, to Mr.
Casaubon's uneasiness. doubtless some ancient Greek have observe that
behind the big mask and the speak-trumpet, there must always be our
poor little eye peep as usual and our timorous lip more or less
under anxious control.
to this mental estate map out a quarter of a century before, to

CHAPTER XXXI.
how will you know the pitch of that great bell
too large for you to stir? let but a flute
play 'neath the fine-mixed metal listen close
till the right note flow forth, a silvery rill.
then shall the huge bell tremble--then the mass
with myriad wave concurrent shall respond
in low soft unison.
lydgate that evening speak to Miss Vincy of Mrs. Casaubon, and lay

deferentially to nonsense."

but this agreeable holiday freedom with which Lydgate hover about the

CHAPTER XXXII.
"theywill take suggestion as a cat lap milk."
--shakespeare: Tempest.
the triumphant confidence of the Mayor found on Mr. Featherstone's

but Brother Jonah, Sister Martha, and all the needy exile, hold a

but some of the visitor alight and do not depart after the handsome

to speak, or let I speak."

"but you cannot take your own time to die in, Brother," begin Mrs.

be naturally affect.

people push themselves before we."
Fred's spirit could not bear this: rise and look at Mr.
Featherstone, he say, "shall my mother and I leave the room, sir, that
you may be alone with your friend?"
"sit down, I tell you," say old Featherstone, snappishly. "stop where
you be. good-by, Solomon," he add, try to wield his stick again,

donot you come again."

allow."

their exit be hasten by their see old Mr. Featherstone pull his

"close up his eye and draw the curtain close;

that night after twelve o'clock Mary Garth relieve the watch in Mr.
Featherstone's room, and sit there alone through the small hour. she
often choose this task, in which she find some pleasure,
notwithstanding the old man's testiness whenever he demand her
attention. there be interval in which she could sit perfectly
still, enjoy the outer stillness and the subdue light. the red
fire with its gently audible movement seem like a solemn existence
calmly independent of the petty passion, the imbecile desire, the
straining after worthless uncertainty, which be daily move her
contempt. Mary be fond of her own thought, and could amuse herself
well sit in twilight with her hand in her lap; for, have early
have strong reason to believe that thing be not likely to be arrange
for her peculiar satisfaction, she waste no time in astonishment and
annoyance at that fact. and she have already come to take life very
much as a comedy in which she have a proud, nay, a generous resolution
not to act the mean or treacherous part. Mary might have become
cynical if she have not have parent whom she honor, and a well of
affectionate gratitude within she, which be all the fuller because she
have learn to make no unreasonable claim.
she sit to-night revolving, as she be willnot, the scene of the day, her
lip often curl with amusement at the oddity to which her fancy
add fresh drollery: people be so ridiculous with their illusion,
carry their fool's cap unawares, think their own lie opaque
while everybody else's be transparent, make themselves exception
to everything, as if when all the world look yellow under a lamp they
alone be rosy. yet there be some illusion under Mary's eye which
be not quite comic to she. she be secretly convince, though she
have no other ground than her close observation of old Featherstone's

they be as likely to be disappoint as any of the relation whom he
keep at a distance. she have a good deal of disdain for Mrs. Vincy's
evident alarm lest she and Fred should be alone together, but it do

be affect, if it should turn out that his uncle have leave he as poor
as ever. she could make a butt of Fred when he be present, but she
do not enjoy his folly when he be absent.
yet she like her thought: a vigorous young mind not overbalance by
passion, find a good in make acquaintance with life, and watch its
own power with interest. Mary have plenty of merriment within.
her thought be not vein by any solemnity or pathos about the old man

aged creature whose life be not visibly anything but a remnant of
vice. she have always see the most disagreeable side of Mr.
Featherstone: he be not proud of she, and she be only useful to he.
to be anxious about a soul that be always snap at you must be leave
to the saint of the earth; and Mary be not one of they. she have

be her utmost. Old Featherstone himself be not in the least anxious
about his soul, and have decline to see Mr. Tucker on the subject.
to-night he have not snap, and for the first hour or two he lay

key against the tin box which he always keep in the bed beside he.
about three o'clock he say, with remarkable distinctness, "Missy, come

Mary obey, and find that he have already draw the tin box from under
the clothe, though he usually ask to have this do for he; and he
have select the key. he now unlock the box, and, draw from it

recover all their sharpness and say, "how many of them be in the
house?"
"you mean of your own relation, sir," say Mary, well use to the old
man's way of speech. he nod slightly and she go on.
"Mr. Jonah Featherstone and young Cranch be sleep here."
"oh ay, they stick, do they? and the rest--they come every day, Iwill
warrant--Solomon and Jane, and all the young un? they come peep,
and count and cast up?"
"not all of they every day. Mr. Solomon and Mrs. Waule be here every
day, and the other come often."
the old man listen with a grimace while she speak, and then say,
relax his face, "the more fool they. you hearken, missy. itbe
three o'clock in the morning, and Ihave get all my faculty as well as
ever I have in my life. I know all my property, and where the money's
put out, and everything. and Ihave make everything ready to change my
mind, and do as I like at the last. do you hear, missy? Ihave get my
faculty."
"well, sir?" say Mary, quietly.
he now lower his tone with an air of deep cunning. "Ihave make two
will, and Ibe go to burn one. now you do as I tell you. this be
the key of my iron chest, in the closet there. you push well at the
side of the brass plate at the top, till it go like a bolt: then you
can put the key in the front lock and turn it. see and do that; and
take out the topmost paper--last Will and Testament--big print."
"no, sir," say Mary, in a firm voice, "I cannot do that."
"not do it? I tell you, you must," say the old man, his voice
begin to shake under the shock of this resistance.
"I cannot touch your iron chest or your will. I must refuse to do
anything that might lay I open to suspicion."
"I tell you, Ibe in my right mind. shallnot I do as I like at the last?
I make two will on purpose. take the key, I say."
"no, sir, I will not," say Mary, more resolutely still. her repulsion
be get strong.
"I tell you, therebe no time to lose."
"I cannot help that, sir. I will not let the close of your life soil
the beginning of mine. I will not touch your iron chest or your will."
she move to a little distance from the bedside.
the old man pause with a blank stare for a little while, hold the
one key erect on the ring; then with an agitated jerk he begin to work
with his bony leave hand at empty the tin box before he.
"Missy," he begin to say, hurriedly, "look here! take the money--the
note and gold--look here--take it--you shall have it all--do as I
tell you."
he make an effort to stretch out the key towards she as far as
possible, and Mary again retreat.
"I will not touch your key or your money, sir. pray donot ask I to do
it again. if you do, I must go and call your brother."
he let his hand fall, and for the first time in her life Mary see old
Peter Featherstone begin to cry childishly. she say, in as gentle a
tone as she could command, "pray put up your money, sir;" and then go
away to her seat by the fire, hop this would help to convince he
that it be useless to say more. presently he rally and say
eagerly--
"look here, then. call the young chap. call Fred Vincy."
Mary's heart begin to beat more quickly. various idea rush through
her mind as to what the burning of a second will might imply. she have
to make a difficult decision in a hurry.
"I will call he, if you will let I call Mr. Jonah and other with
he."
"nobody else, I say. the young chap. I shall do as I like."
"wait till broad daylight, sir, when every one be stir. or let I

than two hour."
"Lawyer? what do I want with the lawyer? nobody shall know--I say,
nobody shall know. I shall do as I like."
"let I call some one else, sir," say Mary, persuasively. she do not
like her position--alone with the old man, who seem to show a strange
flaring of nervous energy which enable he to speak again and again
without fall into his usual cough; yet she desire not to push
unnecessarily the contradiction which agitate he. "let I, pray,
call some one else."
"you let I alone, I say. look here, missy. take the money. youwill
never have the chance again. itbe pretty nigh two hundred--therebe
more in the box, and nobody know how much there be. take it and do
as I tell you."
Mary, stand by the fire, see its red light fall on the old man,
prop up on his pillow and bed-rest, with his bony hand hold out

way in which he have put the offer of the money urge she to speak with
hard resolution than ever.
"it be of no use, sir. I will not do it. put up your money. I will
not touch your money. I will do anything else I can to comfort you;
but I will not touch your key or your money."
"anything else anything else!" say old Featherstone, with hoarse rage,

audible. "I want nothing else. you come here--you come here."
Mary approach he cautiously, know he too well. she see he

like an aged hyena, the muscle of his face get distort with the
effort of his hand. she pause at a safe distance.
"let I give you some cordial," she say, quietly, "and try to compose
yourself. you will perhaps go to sleep. and to-morrow by daylight you
can do as you like."
he lift the stick, in spite of her be beyond his reach, and throw
it with a hard effort which be but impotence. it fall, slip over
the foot of the bed. Mary let it lie, and retreat to her chair by
the fire. by-and-by she would go to he with the cordial. fatigue
would make he passive. it be get towards the chill moment of

between the moreen window-curtain the light whiten by the blind.
have put some wood on the fire and throw a shawl over she, she sit

near he the irritation might be keep up. he have say nothing after
throw the stick, but she have see he take his key again and

she think that he be drop off to sleep.
but Mary herself begin to be more agitated by the remembrance of what
she have go through, than she have be by the reality--question
those act of hers which have come imperatively and exclude all
question in the critical moment.
presently the dry wood send out a flame which illuminate every
crevice, and Mary see that the old man be lie quietly with his head

step, and think that his face look strangely motionless; but the

the next moment she run to the bell and ring it energetically. in a
very little while there be no long any doubt that Peter Featherstone
be dead, with his right hand clasp the key, and his left hand

1st gent. such man as this be feather, chip, and straw.

2d Gent. but levity
be causal too, and make the sum of weight.

it be on a morning of May that Peter Featherstone be bury. in the
prosaic neighborhood of Middlemarch, May be not always warm and sunny,
and on this particular morning a chill wind be blow the blossom
from the surround garden on to the green mound of Lowick
churchyard. swiftly move cloud only now and then allow a gleam to
light up any object, whether ugly or beautiful, that happen to stand
within its golden shower. in the churchyard the object be
remarkably various, for there be a little country crowd wait to see
the funeral. the news have spread that it be to be a "big burying;"
the old gentleman have leave write direction about everything and
mean to have a funeral "beyond his better." this be true; for old
Featherstone have not be a Harpagon whose passion have all be
devour by the ever-lean and ever-hungry passion of saving, and who
would drive a bargain with his undertaker beforehand. he love money,
but he also love to spend it in gratify his peculiar taste, and
perhaps he love it well of all as a means of make other feel his
power more or less uncomfortably. if any one will here contend that
there must have be trait of goodness in old Featherstone, I will not
presume to deny this; but I must observe that goodness be of a modest
nature, easily discourage, and when much privacy, elbow in early
life by unabashed vice, be apt to retire into extreme privacy, so that
it be more easily believe in by those who construct a selfish old
gentleman theoretically, than by those who form the narrow judgment
base on his personal acquaintance. in any case, he have be bent on
have a handsome funeral, and on have person "bid" to it who would
rather have stay at home. he have even desire that female relative
should follow he to the grave, and poor sister Martha have take a
difficult journey for this purpose from the Chalky Flats. she and Jane
would have be altogether cheer (in a tearful manner) by this sign
that a brother who dislike see they while he be live have be
prospectively fond of their presence when he should have become a
testator, if the sign have not be make equivocal by be extend to
Mrs. Vincy, whose expense in handsome crape seem to imply the most
presumptuous hope, aggravate by a bloom of complexion which tell
pretty plainly that she be not a blood-relation, but of that generally
objectionable class call wife's kin.
we be all of we imaginative in some form or other, for image be the
brood of desire; and poor old Featherstone, who laugh much at the way
in which other cajole themselves, do not escape the fellowship of
illusion. in write the programme for his burial he certainly do not
make clear to himself that his pleasure in the little drama of which it
form a part be confine to anticipation. in chuckle over the
vexation he could inflict by the rigid clutch of his dead hand, he
inevitably mingle his consciousness with that livid stagnant presence,
and so far as he be preoccupy with a future life, it be with one of
gratification inside his coffin. thus old Featherstone be
imaginative, after his fashion.
however, the three mourning-coach be fill accord to the
write order of the deceased. there be pall-bearer on horseback,
with the rich scarf and hatband, and even the under-bearer have
trapping of woe which be of a good well-price quality. the black
procession, when dismount, look the large for the smallness of the
churchyard; the heavy human face and the black drapery shiver in
the wind seem to tell of a world strangely incongruous with the
lightly drop blossom and the gleam of sunshine on the daisy.
the clergyman who meet the procession be Mr. Cadwallader--also
accord to the request of Peter Featherstone, prompt as usual by
peculiar reason. have a contempt for curate, whom he always call
understrapper, he be resolve to be bury by a benefice clergyman.
Mr. Casaubon be out of the question, not merely because he decline
duty of this sort, but because Featherstone have an especial dislike to
he as the rector of his own parish, who have a lien on the land in the
shape of tithe, also as the deliverer of morning sermon, which the old
man, be in his pew and not at all sleepy, have be oblige to sit
through with an inward snarl. he have an objection to a parson stick up
above his head preach to he. but his relation with Mr.
Cadwallader have be of a different kind: the trout-stream which run
through Mr. Casaubon's land take its course through Featherstone's
also, so that Mr. Cadwallader be a parson who have have to ask a favor
instead of preach. moreover, he be one of the high gentry live
four mile away from Lowick, and be thus exalt to an equal sky with

"Non, je ne comprend pas de plus charmant plaisir
Que de voir d'heritiers une troupe affligee
Le maintien interdit, et la mine allongee,
Lire un long testament ou pale, etonne
on leur laisse un bonsoir avec un pied de nez.
pour voir au naturel leur tristesse profonde
Je reviendrais, je crois, expre de l'autre monde."

not help be sly. "Peter be a bad liver, and almshouse willnot cover

"and all the while have get his own lawful family--brother and sister
and nephew and niece--and have sit in church with them whenever he
think well to come," say Mrs. Waule. "and might have leave his
property so respectable, to they thatbe never be use to extravagance
or unsteadiness in no manner of way--and not so poor but what they
could have save every penny and make more of it. and I--the trouble
Ihave be at, time and time, to come here and be sisterly--and he
with thing on his mind all the while that might make anybody's flesh
creep. but if the Almighty's allow it, he mean to punish he for
it. Brother Solomon, I shall be go, if youwill drive I."
"Ihave no desire to put my foot on the premise again," say Solomon.
"Ihave get land of my own and property of my own to will away."
"itbe a poor tale how luck go in the world," say Jonah. "it never
answer to have a bit of spirit in you. youwould well be a dog in the
manger. but those above ground might learn a lesson. one fool's will
be enough in a family."
"therebe more way than one of be a fool," say Solomon. "I shallnot
leave my money to be pour down the sink, and I shallnot leave it to
foundling from Africay. I like Feather, stone that be brew such,
and not turn featherstone with stick the name on them."
Solomon address these remark in a loud aside to Mrs. Waule as he
rise to accompany she. brother Jonah feel himself capable of much more
sting wit than this, but he reflect that there be no use in
offend the new proprietor of Stone Court, until you be certain
that he be quite without intention of hospitality towards witty man
whose name he be about to bear.
Mr. Joshua Rigg, in fact, appear to trouble himself little about any
innuendo, but show a notable change of manner, walk coolly up to
Mr. Standish and put business question with much coolness. he have
a high chirping voice and a vile accent. Fred, whom he no long move
to laughter, think he the low monster he have ever see. but Fred
be feel rather sick. the Middlemarch mercer wait for an
opportunity of engage Mr. Rigg in conversation: there be no knowing
how many pair of leg the new proprietor might require hose for, and
profit be more to be rely on than legacy. also, the mercer, as
a second cousin, be dispassionate enough to feel curiosity.
Mr. Vincy, after his one outburst, have remain proudly silent, though
too much preoccupy with unpleasant feeling to think of move, till
he observe that his wife have go to Fred's side and be cry
silently while she hold her darling's hand. he rise immediately, and
turn his back on the company while he say to she in an
undertone,--"don't give way, Lucy; donot make a fool of yourself, my
dear, before these people," he add in his usual loud voice--"go and
order the phaeton, Fred; I have no time to waste."
Mary Garth have before this be get ready to go home with her
father. she meet Fred in the hall, and now for the first time have the
courage to look at he. he have that wither sort of paleness which
will sometimes come on young face, and his hand be very cold when she
shake it. Mary too be agitate; she be conscious that fatally,
without will of her own, she have perhaps make a great difference to
Fred's lot.
"good-by," she say, with affectionate sadness. "be brave, Fred. I do
believe you be well without the money. what be the good of it to
Mr. Featherstone?"
"thatbe all very fine," say Fred, pettishly. "what be a fellow to do?
I must go into the Church now." (he know that this would vex Mary:
very well; then she must tell he what else he could do.) "and I
think I should be able to pay your father at once and make everything
right. and you have not even a hundred pound leave you. what shall
you do now, Mary?"
"take another situation, of course, as soon as I can get one. my
father have enough to do to keep the rest, without I. good-by."
in a very short time Stone Court be clear of well-brew
Featherstones and other long-accustomed visitor. another stranger have
be bring to settle in the neighborhood of Middlemarch, but in the
case of Mr. Rigg Featherstone there be more discontent with immediate
visible consequence than speculation as to the effect which his
presence might have in the future. no soul be prophetic enough to
have any foreboding as to what might appear on the trial of Joshua Rigg.
and here I be naturally lead to reflect on the mean of elevate a low
subject. historical parallel be remarkably efficient in this way.
the chief objection to they be, that the diligent narrator may lack
space, or (what be often the same thing) may not be able to think of
they with any degree of particularity, though he may have a
philosophical confidence that if know they would be illustrative. it
seem an easy and short way to dignity, to observe that--since
there never be a true story which could not be tell in parable, where
you might put a monkey for a margrave, and vice versa--whatever have
be or be to be narrate by I about low people, may be ennoble by
be consider a parable; so that if any bad habit and ugly
consequence be bring into view, the reader may have the relief of
regard they as not more than figuratively ungenteel, and may feel
himself virtually in company with person of some style. thus while I
tell the truth about loobie, my reader's imagination need not be
entirely exclude from an occupation with lord; and the petty sum
which any bankrupt of high standing would be sorry to retire upon, may
be lift to the level of high commercial transaction by the
inexpensive addition of proportional cipher.
as to any provincial history in which the agent be all of high moral
rank, that must be of a date long posterior to the first Reform Bill,
and Peter Featherstone, you perceive, be dead and bury some month
before Lord Grey come into office.
CHAPTER XXXVI.
"'tis strange to see the humor of these man,
these great aspire spirit, that should be wise:
. . . . . . . .
for be the nature of great spirit to love
to be where they may be most eminent;
they, rating of themselves so farre above
Us in conceit, with whom they do frequent,
imagine how we wonder and esteeme
all that they do or say; which make they strive
to make our admiration more extreme,
which they suppose they cannot, 'less they give
notice of their extreme and high thought.
--DANIEL: Tragedy of Philotas.
Mr. Vincy go home from the reading of the will with his point of view
considerably change in relation to many subject. he be an
open-minded man, but give to indirect mode of express himself:
when he be disappoint in a market for his silk braid, he swear at
the groom; when his brother-in-law Bulstrode have vex he, he make
cut remark on methodism; and it be now apparent that he regard
Fred's idleness with a sudden increase of severity, by his throw an
embroider cap out of the smoking-room on to the hall-floor.
"well, sir," he observe, when that young gentleman be move off to
bed, "I hope youhave make up your mind now to go up next term and pass
your examination. Ihave take my resolution, so I advise you to lose no
time in take your."
Fred make no answer: he be too utterly depressed. twenty-four hour
ago he have think that instead of need to know what he should do,
he should by this time know that he need to do nothing: that he
should hunt in pink, have a first-rate hunter, ride to cover on a fine
hack, and be generally respect for do so; moreover, that he should
be able at once to pay Mr. Garth, and that Mary could no long have
any reason for not marry he. and all this be to have come without
study or other inconvenience, purely by the favor of providence in the
shape of an old gentleman's caprice. but now, at the end of the
twenty-four hour, all those firm expectation be upset. it be
"rather hard line" that while he be smart under this
disappointment he should be treat as if he could have help it. but
he go away silently and his mother plead for he.
"donot be hard on the poor boy, Vincy. hewill turn out well yet, though
that wicked man have deceive he. I feel as sure as I sit here, Fred
will turn out well--else why be he bring back from the brink of the
grave? and I call it a robbery: it be like give he the land, to
promise it; and what be promise, if make everybody believe be not
promise? and you see he do leave he ten thousand pound, and then
take it away again."
"took it away again!" say Mr. Vincy, pettishly. "I tell you the lad's
an unlucky lad, Lucy. and youhave always spoil he."
"well, Vincy, he be my first, and you make a fine fuss with he when
he come. you be as proud as proud," say Mrs. Vincy, easily
recover her cheerful smile.
"who know what baby will turn to? I be fool enough, I dare say,"
say the husband--more mildly, however.
"but who have handsomer, well child than our? Fred be far beyond
other people's son: you may hear it in his speech, that he have keep
college company. and Rosamond--where be there a girl like she? she
might stand beside any lady in the land, and only look the well for
it. you see--Mr. Lydgate have keep the high company and be
everywhere, and he fall in love with she at once. not but what I could
have wish Rosamond have not engage herself. she might have meet
somebody on a visit who would have be a far well match; I mean at
her schoolfellow Miss Willoughby's. there be relation in that family
quite as high as Mr. Lydgate's."
"Damn relation!" say Mr. Vincy; "Ihave have enough of they. I donot
want a son-in-law who have get nothing but his relation to recommend
he."
"why, my dear," say Mrs. Vincy, "you seem as pleased as could be
about it. itbe true, I benot at home; but Rosamond tell I you havenot
a word to say against the engagement. and she have begin to buy in the
good linen and cambric for her underclothing."
"not by my will," say Mr. Vincy. "I shall have enough to do this
year, with an idle scamp of a son, without pay for wedding-clothe.
the time be as tight as can be; everybody be be ruin; and I
donot believe Lydgate have get a farthing. I shallnot give my consent to
their marrying. let them wait, as their elder have do before them."
"rosamond will take it hard, Vincy, and you know you never could bear
to cross she."
"yes, I could. the soon the engagement's off, the well. I donot
believe hewill ever make an income, the way he go on. he make
enemy; thatbe all I hear of his making."
"but he stand very high with Mr. Bulstrode, my dear. the marriage
would please _he_, I should think."
"please the deuce!" say Mr. Vincy. "Bulstrode willnot pay for their
keep. and if Lydgate think Ibe go to give money for they to set up
housekeeping, hebe mistaken, thatbe all. I expect I shall have to put
down my horse soon. youwould well tell Rosy what I say."
this be a not infrequent procedure with Mr. Vincy--to be rash in
jovial assent, and on become subsequently conscious that he have be
rash, to employ other in make the offensive retractation. however,
Mrs. Vincy, who never willingly oppose her husband, lose no time the
next morning in let Rosamond know what he have say. Rosamond,
examine some muslin-work, listen in silence, and at the end give a
certain turn of her graceful neck, of which only long experience could
teach you that it mean perfect obstinacy.
"what do you say, my dear?" say her mother, with affectionate
deference.
"Papa do not mean anything of the kind," say Rosamond, quite calmly.
"he have always say that he wish I to marry the man I love. and I
shall marry Mr. Lydgate. it be seven week now since papa give his
consent. and I hope we shall have Mrs. Bretton's house."
"well, my dear, I shall leave you to manage your papa. you always do
manage everybody. but if we ever do go and get damask, Sadler's be the
place--far well than Hopkins's. Mrs. Bretton's be very large, though:
I should love you to have such a house; but it will take a great deal
of furniture--carpeting and everything, besides plate and glass. and
you hear, your papa say he will give no money. do you think Mr.
Lydgate expect it?"
"you cannot imagine that I should ask he, mamma. of course he
understand his own affair."
"but he may have be look for money, my dear, and we all think of
your have a pretty legacy as well as fred;--and now everything be so
dreadful--therebe no pleasure in thinking of anything, with that poor
boy disappoint as he be."
"that have nothing to do with my marriage, mamma. Fred must leave off
be idle. I be go up-stair to take this work to Miss Morgan: she
do the open hemming very well. Mary Garth might do some work for I
now, I should think. her sewing be exquisite; it be the nice thing I
know about Mary. I should so like to have all my cambric frilling
double-hem. and it take a long time."
Mrs. Vincy's belief that Rosamond could manage her papa be well
found. apart from his dinner and his coursing, Mr. Vincy,
bluster as he be, have as little of his own way as if he have be a
prime minister: the force of circumstance be easily too much for he,
as it be for most pleasure-love florid man; and the circumstance
call Rosamond be particularly forcible by mean of that mild
persistence which, as we know, enable a white soft live substance to
make its way in spite of oppose rock. Papa be not a rock: he have no
other fixity than that fixity of alternate impulse sometimes call
habit, and this be altogether unfavorable to his take the only
decisive line of conduct in relation to his daughter's
engagement--namely, to inquire thoroughly into Lydgate's circumstance,
declare his own inability to furnish money, and forbid alike either a
speedy marriage or an engagement which must be too lengthy. that seem
very simple and easy in the statement; but a disagreeable resolve
form in the chill hour of the morning have as many condition against
it as the early frost, and rarely persist under the warming
influence of the day. the indirect though emphatic expression of
opinion to which Mr. Vincy be prone suffer much restraint in this
case: Lydgate be a proud man towards whom innuendo be obviously
unsafe, and throw his hat on the floor be out of the question. Mr.
Vincy be a little in awe of he, a little vain that he want to marry
Rosamond, a little indisposed to raise a question of money in which his
own position be not advantageous, a little afraid of be worst in
dialogue with a man well educate and more highly breed than himself,
and a little afraid of do what his daughter would not like. the
part Mr. Vincy preferred playing be that of the generous host whom
nobody criticise. in the early half of the day there be business
to hinder any formal communication of an adverse resolve; in the later
there be dinner, wine, whist, and general satisfaction. and in the
mean while the hour be each leave their little deposit and
gradually form the final reason for inaction, namely, that action
be too late. the accepted lover spend most of his evening in Lowick
Gate, and a love-making not at all dependent on money-advance from
father-in-law, or prospective income from a profession, go on
flourishingly under Mr. Vincy's own eye. young love-making--that
gossamer web! even the point it cling to--the thing whence its
subtle interlacing be swing--be scarcely perceptible: momentary
touch of fingertip, meeting of ray from blue and dark orb,
unfinished phrase, light change of cheek and lip, faint
tremor. the web itself be make of spontaneous belief and indefinable
joy, yearning of one life towards another, vision of completeness,
indefinite trust. and Lydgate fall to spin that web from his
inward self with wonderful rapidity, in spite of experience suppose to
be finish off with the drama of Laure--in spite too of medicine and
biology; for the inspection of macerate muscle or of eye present in
a dish (like Santa Lucia's), and other incident of scientific inquiry,
be observe to be less incompatible with poetic love than a native
dulness or a lively addiction to the low prose. as for Rosamond,
she be in the water-lily's expand wonderment at its own full
life, and she too be spin industriously at the mutual web. all
this go on in the corner of the drawing-room where the piano stand,
and subtle as it be, the light make it a sort of rainbow visible to
many observer besides Mr. Farebrother. the certainty that Miss Vincy
and Mr. Lydgate be engage become general in Middlemarch without the
aid of formal announcement.
Aunt Bulstrode be again stir to anxiety; but this time she
address herself to her brother, go to the warehouse expressly to
avoid Mrs. Vincy's volatility. his reply be not satisfactory.
"Walter, you never mean to tell I that you have allow all this to go
on without inquiry into Mr. Lydgate's prospect?" say Mrs. Bulstrode,
open her eye with wide gravity at her brother, who be in his
peevish warehouse humor. "think of this girl bring up in luxury--in
too worldly a way, I be sorry to say--what will she do on a small
income?"
"oh, confound it, Harriet! what can I do when man come into the town
without any asking of mine? do you shut your house up against
Lydgate? Bulstrode have push he forward more than anybody. I never
make any fuss about the young fellow. you should go and talk to your
husband about it, not I."
"well, really, Walter, how can Mr. Bulstrode be to blame? I be sure he
do not wish for the engagement."
"oh, if Bulstrode have not take he by the hand, I should never have
invite he."
"but you call he in to attend on Fred, and I be sure that be a
mercy," say Mrs. Bulstrode, lose her clew in the intricacy of the
subject.
"I donot know about mercy," say Mr. Vincy, testily. "I know I be
worry more than I like with my family. I be a good brother to you,
Harriet, before you marry Bulstrode, and I must say he donot always
show that friendly spirit towards your family that might have be
expect of he." Mr. Vincy be very little like a jesuit, but no
accomplish Jesuit could have turn a question more adroitly.
Harriet have to defend her husband instead of blame her brother, and
the conversation end at a point as far from the beginning as some
recent sparring between the brother-in-law at a vestry meeting.
Mrs. Bulstrode do not repeat her brother's complaint to her husband,
but in the evening she speak to he of Lydgate and Rosamond. he do
not share her warm interest, however; and only speak with resignation
of the risk attendant on the beginning of medical practice and the
desirability of prudence.
"I be sure we be bind to pray for that thoughtless girl--bring up
as she have be," say Mrs. Bulstrode, wish to rouse her husband's
feeling.
"truly, my dear," say Mr. Bulstrode, assentingly. "those who be not
of this world can do little else to arrest the error of the
obstinately worldly. that be what we must accustom ourselves to
recognize with regard to your brother's family. I could have wish
that Mr. Lydgate have not enter into such a union; but my relation
with he be limit to that use of his gift for God's purpose which
be teach we by the divine government under each dispensation."
Mrs. Bulstrode say no more, attribute some dissatisfaction which she
feel to her own want of spirituality. she believe that her husband
be one of those man whose memoir should be write when they die.
as to lydgate himself, have be accept, he be prepared to accept
all the consequence which he believe himself to foresee with perfect
clearness. of course he must be marry in a year--perhaps even in
half a year. this be not what he have intend; but other scheme
would not be hinder: they would simply adjust themselves anew.
marriage, of course, must be prepare for in the usual way. a house
must be take instead of the room he at present occupy; and Lydgate,
have hear Rosamond speak with admiration of old Mrs. Bretton's house
(situate in Lowick Gate), take notice when it fall vacant after the
old lady's death, and immediately enter into treaty for it.
he do this in an episodic way, very much as he give order to his
tailor for every requisite of perfect dress, without any notion of
be extravagant. on the contrary, he would have despise any
ostentation of expense; his profession have familiarize he with all
grade of poverty, and he care much for those who suffer hardship.
he would have behave perfectly at a table where the sauce be serve
in a jug with the handle off, and he would have remember nothing
about a grand dinner except that a man be there who talk well. but
it have never occur to he that he should live in any other than what
he would have call an ordinary way, with green glass for hock, and
excellent wait at table. in warm himself at french social
theory he have bring away no smell of scorching. we may handle even
extreme opinion with impunity while our furniture, our dinner-giving,
and preference for armorial bearing in our own case, link we
indissolubly with the establish order. and Lydgate's tendency be
not towards extreme opinion: he would have like no barefooted
doctrine, be particular about his boot: he be no radical in
relation to anything but medical reform and the prosecution of
discovery. in the rest of practical life he walk by hereditary
habit; half from that personal pride and unreflecte egoism which I
have already call commonness, and half from that naivete which
belong to preoccupation with favorite idea.
any inward debate Lydgate have as to the consequence of this engagement
which have steal upon he, turn on the paucity of time rather than of
money. certainly, be in love and be expect continually by some
one who always turn out to be pretty than memory could represent
she to be, do interfere with the diligent use of spare hour which
might serve some "plod fellow of a german" to make the great,
imminent discovery. this be really an argument for not defer the
marriage too long, as he imply to Mr. Farebrother, one day that the
Vicar come to his room with some pond-product which he want to
examine under a well microscope than his own, and, find Lydgate's

"Eros have degenerate; he begin by introduce order and harmony, and
now he bring back chaos."
"yes, at some stage," say Lydgate, lift his brow and smile,
while he begin to arrange his microscope. "but a well order will
begin after."
"soon?" say the Vicar.
"I hope so, really. this unsettled state of affair use up the time,
and when one have notion in science, every moment be an opportunity. I
feel sure that marriage must be the good thing for a man who want to
work steadily. he have everything at home then--no teasing with
personal speculation--he can get calmness and freedom."
"you be an enviable dog," say the Vicar, "to have such a
prospect--rosamond, calmness and freedom, all to your share. here be
I with nothing but my pipe and pond-animalcule. now, be you ready?"
Lydgate do not mention to the Vicar another reason he have for wish
to shorten the period of courtship. it be rather irritate to he,
even with the wine of love in his vein, to be oblige to mingle so
often with the family party at the Vincys', and to enter so much into
middlemarch gossip, protract good cheer, whist-playing, and general
futility. he have to be deferential when Mr. Vincy decide question
with trenchant ignorance, especially as to those liquor which be the

be descend a little in relation to Rosamond's family. but that

a much-need transplantation.
"dear!" he say to her one evening, in his gentle tone, as he sit
down by she and look closely at her face--
but I must first say that he have find she alone in the drawing-room,
where the great old-fashioned window, almost as large as the side of
the room, be open to the summer scent of the garden at the back of
the house. her father and mother be go to a party, and the rest
be all out with the butterfly.
"Dear! your eyelid be red."
"be they?" say Rosamond. "I wonder why." it be not in her nature
to pour forth wish or grievance. they only come forth gracefully on
solicitation.
"as if you could hide it from I!"? say Lydgate, lay his hand
tenderly on both of her. "donot I see a tiny drop on one of the
lash? thing trouble you, and you donot tell I. that be unloving."
"why should I tell you what you cannot alter? they be every-day
things:--perhap they have be a little bad lately."
"family annoyance. donot fear speak. I guess they."
"Papa have be more irritable lately. Fred make he angry, and this
morning there be a fresh quarrel because Fred threaten to throw his

besides--"
Rosamond hesitate, and her cheek be gather a slight flush.
Lydgate have never see she in trouble since the morning of their

they.
"I feel that papa be not quite pleased about our engagement," Rosamond
continue, almost in a whisper; "and he say last night that he should
certainly speak to you and say it must be give up."
"will you give it up?" say Lydgate, with quick energy--almost angrily.
"I never give up anything that I choose to do," say Rosamond,
recover her calmness at the touching of this chord.
"God bless you!" say Lydgate, kiss she again. this constancy of
purpose in the right place be adorable. he go on:--
"it be too late now for your father to say that our engagement must be

do to make you unhappy,--that be a reason for hasten our marriage."
an unmistakable delight shine forth from the blue eye that meet his,
and the radiance seem to light up all his future with mild sunshine.
ideal happiness (of the kind know in the Arabian Nights, in which you
be invite to step from the labor and discord of the street into a

"why should we defer it?" he say, with ardent insistence. "I have
take the house now: everything else can soon be get ready--can it

"what original notion you clever man have!" say Rosamond, dimple
with more thorough laughter than usual at this humorous incongruity.

speedy marriage. "remember, we be look forward to a well sort of
happiness even than this--be continually together, independent of
other, and order our life as we will. come, dear, tell I how
soon you can be altogether mine."
there be a serious pleading in Lydgate's tone, as if he feel that she
would be injure he by any fantastic delay. Rosamond become serious

to give an answer that would at least be approximative.
"six week would be ample--say so, Rosamond," insist Lydgate,
release her hand to put his arm gently round she.
one little hand immediately go to pat her hair, while she give she

"there would be the house-linen and the furniture to be prepare.
still, mamma could see to those while we be away."
"yes, to be sure. we must be away a week or so."
"oh, more than that!" say Rosamond, earnestly. she be think of

though sober kind of rank, when sustain by blood). she look at her

"whatever you wish, my darling, when the day be fix. but let we take
a decided course, and put an end to any discomfort you may be
suffer. six weeks!--i be sure they would be ample."
"I could certainly hasten the work," say Rosamond. "will you, then,
mention it to papa?--i think it would be well to write to he." she

forth happily among they in the transcendent evening light: be there
not a soul beyond utterance, half nymph, half child, in those delicate
petal which glow and breathe about the centre of deep color?
he touch her ear and a little bit of neck under it with his lip, and
they sit quite still for many minute which flow by they like a small

that after all his wild mistake and absurd credulity, he have find
perfect womanhood--feel as if already breathe upon by exquisite wed

venerate his high musing and momentous labor and would never

transform life into romance at any moment; who be instruct to the
true womanly limit and not a hair's-breadth beyond--docile, therefore,
and ready to carry out behest which come from that limit. it be
plainer now than ever that his notion of remain much long a

furtherance. and happen the next day to accompany a patient to
Brassing, he see a dinner-service there which strike he as so exactly
the right thing that he buy it at once. it save time to do these
thing just when you think of they, and Lydgate hat ugly crockery.
the dinner-service in question be expensive, but that might be in the
nature of dinner-service. Furnishing be necessarily expensive; but
then it have to be do only once.
"it must be lovely," say Mrs. Vincy, when Lydgate mention his
purchase with some descriptive touch. "just what Rosy ought to have.
I trust in heaven it willnot be break!"
"one must hire servant who will not break thing," say Lydgate.
(certainly, this be reason with an imperfect vision of sequence.
but at that period there be no sort of reasoning which be not more or
less sanction by man of science.)
of course it be unnecessary to defer the mention of anything to mamma,
who do not readily take view that be not cheerful, and be a
happy wife herself, have hardly any feeling but pride in her daughter's
marriage. but Rosamond have good reason for suggest to lydgate that
papa should be appeal to in writing. she prepare for the arrival of
the letter by walk with her papa to the warehouse the next morning,
and tell he on the way that Mr. Lydgate wish to be marry soon.
"Nonsense, my dear!" say Mr. Vincy. "what have he get to marry on?
youwould much well give up the engagement. Ihave tell you so pretty
plainly before this. what have you have such an education for, if you
be to go and marry a poor man? itbe a cruel thing for a father to
see."
"Mr. Lydgate be not poor, papa. he buy Mr. Peacock's practice,
which, they say, be worth eight or nine hundred a-year."
"stuff and nonsense! whatbe buy a practice? he might as well buy
next year's swallow. itwill all slip through his finger."
"on the contrary, papa, he will increase the practice. see how he have
be call in by the Chettams and Casaubons."
"I hope he know I shallnot give anything--with this disappointment about
Fred, and Parliament go to be dissolve, and machine-break
everywhere, and an election come on--"
"dear papa! what can that have to do with my marriage?"
"a pretty deal to do with it! we may all be ruin for what I know--the
country's in that state! some say itbe the end of the world, and
be hang if I donot think it look like it! anyhow, itbe not a time
for I to be draw money out of my business, and I should wish
Lydgate to know that."
"I be sure he expect nothing, papa. and he have such very high
connection: he be sure to rise in one way or another. he be engage
in make scientific discovery."
Mr. Vincy be silent.
"I cannot give up my only prospect of happiness, papa Mr. Lydgate be a
gentleman. I could never love any one who be not a perfect gentleman.
you would not like I to go into a consumption, as Arabella Hawley do.
and you know that I never change my mind."
again papa be silent.
"promise I, papa, that you will consent to what we wish. we shall
never give each other up; and you know that you have always object to
long courtship and late marriage."
there be a little more urgency of this kind, till Mr. Vincy say,
"well, well, child, he must write to I first before I can answer
him,"--and Rosamond be certain that she have gain her point.
Mr. Vincy's answer consist chiefly in a demand that Lydgate should
insure his life--a demand immediately concede. this be a
delightfully reassuring idea suppose that Lydgate die, but in the
mean time not a self-support idea. however, it seem to make
everything comfortable about Rosamond's marriage; and the necessary
purchase go on with much spirit. not without prudential
consideration, however. a bride (who be go to visit at a
baronet's) must have a few first-rate pocket-handkerchief; but beyond
the absolutely necessary half-dozen, Rosamond content herself without

he be not go to do anything extravagant, but the requisite thing

quality. all these matter be by the bye. lydgate foresee that
science and his profession be the object he should alone pursue
enthusiastically; but he could not imagine himself pursue they in
such a home as Wrench have--the door all open, the oil-cloth wear, the
child in soil pinafore, and lunch linger in the form of bone,
black-handle knife, and willow-pattern. but Wrench have a wretched
lymphatic wife who make a mummy of herself indoor in a large shawl;
and he must have altogether begin with an ill-choose domestic apparatus.
Rosamond, however, be on her side much occupy with conjecture,
though her quick imitative perception warn she against betray they
too crudely.
"I shall like so much to know your family," she say one day, when the
wedding journey be be discuss. "we might perhaps take a
direction that would allow we to see they as we return. which of
your uncle do you like good?"
"Oh,--my uncle Godwin, I think. he be a good-natured old fellow."

"no," say Lydgate, carelessly, turn in his chair and rub his

Lydgate smile at her tenderly, and really accept the suggestion that
the proud pleasure of show so charming a bride be worth some

spot with Rosamond.
"I will write to he, then. but my cousin be bore."
it seem magnificent to Rosamond to be able to speak so slightingly of
a baronet's family, and she feel much contentment in the prospect of
be able to estimate they contemptuously on her own account.
but mamma be near spoil all, a day or two later, by saying--
"I hope your uncle Sir Godwin will not look down on Rosy, Mr. Lydgate.
I should think he would do something handsome. a thousand or two can
be nothing to a baronet."
"Mamma!" say Rosamond, blushing deeply; and Lydgate pity she so much

desirable that Lydgate should by-and-by get some first-rate position
elsewhere than in Middlemarch; and this could hardly be difficult in
the case of a man who have a title uncle and could make discovery.
Lydgate, you perceive, have talk fervidly to Rosamond of his hope as
to the high us of his life, and have find it delightful to be
listen to by a creature who would bring he the sweet furtherance of
satisfy affection--beauty--repose--such help as our thought get
from the summer sky and the flower-fringe meadow.
Lydgate rely much on the psychological difference between what for
the sake of variety I will call goose and gander: especially on the
innate submissiveness of the goose as beautifully correspond to the
strength of the gander.
CHAPTER XXXVII.
"Thrice happy she that be so well assure
Unto herself and settle so in heart
that neither will for well be allure
Ne fear to bad with any chance to start,
but like a steddy ship doth strongly part
the rag wave and keep she course aright;
Ne aught for tempest doth from it depart,
Ne aught for fair weather's false delight.
such self-assurance need not fear the spight
of grudging foe; ne favour seek of friend;
but in the stay of her own stedfast might
neither to one herself nor other bend.
most happy she that most assure doth rest,
but he most happy who such one love good."
--SPENSER.
the doubt hint by Mr. Vincy whether it be only the general election
or the end of the world that be come on, now that George the Fourth
be dead, Parliament dissolve, Wellington and Peel generally
depreciate and the new King apologetic, be a feeble type of the
uncertainty in provincial opinion at that time. with the glow-worm
light of country place, how could man see which be their own
thought in the confusion of a Tory Ministry pass liberal measure,
of Tory noble and elector be anxious to return Liberals rather
than friend of the recreant Ministers, and of outcry for remedy
which seem to have a mysteriously remote bearing on private interest,
and be make suspicious by the advocacy of disagreeable neighbor?
buyer of the middlemarch newspaper find themselves in an anomalous
position: during the agitation on the Catholic Question many have give
up the "Pioneer"--which have a motto from Charles James Fox and be in
the van of progress--because it have take Peel's side about the
Papists, and have thus blot its Liberalism with a toleration of
Jesuitry and Baal; but they be ill-satisfied with the "Trumpet,"
which--since its blast against Rome, and in the general flaccidity of
the public mind (nobody know who would support whom)--had become
feeble in its blowing.
it be a time, accord to a noticeable article in the "Pioneer," when
the cry need of the country might well counteract a reluctance to
public action on the part of man whose mind have from long experience
acquire breadth as well as concentration, decision of judgment as well
as tolerance, dispassionateness as well as energy--in fact, all those
quality which in the melancholy experience of mankind have be the
least disposed to share lodging.
Mr. Hackbutt, whose fluent speech be at that time float more widely
than usual, and leave much uncertainty as to its ultimate channel,
be hear to say in Mr. Hawley's office that the article in question
"emanate" from Brooke of Tipton, and that Brooke have secretly buy
the "Pioneer" some month ago.
"that mean mischief, eh?" say Mr. Hawley. "hebe get the freak of
be a popular man now, after dangle about like a stray tortoise.
so much the bad for he. Ihave have my eye on he for some time. he
shall be prettily pump upon. hebe a damned bad landlord. what
business have an old county man to come curry favor with a low set of
dark-blue freeman? as to his paper, I only hope he may do the writing
himself. it would be worth our pay for."
"I understand he have get a very brilliant young fellow to edit it, who
can write the high style of lead article, quite equal to anything
in the London paper. and he mean to take very high ground on Reform."
"let Brooke reform his rent-roll. hebe a cursed old screw, and the
building all over his estate be go to rack. I suppose this young
fellow be some loose fish from London."
"his name be Ladislaw. he be say to be of foreign extraction."
"I know the sort," say Mr. Hawley; "some emissary. hewill begin with
flourish about the Rights of Man and end with murder a wench.
thatbe the style."
"you must concede that there be abuse, Hawley," say Mr. Hackbutt,
foresee some political disagreement with his family lawyer. "I
myself should never favor immoderate view--in fact I take my stand
with Huskisson--but I cannot blind myself to the consideration that the
non-representation of large towns--"
"large town be damn!" say Mr. Hawley, impatient of exposition. "I
know a little too much about middlemarch election. let them quash
every pocket borough to-morrow, and bring in every mushroom town in the
kingdom--they'll only increase the expense of get into Parliament.
I go upon fact."
Mr. Hawley's disgust at the notion of the "Pioneer" be edit by an
emissary, and of Brooke become actively political--as if a tortoise
of desultory pursuit should protrude its small head ambitiously and
become rampant--be hardly equal to the annoyance feel by some member
of Mr. Brooke's own family. the result have ooze forth gradually, like
the discovery that your neighbor have set up an unpleasant kind of
manufacture which will be permanently under your nostril without legal
remedy. the "Pioneer" have be secretly buy even before Will
Ladislaw's arrival, the expect opportunity have offer itself in
the readiness of the proprietor to part with a valuable property which
do not pay; and in the interval since Mr. Brooke have write his
invitation, those germinal idea of make his mind tell upon the world
at large which have be present in he from his young year, but have
hitherto lain in some obstruction, have be sprout under cover.
the development be much further by a delight in his guest which
prove great even than he have anticipate. for it seem that will
be not only at home in all those artistic and literary subject which
Mr. Brooke have go into at one time, but that he be strikingly ready
at seize the point of the political situation, and deal with they
in that large spirit which, aid by adequate memory, lend itself to
quotation and general effectiveness of treatment.
"he seem to I a kind of Shelley, you know," Mr. Brooke take an
opportunity of say, for the gratification of Mr. Casaubon. "I donot
mean as to anything objectionable--laxity or atheism, or anything of
that kind, you know--Ladislaw's sentiment in every way I be sure be

he be a relation of your, Casaubon."
if the right tack imply anything more precise than the rest of Mr.

occupation at a great distance from Lowick. he have dislike will while

invitation of the formal kind have be want, however, for Will have

but will Ladislaw always seem to see more in what she say than she
herself see. Dorothea have little vanity, but she have the ardent
woman's need to rule beneficently by make the joy of another soul.
hence the mere chance of see will occasionally be like a lunette
open in the wall of her prison, give she a glimpse of the sunny

husband might think about the introduction of Will as her uncle's
guest. on this subject Mr. Casaubon have remain dumb.
but will want to talk with Dorothea alone, and be impatient of slow
circumstance. however slight the terrestrial intercourse between Dante
and Beatrice or Petrarch and Laura, time change the proportion of
thing, and in later day it be preferable to have few sonnet and
more conversation. necessity excuse stratagem, but stratagem be
limit by the dread of offend Dorothea. he find out at last that
he want to take a particular sketch at Lowick; and one morning when
Mr. Brooke have to drive along the Lowick road on his way to the county

Lowick, and without announce himself at the Manor settle himself to

but the stratagem be defeat by the weather. cloud gather with

go into the drawing-room and wait there without be announce; and
see his old acquaintance the butler in the hall, he say, "donot
mention that I be here, Pratt; I will wait till luncheon; I know Mr.
Casaubon do not like to be disturb when he be in the library."
"Master be out, sir; therebe only Mrs. Casaubon in the library. Iwould
well tell she yoube here, sir," say Pratt, a red-cheeked man give
to lively converse with Tantripp, and often agree with she that it
must be dull for Madam.
"oh, very well; this confound rain have hinder I from sketch,"
say will, feel so happy that he affect indifference with

in another minute he be in the library, and Dorothea be meet he

"Mr. Casaubon have go to the Archdeacon's," she say, at once. "I

will be not without his intention to be always generous, but our

but her face, too, break into a smile as she say, "that be your

to Mr. Casaubon's wish. you must remember that you have not do
what he think well for you. and if he dislike you--you be
speak of dislike a little while ago--but I should rather say, if he
have show any painful feeling towards you, you must consider how
sensitive he have become from the wear effect of study. perhaps,"
she continue, get into a plead tone, "my uncle have not tell you
how serious Mr. Casaubon's illness be. it would be very petty of we
who be well and can bear thing, to think much of small offence from
those who carry a weight of trial."
"you teach I well," say will. "I will never grumble on that
subject again." there be a gentleness in his tone which come from the
unutterable contentment of perceive--what Dorothea be hardly
conscious of--that she be travel into the remoteness of pure pity
and loyalty towards her husband. will be ready to adore her pity and
loyalty, if she would associate himself with she in manifest they.
"I have really sometimes be a perverse fellow," he go on, "but I
will never again, if I can help it, do or say what you would

"that be very good of you," say Dorothea, with another open smile. "I
shall have a little kingdom then, where I shall give law. but you
will soon go away, out of my rule, I imagine. you will soon be tire
of stay at the Grange."
"that be a point I want to mention to you--one of the reason why I
wish to speak to you alone. Mr. Brooke propose that I should stay
in this neighborhood. he have buy one of the Middlemarch newspaper,
and he wish I to conduct that, and also to help he in other way."
"would not that be a sacrifice of high prospect for you?" say
Dorothea.
"perhaps; but I have always be blame for thinking of prospect, and
not settle to anything. and here be something offer to I. if you
would not like I to accept it, I will give it up. otherwise I would
rather stay in this part of the country than go away. I belong to
nobody anywhere else."

simply and readily as she have speak at Rome. there be not the shadow
of a reason in her mind at the moment why she should not say so.

but the next moment, Dorothea, accord to a habit which be get

if be face be not turn towards she, and this make it easy to say--

shall strike across Halsell Common, and see the gleam on the wet
grass. I like that."
he approach she to shake hand quite hurriedly, longing but not
dare to say, "donot mention the subject to Mr. Casaubon." no, he
dare not, could not say it. to ask she to be less simple and direct

through. and there be always the other great dread--of himself
become dim and forever ray-shorn in her eye.

this might seem an undue dictation.
so they only say "good-by," and will quit the house, strike

she feel be really concerned in this question. even with her

Dorothea look at her husband while she speak, but he have at first

"I fear that you might feel some objection. but certainly a young

Dorothea feel that this be a consideration to soften her husband.

the next morning, without Dorothea's knowledge, Mr. Casaubon despatch
the follow letter, begin "dear Mr. Ladislaw" (he have always

"Mrs. Casaubon inform I that a proposal have be make to you, and

own, but associate at well with the sciolism of literary or political
adventurer. at any rate, the contrary issue must exclude you from

your faithfully,
"EDWARD CASAUBON."
meanwhile Dorothea's mind be innocently at work towards the further
embitterment of her husband; dwelling, with a sympathy that grow to

and now, since her conversation with Will, many fresh image have

here be a daughter whose child--even accord to the ordinary aping
of aristocratic institution by people who be no more aristocratic

nature go on the side of responsibility--the fulfilment of claim
found on our own deed, such as marriage and parentage.
it be true, she say to herself, that Mr. Casaubon have a debt to the

there be a peculiar fascination for Dorothea in this division of
property intend for herself, and always regard by she as excessive.
she be blind, you see, to many thing obvious to other--likely to

the thought which have gather vividness in the solitude of she

agitate he. Bat when young ardor be set brood over the

"Dorothea, since you be up, will you light a candle?"

"no, not at all; but I shall be oblige, since you be up, if you will
read I a few page of Lowth."

"certainly."
"I have be think about money all day--that I have always have too
much, and especially the prospect of too much."
"these, my dear Dorothea, be providential arrangement."
"but if one have too much in consequence of other be wrong, it

"that you have be too liberal in arrangement for I--I mean, with

that 'more' not be do."
there be a perceptible pause before Mr. Casaubon reply, not quickly
as before, but with a still more biting emphasis.
"Dorothea, my love, this be not the first occasion, but it be well

on my procedure."
Poor Dorothea, shroud in the darkness, be in a tumult of conflicting

long while sleepless, without speak again.
the next day, Mr. Casaubon receive the following answer from will
ladislaw:--
"DEAR MR. CASAUBON,--I have give all due consideration to your letter
of yesterday, but I be unable to take precisely your view of our mutual
position. with the full acknowledgment of your generous conduct to
I in the past, I must still maintain that an obligation of this kind
cannot fairly fetter I as you appear to expect that it should.
grant that a benefactor's wish may constitute a claim; there must
always be a reservation as to the quality of those wish. they may
possibly clash with more imperative consideration. or a benefactor's
veto might impose such a negation on a man's life that the consequent
blank might be more cruel than the benefaction be generous. I be
merely use strong illustration. in the present case I be unable to
take your view of the bearing which my acceptance of occupation--not
enrich certainly, but not dishonorable--will have on your own
position which seem to I too substantial to be affect in that
shadowy manner. and though I do not believe that any change in our
relation will occur (certainly none have yet occur) which can
nullify the obligation impose on I by the past, pardon I for not
see that those obligation should restrain I from use the
ordinary freedom of live where I choose, and maintain myself by
any lawful occupation I may choose. regret that there exist this
difference between we as to a relation in which the conferring of
benefit have be entirely on your side--
I remain, your with persistent obligation,
WILL LADISLAW."
poor Mr. Casaubon feel (and must not we, be impartial, feel with he
a little?) that no man have juster cause for disgust and suspicion than
he. Young Ladislaw, he be sure, mean to defy and annoy he, mean to
win Dorothea's confidence and sow her mind with disrespect, and perhaps
aversion, towards her husband. some motive beneath the surface have
be need to account for Will's sudden change of in reject Mr.
Casaubon's aid and quit his travel; and this defiant determination
to fix himself in the neighborhood by take up something so much at
variance with his former choice as Mr. Brooke's Middlemarch project,
reveal clearly enough that the undeclared motive have relation to
Dorothea. not for one moment do Mr. Casaubon suspect Dorothea of any
doubleness: he have no suspicion of she, but he have (what be little
less uncomfortable) the positive knowledge that her tendency to form
opinion about her husband's conduct be accompany with a disposition
to regard Will Ladislaw favorably and be influence by what he say.
his own proud reticence have prevent he from ever be undeceive in
the supposition that Dorothea have originally ask her uncle to invite
will to his house.
and now, on receive Will's letter, Mr. Casaubon have to consider his
duty. he would never have be easy to call his action anything else
than duty; but in this case, contend motif thrust he back into
negation.
should he apply directly to Mr. Brooke, and demand of that troublesome
gentleman to revoke his proposal? or should he consult Sir James
Chettam, and get he to concur in remonstrance against a step which
touch the whole family? in either case Mr. Casaubon be aware that
failure be just as probable as success. it be impossible for he to
mention Dorothea's name in the matter, and without some alarming
urgency Mr. Brooke be as likely as not, after meet all

fear, Casaubon! depend upon it, young Ladislaw will do you credit.
depend upon it, I have put my finger on the right thing." and Mr.
Casaubon shrink nervously from communicate on the subject with Sir
James Chettam, between whom and himself there have never be any
cordiality, and who would immediately think of Dorothea without any
mention of she.
poor Mr. Casaubon be distrustful of everybody's feeling towards he,
especially as a husband. to let any one suppose that he be jealous
would be to admit their (suspect) view of his disadvantage: to let
they know that he do not find marriage particularly blissful would
imply his conversion to their (probably) early disapproval. it would
be as bad as let carp, and Brasenose generally, know how backward
he be in organize the matter for his "key to all Mythologies." all
