himself the inward sore of self-doubt and jealousy. and on the most
delicate of all personal subject, the habit of proud suspicious
reticence tell doubly.
thus Mr. Casaubon remain proudly, bitterly silent. but he have
forbid Will to come to Lowick Manor, and he be mentally prepare
other measure of frustration.
CHAPTER XXXVIII.
"C'est beaucoup que le jugement des homme sur les action
humaine; tot ou tard il devient efficace."--guizot.
Sir James Chettam could not look with any satisfaction on Mr. Brooke's
new course; but it be easy to object than to hinder. Sir James
account for his have come in alone one day to lunch with the
Cadwalladers by saying--
"I cannot talk to you as I want, before Celia: it might hurt she.
indeed, it would not be right."
"I know what you mean--the 'Pioneer' at the Grange!" dart in Mrs.
Cadwallader, almost before the last word be off her friend's tongue.
"it be frightful--this taking to buy whistle and blow they in
everybody's hearing. lie in bed all day and play at domino,
like poor Lord Plessy, would be more private and bearable."
"I see they be begin to attack our friend Brooke in the
'Trumpet,'" say the Rector, lounge back and smile easily, as he
would have do if he have be attack himself. "there be tremendous
sarcasm against a landlord not a hundred mile from Middlemarch, who
receive his own rent, and make no return."
"I do wish Brooke would leave that off," say Sir James, with his
little frown of annoyance.
"be he really go to be put in nomination, though?" say Mr.
Cadwallader. "I see Farebrother yesterday--hebe Whiggish himself,
hoist Brougham and useful Knowledge; thatbe the bad I know of
him;--and he say that Brooke be get up a pretty strong party.
Bulstrode, the banker, be his foremost man. but he think Brooke would
come off badly at a nomination."
"exactly," say Sir James, with earnestness. "I have be inquire
into the thing, for Ihave never know anything about Middlemarch
politic before--the county be my business. what Brooke trust to,
be that they be go to turn out Oliver because he be a Peelite. but
Hawley tell I that if they send up a Whig at all it be sure to be
Bagster, one of those candidate who come from heaven know where, but
dead against Ministers, and an experienced parliamentary man. Hawley's
rather rough: he forget that he be speak to I. he say if Brooke
want a pelting, he could get it cheap than by go to the
husting."
"I warn you all of it," say Mrs. Cadwallader, wave her hand
outward. "I say to Humphrey long ago, Mr. Brooke be go to make a
splash in the mud. and now he have do it."
"well, he might have take it into his head to marry," say the Rector.
"that would have be a graver mess than a little flirtation with
politic."
"he may do that afterwards," say Mrs. Cadwallader--"when he have come
out on the other side of the mud with an ague."
"what I care for most be his own dignity," say Sir James. "of course
I care the more because of the family. but hebe get on in life
now, and I donot like to think of his expose himself. they will be
rake up everything against he."
"I suppose itbe no use try any persuasion," say the Rector.
"therebe such an odd mixture of obstinacy and changeableness in Brooke.
have you try he on the subject?"
"well, no," say Sir James; "I feel a delicacy in appear to dictate.
but I have be talk to this young Ladislaw that Brooke be make a
factotum of. Ladislaw seem clever enough for anything. I think it
as well to hear what he have to say; and he be against Brooke's standing
this time. I think hewill turn he round: I think the nomination may be
stave off."
"I know," say Mrs. Cadwallader, nodding. "the independent member
havenot get his speech well enough by heart."
"but this Ladislaw--there again be a vexatious business," say Sir
James. "we have have he two or three time to dine at the Hall (you
have meet he, by the bye) as Brooke's guest and a relation of
Casaubon's, think he be only on a fly visit. and now I find
hebe in everybody's mouth in Middlemarch as the editor of the
'Pioneer.' there be story go about he as a quill-drive alien,
a foreign emissary, and what not."
"Casaubon willnot like that," say the Rector.
"there _be_ some foreign blood in Ladislaw," return Sir James. "I
hope he willnot go into extreme opinion and carry Brooke on."
"oh, hebe a dangerous young sprig, that Mr. Ladislaw," say Mrs.
Cadwallader, "with his opera song and his ready tongue. a sort of
byronic hero--an amorous conspirator, it strike I. and Thomas
Aquinas be not fond of he. I could see that, the day the picture be
bring."
"I donot like to begin on the subject with Casaubon," say Sir James.
"he have more right to interfere than i. but itbe a disagreeable affair
all round. what a character for anybody with decent connection to
show himself in!--one of those newspaper fellow! you have only to
look at Keck, who manage the 'Trumpet.' I see he the other day with
Hawley. his writing be sound enough, I believe, but hebe such a low
fellow, that I wish he have be on the wrong side."
"what can you expect with these peddle Middlemarch paper?" say the
Rector. "I donot suppose you could get a high style of man anywhere to
be write up interest he donot really care about, and for pay that
hardly keep he in at elbow."
"exactly: that make it so annoying that Brooke should have put a man
who have a sort of connection with the family in a position of that
kind. for my part, I think Ladislaw be rather a fool for accept."
"it be Aquinas's fault," say Mrs. Cadwallader. "why donot he use his
interest to get Ladislaw make an attache or send to India? that be how
family get rid of troublesome sprig."
"there be no knowing to what length the mischief may go," say Sir
James, anxiously. "but if Casaubon say nothing, what can I do?"
"oh my dear Sir James," say the Rector, "donot let we make too much of
all this. it be likely enough to end in mere smoke. after a month or
two Brooke and this Master Ladislaw will get tired of each other;
Ladislaw will take wing; Brooke will sell the 'Pioneer,' and everything
will settle down again as usual."
"there be one good chance--that he will not like to feel his money
ooze away," say Mrs. Cadwallader. "if I know the item of election
expense I could scare he. itbe no use ply he with wide word
like expenditure: I wouldnot talk of phlebotomy, I would empty a pot of
leech upon he. what we good stingy people donot like, be have our
sixpence suck away from we."
"and he will not like have thing rake up against he," say Sir
James. "there be the management of his estate. they have begin upon
that already. and it really be painful for I to see. it be a
nuisance under one's very nose. I do think one be bind to do the good
for one's land and tenant, especially in these hard time."
"perhaps the 'Trumpet' may rouse he to make a change, and some good
may come of it all," say the Rector. "I know I should be glad. I
should hear less grumbling when my tithe be pay. I donot know what I
should do if there be not a modus in Tipton."
"I want he to have a proper man to look after thing--I want he to
take on Garth again," say Sir James. "he get rid of Garth twelve
year ago, and everything have be go wrong since. I think of
get Garth to manage for I--he have make such a capital plan for my
building; and Lovegood be hardly up to the mark. but Garth would not
undertake the Tipton estate again unless Brooke leave it entirely to
he."
"in the right of it too," say the Rector. "Garth be an independent
fellow: an original, simple-minded fellow. one day, when he be do
some valuation for I, he tell I point-blank that clergyman seldom
understand anything about business, and do mischief when they meddle;
but he say it as quietly and respectfully as if he have be talk to
I about sailor. he would make a different parish of Tipton, if
Brooke would let he manage. I wish, by the help of the 'Trumpet,' you
could bring that round."
"if Dorothea have keep near her uncle, there would have be some
chance," say Sir James. "she might have get some power over he in
time, and she be always uneasy about the estate. she have wonderfully
good notion about such thing. but now Casaubon take she up
entirely. Celia complain a good deal. we can hardly get she to dine
with we, since he have that fit." Sir James end with a look of pity
disgust, and Mrs. Cadwallader shrug her shoulder as much as to say
that _she_ be not likely to see anything new in that direction.
"Poor Casaubon!" the Rector say. "that be a nasty attack. I think
he look shatter the other day at the Archdeacon's."
"in point of fact," resume Sir James, not choose to dwell on "fit,"
"Brooke donot mean badly by his tenant or any one else, but he have
get that way of paring and clip at expense."
"come, thatbe a blessing," say Mrs. Cadwallader. "that help he to
find himself in a morning. he may not know his own opinion, but he
do know his own pocket."
"I donot believe a man be in pocket by stinginess on his land," say
Sir James.
"oh, stinginess may be abuse like other virtue: it will not do to
keep one's own pig lean," say Mrs. Cadwallader, who have rise to look
out of the window. "but talk of an independent politician and he will
appear."
"what! Brooke?" say her husband.
"yes. now, you ply he with the 'Trumpet,' Humphrey; and I will put
the leech on he. what will you do, Sir James?"
"the fact be, I donot like to begin about it with Brooke, in our mutual
position; the whole thing be so unpleasant. I do wish people would
behave like gentleman," say the good baronet, feel that this be a
simple and comprehensive programme for social well-being.
"here you all be, eh?" say Mr. Brooke, shuffle round and shake
hand. "I be go up to the Hall by-and-by, Chettam. but itbe
pleasant to find everybody, you know. well, what do you think of
things?--going on a little fast! it be true enough, what Lafitte
said--'since yesterday, a century have pass away:'--they're in the
next century, you know, on the other side of the water. go on
fast than we be."
"why, yes," say the Rector, take up the newspaper. "here be the
'trumpet' accuse you of lag behind--do you see?"
"eh? no," say Mr. Brooke, drop his glove into his hat and hastily
adjust his eye-glass. but Mr. Cadwallader keep the paper in his
hand, say, with a smile in his eyes--
"look here! all this be about a landlord not a hundred mile from
Middlemarch, who receive his own rent. they say he be the most
retrogressive man in the county. I think you must have teach they
that word in the 'Pioneer.'"
"oh, that be Keek--an illiterate fellow, you know. Retrogressive, now!
come, thatbe capital. he think it mean destructive: they want to
make I out a destructive, you know," say Mr. Brooke, with that
cheerfulness which be usually sustain by an adversary's ignorance.
"I think he know the meaning of the word. here be a sharp stroke or
two. if we have to describe a man who be retrogressive in the most evil
sense of the word--we should say, he be one who would dub himself a
reformer of our constitution, while every interest for which he be
immediately responsible be go to decay: a philanthropist who cannot
bear one rogue to be hang, but do not mind five honest tenant
be half-starve: a man who shriek at corruption, and keep his
farm at rack-rent: who roar himself red at rotten borough, and do
not mind if every field on his farm have a rotten gate: a man very
open-hearted to Leeds and Manchester, no doubt; he would give any
number of representative who will pay for their seat out of their own
pocket: what he object to give, be a little return on rent-day to
help a tenant to buy stock, or an outlay on repair to keep the weather
out at a tenant's barn-door or make his house look a little less like
an irish cottier's. but we all know the wag's definition of a
philanthropist: a man whose charity increase directly as the square of
the distance. and so on. all the rest be to show what sort of
legislator a philanthropist be likely to make," end the Rector,
throw down the paper, and clasp his hand at the back of his
head, while he look at Mr. Brooke with an air of amused neutrality.
"come, thatbe rather good, you know," say Mr. Brooke, take up the
paper and try to bear the attack as easily as his neighbor do, but
color and smile rather nervously; "that about roar himself red
at rotten borough--I never make a speech about rotten borough in my
life. and as to roar myself red and that kind of thing--these man
never understand what be good satire. Satire, you know, should be true
up to a certain point. I recollect they say that in 'the Edinburgh'
somewhere--it must be true up to a certain point."
"well, that be really a hit about the gate," say Sir James, anxious
to tread carefully. "Dagley complain to I the other day that he
havenot get a decent gate on his farm. Garth have invent a new pattern
of gate--I wish you would try it. one ought to use some of one's
timber in that way."
"you go in for fancy farming, you know, Chettam," say Mr. Brooke,
appear to glance over the column of the "Trumpet." "thatbe your
hobby, and you donot mind the expense."
"I think the most expensive hobby in the world be stand for
Parliament," say Mrs. Cadwallader. "they say the last unsuccessful
candidate at Middlemarch--Giles, benot his name?--spent ten thousand
pound and fail because he do not bribe enough. what a bitter
reflection for a man!"
"somebody be say," say the Rector, laughingly, "that East Retford
be nothing to Middlemarch, for bribery."
"nothing of the kind," say Mr. Brooke. "the Tories bribe, you know:
Hawley and his set bribe with treating, hot codling, and that sort of
thing; and they bring the voter drunk to the poll. but they be not
go to have it their own way in future--not in future, you know.
Middlemarch be a little backward, I admit--the freeman be a little
backward. but we shall educate they--we shall bring they on, you
know. the good people there be on our side."
"Hawley say you have man on your side who will do you harm," remark
Sir James. "he say Bulstrode the banker will do you harm."
"and that if you get pelt," interpose Mrs. Cadwallader, "half the
rotten egg would mean hatred of your committee-man. good heaven!
think what it must be to be pelt for wrong opinion. and I seem to
remember a story of a man they pretend to chair and let he fall into
a dust-heap on purpose!"
"pelt be nothing to their finding hole in one's coat," say the
Rector. "I confess thatbe what I should be afraid of, if we parson
have to stand at the husting for preferment. I should be afraid of
their reckon up all my fishing day. upon my word, I think the
truth be the hard missile one can be pelt with."
"the fact be," say Sir James, "if a man go into public life he must
be prepare for the consequence. he must make himself proof against
calumny."
"my dear Chettam, that be all very fine, you know," say Mr. Brooke.
"but how will you make yourself proof against calumny? you should read
history--look at ostracism, persecution, martyrdom, and that kind of
thing. they always happen to the good man, you know. but what be that
in Horace?--'fiat justitia, ruat . . . something or other."
"exactly," say Sir James, with a little more heat than usual. "what I
mean by be proof against calumny be be able to point to the fact
as a contradiction."
"and it be not martyrdom to pay bill that one have run into one's
self," say Mrs. Cadwallader.
but it be Sir James's evident annoyance that most stir Mr. Brooke.
"well, you know, Chettam," he say, rise, take up his hat and
lean on his stick, "you and I have a different system. you be all
for outlay with your farm. I donot want to make out that my system be
good under all circumstance--under all circumstance, you know."
"there ought to be a new valuation make from time to time," say Sir
James. "return be very well occasionally, but I like a fair
valuation. what do you say, Cadwallader?"
"I agree with you. if I be Brooke, I would choke the 'Trumpet' at
once by get Garth to make a new valuation of the farm, and give
he carte blanche about gate and repair: thatbe my view of the
political situation," say the Rector, broaden himself by stick
his thumb in his armhole, and laugh towards Mr. Brooke.
"thatbe a showy sort of thing to do, you know," say Mr. Brooke. "but
I should like you to tell I of another landlord who have distress his
tenant for arrear as little as I have. I let the old tenant stay
on. Ibe uncommonly easy, let I tell you, uncommonly easy. I have my
own idea, and I take my stand on they, you know. a man who do that
be always charge with eccentricity, inconsistency, and that kind of
thing. when I change my line of action, I shall follow my own idea."
after that, Mr. Brooke remember that there be a packet which he have
omit to send off from the Grange, and he bid everybody hurriedly
good-by.
"I donot want to take a liberty with Brooke," say Sir James; "I see
he be nettle. but as to what he say about old tenant, in point of
fact no new tenant would take the farm on the present term."
"I have a notion that he will be bring round in time," say the
Rector. "but you be pull one way, Elinor, and we be pull
another. you want to frighten he away from expense, and we want to
frighten he into it. well let he try to be popular and see that
his character as a landlord stand in his way. I donot think it
signify two straw about the 'Pioneer,' or Ladislaw, or Brooke's
speechifying to the Middlemarchers. but it do signify about the
parishioner in Tipton be comfortable."
"excuse I, it be you two who be on the wrong tack," say Mrs.
Cadwallader. "you should have prove to he that he lose money by bad
management, and then we should all have pull together. if you put
he a-horseback on politic, I warn you of the consequence. it be
all very well to ride on stick at home and call they idea."
CHAPTER XXXIX.
"if, as I have, you also doe,
Vertue attire in woman see,
and dare love that, and say so too,
and forget the He and she;
and if this love, though place so,
from prophane man you hide,
which will no faith on this bestow,
or, if they doe, deride:
then you have do a braver thing
than all the Worthies do,
and a braver thence will spring,
which be, to keep that hid."
--DR. DONNE.
Sir James Chettam's mind be not fruitful in device, but his grow
anxiety to "act on Brooke," once bring close to his constant belief
in Dorothea's capacity for influence, become formative, and issue in a
little plan; namely, to plead Celia's indisposition as a reason for
fetch Dorothea by herself to the Hall, and to leave she at the
Grange with the carriage on the way, after make she fully aware of
the situation concern the management of the estate.
in this way it happen that one day near four o'clock, when Mr. Brooke
and Ladislaw be seat in the library, the door open and Mrs.
Casaubon be announce.
will, the moment before, have be low in the depth of boredom, and,
oblige to help Mr. Brooke in arrange "document" about hang
sheep-stealer, be exemplify the power our mind have of rid
several horse at once by inwardly arrange measure towards get a
lodge for himself in Middlemarch and cut short his constant
residence at the Grange; while there flit through all these steady
image a tickling vision of a sheep-steal epic write with homeric
particularity. when Mrs. Casaubon be announce he start up as from
an electric shock, and feel a tingling at his finger-end. any one
observe he would have see a change in his complexion, in the
adjustment of his facial muscle, in the vividness of his glance, which
might have make they imagine that every molecule in his body have pass
the message of a magic touch. and so it have. for effective magic be
transcendent nature; and who shall measure the subtlety of those
touch which convey the quality of soul as well as body, and make a
man's passion for one woman differ from his passion for another as joy
in the morning light over valley and river and white mountain-top
differ from joy among chinese lantern and glass panel? will, too,
be make of very impressible stuff. the bow of a violin draw near he
cleverly, would at one stroke change the aspect of the world for he,
and his point of view shift--as easily as his mood. Dorothea's
entrance be the freshness of morning.
"well, my dear, this be pleasant, now," say Mr. Brooke, meeting and
kiss she. "you have leave Casaubon with his book, I suppose.
thatbe right. we must not have you get too learn for a woman,
you know."
"there be no fear of that, uncle," say Dorothea, turn to Will and
shake hand with open cheerfulness, while she make no other form of
greeting, but go on answer her uncle. "I be very slow. when I
want to be busy with book, I be often play truant among my
thought. I find it be not so easy to be learn as to plan cottage."
she seat herself beside her uncle opposite to Will, and be evidently
preoccupy with something that make she almost unmindful of he. he
be ridiculously disappointed, as if he have imagine that her come
have anything to do with he.
"why, yes, my dear, it be quite your hobby to draw plan. but it be
good to break that off a little. hobby be apt to run away with we,
you know; it donot do to be run away with. we must keep the rein.
I have never let myself be run away with; I always pull up. that be
what I tell Ladislaw. he and I be alike, you know: he like to go
into everything. we be work at capital punishment. we shall do a
great deal together, Ladislaw and I."
"yes," say Dorothea, with characteristic directness, "Sir James have
be tell I that he be in hope of see a great change make soon
in your management of the estate--that you be think of have the
farm value, and repair make, and the cottage improve, so that
Tipton may look quite another place. oh, how happy!"--she go on,
clasp her hand, with a return to that more childlike impetuous
manner, which have be subdue since her marriage. "if I be at home
still, I should take to rid again, that I might go about with you
and see all that! and you be go to engage Mr. Garth, who praise
my cottage, Sir James say."
"chettam be a little hasty, my dear," say Mr. Brooke, color
slightly; "a little hasty, you know. I never say I should do anything
of the kind. I never say I should _not_ do it, you know."
"he only feel confident that you will do it," say Dorothea, in a
voice as clear and unhesitate as that of a young chorister chant a
credo, "because you mean to enter Parliament as a member who care for
the improvement of the people, and one of the first thing to be make
well be the state of the land and the laborer. think of Kit Downes,
uncle, who live with his wife and seven child in a house with one
sitting room and one bedroom hardly large than this table!--and those
poor dagley, in their tumble-down farmhouse, where they live in the
back kitchen and leave the other room to the rat! that be one reason
why I do not like the picture here, dear uncle--which you think I
stupid about. I use to come from the village with all that dirt and
coarse ugliness like a pain within I, and the simper picture in
the drawing-room seem to I like a wicked attempt to find delight in
what be false, while we donot mind how hard the truth be for the
neighbor outside our wall. I think we have no right to come forward
and urge wide change for good, until we have try to alter the evil
which lie under our own hand."
Dorothea have gather emotion as she go on, and have forget
everything except the relief of pour forth her feeling, unchecked:
an experience once habitual with she, but hardly ever present since her
marriage, which have be a perpetual struggle of energy with fear. for
the moment, Will's admiration be accompany with a chilling sense of
remoteness. a man be seldom ashamed of feel that he cannot love a
woman so well when he see a certain greatness in she: nature have
intend greatness for man. but nature have sometimes make sad
oversight in carry out her intention; as in the case of good Mr.
Brooke, whose masculine consciousness be at this moment in rather a
stammering condition under the eloquence of his niece. he could not
immediately find any other mode of express himself than that of
rise, fix his eye-glass, and finger the paper before he. at
last he said--
"there be something in what you say, my dear, something in what you
say--but not everything--eh, Ladislaw? you and I donot like our
picture and statue be find fault with. young lady be a little
ardent, you know--a little one-side, my dear. fine art, poetry, that
kind of thing, elevate a nation--emollit more--you understand a
little Latin now. but--eh? what?"
these interrogative be address to the footman who have come in to
say that the keeper have find one of Dagley's boy with a leveret in
his hand just kill.
"Iwill come, Iwill come. I shall let he off easily, you know," say Mr.
Brooke aside to Dorothea, shuffle away very cheerfully.
"I hope you feel how right this change be that I--that Sir James wish
for," say Dorothea to will, as soon as her uncle be go.
"I do, now I have hear you speak about it. I shall not forget what
you have say. but can you think of something else at this moment? I
may not have another opportunity of speak to you about what have
occur," say Will, rise with a movement of impatience, and hold
the back of his chair with both hand.
"pray tell I what it be," say Dorothea, anxiously, also rise and
go to the open window, where Monk be look in, pant and
wag his tail. she lean she back against the window-frame, and
lay her hand on the dog's head; for though, as we know, she be not
fond of pet that must be hold in the hand or trodden on, she be
always attentive to the feeling of dog, and very polite if she have to
decline their advance.
will follow she only with his eye and say, "I presume you know that
Mr. Casaubon have forbid I to go to his house."
"no, I do not," say Dorothea, after a moment's pause. she be
evidently much move. "I be very, very sorry," she add, mournfully.
she be think of what will have no knowledge of--the conversation
between she and her husband in the darkness; and she be anew smite
with hopelessness that she could influence Mr. Casaubon's action. but
the marked expression of her sorrow convince will that it be not all
give to he personally, and that Dorothea have not be visit by the
idea that Mr. Casaubon's dislike and jealousy of he turn upon
herself. he feel an odd mixture of delight and vexation: of delight
that he could dwell and be cherish in her thought as in a pure home,
without suspicion and without stint--of vexation because he be of too
little account with she, be not formidable enough, be treat with an
unhesitate benevolence which do not flatter he. but his dread of
any change in Dorothea be strong than his discontent, and he begin
to speak again in a tone of mere explanation.
"Mr. Casaubon's reason be, his displeasure at my take a position here
which he consider unsuited to my rank as his cousin. I have tell he
that I cannot give way on this point. it be a little too hard on I to
expect that my course in life be to be hamper by prejudice which I
think ridiculous. obligation may be stretch till it be no well
than a brand of slavery stamp on we when we be too young to know
its meaning. I would not have accept the position if I have not mean
to make it useful and honorable. I be not bind to regard family
dignity in any other light."
Dorothea feel wretched. she think her husband altogether in the
wrong, on more ground than will have mention.
"it be well for we not to speak on the subject," she say, with a
tremulousness not common in her voice, "since you and Mr. Casaubon
disagree. you intend to remain?" she be look out on the lawn,
with melancholy meditation.
"yes; but I shall hardly ever see you now," say will, in a tone of
almost boyish complaint.
"no," say Dorothea, turn her eye full upon he, "hardly ever. but
I shall hear of you. I shall know what you be do for my uncle."
"I shall know hardly anything about you," say will. "no one will tell
I anything."
"oh, my life be very simple," say Dorothea, her lip curl with an
exquisite smile, which irradiate her melancholy. "I be always at
Lowick."
"that be a dreadful imprisonment," say will, impetuously.
"no, donot think that," say Dorothea. "I have no longing."
he do not speak, but she reply to some change in his expression. "I
mean, for myself. except that I should like not to have so much more
than my share without do anything for other. but I have a belief
of my own, and it comfort I."
"what be that?" say will, rather jealous of the belief.
"that by desire what be perfectly good, even when we donot quite know
what it be and cannot do what we would, we be part of the divine power
against evil--widen the skirt of light and make the struggle with
darkness narrower."
"that be a beautiful mysticism--it be a--"
"please not to call it by any name," say Dorothea, put out her
hand entreatingly. "you will say it be Persian, or something else
geographical. it be my life. I have find it out, and cannot part
with it. I have always be find out my religion since I be a
little girl. I use to pray so much--now I hardly ever pray. I try
not to have desire merely for myself, because they may not be good for
other, and I have too much already. I only tell you, that you might
know quite well how my day go at Lowick."
"God bless you for tell I!" say will, ardently, and rather
wonder at himself. they be look at each other like two fond
child who be talk confidentially of bird.
"what be _your_ religion?" say Dorothea. "I mean--not what you know
about religion, but the belief that help you most?"
"to love what be good and beautiful when I see it," say will. "but I
be a rebel: I donot feel bound, as you do, to submit to what I donot
like."
"but if you like what be good, that come to the same thing," say
Dorothea, smile.
"now you be subtle," say will.
"yes; Mr. Casaubon often say I be too subtle. I donot feel as if I
be subtle," say Dorothea, playfully. "but how long my uncle be! I
must go and look for he. I must really go on to the Hall. Celia be
expect I."
will offer to tell Mr. Brooke, who presently come and say that he
would step into the carriage and go with Dorothea as far as Dagley's,
to speak about the small delinquent who have be catch with the
leveret. Dorothea renew the subject of the estate as they drive
along, but Mr. Brooke, not be take unawares, get the talk under his
own control.
"Chettam, now," he reply; "he find fault with I, my dear; but I
should not preserve my game if it be not for Chettam, and he cannot
say that that expense be for the sake of the tenant, you know. itbe a
little against my feeling:--poaching, now, if you come to look into
it--I have often think of get up the subject. not long ago,
Flavell, the Methodist preacher, be bring up for knock down a
hare that come across his path when he and his wife be walk out
together. he be pretty quick, and knock it on the neck."
"that be very brutal, I think," say Dorothea
"well, now, it seem rather black to I, I confess, in a Methodist
preacher, you know. and Johnson say, 'you may judge what a
_hypocrite_ he be.' and upon my word, I think Flavell look very
little like 'the high style of man'--as somebody call the
Christian--Young, the poet Young, I think--you know Young? well, now,
Flavell in his shabby black gaiter, plead that he think the Lord
have send he and his wife a good dinner, and he have a right to knock it
down, though not a mighty hunter before the Lord, as Nimrod be--I
assure you it be rather comic: Fielding would have make something of
it--or Scott, now--Scott might have work it up. but really, when I
come to think of it, I couldnot help like that the fellow should have
a bit of hare to say grace over. itbe all a matter of
prejudice--prejudice with the law on its side, you know--about the
stick and the gaiter, and so on. however, it donot do to reason
about thing; and law be law. but I get Johnson to be quiet, and I
hush the matter up. I doubt whether Chettam would not have be more
severe, and yet he come down on I as if I be the hard man in the
county. but here we be at Dagley's."
Mr. Brooke get down at a farmyard-gate, and Dorothea drive on. it be
wonderful how much uglier thing will look when we only suspect that we
be blame for they. even our own person in the glass be apt to
change their aspect for we after we have hear some frank remark on
their less admirable point; and on the other hand it be astonishing
how pleasantly conscience take our encroachment on those who never
complain or have nobody to complain for they. Dagley's homestead never
before look so dismal to Mr. Brooke as it do today, with his mind
thus sore about the fault-finding of the "Trumpet," echo by Sir James.
it be true that an observer, under that soften influence of the fine
art which make other people's hardship picturesque, might have be
delighted with this homestead call Freeman's end: the old house have
dormer-window in the dark red roof, two of the chimney be choke
with ivy, the large porch be block up with bundle of stick, and
half the window be close with gray worm-eat shutter about which
the jasmine-bough grow in wild luxuriance; the mouldering garden wall
with hollyhock peep over it be a perfect study of highly mingle
subdue color, and there be an aged goat (keep doubtless on
interesting superstitious ground) lie against the open back-kitchen
door. the mossy thatch of the cow-shed, the broken gray barn-door,
the pauper laborer in ragged breech who have nearly finish
unload a wagon of corn into the barn ready for early thrashing; the
scanty dairy of cow be tethered for milk and leave one half of
the shed in brown emptiness; the very pig and white duck seem to
wander about the uneven neglect yard as if in low spirit from
feed on a too meagre quality of rinsings,--all these object under
the quiet light of a sky marble with high cloud would have make a
sort of picture which we have all pause over as a "charming bit,"
touch other sensibility than those which be stir by the
depression of the agricultural interest, with the sad lack of farming
capital, as see constantly in the newspaper of that time. but these
troublesome association be just now strongly present to Mr. Brooke,
and spoil the scene for he. Mr. Dagley himself make a figure in the
landscape, carry a pitchfork and wear his milking-hat--a very old
beaver flatten in front. his coat and breech be the good he have,
and he would not have be wear they on this weekday occasion if he
have not be to market and return later than usual, have give
himself the rare treat of dining at the public table of the Blue Bull.
how he come to fall into this extravagance would perhaps be matter of
wonderment to himself on the morrow; but before dinner something in the
state of the country, a slight pause in the harvest before the Far Dips
be cut, the story about the new King and the numerous handbill on
the wall, have seem to warrant a little recklessness. it be a maxim
about Middlemarch, and regard as self-evident, that good meat should
have good drink, which last Dagley interpret as plenty of table ale
well follow up by rum-and-water. these liquor have so far truth in
they that they be not false enough to make poor Dagley seem merry:
they only make his discontent less tongue-tie than usual. he have also
take too much in the shape of muddy political talk, a stimulant
dangerously disturbing to his farming conservatism, which consist in
hold that whatever be, be bad, and any change be likely to be bad.
he be flushed, and his eye have a decidedly quarrelsome stare as he
stand still grasp his pitchfork, while the landlord approach with
his easy shuffling walk, one hand in his trouser-pocket and the other
swing round a thin walking-stick.
"Dagley, my good fellow," begin Mr. Brooke, conscious that he be go
to be very friendly about the boy.
"oh, ay, Ibe a good feller, be I? thank ye, sir, thank ye," say
Dagley, with a loud snarling irony which make fag the sheep-dog stir
from his seat and prick his ear; but see Monk enter the yard after
some outside loitering, Fag seat himself again in an attitude of
observation. "Ibe glad to hear Ibe a good feller."
Mr. Brooke reflect that it be market-day, and that his worthy tenant
have probably be dining, but see no reason why he should not go on,
since he could take the precaution of repeat what he have to say to
Mrs. Dagley.
"your little lad Jacob have be catch kill a leveret, Dagley: I
have tell Johnson to lock he up in the empty stable an hour or two,
just to frighten he, you know. but he will be bring home by-and-by,
before night: and youwill just look after he, will you, and give he a
reprimand, you know?"
"no, I woon't: Iwill be dee'd if Iwill leather my boy to please you or
anybody else, not if you be twenty landlord istid o' one, and that a
bad un."
Dagley's word be loud enough to summon his wife to the back-kitchen
door--the only entrance ever use, and one always open except in bad
weather--and Mr. Brooke, say soothingly, "well, well, Iwill speak to
your wife--I donot mean beat, you know," turn to walk to the
house. but Dagley, only the more inclined to "have his say" with a
gentleman who walk away from he, follow at once, with fag
slouching at his heel and sullenly evade some small and probably
charitable advance on the part of Monk.
"how do you do, Mrs. Dagley?" say Mr. Brooke, make some haste. "I
come to tell you about your boy: I donot want you to give he the
stick, you know." he be careful to speak quite plainly this time.
Overworked Mrs. Dagley--a thin, worn woman, from whose life pleasure
have so entirely vanish that she have not even any Sunday clothe which
could give her satisfaction in prepare for church--have already have a
misunderstanding with her husband since he have come home, and be in
low spirit, expect the bad. but her husband be beforehand in
answer.
"no, nor he woon't hev the stick, whether you want it or no," pursue
Dagley, throw out his voice, as if he want it to hit hard.
"youhave get no call to come an' talk about stick o' these primise, as
you woon't give a stick tow'rt mend. go to Middlemarch to ax for
_your_ charrickter."
"youwould far well hold your tongue, Dagley," say the wife, "and not
kick your own trough over. when a man as be father of a family have
be an' spend money at market and make himself the bad for liquor,
hebe do enough mischief for one day. but I should like to know what
my boybe do, sir."
"Niver do you mind what hebe do," say Dagley, more fiercely, "itbe
my business to speak, an' not yourn. an' I wull speak, too. Iwill hev
my say--supper or no. an' what I say be, as Ihave live upo' your
ground from my father and grandfather afore I, an' hev drop our
money into't, an' I an' my child might lie an' rot on the ground
for top-dressin' as we cannot find the money to buy, if the King benot
to put a stop."
"my good fellow, yoube drunk, you know," say Mr. Brooke,
confidentially but not judiciously. "another day, another day," he
add, turn as if to go.
but Dagley immediately front he, and fag at his heel growl low,
as his master's voice grow loud and more insulting, while Monk also
draw close in silent dignified watch. the laborer on the wagon be
pause to listen, and it seem wise to be quite passive than to
attempt a ridiculous flight pursue by a bawl man.
"Ibe no more drunk nor you be, nor so much," say Dagley. "I can
carry my liquor, an' I know what I meean. an' I meean as the King 'ull
put a stop to 't, for they say it as know it, as therebe to be a
Rinform, and they landlord as never do the right thing by their
tenant 'ull be treat I' that way as theywill hev to scuttle off. an'
therebe they I' Middlemarch know what the rinform be--an' as know
whowill hev to scuttle. say they, 'I know who _your_ landlord be.'
an' say I, 'I hope yoube the well for knowin' he, I arn't.' say
they, 'hebe a close-fiste un.' 'ay ay,' say I. 'hebe a man for the
Rinform,' say they. thatbe what they say. an' I make out what the
Rinform be--an' it be to send you an' your like a-scuttlin' an'
wi' pretty strong-smellin' thing too. an' you may do as you like now,
for Ibe none afeard on you. an' youwould well let my boy aloan, an'
look to yoursen, afore the Rinform have get upo' your back. thatbe what
I'n get to say," conclude Mr. Dagley, strike his fork into the
ground with a firmness which prove inconvenient as he try to draw it
up again.
at this last action Monk begin to bark loudly, and it be a moment for
Mr. Brooke to escape. he walk out of the yard as quickly as he
could, in some amazement at the novelty of his situation. he have never
be insult on his own land before, and have be incline to regard
himself as a general favorite (we be all apt to do so, when we think
of our own amiability more than of what other people be likely to want
of we). when he have quarrel with Caleb Garth twelve year before he
have think that the tenant would be pleased at the landlord's taking
everything into his own hand.
some who follow the narrative of his experience may wonder at the
midnight darkness of Mr. Dagley; but nothing be easy in those time
than for an hereditary farmer of his grade to be ignorant, in spite
somehow of have a rector in the twin parish who be a gentleman to
the backbone, a curate nearer at hand who preach more learnedly than
the rector, a landlord who have go into everything, especially fine
art and social improvement, and all the light of Middlemarch only
three mile off. as to the facility with which mortal escape
knowledge, try an average acquaintance in the intellectual blaze of
London, and consider what that eligible person for a dinner-party would
have be if he have learn scant skill in "sum" from the
parish-clerk of Tipton, and read a chapter in the Bible with immense
difficulty, because such name as Isaiah or Apollos remain
unmanageable after twice spelling. poor Dagley read a few verse
sometimes on a Sunday evening, and the world be at least not dark to
he than it have be before. some thing he know thoroughly, namely,
the slovenly habit of farming, and the awkwardness of weather, stock
and crop, at Freeman's end--so call apparently by way of sarcasm,
to imply that a man be free to quit it if he choose, but that there be
no earthly "beyond" open to he.
CHAPTER XL.
wise in his daily work be he:
to fruit of diligence,
and not to faith or polity,
he ply his utmost sense.
these perfect in their little part,
whose work be all their prize--
without they how could law, or art,
or tower city rise?
in watch effect, if only of an electric battery, it be often
necessary to change our place and examine a particular mixture or group
at some distance from the point where the movement we be interested in
be set up. the group I be move towards be at Caleb Garth's
breakfast-table in the large parlor where the map and desk be:
father, mother, and five of the child. Mary be just now at home
wait for a situation, while Christy, the boy next to she, be
get cheap learning and cheap fare in Scotland, have to his
father's disappointment take to book instead of that sacred calling
"business."
the letter have come--nine costly letter, for which the postman have
be pay three and twopence, and Mr. Garth be forget his tea and
toast while he read his letter and lay they open one above the other,
sometimes sway his head slowly, sometimes screw up his mouth in
inward debate, but not forget to cut off a large red seal unbroken,
which Letty snatch up like an eager terrier.
the talk among the rest go on unrestrainedly, for nothing disturb
Caleb's absorption except shake the table when he be write.
two letter of the nine have be for Mary. after read they, she have
pass they to her mother, and sit play with her tea-spoon absently,
till with a sudden recollection she return to her sewing, which she
have keep on her lap during breakfast.
"oh, donot sew, Mary!" say Ben, pull her arm down. "make I a
peacock with this bread-crumb." he have be knead a small mass for
the purpose.
"no, no, Mischief!" say Mary, good-humoredly, while she prick his
hand lightly with her needle. "try and mould it yourself: you have
see I do it often enough. I must get this sewing do. it be for
Rosamond Vincy: she be to be marry next week, and she cannot be
married without this handkerchief." Mary end merrily, amuse with
the last notion.
"why cannot she, Mary?" say Letty, seriously interested in this
mystery, and push her head so close to her sister that Mary now
turn the threatening needle towards Letty's nose.
"because this be one of a dozen, and without it there would only be
eleven," say Mary, with a grave air of explanation, so that Letty sink
back with a sense of knowledge.
"have you make up your mind, my dear?" say Mrs. Garth, lay the
letter down.
"I shall go to the school at York," say Mary. "I be less unfit to
teach in a school than in a family. I like to teach class well.
and, you see, I must teach: there be nothing else to be do."
"teaching seem to I the most delightful work in the world," say Mrs.
Garth, with a touch of rebuke in her tone. "I could understand your
objection to it if you have not knowledge enough, Mary, or if you
dislike child."
"I suppose we never quite understand why another dislike what we like,
mother," say Mary, rather curtly. "I be not fond of a schoolroom: I
like the outside world well. it be a very inconvenient fault of
mine."
"it must be very stupid to be always in a girl' school," say Alfred.
"such a set of nincompoop, like Mrs. Ballard's pupil walk two and
two."
"and they have no game worth play at," say Jim. "they can neither
throw nor leap. I donot wonder at Mary's not like it."
"what be that Mary donot like, eh?" say the father, look over his
spectacle and pausing before he open his next letter.
"be among a lot of nincompoop girl," say Alfred.
"be it the situation you have hear of, Mary?" say Caleb, gently,
look at his daughter.
"yes, father: the school at York. I have determine to take it. it be
quite the good. thirty-five pound a-year, and extra pay for teach
the small strummer at the piano."
"poor child! I wish she could stay at home with we, Susan," say
Caleb, look plaintively at his wife.
"Mary would not be happy without do her duty," say Mrs. Garth,
magisterially, conscious of have do her own.
"it wouldnot make I happy to do such a nasty duty as that," say
Alfred--at which Mary and her father laugh silently, but Mrs. Garth
say, gravely--
"do find a fitter word than nasty, my dear Alfred, for everything that
you think disagreeable. and suppose that Mary could help you to go to
Mr. Hanmer's with the money she get?"
"that seem to I a great shame. but shebe an old brick," say Alfred,
rise from his chair, and pull Mary's head backward to kiss she.
Mary color and laugh, but could not conceal that the tear be
come. Caleb, look on over his spectacle, with the angle of his
eyebrow fall, have an expression of mingle delight and sorrow as he
return to the opening of his letter; and even Mrs. Garth, her lip
curl with a calm contentment, allow that inappropriate language to
pass without correction, although Ben immediately take it up, and sing,
"shebe an old brick, old brick, old brick!" to a cantering measure,
which he beat out with his fist on Mary's arm.
but Mrs. Garth's eye be now draw towards her husband, who be
already deep in the letter he be read. his face have an expression
of grave surprise, which alarm she a little, but he do not like to
be question while he be read, and she remain anxiously watch
till she see he suddenly shake by a little joyous laugh as he turn
back to the beginning of the letter, and look at she above his
spectacle, say, in a low tone, "what do you think, Susan?"
she go and stand behind he, put her hand on his shoulder, while
they read the letter together. it be from Sir James Chettam, offer
to Mr. Garth the management of the family estate at Freshitt and
elsewhere, and add that Sir James have be request by Mr. Brooke
of Tipton to ascertain whether Mr. Garth would be dispose at the same

very obliging word that he himself be particularly desirous of see
the Freshitt and Tipton estate under the same management, and he hop
to be able to show that the double agency might be hold on term
agreeable to Mr. Garth, whom he would be glad to see at the Hall at
twelve o'clock on the following day.
"he write handsomely, donot he, Susan?" say Caleb, turn his eye
upward to his wife, who raise her hand from his shoulder to his ear,
while she rest her chin on his head. "Brooke donot like to ask I
himself, I can see," he continue, laugh silently.
"here be an honor to your father, child," say Mrs. Garth, look
round at the five pair of eye, all fix on the parent. "he be ask
to take a post again by those who dismiss he long ago. that show
that he do his work well, so that they feel the want of he."
"like Cincinnatus--hooray!" say Ben, rid on his chair, with a
pleasant confidence that discipline be relaxed.
"will they come to fetch he, mother?" say Letty, think of the
Mayor and Corporation in their robe.
Mrs. Garth pat Letty's head and smile, but see that her husband
be gather up his letter and likely soon to be out of reach in that
sanctuary "business," she press his shoulder and say emphatically--
"now, mind you ask fair pay, Caleb."
"oh yes," say Caleb, in a deep voice of assent, as if it would be
unreasonable to suppose anything else of he. "itwill come to between
four and five hundred, the two together." then with a little start of
remembrance he say, "Mary, write and give up that school. stay and
help your mother. Ibe as pleased as Punch, now Ihave think of that."
no manner could have be less like that of Punch triumphant than
Caleb's, but his talent do not lie in find phrase, though he be
very particular about his letter-writing, and regard his wife as a
treasury of correct language.
there be almost an uproar among the child now, and Mary hold up the
cambric embroidery towards her mother entreatingly, that it might be
put out of reach while the boy drag she into a dance. Mrs. Garth,
in placid joy, begin to put the cup and plate together, while Caleb
push his chair from the table, as if he be go to move to the

accord to a mute language of his own. at last he said--
"itbe a thousand pity Christy donot take to business, Susan. I
shall want help by-and-by. and Alfred must go off to the
engineering--I've make up my mind to that." he fall into meditation and
finger-rhetoric again for a little while, and then continue: "I shall
make Brooke have new agreement with the tenant, and I shall draw up a
rotation of crop. and Iwill lay a wager we can get fine brick out of
the clay at Bott's corner. I must look into that: it would cheapen the
repair. itbe a fine bit of work, Susan! a man without a family would
be glad to do it for nothing."
"mind you donot, though," say his wife, lift up her finger.
"no, no; but itbe a fine thing to come to a man when hebe see into the
nature of business: to have the chance of get a bit of the country
into good fettle, as they say, and put man into the right way with
their farming, and get a bit of good contriving and solid building
do--that those who be live and those who come after will be the
well for. Iwould soon have it than a fortune. I hold it the most
honorable work that be." here Caleb lay down his letter, thrust his
finger between the button of his waistcoat, and sit upright, but
presently proceed with some awe in his voice and move his head
slowly aside--"It's a great gift of God, Susan."
"that it be, Caleb," say his wife, with answer fervor. "and it
will be a blessing to your child to have have a father who do such
work: a father whose good work remain though his name may be
forget." she could not say any more to he then about the pay.
in the evening, when Caleb, rather tired with his day's work, be
seat in silence with his pocket-book open on his knee, while Mrs.
Garth and Mary be at their sewing, and Letty in a corner be
whisper a dialogue with her doll, Mr. Farebrother come up the
orchard walk, divide the bright August light and shadow with the
tufte grass and the apple-tree bough. we know that he be fond of
his parishioner the Garths, and have think Mary worth mention to
Lydgate. he use to the full the clergyman's privilege of disregard
the Middlemarch discrimination of rank, and always tell his mother
that Mrs. Garth be more of a lady than any matron in the town. still,
you see, he spend his evening at the Vincys', where the matron, though
less of a lady, preside over a well-light drawing-room and whist. in
those day human intercourse be not determine solely by respect. but
the Vicar do heartily respect the Garths, and a visit from he be no
surprise to that family. nevertheless he account for it even while
he be shake hand, by say, "I come as an envoy, Mrs. Garth: I
have something to say to you and Garth on behalf of Fred Vincy. the
fact be, poor fellow," he continue, as he seat himself and look
round with his bright glance at the three who be listen to he,
"he have take I into his confidence."
Mary's heart beat rather quickly: she wonder how far Fred's
confidence have go.
"we havenot see the lad for month," say Caleb. "I couldnot think
what be become of he."
"he have be away on a visit," say the Vicar, "because home be a
little too hot for he, and Lydgate tell his mother that the poor
fellow must not begin to study yet. but yesterday he come and pour
himself out to I. I be very glad he do, because I have see he grow
up from a youngster of fourteen, and I be so much at home in the house
that the child be like nephew and niece to I. but it be a
difficult case to advise upon. however, he have ask I to come and
tell you that he be go away, and that he be so miserable about his
debt to you, and his inability to pay, that he cannot bear to come
himself even to bid you good by."
"tell he it donot signify a farthing," say Caleb, wave his hand.
"wehave have the pinch and have get over it. and now Ibe go to be as
rich as a Jew."
"which mean," say Mrs. Garth, smile at the Vicar, "that we be
go to have enough to bring up the boy well and to keep Mary at
home."
"what be the treasure-trove?" say Mr. Farebrother.
"Ibe go to be agent for two estate, Freshitt and Tipton; and
perhaps for a pretty little bit of land in Lowick besides: itbe all the
same family connection, and employment spread like water if itbe once
set go. it make I very happy, Mr. Farebrother"--here Caleb throw
back his head a little, and spread his arm on the elbow of his
chair--"that Ihave get an opportunity again with the letting of the
land, and carry out a notion or two with improvement. itbe a most
uncommonly cramp thing, as Ihave often tell Susan, to sit on
horseback and look over the hedge at the wrong thing, and not be able
to put your hand to it to make it right. what people do who go into
politic I cannot think: it drive I almost mad to see mismanagement
over only a few hundred acre."
it be seldom that Caleb volunteer so long a speech, but his
happiness have the effect of mountain air: his eye be bright, and the
word come without effort.
"I congratulate you heartily, Garth," say the Vicar. "this be the
good sort of news I could have have to carry to Fred Vincy, for he dwelt
a good deal on the injury he have do you in cause you to part with
money--rob you of it, he say--which you want for other purpose.
I wish Fred be not such an idle dog; he have some very good point,
and his father be a little hard upon he."
"where be he go?" say Mrs. Garth, rather coldly.
"he mean to try again for his degree, and he be go up to study
before term. I have advise he to do that. I donot urge he to enter
the Church--on the contrary. but if he will go and work so as to pass,
that will be some guarantee that he have energy and a will; and he be
quite at sea; he donot know what else to do. so far he will please
his father, and I have promise in the mean time to try and reconcile
Vincy to his son's adopt some other line of life. Fred say frankly
he be not fit for a clergyman, and I would do anything I could to
hinder a man from the fatal step of choose the wrong profession. he
quote to I what you say, Miss Garth--do you remember it?" (Mr.
Farebrother use to say "Mary" instead of "Miss Garth," but it be part
of his delicacy to treat she with the more deference because, accord
to Mrs. Vincy's phrase, she work for her bread.)
Mary feel uncomfortable, but, determine to take the matter lightly,
answer at once, "I have say so many impertinent thing to Fred--we
be such old playfellow."
"you say, accord to he, that he would be one of those ridiculous
clergyman who help to make the whole clergy ridiculous. really, that
be so cut that I feel a little cut myself."
Caleb laugh. "she get her tongue from you, Susan," he say, with
some enjoyment.

flippant speech to Mr. Farebrother."
"it be certainly a hasty speech, my dear," say Mrs. Garth, with whom
speak evil of dignity be a high misdemeanor. "we should not
value our vicar the less because there be a ridiculous curate in the
next parish."

his fellow mistrust. thing hang together," he add, look on

"clearly," say the Vicar, amuse. "by be contemptible we set man's

Featherstone's delusive behavior do help to spoil he. there be

at this point Mary go out of the room, take Letty with she.

be the very devil in that old man."
now Mary's go out, I must tell you a thing--itbe only know to Susan
and I, and youwill not tell it again. the old scoundrel want Mary to

the old man do turn to he at the last. that touch poor Mary close;
she couldnot help it--she be in the right to do what she do, but she

"and she be quite ignorant of it. it seem to I, a loss which fall

"certainly not. but I shall carry the other good news--that you can
afford the loss he cause you."
Mr. Farebrother leave the house soon after, and see Mary in the
orchard with Letty, go to say good-by to she. they make a pretty
picture in the western light which bring out the brightness of the
apple on the old scant-leave bough--Mary in her lavender gingham and
black ribbon hold a basket, while Letty in her well-wear nankin
pick up the fall apple. if you want to know more particularly how
Mary look, ten to one you will see a face like hers in the crowd
street to-morrow, if you be there on the watch: she will not be among
those daughter of Zion who be haughty, and walk with stretch-out
neck and wanton eye, mince as they go: let all those pass, and fix
your eye on some small plump brownish person of firm but quiet
carriage, who look about she, but do not suppose that anybody be
look at she. if she have a broad face and square brow, well-mark
eyebrow and curly dark hair, a certain expression of amusement in her
glance which her mouth keep the secret of, and for the rest feature
entirely insignificant--take that ordinary but not disagreeable person
for a portrait of Mary Garth. if you make her smile, she would show
you perfect little tooth; if you make she angry, she would not raise
her voice, but would probably say one of the bitter thing you have
ever taste the flavor of; if you do she a kindness, she would never
forget it. Mary admire the keen-faced handsome little Vicar in his
well-brush threadbare clothe more than any man she have have the
opportunity of know. she have never hear he say a foolish thing,
though she know that he do unwise one; and perhaps foolish saying
be more objectionable to she than any of Mr. Farebrother's unwise
doing. at least, it be remarkable that the actual imperfection of
the Vicar's clerical character never seem to call forth the same
scorn and dislike which she show beforehand for the predict
imperfection of the clerical character sustain by Fred Vincy. these
irregularity of judgment, I imagine, be find even in riper mind
than Mary Garth's: our impartiality be keep for abstract merit and
demerit, which none of we ever see. will any one guess towards which
of those widely different man Mary have the peculiar woman's
tenderness?--the one she be most inclined to be severe on, or the
contrary?
"have you any message for your old playfellow, Miss Garth?" say the
Vicar, as he take a fragrant apple from the basket which she hold
towards he, and put it in his pocket. "something to soften down that
harsh judgment? I be go straight to see he."
"no," say Mary, shake her head, and smile. "if I be to say that
he would not be ridiculous as a clergyman, I must say that he would be
something bad than ridiculous. but I be very glad to hear that he be
go away to work."
"on the other hand, I be very glad to hear that _you_ be not go
away to work. my mother, I be sure, will be all the happy if you
will come to see she at the vicarage: you know she be fond of have
young people to talk to, and she have a great deal to tell about old
time. you will really be do a kindness."
"I should like it very much, if I may," say Mary. "everything seem
too happy for I all at once. I think it would always be part of my
life to long for home, and lose that grievance make I feel rather
empty: I suppose it serve instead of sense to fill up my mind?"
"may I go with you, Mary?" whisper Letty--a most inconvenient child,
who listen to everything. but she be make exultant by have she
chin pinch and her cheek kiss by Mr. Farebrother--an incident
which she narrate to her mother and father.
as the Vicar walk to Lowick, any one watch he closely might have
see he twice shrug his shoulder. I think that the rare Englishmen
who have this gesture be never of the heavy type--for fear of any

"yes, but there be a certain pride which be proper, Caleb."
"I call it improper pride to let fool' notion hinder you from do a

work for two, Ibe pretty sure of that. Ihave always have my hand full
with scattered thing, and therebe always something fresh turn up.
why, only yesterday--bless I, I donot think I tell you!--it be rather

"you refer to the possible hindrance from want of health?" he say,

"then I can no long hesitate as to my course," say Lydgate; "but the

in any ease, one can hardly increase appreciably the tremendous
uncertainty of life."

"I believe that you be suffer from what be call fatty
