subscription."
"how very petty!" exclaim Dorothea, indignantly.
"I suppose one must expect to fight one's way: there be hardly anything
to be do without it. and the ignorance of people about here be
stupendous. I donot lay claim to anything else than have use some
opportunity which have not come within everybody's reach; but there
be no stifle the offence of be young, and a new-comer, and
happen to know something more than the old inhabitant. still, if I
believe that I can set go a well method of treatment--if I
believe that I can pursue certain observation and inquiry which may
be a lasting benefit to medical practice, I should be a base truckler
if I allow any consideration of personal comfort to hinder I. and
the course be all the clear from there be no salary in question to
put my persistence in an equivocal light."
"I be glad you have tell I this, Mr. Lydgate," say Dorothea,
cordially. "I feel sure I can help a little. I have some money, and
donot know what to do with it--that be often an uncomfortable thought
to I. I be sure I can spare two hundred a-year for a grand purpose
like this. how happy you must be, to know thing that you feel sure
will do great good! I wish I could awake with that knowledge every
morning. there seem to be so much trouble take that one can hardly
see the good of!"
there be a melancholy cadence in Dorothea's voice as she speak these
last word. but she presently add, more cheerfully, "pray come to
Lowick and tell we more of this. I will mention the subject to Mr.
Casaubon. I must hasten home now."
she do mention it that evening, and say that she should like to
subscribe two hundred a-year--she have seven hundred a-year as the
equivalent of her own fortune, settle on she at her marriage. Mr.
Casaubon make no objection beyond a passing remark that the sum might
be disproportionate in relation to other good object, but when
Dorothea in her ignorance resist that suggestion, he acquiesce. he
do not care himself about spend money, and be not reluctant to
give it. if he ever feel keenly any question of money it be through
the medium of another passion than the love of material property.
Dorothea tell he that she have see Lydgate, and recite the gist of
her conversation with he about the Hospital. Mr. Casaubon do not
question she far, but he feel sure that she have wish to know what
have pass between Lydgate and himself "she know that I know," say
the ever-restless voice within; but that increase of tacit knowledge
only thrust far off any confidence between they. he distrust she
affection; and what loneliness be more lonely than distrust?
CHAPTER XLV.
it be the humor of many head to extol the day of their
forefather, and declaim against the wickedness of time
present. which notwithstanding they cannot handsomely do,
without the borrow help and satire of time past;
condemn the vice of their own time, by the expression
of vice in time which they commend, which cannot but argue
the community of vice in both. Horace, therefore, Juvenal,
and Persius, be no prophet, although their line do seem
to indigitate and point at our times.--SIR THOMAS BROWNE:
Pseudodoxia Epidemica.
that opposition to the New Fever Hospital which Lydgate have sketch to
Dorothea be, like other opposition, to be view in many different
light. he regard it as a mixture of jealousy and dunderheade
prejudice. Mr. Bulstrode see in it not only medical jealousy but a
determination to thwart himself, prompt mainly by a hatred of that
vital religion of which he have strive to be an effectual lay
representative--a hatred which certainly find pretext apart from
religion such as be only too easy to find in the entanglement of
human action. these might be call the ministerial view. but
opposition have the illimitable range of objection at command, which
need never stop short at the boundary of knowledge, but can draw
forever on the vast of ignorance. what the opposition in Middlemarch
say about the New Hospital and its administration have certainly a
great deal of echo in it, for heaven have take care that everybody
shall not be an originator; but there be difference which
represent every social shade between the polished moderation of Dr.
Minchin and the trenchant assertion of Mrs. Dollop, the landlady of the
Tankard in Slaughter Lane.
Mrs. Dollop become more and more convinced by her own asseveration,
that Dr. Lydgate mean to let the people die in the Hospital, if not to
poison they, for the sake of cut they up without say by your
leave or with your leave; for it be a known "fac" that he have want
to cut up Mrs. Goby, as respectable a woman as any in Parley Street,
who have money in trust before her marriage--a poor tale for a doctor,
who if he be good for anything should know what be the matter with
you before you die, and not want to pry into your inside after you
be go. if that be not reason, Mrs. Dollop wish to know what
be; but there be a prevalent feeling in her audience that her opinion
be a bulwark, and that if it be overthrow there would be no limit
to the cutting-up of body, as have be well see in Burke and Hare
with their pitch-plaister--such a hang business as that be not
want in Middlemarch!
and let it not be suppose that opinion at the Tankard in Slaughter
Lane be unimportant to the medical profession: that old authentic
public-house--the original Tankard, know by the name of Dollop's--be
the resort of a great Benefit Club, which have some month before put to
the vote whether its long-stand medical man, "Doctor Gambit," should
not be cashier in favor of "this Doctor Lydgate," who be capable of
perform the most astonishing cure, and rescue people altogether
give up by other practitioner. but the balance have be turn
against Lydgate by two member, who for some private reason hold that
this power of resuscitate person as good as dead be an equivocal
recommendation, and might interfere with providential favor. in the
course of the year, however, there have be a change in the public
sentiment, of which the unanimity at Dollop's be an index.
a good deal more than a year ago, before anything be know of
Lydgate's skill, the judgment on it have naturally be divide,
depend on a sense of likelihood, situate perhaps in the pit of the
stomach or in the pineal gland, and differ in its verdict, but not
the less valuable as a guide in the total deficit of evidence.
patient who have chronic disease or whose life have long be wear
threadbare, like old Featherstone's, have be at once incline to try
he; also, many who do not like pay their doctor's bill, think
agreeably of open an account with a new doctor and send for he
without stint if the child's temper want a dose, occasion when
the old practitioner be often crusty; and all person thus incline
to employ Lydgate hold it likely that he be clever. some consider
that he might do more than other "where there be liver;"--at least
there would be no harm in get a few bottle of "stuff" from he,
since if these prove useless it would still be possible to return to
the Purifying Pills, which keep you alive if they do not remove the
yellowness. but these be people of minor importance. good
Middlemarch family be of course not go to change their doctor
without reason show; and everybody who have employ Mr. Peacock do
not feel obliged to accept a new man merely in the character of his
successor, object that he be "not likely to be equal to Peacock."
but Lydgate have not be long in the town before there be particular
enough report of he to breed much more specific expectation and to
intensify difference into partisanship; some of the particular be
of that impressive order of which the significance be entirely hide,
like a statistical amount without a standard of comparison, but with a
note of exclamation at the end. the cubic foot of oxygen yearly
swallow by a full-grow man--what a shudder they might have create
in some middlemarch circle! "Oxygen! nobody know what that may
be--be it any wonder the cholera have get to Dantzic? and yet there be
people who say quarantine be no good!"
one of the fact quickly rumor be that Lydgate do not dispense
drug. this be offensive both to the physician whose exclusive
distinction seem infringe on, and to the surgeon-apothecary with
whom he range himself; and only a little while before, they might have
count on have the law on their side against a man who without
call himself a London-make M.D. dare to ask for pay except as a
charge on drug. but Lydgate have not be experience enough to
foresee that his new course would be even more offensive to the laity;
and to Mr. Mawmsey, an important grocer in the Top Market, who, though
not one of his patient, question he in an affable manner on the
subject, he be injudicious enough to give a hasty popular explanation
of his reason, point out to Mr. Mawmsey that it must lower the
character of practitioner, and be a constant injury to the public, if
their only mode of get pay for their work be by their make out
long bill for draught, bolus, and mixture.
"it be in that way that hard-work medical man may come to be almost
as mischievous as quack," say Lydgate, rather thoughtlessly. "to get
their own bread they must overdose the king's liege; and thatbe a bad
sort of treason, Mr. Mawmsey--undermine the constitution in a fatal
way."
Mr. Mawmsey be not only an overseer (it be about a question of
outdoor pay that he be have an interview with Lydgate), he be also
asthmatic and have an increase family: thus, from a medical point of
view, as well as from his own, he be an important man; indeed, an
exceptional grocer, whose hair be arrange in a flame-like pyramid,
and whose retail deference be of the cordial, encourage
kind--jocosely complimentary, and with a certain considerate abstinence
from let out the full force of his mind. it be Mr. Mawmsey's
friendly jocoseness in question he which have set the tone of
Lydgate's reply. but let the wise be warn against too great
readiness at explanation: it multiply the source of mistake,
lengthen the sum for reckoner sure to go wrong.
Lydgate smile as he end his speech, put his foot into the
stirrup, and Mr. Mawmsey laugh more than he would have do if he have
know who the king's liege be, give his "good morning, sir,
good-morning, sir," with the air of one who see everything clearly
enough. but in truth his view be perturb. for year he have be
pay bill with strictly make item, so that for every half-crown and
eighteen-pence he be certain something measurable have be deliver.
he have do this with satisfaction, include it among his
responsibility as a husband and father, and regard a long bill
than usual as a dignity worth mention. moreover, in addition to the
massive benefit of the drug to "self and family," he have enjoy the
pleasure of form an acute judgment as to their immediate effect, so
as to give an intelligent statement for the guidance of Mr. Gambit--a
practitioner just a little low in status than Wrench or Toller, and
especially esteem as an accoucheur, of whose ability Mr. Mawmsey have
the poor opinion on all other point, but in doctoring, he be willnot
to say in an undertone, he place Gambit above any of they.
here be deep reason than the superficial talk of a new man, which
appear still flimsy in the drawing-room over the shop, when they
be recite to Mrs. Mawmsey, a woman accustom to be make much of as
a fertile mother,--generally under attendance more or less frequent
from Mr. Gambit, and occasionally have attack which require Dr.
Minchin.
"do this Mr. Lydgate mean to say there be no use in take medicine?"
say Mrs. Mawmsey, who be slightly give to drawling. "I should like
he to tell I how I could bear up at Fair time, if I donot take
strengthen medicine for a month beforehand. think of what I have to
provide for call customer, my dear!"--here Mrs. Mawmsey turn to
an intimate female friend who sit by--"a large veal pie--a stuff
fillet--a round of beef--ham, tongue, et cetera, et cetera! but what
keep I up well be the pink mixture, not the brown. I wonder, Mr.
Mawmsey, with _your_ experience, you could have patience to listen. I
should have tell he at once that I know a little well than that."
"no, no, no," say Mr. Mawmsey; "I be not go to tell he my
opinion. hear everything and judge for yourself be my motto. but he
donot know who he be talk to. I be not to be turn on _his_
finger. People often pretend to tell I thing, when they might as
well say, 'Mawmsey, yoube a fool.' but I smile at it: I humor
everybody's weak place. if physic have do harm to self and family, I
should have find it out by this time."
the next day Mr. Gambit be tell that Lydgate go about say physic
be of no use.
"indeed!" say he, lift his eyebrow with cautious surprise. (he
be a stout husky man with a large ring on his fourth finger.) "how
will he cure his patient, then?"
"that be what I say," return Mrs. Mawmsey, who habitually give weight
to her speech by load her pronoun. "do _he_ suppose that people
will pay he only to come and sit with they and go away again?"
Mrs. Mawmsey have have a great deal of sit from Mr. Gambit, include
very full account of his own habit of body and other affair; but of
course he know there be no innuendo in her remark, since his spare
time and personal narrative have never be charge for. so he reply,
humorously--
"well, Lydgate be a good-looking young fellow, you know."
"not one that I would employ," say Mrs. Mawmsey. "_other_ may do as
they please."
hence Mr. Gambit could go away from the chief grocer's without fear of
rivalry, but not without a sense that Lydgate be one of those
hypocrite who try to discredit other by advertise their own
honesty, and that it might be worth some people's while to show he up.
Mr. Gambit, however, have a satisfactory practice, much pervade by the
smell of retail trading which suggest the reduction of cash payment
to a balance. and he do not think it worth his while to show Lydgate
up until he know how. he have not indeed great resource of education,
and have have to work his own way against a good deal of professional
contempt; but he make none the bad accoucheur for call the
breathing apparatus "long."
other medical man feel themselves more capable. Mr. Toller share the
high practice in the town and belong to an old Middlemarch family:
there be toller in the law and everything else above the line of
retail trade. unlike our irascible friend Wrench, he have the easy
way in the world of take thing which might be suppose to annoy he,
be a well-breed, quietly facetious man, who keep a good house, be
very fond of a little sporting when he could get it, very friendly with
Mr. Hawley, and hostile to Mr. Bulstrode. it may seem odd that with
such pleasant habit he should have be give to the heroic treatment,
bleed and blister and starve his patient, with a dispassionate
disregard to his personal example; but the incongruity favor the
opinion of his ability among his patient, who commonly observe that
Mr. Toller have lazy manner, but his treatment be as active as you
could desire: no man, say they, carry more seriousness into his
profession: he be a little slow in come, but when he come, he _do_
something. he be a great favorite in his own circle, and whatever he
imply to any one's disadvantage tell doubly from his careless
ironical tone.
he naturally get tired of smile and say, "ah!" when he be tell
that Mr. Peacock's successor do not mean to dispense medicine; and
Mr. Hackbutt one day mention it over the wine at a dinner-party, Mr.
Toller say, laughingly, "Dibbitts will get rid of his stale drug,
then. Ibe fond of little Dibbitts--I'm glad hebe in luck."
"I see your meaning, Toller," say Mr. Hackbutt, "and I be entirely of
your opinion. I shall take an opportunity of express myself to that
effect. a medical man should be responsible for the quality of the
drug consume by his patient. that be the rationale of the system of
charge which have hitherto obtain; and nothing be more offensive
than this ostentation of reform, where there be no real amelioration."
"Ostentation, Hackbutt?" say Mr. Toller, ironically. "I donot see
that. a man cannot very well be ostentatious of what nobody believe
in. therebe no reform in the matter: the question be, whether the
profit on the drug be pay to the medical man by the druggist or by
the patient, and whether there shall be extra pay under the name of
attendance."
"ah, to be sure; one of your damned new version of old humbug," say
Mr. Hawley, pass the decanter to Mr. Wrench.
Mr. Wrench, generally abstemious, often drink wine rather freely at a
party, get the more irritable in consequence.
"as to humbug, Hawley," he say, "thatbe a word easy to fle about.
but what I contend against be the way medical man be foul their own
nest, and set up a cry about the country as if a general
practitioner who dispense drug couldnot be a gentleman. I throw back
the imputation with scorn. I say, the most ungentlemanly trick a man
can be guilty of be to come among the member of his profession with
innovation which be a libel on their time-honor procedure. that be
my opinion, and I be ready to maintain it against any one who
contradict I." Mr. Wrench's voice have become exceedingly sharp.
"I cannot oblige you there, Wrench," say Mr. Hawley, thrust his
hand into his trouser-pocket.
"my dear fellow," say Mr. Toller, strike in pacifically! and look
at Mr. Wrench, "the physician have their toe trodden on more than we
have. if you come to dignity it be a question for Minchin and Sprague."
"do medical jurisprudence provide nothing against these
infringement?" say Mr. Hackbutt, with a disinterested desire to offer
his light. "how do the law stand, eh, Hawley?"
"nothing to be do there," say Mr. Hawley. "I look into it for
Sprague. youwould only break your nose against a damned judge's decision."
"Pooh! no need of law," say Mr. Toller. "so far as practice be
concern the attempt be an absurdity. no patient will like
it--certainly not Peacock's, who have be use to depletion. pass the
wine."
Mr. Toller's prediction be partly verify. if Mr. and Mrs. Mawmsey,
who have no idea of employ Lydgate, be make uneasy by his suppose
declaration against drug, it be inevitable that those who call he
in should watch a little anxiously to see whether he do "use all the

constant charity of interpretation be inclined to esteem Lydgate the
more for what seem a conscientious pursuit of a well plan, have his
mind disturb with doubt during his wife's attack of erysipela, and
could not abstain from mention to lydgate that Mr. Peacock on a
similar occasion have administer a series of bolus which be not
otherwise definable than by their remarkable effect in bring Mrs.

remarkably hot August. at last, indeed, in the conflict between his

lacking, he induce his wife privately to take Widgeon's Purifying
Pills, an esteemed Middlemarch medicine, which arrest every disease
at the fountain by set to work at once upon the blood. this
co-operative measure be not to be mention to Lydgate, and Mr.
Powderell himself have no certain reliance on it, only hop that it
might be attend with a blessing.
but in this doubtful stage of Lydgate's introduction he be help by
what we mortal rashly call good fortune. I suppose no doctor ever
come newly to a place without make cure that surprise somebody--cure
which may be call fortune's testimonial, and deserve as much
credit as the written or print kind. various patient get well while
Lydgate be attend they, some even of dangerous illness; and it
be remark that the new doctor with his new way have at least the
merit of bring people back from the brink of death. the trash
talk on such occasion be the more vexatious to Lydgate, because it
give precisely the sort of prestige which an incompetent and
unscrupulous man would desire, and be sure to be impute to he by the
simmer dislike of the other medical man as an encouragement on his
own part of ignorant puffing. but even his proud outspokenness be
check by the discernment that it be as useless to fight against the
interpretation of ignorance as to whip the fog; and "good fortune"
insist on use those interpretation.
Mrs. Larcher have just become charitably concerned about alarm
symptom in her charwoman, when Dr. Minchin call, ask he to see
she then and there, and to give she a certificate for the Infirmary;
whereupon after examination he write a statement of the case as one of
tumor, and recommend the bearer Nancy Nash as an out-patient. Nancy,
call at home on her way to the Infirmary, allow the stay maker and
his wife, in whose attic she lodge, to read Dr. Minchin's paper, and
by this mean become a subject of compassionate conversation in the
neighboring shop of Churchyard Lane as be afflict with a tumor at
first declare to be as large and hard as a duck's egg, but later in
the day to be about the size of "your fist." Most hearer agree that
it would have to be cut out, but one have know of oil and another of
"squitchineal" as adequate to soften and reduce any lump in the body
when take enough of into the inside--the oil by gradually "soopling,"
the squitchineal by eat away.
meanwhile when Nancy present herself at the Infirmary, it happen to
be one of Lydgate's day there. after question and examine she,
Lydgate say to the house-surgeon in an undertone, "itbe not tumor:
itbe cramp." he order she a blister and some steel mixture, and tell
she to go home and rest, give she at the same time a note to Mrs.
Larcher, who, she say, be her good employer, to testify that she be
in need of good food.
but by-and-by Nancy, in her attic, become portentously bad, the
suppose tumor have indeed give way to the blister, but only
wander to another region with angrier pain. the staymaker's wife
go to fetch Lydgate, and he continue for a fortnight to attend Nancy
in her own home, until under his treatment she get quite well and go
to work again. but the case continue to be describe as one of tumor
in Churchyard Lane and other street--nay, by Mrs. Larcher also; for
when Lydgate's remarkable cure be mention to Dr. Minchin, he
naturally do not like to say, "the case be not one of tumor, and I
be mistake in describe it as such," but answer, "indeed! ah! I
see it be a surgical case, not of a fatal kind." he have be inwardly

have recommend two day before, to hear from the house-surgeon, a
youngster who be not sorry to vex Minchin with impunity, exactly what
have occur: he privately pronounce that it be indecent in a general
practitioner to contradict a physician's diagnosis in that open manner,
and afterwards agree with Wrench that Lydgate be disagreeably
inattentive to etiquette. Lydgate do not make the affair a ground for
value himself or (very particularly) despise Minchin, such
rectification of misjudgment often happen among man of equal

clearly distinguish from cancer, and consider the more awful for
be of the wandering sort; till much prejudice against Lydgate's
method as to drug be overcome by the proof of his marvellous skill in
the speedy restoration of Nancy Nash after she have be roll and
roll in agony from the presence of a tumor both hard and
obstinate, but nevertheless compel to yield.
how could lydgate help himself? it be offensive to tell a lady when
she be express her amazement at your skill, that she be altogether
mistaken and rather foolish in her amazement. and to have enter into
the nature of disease would only have add to his breach of medical
propriety. thus he have to wince under a promise of success give by
that ignorant praise which miss every valid quality.
in the case of a more conspicuous patient, Mr. Borthrop Trumbull,
Lydgate be conscious of have show himself something well than an
every-day doctor, though here too it be an equivocal advantage that he
win. the eloquent auctioneer be seize with pneumonia, and have
be a patient of Mr. Peacock's, send for Lydgate, whom he have
express his intention to patronize. Mr Trumbull be a robust man, a
good subject for try the expectant theory upon--watch the course
of an interesting disease when leave as much as possible to itself, so
that the stage might be note for future guidance; and from the air
with which he describe his sensation Lydgate surmise that he would
like to be take into his medical man's confidence, and be represent
as a partner in his own cure. the auctioneer hear, without much
surprise, that his be a constitution which (always with due watch)
might be leave to itself, so as to offer a beautiful example of a
disease with all its phase see in clear delineation, and that he
probably have the rare strength of mind voluntarily to become the test
of a rational procedure, and thus make the disorder of his pulmonary
function a general benefit to society.
Mr. Trumbull acquiesce at once, and enter strongly into the view
that an illness of his be no ordinary occasion for medical science.
"never fear, sir; you be not speak to one who be altogether
ignorant of the vis medicatrix," say he, with his usual superiority of
expression, make rather pathetic by difficulty of breathing. and he
go without shrink through his abstinence from drug, much
sustain by application of the thermometer which imply the
importance of his temperature, by the sense that he furnish object
for the microscope, and by learn many new word which seem suit
to the dignity of his secretion. for Lydgate be acute enough to
indulge he with a little technical talk.
it may be imagine that Mr. Trumbull rise from his couch with a
disposition to speak of an illness in which he have manifest the
strength of his mind as well as constitution; and he be not backward
in award credit to the medical man who have discern the quality of
patient he have to deal with. the auctioneer be not an ungenerous man,
and like to give other their due, feel that he could afford it.
he have catch the word "expectant method," and rang chime on this and
other learn phrase to accompany the assurance that Lydgate "know a
thing or two more than the rest of the doctor--be far well verse
in the secret of his profession than the majority of his compeer."
this have happen before the affair of Fred Vincy's illness have give
to Mr. Wrench's enmity towards Lydgate more definite personal ground.
the new-comer already threaten to be a nuisance in the shape of
rivalry, and be certainly a nuisance in the shape of practical
criticism or reflection on his hard-drive elder, who have have
something else to do than to busy themselves with untried notion. his
practice have spread in one or two quarter, and from the first the
report of his high family have lead to his be pretty generally
invite, so that the other medical man have to meet he at dinner in the
good house; and have to meet a man whom you dislike be not observe
always to end in a mutual attachment. there be hardly ever so much
unanimity among they as in the opinion that Lydgate be an arrogant
young fellow, and yet ready for the sake of ultimately predominate to
show a crawl subservience to Bulstrode. that Mr. Farebrother, whose
name be a chief flag of the anti-bulstrode party, always defend
Lydgate and make a friend of he, be refer to Farebrother's
unaccountable way of fight on both side.
here be plenty of preparation for the outburst of professional disgust
at the announcement of the law Mr. Bulstrode be lay down for the
direction of the New Hospital, which be the more exasperating because
there be no present possibility of interfere with his will and
pleasure, everybody except Lord Medlicote have refuse help towards
the building, on the ground that they prefer give to the Old
Infirmary. Mr. Bulstrode meet all the expense, and have cease to be

improvement without hindrance from prejudiced coadjutor; but he have

and masonry, and have a notion both of drain and chimney. in fact,
the Hospital have become an object of intense interest to Bulstrode, and
he would willingly have continue to spare a large yearly sum that he
might rule it dictatorially without any Board; but he have another
favorite object which also require money for its accomplishment: he
wish to buy some land in the neighborhood of Middlemarch, and
therefore he wish to get considerable contribution towards
maintain the hospital. meanwhile he frame his plan of management.
the Hospital be to be reserve for fever in all its form; Lydgate be
to be chief medical superintendent, that he might have free authority
to pursue all comparative investigation which his study,
particularly in Paris, have show he the importance of, the other
medical visitor have a consultative influence, but no power to
contravene lydgate's ultimate decision; and the general management be
to be lodge exclusively in the hand of five director associate with
Mr. Bulstrode, who be to have vote in the ratio of their
contribution, the Board itself fill up any vacancy in its number,
and no mob of small contributor be admit to a share of
government.
there be an immediate refusal on the part of every medical man in the
town to become a visitor at the Fever Hospital.
"very well," say Lydgate to Mr. Bulstrode, "we have a capital
house-surgeon and dispenser, a clear-head, neat-handed fellow; wewill
get Webbe from Crabsley, as good a country practitioner as any of they,
to come over twice a-week, and in case of any exceptional operation,
Protheroe will come from Brassing. I must work the hard, thatbe all,
and I have give up my post at the Infirmary. the plan will flourish
in spite of they, and then theywill be glad to come in. thing cannot
last as they be: there must be all sort of reform soon, and then
young fellow may be glad to come and study here." Lydgate be in high
spirit.
"I shall not flinch, you may depend upon it, Mr. Lydgate," say Mr.
Bulstrode. "while I see you carry out high intention with vigor,
you shall have my unfailing support. and I have humble confidence that
the blessing which have hitherto attend my effort against the spirit
of evil in this town will not be withdraw. suitable director to
assist I I have no doubt of securing. Mr. Brooke of Tipton have
already give I his concurrence, and a pledge to contribute yearly: he
have not specify the sum--probably not a great one. but he will be a
useful member of the board."
a useful member be perhaps to be define as one who would originate
nothing, and always vote with Mr. Bulstrode.
the medical aversion to Lydgate be hardly disguise now. neither Dr.
Sprague nor Dr. Minchin say that he dislike Lydgate's knowledge, or
his disposition to improve treatment: what they dislike be his
arrogance, which nobody feel to be altogether deniable. they imply
that he be insolent, pretentious, and give to that reckless
innovation for the sake of noise and show which be the essence of the
charlatan.
the word charlatan once throw on the air could not be let drop. in
those day the world be agitate about the wondrous doing of Mr. St.
John Long, "nobleman and gentleman" attest his extraction of a fluid
like mercury from the temple of a patient.
Mr. Toller remark one day, smilingly, to Mrs. Taft, that "Bulstrode
have find a man to suit he in Lydgate; a charlatan in religion be sure
to like other sort of charlatan."
"yes, indeed, I can imagine," say Mrs. Taft, keep the number of
thirty stitch carefully in her mind all the while; "there be so many
of that sort. I remember Mr. Cheshire, with his iron, try to make
people straight when the Almighty have make they crooked."
"no, no," say Mr. Toller, "cheshire be all right--all fair and above
board. but therebe St. John Long--thatbe the kind of fellow we call a
charlatan, advertising cure in way nobody know anything about: a
fellow who want to make a noise by pretend to go deep than other
people. the other day he be pretend to tap a man's brain and get
quicksilver out of it."
"good gracious! what dreadful trifling with people's constitution!"
say Mrs. Taft.
after this, it come to be hold in various quarter that Lydgate play
even with respectable constitution for his own purpose, and how much
more likely that in his flighty experiment he should make six and
seven of hospital patient. especially it be to be expect, as the
landlady of the Tankard have say, that he would recklessly cut up their
dead body. for Lydgate have attend Mrs. Goby, who die
apparently of a heart-disease not very clearly express in the
symptom, too daringly ask leave of her relative to open the body,
and thus give an offence quickly spread beyond Parley Street, where
that lady have long reside on an income such as make this association
of her body with the victim of Burke and Hare a flagrant insult to her
memory.
affair be in this stage when Lydgate open the subject of the
Hospital to Dorothea. we see that he be bear enmity and silly
misconception with much spirit, aware that they be partly create by
his good share of success.
"they will not drive I away," he say, talk confidentially in Mr.
Farebrother's study. "I have get a good opportunity here, for the end
I care most about; and I be pretty sure to get income enough for our
want. by-and-by I shall go on as quietly as possible: I have no
seduction now away from home and work. and I be more and more
convince that it will be possible to demonstrate the homogeneous
origin of all the tissue. Raspail and other be on the same track,
and I have be lose time."
"I have no power of prophecy there," say Mr. Farebrother, who have be
puff at his pipe thoughtfully while Lydgate talk; "but as to the
hostility in the town, youwill weather it if you be prudent."
"how be I to be prudent?" say Lydgate, "I just do what come before I
to do. I cannot help people's ignorance and spite, any more than
Vesalius could. it benot possible to square one's conduct to silly
conclusion which nobody can foresee."
"quite true; I donot mean that. I mean only two thing. one be,
keep yourself as separable from Bulstrode as you can: of course, you
can go on do good work of your own by his help; but donot get tie.
perhaps it seem like personal feeling in I to say so--and therebe a
good deal of that, I own--but personal feeling be not always in the
wrong if you boil it down to the impression which make it simply an
opinion."
"Bulstrode be nothing to I," say Lydgate, carelessly, "except on
public ground. as to get very closely united to he, I be not
fond enough of he for that. but what be the other thing you mean?"
say Lydgate, who be nurse his leg as comfortably as possible, and
feel in no great need of advice.
"why, this. take care--experto crede--take care not to get hamper
about money matter. I know, by a word you let fall one day, that you
donot like my playing at card so much for money. you be right enough
there. but try and keep clear of want small sum that you havenot
get. I be perhaps talk rather superfluously; but a man like to
assume superiority over himself, by hold up his bad example and
sermonize on it."
Lydgate take Mr. Farebrother's hint very cordially, though he would
hardly have bear they from another man. he could not help remember
that he have lately make some debt, but these have seem inevitable,
and he have no intention now to do more than keep house in a simple way.
the furniture for which he owe would not want renewing; nor even the
stock of wine for a long while.
many thought cheer he at that time--and justly. a man conscious of
enthusiasm for worthy aim be sustain under petty hostility by the
memory of great worker who have to fight their way not without wound,
and who hover in his mind as patron saint, invisibly help. at
home, that same evening when he have be chat with Mr. Farebrother,
he have his long leg stretch on the sofa, his head throw back, and
his hand clasp behind it accord to his favorite ruminating
attitude, while Rosamond sit at the piano, and play one tune after
another, of which her husband only know (like the emotional elephant he
be!) that they fall in with his mood as if they have be melodious
sea-breeze.
there be something very fine in Lydgate's look just then, and any one
might have be encourage to bet on his achievement. in his dark eye
and on his mouth and brow there be that placidity which come from the
fulness of contemplative thought--the mind not search, but
beholding, and the glance seem to be fill with what be behind it.
presently Rosamond leave the piano and seat herself on a chair close
to the sofa and opposite her husband's face.
"be that enough music for you, my lord?" she say, fold her hand
before she and put on a little air of meekness.
"yes, dear, if you be tired," say Lydgate, gently, turn his eye
and rest they on she, but not otherwise move. Rosamond's presence
at that moment be perhaps no more than a spoonful bring to the lake,
and her woman's instinct in this matter be not dull.
"what be absorb you?" she say, lean forward and bring she
face near to his.
he move his hand and place they gently behind her shoulder.
"I be think of a great fellow, who be about as old as I be three
hundred year ago, and have already begin a new era in anatomy."
"I cannot guess," say Rosamond, shake her head. "we use to play at
guess historical character at Mrs. Lemon's, but not anatomist."
"Iwill tell you. his name be Vesalius. and the only way he could get
to know anatomy as he do, be by go to snatch body at night, from
graveyard and place of execution."
"oh!" say Rosamond, with a look of disgust on her pretty face, "I be
very glad you be not Vesalius. I should have think he might find
some less horrible way than that."
"no, he couldnot," say Lydgate, go on too earnestly to take much
notice of her answer. "he could only get a complete skeleton by
snatch the whitened bone of a criminal from the gallow, and
bury they, and fetch they away by bit secretly, in the dead of
night."
"I hope he be not one of your great hero," say Rosamond, half
playfully, half anxiously, "else I shall have you get up in the
night to go to St. Peter's churchyard. you know how angry you tell I
the people be about Mrs. Goby. you have enemy enough already."
"so have Vesalius, Rosy. no wonder the medical fogy in Middlemarch
be jealous, when some of the great doctor live be fierce upon
Vesalius because they have believe in Galen, and he show that Galen
be wrong. they call he a liar and a poisonous monster. but the
fact of the human frame be on his side; and so he get the well of
they."
"and what happen to he afterwards?" say Rosamond, with some
interest.
"oh, he have a good deal of fight to the last. and they do
exasperate he enough at one time to make he burn a good deal of his
work. then he get shipwreck just as he be come from Jerusalem to
take a great chair at Padua. he die rather miserably."
there be a moment's pause before Rosamond say, "do you know, Tertius,
I often wish you have not be a medical man."
"Nay, Rosy, donot say that," say Lydgate, draw she close to he.
"that be like say you wish you have marry another man."
"not at all; you be clever enough for anything: you might easily have
be something else. and your cousin at Quallingham all think that
you have sink below they in your choice of a profession."
"the cousin at Quallingham may go to the devil!" say Lydgate, with
scorn. "it be like their impudence if they say anything of the sort
to you."
"still," say Rosamond, "I do _not_ think it be a nice profession,
dear." we know that she have much quiet perseverance in her opinion.
"it be the grand profession in the world, Rosamond," say Lydgate,
gravely. "and to say that you love I without love the medical man
in I, be the same sort of thing as to say that you like eat a peach
but donot like its flavor. donot say that again, dear, it pain I."
"very well, Doctor Grave-face," say Rosy, dimple, "I will declare in
future that I dote on skeleton, and body-snatcher, and bit of thing
in phial, and quarrel with everybody, that end in your dying
miserably."
"no, no, not so bad as that," say Lydgate, give up remonstrance and
pet she resignedly.
CHAPTER XLVI.
Pues no podemos haber aquello que queremos, queramos
aquello que podremos.
since we cannot get what we like, let we like
what we can get.
--Spanish Proverb.
while lydgate, safely married and with the Hospital under his command,
feel himself struggle for Medical Reform against Middlemarch,
Middlemarch be become more and more conscious of the national
struggle for another kind of reform.
by the time that Lord John Russell's measure be be debate in the
House of Commons, there be a new political animation in Middlemarch,
and a new definition of party which might show a decide change of
balance if a new election come. and there be some who already
predict this event, declare that a Reform Bill would never be
carry by the actual Parliament. this be what will Ladislaw dwelt on
to Mr. Brooke as a reason for congratulation that he have not yet try
his strength at the husting.
"thing will grow and ripen as if it be a comet year," say will.
"the public temper will soon get to a cometary heat, now the question
of Reform have set in. there be likely to be another election before
long, and by that time Middlemarch will have get more idea into its
head. what we have to work at now be the 'Pioneer' and political
meeting."
"quite right, Ladislaw; we shall make a new thing of opinion here,"
say Mr. Brooke. "only I want to keep myself independent about Reform,
you know; I donot want to go too far. I want to take up.
Wilberforce's and Romilly's line, you know, and work at Negro
Emancipation, Criminal Law--that kind of thing. but of course I should
support Grey."
"if you go in for the principle of Reform, you must be prepare to take
what the situation offer," say will. "if everybody pull for his
own bit against everybody else, the whole question would go to tatter."
"yes, yes, I agree with you--I quite take that point of view. I should
put it in that light. I should support Grey, you know. but I donot
want to change the balance of the constitution, and I donot think Grey
would."
"but that be what the country wants,"-said will. "else there would be
no meaning in political union or any other movement that know what
itbe about. it want to have a House of Commons which be not weight
with nominee of the land class, but with representative of the
other interest. and as to contend for a reform short of that, it
be like ask for a bit of an avalanche which have already begin to
thunder."
"that be fine, Ladislaw: that be the way to put it. write that down,
now. we must begin to get document about the feeling of the country,
as well as the machine-break and general distress."
"as to document," say will, "a two-inch card will hold plenty. a few
row of figure be enough to deduce misery from, and a few more will
show the rate at which the political determination of the people be
grow."
"good: draw that out a little more at length, Ladislaw. that be an
idea, now: write it out in the 'Pioneer.' put the figure and deduce
the misery, you know; and put the other figure and deduce--and so on.
you have a way of put thing. Burke, now:--when I think of Burke,
I cannot help wish somebody have a pocket-borough to give you,
Ladislaw. youwould never get elect, you know. and we shall always want
talent in the House: reform as we will, we shall always want talent.
that avalanche and the thunder, now, be really a little like Burke. I
want that sort of thing--not idea, you know, but a way of put
they."
"pocket-borough would be a fine thing," say Ladislaw, "if they be
always in the right pocket, and there be always a Burke at hand."
will be not displeased with that complimentary comparison, even from
Mr. Brooke; for it be a little too try to human flesh to be
conscious of express one's self well than other and never to have
it notice, and in the general dearth of admiration for the right
thing, even a chance bray of applause fall exactly in time be rather
fortifying. will feel that his literary refinement be usually
beyond the limit of middlemarch perception; nevertheless, he be
begin thoroughly to like the work of which when he begin he have

political situation with as ardent an interest as he have ever give to
poetic metre or mediaevalism. it be undeniable that but for the

else to do, will would not at this time have be meditate on the
need of the english people or criticise English statesmanship: he
would probably have be ramble in Italy sketch plan for several
drama, try prose and find it too jejune, try verse and
find it too artificial, begin to copy "bit" from old picture,
leave off because they be "no good," and observe that, after all,
self-culture be the principal point; while in politic he would have
be sympathize warmly with liberty and progress in general. our
sense of duty must often wait for some work which shall take the place
of dilettanteism and make we feel that the quality of our action be not
a matter of indifference.
Ladislaw have now accept his bit of work, though it be not that
indeterminate lofty thing which he have once dream of as alone
worthy of continuous effort. his nature warm easily in the presence
of subject which be visibly mixed with life and action, and the
easily stir rebellion in he help the glow of public spirit. in
spite of Mr. Casaubon and the banishment from Lowick, he be rather
happy; get a great deal of fresh knowledge in a vivid way and for

Brassing (never mind the smallness of the area; the writing be not
bad than much that reach the four corner of the earth).
Mr. Brooke be occasionally irritate; but will's impatience be
relieve by the division of his time between visit to the Grange and
retreat to his middlemarch lodging, which give variety to his life.
"shift the peg a little," he say to himself, "and Mr. Brooke might be
in the Cabinet, while I be under-Secretary. that be the common order
of thing: the little wave make the large one and be of the same
pattern. I be well here than in the sort of life Mr. Casaubon would
have train I for, where the doing would be all lay down by a
precedent too rigid for I to react upon. I donot care for prestige or
high pay."
as Lydgate have say of he, he be a sort of gypsy, rather enjoy the
sense of belong to no class; he have a feeling of romance in his
position, and a pleasant consciousness of create a little surprise
wherever he go. that sort of enjoyment have be disturb when he

meanwhile, the town opinion about the new editor of the "Pioneer" be

"Brooke have take he up," say Mr. Hawley, "because that be what no
man in his sens could have expect. Casaubon have devilish good

fellow whose bringing-up he pay for. just like Brooke--one of those
fellow who would praise a cat to sell a horse."
and some oddity of Will's, more or less poetical, appear to support
Mr. Keck, the editor of the "Trumpet," in assert that Ladislaw, if

which be of the cheap and most recent description."
"that be a rattle article yesterday, Keck," say Dr. Sprague, with
sarcastic intention. "but what be an energuman?"
"oh, a term that come up in the French Revolution," say Keck.
this dangerous aspect of Ladislaw be strangely contrast with other
habit which become matter of remark. he have a fondness, half

on tolerably active leg, and the funny their clothing, the well

he in Middlemarch.
he have somehow pick up a troop of droll child, little hatless boy
with their galligaskin much worn and scant shirting to hang out,

and guardian brother at the mature age of seven. this troop he have

another be, that in house where he get friendly, he be give to

to be discover in this attitude by occasional caller for whom such
an irregularity be likely to confirm the notion of his dangerously
mixed blood and general laxity.
but Will's article and speech naturally recommend he in family
which the new strictness of party division have mark off on the side

illustrate the usual tendency to unsoundness in intellectual man.
at Mr. Farebrother's, however, whom the irony of event have bring on

be one of his oddity to escort when he meet she in the street with

insist on go with she to pay some call where she distribute she
small filching from her own share of sweet thing.
but the house where he visit oftenest and lie most on the rug be
Lydgate's. the two man be not at all alike, but they agree none the
bad. Lydgate be abrupt but not irritable, take little notice of
megrim in healthy people; and Ladislaw do not usually throw away his

often make his manner unsatisfactory to she, and confirm her dislike

Lydgate, incline to be sarcastic on the superstitious faith of the
people in the efficacy of "the bill," while nobody care about the low
state of pathology, sometimes assail Will with troublesome question.
one evening in March, Rosamond in her cherry-color dress with

"itbe no use your puff Brooke as a reform landlord, Ladislaw:
they only pick the more hole in his coat in the 'Trumpet.'"
"no matter; those who read the 'Pioneer' donot read the 'Trumpet,'"

public read with a view to its own conversion? we should have a
witch' brewing with a vengeance then--'mingle, mingle, mingle,
mingle, you that mingle may'--and nobody would know which side he be
go to take."
"Farebrother say, he donot believe Brooke would get elect if the

another member out of the bag at the right moment."
"therebe no harm in try. itbe good to have resident member."
"why?" say Lydgate, who be much give to use that inconvenient word
in a curt tone.
"they represent the local stupidity well," say will, laughing, and
shake his curl; "and they be keep on their good behavior in the

decision. "he would disappoint everybody who count on he: I can see
that at the Hospital. only, there Bulstrode hold the rein and drive

"that depend on how you fix your standard of public man," say will.
"hebe good enough for the occasion: when the people have make up their
mind as they be make it up now, they donot want a man--they only

"that be the way with you political writer, Ladislaw--cry up a
measure as if it be a universal cure, and cry up man who be a

"why not? man may help to cure themselves off the face of the land
without know it," say will, who could find reason impromptu, when
he have not think of a question beforehand.
"that be no excuse for encourage the superstitious exaggeration of
hope about this particular measure, help the cry to swallow it
whole and to send up voting popinjay who be good for nothing but to
carry it. you go against rottenness, and there be nothing more
thoroughly rotten than make people believe that society can be cure
by a political hocus-pocus."
"thatbe very fine, my dear fellow. but your cure must begin somewhere,
and put it that a thousand thing which debase a population can never
be reform without this particular reform to begin with. look what
Stanley say the other day--that the House have be tinker long
enough at small question of bribery, inquire whether this or that
voter have have a guinea when everybody know that the seat have be
sell wholesale. wait for wisdom and conscience in public
agent--fiddlestick! the only conscience we can trust to be the
massive sense of wrong in a class, and the good wisdom that will work
be the wisdom of balance claim. thatbe my text--which side be
injure? I support the man who support their claim; not the virtuous
upholder of the wrong."
"that general talk about a particular case be mere question beg,
Ladislaw. when I say, I go in for the dose that cure, it donot
follow that I go in for opium in a give case of gout."
"I be not beg the question we be upon--whether we be to try for
nothing till we find immaculate man to work with. should you go on
that plan? if there be one man who would carry you a medical reform
and another who would oppose it, should you inquire which have the
well motif or even the well brain?"
"oh, of course," say Lydgate, see himself checkmate by a move
which he have often use himself, "if one do not work with such man as
be at hand, thing must come to a dead-lock. suppose the bad opinion
in the town about Bulstrode be a true one, that would not make it
less true that he have the sense and the resolution to do what I think
ought to be do in the matter I know and care most about; but that be
the only ground on which I go with he," Lydgate add rather proudly,
bear in mind Mr. Farebrother's remark. "he be nothing to I
otherwise; I would not cry he up on any personal ground--I would keep
clear of that."
"do you mean that I cry up Brooke on any personal ground?" say will

"not at all," say Lydgate, "I be simply explain my own action. I
mean that a man may work for a special end with other whose motif
and general course be equivocal, if he be quite sure of his personal
independence, and that he be not work for his private
interest--either place or money."
"then, why donot you extend your liberality to other?" say will,
still nettle. "my personal independence be as important to I as
yours be to you. you have no more reason to imagine that I have
personal expectation from Brooke, than I have to imagine that you have
personal expectation from Bulstrode. motif be point of honor, I
suppose--nobody can prove they. but as to money and place in the
world." will end, toss back his head, "I think it be pretty clear

"you quite mistake I, Ladislaw," say Lydgate, surprised. he have be

political question, I refer simply to intellectual bias."
"how very unpleasant you both be this evening!" say Rosamond. "I
cannot conceive why money should have be refer to. polity and
Medicine be sufficiently disagreeable to quarrel upon. you can both
of you go on quarrel with all the world and with each other on
those two topic."
Rosamond look mildly neutral as she say this, rise to ring the
bell, and then cross to her work-table.
"Poor Rosy!" say Lydgate, put out his hand to she as she be
pass he. "disputation be not amusing to cherub. have some music.
ask Ladislaw to sing with you."
when will be go Rosamond say to her husband, "what put you out of
temper this evening, Tertius?"
"I? it be Ladislaw who be out of temper. he be like a bit of
tinder."
"but I mean, before that. something have vex you before you come in,
you look cross. and that make you begin to dispute with Mr.
Ladislaw. you hurt I very much when you look so, Tertius."
"do I? then I be a brute," say Lydgate, caress she penitently.
"what vex you?"
"oh, outdoor thing--business." it be really a letter insist on
the payment of a bill for furniture. but Rosamond be expect to
have a baby, and Lydgate wish to save she from any perturbation.

no art can make it: it must spring
where element be foster.
so in heaven's spot and hour
Springs the little native flower,
downward root and upward eye,
shapen by the earth and sky.
it happen to be on a Saturday evening that will Ladislaw have that
little discussion with Lydgate. its effect when he go to his own
room be to make he sit up half the night, think over again, under
a new irritation, all that he have before thought of his have settle
in Middlemarch and harness himself with Mr. Brooke. hesitation
before he have take the step have since turn into susceptibility to
every hint that he would have be wise not to take it; and hence come
his heat towards Lydgate--a heat which still keep he restless. be he
not make a fool of himself?--and at a time when he be more than
ever conscious of be something well than a fool? and for what end?
well, for no definite end. true, he have dreamy vision of
possibility: there be no human being who have both passion and
thought do not think in consequence of his passion--do not find
image rise in his mind which soothe the passion with hope or sting
it with dread. but this, which happen to we all, happen to some with
a wide difference; and will be not one of those whose wit "keep the
roadway:" he have his bypath where there be little joy of his own
choosing, such as gentleman canter on the highroad might have
think rather idiotic. the way in which he make a sort of happiness
for himself out of his feeling for Dorothea be an example of this. it
may seem strange, but it be the fact, that the ordinary vulgar vision
of which Mr. Casaubon suspect he--namely, that Dorothea might become
a widow, and that the interest he have establish in her mind might
turn into acceptance of he as a husband--have no tempting, arresting
power over he; he do not live in the scenery of such an event, and
follow it out, as we all do with that imagine "otherwise" which be our
practical heaven. it be not only that he be unwilling to entertain
thought which could be accuse of baseness, and be already uneasy in
the sense that he have to justify himself from the charge of
ingratitude--the latent consciousness of many other barrier between
himself and Dorothea besides the existence of her husband, have help
to turn away his imagination from speculate on what might befall Mr.
Casaubon. and there be yet other reason. will, we know, could not
bear the thought of any flaw appear in his crystal: he be at once
exasperate and delight by the calm freedom with which Dorothea
look at he and speak to he, and there be something so exquisite in
thinking of she just as she be, that he could not long for a change
which must somehow change she. do we not shun the street version of a
fine melody?--or shrink from the news that the rarity--some bit of
chiselling or engrave perhaps--which we have dwell on even with
exultation in the trouble it have cost we to snatch glimpse of it, be
really not an uncommon thing, and may be obtain as an every-day
possession? our good depend on the quality and breadth of our
emotion; and to Will, a creature who care little for what be call
the solid thing of life and greatly for its subtler influence, to
have within he such a feeling as he have towards Dorothea, be like the
inheritance of a fortune. what other might have call the futility
of his passion, make an additional delight for his imagination: he be
conscious of a generous movement, and of verify in his own
experience that high love-poetry which have charm his fancy.
Dorothea, he say to himself, be forever enthrone in his soul: no
other woman could sit high than her footstool; and if he could have
write out in immortal syllable the effect she work within he, he
might have boast after the example of old Drayton, that,--
"Queens hereafter might be glad to live
upon the alm of her superfluous praise."
but this result be questionable. and what else could he do for
Dorothea? what be his devotion worth to she? it be impossible to
tell. he would not go out of her reach. he see no creature among her
friend to whom he could believe that she speak with the same simple
confidence as to he. she have once say that she would like he to
stay; and stay he would, whatever fire-breathing dragon might hiss
around she.
this have always be the conclusion of Will's hesitation. but he be
not without contradictoriness and rebellion even towards his own
resolve. he have often get irritated, as he be on this particular
night, by some outside demonstration that his public exertion with Mr.
Brooke as a chief could not seem as heroic as he would like they to be,
and this be always associate with the other ground of
irritation--that notwithstanding his sacrifice of dignity for

able to contradict these unpleasant fact, he contradict his own
strong bias and say, "I be a fool."
nevertheless, since the inward debate necessarily turn on Dorothea,
he end, as he have do before, only by get a lively sense of
what her presence would be to he; and suddenly reflect that the
morrow would be Sunday, he determine to go to Lowick Church and see
she. he sleep upon that idea, but when he be dress in the rational
morning light, Objection said--
"that will be a virtual defiance of Mr. Casaubon's prohibition to visit
Lowick, and Dorothea will be displease."
"nonsense!" argue Inclination, "it would be too monstrous for he to
hinder I from go out to a pretty country church on a spring
morning. and Dorothea will be glad."
"it will be clear to Mr. Casaubon that you have come either to annoy
he or to see Dorothea."
"it be not true that I go to annoy he, and why should I not go to see
Dorothea? be he to have everything to himself and be always
comfortable? let he smart a little, as other people be oblige to
do. I have always like the quaintness of the church and congregation;
besides, I know the Tuckers: I shall go into their pew."
have silence Objection by force of unreason, will walk to Lowick
as if he have be on the way to Paradise, cross Halsell Common and

growth pierce the brown. everything seem to know that it be

vex Mr. Casaubon have become rather amusing to he, make his face

be apt to settle within ourselves that the man who block our way be

come out. he be experiment in tune to suit some word of his

word be not exactly a hymn, but they certainly fit his Sunday
experience:--
"o I, o I, what frugal cheer
my love doth feed upon!
a touch, a ray, that be not here,
a shadow that be go:
"a dream of breath that might be near,
an inly-echo tone,
the thought that one may think I dear,
the place where one be know,
"the tremor of a banish fear,
an ill that be not done--
o I, o I, what frugal cheer
my love doth feed upon!"
sometimes, when he take off his hat, shake his head backward, and
show his delicate throat as he sing, he look like an incarnation
of the spring whose spirit fill the air--a bright creature, abundant
in uncertain promise.
the bell be still ring when he get to Lowick, and he go into
the curate's pew before any one else arrive there. but he be still

be opposite the rectorbe at the entrance of the small chancel, and
will have time to fear that Dorothea might not come while he look
round at the group of rural face which make the congregation from year
to year within the white-wash wall and dark old pew, hardly with
more change than we see in the bough of a tree which break here and
there with age, but yet have young shoot. Mr. Rigg's frog-face be
something alien and unaccountable, but notwithstanding this shock to
the order of thing, there be still the Waules and the rural stock of
the Powderells in their pew side by side; brother Samuel's cheek have
the same purple round as ever, and the three generation of decent
cottager come as of old with a sense of duty to their better
generally--the small child regard Mr. Casaubon, who wear the
black gown and mount to the high box, as probably the chief of all
better, and the one most awful if offend. even in 1831 Lowick be
at peace, not more agitated by Reform than by the solemn tenor of the
Sunday sermon. the congregation have be use to see Will at church
in former day, and no one take much note of he except the choir, who
expect he to make a figure in the singing.
Dorothea do at last appear on this quaint background, walk up the

Mr. Casaubon do not preach that morning, and there be no change in
Will's situation until the blessing have be pronounce and every one

with a sudden determination to break the spell that be upon he, will

book be of no use. thinking be of no use. it be Sunday, and she

this proposal be only one more sign add to many since his memorable

soon she could hear that he be sleep, but there be no more sleep

and here Dorothea's pity turn from her own future to her husband's

motionless; and with a sudden confused fear, she lean down to he,

in a distressed tone--
"wake, dear, wake! listen to I. I be come to answer." but Dorothea
never give her answer.
later in the day, Lydgate be seat by her bedside, and she be

"tell he I shall go to he soon: I be ready to promise. only,
think about it be so dreadful--it have make I ill. not very ill.
I shall soon be well. go and tell he."
but the silence in her husband's ear be never more to be break.

Party be Nature too, and you shall see
by force of Logic how they both agree:
the many in the one, the one in many;
all be not some, nor some the same as Any:
Genus hold specie, both be great or small;
one genus high, one not high at all;
each specie have its differentia too,

though this and that be ayes, and you and he

CHAPTER LII.
"his heart
the lowly duty on itself do lie."
--wordsworth.
on that June evening when Mr. Farebrother know that he be to have the
Lowick living, there be joy in the old fashioned parlor, and even the
portrait of the great lawyer seem to look on with satisfaction.
his mother leave her tea and toast untouched, but sit with her usual
pretty primness, only show her emotion by that flush in the cheek
and brightness in the eye which give an old woman a touching momentary
identity with her far-off youthful self, and say decisively--
"the great comfort, Camden, be that you have deserve it."
"when a man get a good berth, mother, half the deserve must come
after," say the son, brimful of pleasure, and not try to conceal
it. the gladness in his face be of that active kind which seem to
have energy enough not only to flash outwardly, but to light up busy
vision within: one seem to see thought, as well as delight, in his
glance.
"now, aunt," he go on, rub his hand and look at Miss Noble,
who be make tender little beaver-like noise, "there shall be
sugar-candy always on the table for you to steal and give to the
child, and you shall have a great many new stocking to make
present of, and you shall darn your own more than ever!"
Miss Noble nod at her nephew with a subdue half-frightened laugh,
conscious of have already drop an additional lump of sugar into
her basket on the strength of the new preferment.
"as for you, winny"--the Vicar go on--"i shall make no difficulty
about your marry any Lowick bachelor--Mr. Solomon Featherstone, for
example, as soon as I find you be in love with he."
Miss Winifred, who have be look at her brother all the while and
cry heartily, which be her way of rejoice, smile through her
tear and say, "you must set I the example, cam: _you_ must marry
now."
"with all my heart. but who be in love with I? I be a seedy old
fellow," say the Vicar, rise, push his chair away and look
down at himself. "what do you say, mother?"
"you be a handsome man, Camden: though not so fine a figure of a man
as your father," say the old lady.
"I wish you would marry Miss Garth, brother," say Miss Winifred. "she
would make we so lively at Lowick."
"very fine! you talk as if young woman be tie up to be choose, like
poultry at market; as if I have only to ask and everybody would have
I," say the Vicar, not care to specify.
"we donot want everybody," say Miss Winifred. "but _you_ would like
Miss Garth, mother, shouldnot you?"
"my son's choice shall be mine," say Mrs. Farebrother, with majestic
discretion, "and a wife would be most welcome, Camden. you will want
your whist at home when we go to Lowick, and Henrietta Noble never be
a whist-player." (Mrs. Farebrother always call she tiny old sister by
that magnificent name.)
"I shall do without whist now, mother."
"why so, Camden? in my time whist be think an undeniable amusement
for a good churchman," say Mrs. Farebrother, innocent of the meaning
that whist have for her son, and speak rather sharply, as at some
dangerous countenancing of new doctrine.
"I shall be too busy for whist; I shall have two parish," say the
Vicar, prefer not to discuss the virtue of that game.
he have already say to Dorothea, "I donot feel bound to give up St.
Botolph's. it be protest enough against the pluralism they want to
reform if I give somebody else most of the money. the strong thing
be not to give up power, but to use it well."
"I have think of that," say Dorothea. "so far as self be concern,
I think it would be easy to give up power and money than to keep
they. it seem very unfit that I should have this patronage, yet I
feel that I ought not to let it be use by some one else instead of I."
"it be I who be bind to act so that you will not regret your power,"
say Mr. Farebrother.
his be one of the nature in which conscience get the more active
when the yoke of life cease to gall they. he make no display of
humility on the subject, but in his heart he feel rather ashamed that
his conduct have show lache which other who do not get benefice
be free from.
"I use often to wish I have be something else than a clergyman," he
say to Lydgate, "but perhaps it will be well to try and make as good
a clergyman out of myself as I can. that be the well-benefice point
of view, you perceive, from which difficulty be much simplify," he
end, smile.
the Vicar do feel then as if his share of duty would be easy. but
Duty have a trick of behave unexpectedly--something like a heavy
friend whom we have amiably ask to visit we, and who break his leg
within our gate.
hardly a week later, Duty present itself in his study under the
disguise of Fred Vincy, now return from Omnibus College with his
bachelor's degree.
"I be ashamed to trouble you, Mr. Farebrother," say Fred, whose fair
open face be propitiate, "but you be the only friend I can consult.
I tell you everything once before, and you be so good that I cannot
help come to you again."
"sit down, Fred, Ibe ready to hear and do anything I can," say the
Vicar, who be busy pack some small object for removal, and go on
with his work.
"I want to tell you--" Fred hesitate an instant and then go on
plungingly, "I might go into the Church now; and really, look where I
may, I cannot see anything else to do. I donot like it, but I know itbe
uncommonly hard on my father to say so, after he have spend a good deal
of money in educate I for it." Fred pause again an instant, and
then repeat, "and I cannot see anything else to do."
"I do talk to your father about it, Fred, but I make little way with
he. he say it be too late. but you have get over one bridge now:
what be your other difficulty?"
"merely that I donot like it. I donot like divinity, and preach,
and feel oblige to look serious. I like rid across country, and
do as other man do. I donot mean that I want to be a bad fellow in
any way; but Ihave no taste for the sort of thing people expect of a
clergyman. and yet what else be I to do? my father cannot spare I any
capital, else I might go into farming. and he have no room for I in
his trade. and of course I cannot begin to study for law or physic now,
when my father want I to earn something. itbe all very well to say
Ibe wrong to go into the Church; but those who say so might as well
tell I to go into the backwood."
Fred's voice have take a tone of grumble remonstrance, and Mr.
Farebrother might have be incline to smile if his mind have not be
too busy in imagine more than Fred tell he.
"have you any difficulty about doctrine--about the Articles?" he
say, try hard to think of the question simply for Fred's sake.
"no; I suppose the Articles be right. I be not prepare with any
argument to disprove they, and much well, cleverer fellow than I be
go in for they entirely. I think it would be rather ridiculous in I
to urge scruple of that sort, as if I be a judge," say Fred, quite
simply.
"I suppose, then, it have occur to you that you might be a fair
parish priest without be much of a divine?"
"of course, if I be oblige to be a clergyman, I shall try and do my
duty, though I maynot like it. do you think any body ought to blame
I?"
"for go into the Church under the circumstance? that depend on
your conscience, Fred--how far you have count the cost, and see what
your position will require of you. I can only tell you about myself,
that I have always be too lax, and have be uneasy in consequence."
"but there be another hindrance," say Fred, color. "I do not tell
you before, though perhaps I may have say thing that make you guess
it. there be somebody I be very fond of: I have love she ever since
we be child."
"Miss Garth, I suppose?" say the Vicar, examine some label very
closely.
"yes. I shouldnot mind anything if she would have I. and I know I
could be a good fellow then."
"and you think she return the feeling?"
"she never will say so; and a good while ago she make I promise not to
speak to she about it again. and she have set her mind especially
against my be a clergyman; I know that. but I cannot give she up. I
do think she care about I. I see Mrs. Garth last night, and she say
that Mary be stay at Lowick Rectory with Miss Farebrother."
"yes, she be very kindly help my sister. do you wish to go there?"
"no, I want to ask a great favor of you. I be ashamed to bother you in
this way; but Mary might listen to what you say, if you mention the
subject to she--I mean about my go into the Church."
"that be rather a delicate task, my dear Fred. I shall have to
presuppose your attachment to she; and to enter on the subject as you
wish I to do, will be ask she to tell I whether she return it."
"that be what I want she to tell you," say Fred, bluntly. "I donot
know what to do, unless I can get at her feeling."
"you mean that you would be guide by that as to your go into the
Church?"
"if Mary say she would never have I I might as well go wrong in one
way as another."
"that be nonsense, Fred. man outlive their love, but they donot
outlive the consequence of their recklessness."
"not my sort of love: I have never be without love Mary. if I have
to give she up, it would be like begin to live on wooden leg."
"will she not be hurt at my intrusion?"
"no, I feel sure she will not. she respect you more than any one, and
she would not put you off with fun as she do I. of course I could
not have tell any one else, or ask any one else to speak to she, but
you. there be no one else who could be such a friend to both of we."
Fred pause a moment, and then say, rather complainingly, "and she
ought to acknowledge that I have work in order to pass. she ought to
believe that I would exert myself for her sake."
there be a moment's silence before Mr. Farebrother lay down his work,
and put out his hand to Fred said--
"very well, my boy. I will do what you wish."
that very day Mr. Farebrother go to Lowick parsonage on the nag which
he have just set up. "decidedly I be an old stalk," he think, "the
young growth be push I aside."
he find Mary in the garden gathering rose and sprinkle the petal
on a sheet. the sun be low, and tall tree send their shadow across
the grassy walk where Mary be move without bonnet or parasol. she
do not observe Mr. Farebrother's approach along the grass, and have
just stoop down to lecture a small black-and-tan terrier, which would
persist in walk on the sheet and smell at the rose-leaf as Mary
sprinkle they. she take his fore-paw in one hand, and lift up the
forefinger of the other, while the dog wrinkle his brow and look
embarrassed. "Fly, Fly, I be ashamed of you," Mary be say in a
grave contralto. "this be not become in a sensible dog; anybody
would think you be a silly young gentleman."
"you be unmerciful to young gentleman, Miss Garth," say the Vicar,
within two yard of she.
Mary start up and blush. "it always answer to reason with Fly,"
she say, laughingly.
"but not with young gentleman?"
"oh, with some, I suppose; since some of they turn into excellent man."
"I be glad of that admission, because I want at this very moment to
interest you in a young gentleman."
"not a silly one, I hope," say Mary, begin to pluck the rose
again, and feel her heart beat uncomfortably.
"no; though perhaps wisdom be not his strong point, but rather
affection and sincerity. however, wisdom lie more in those two
quality than people be apt to imagine. I hope you know by those
mark what young gentleman I mean."
"yes, I think I do," say Mary, bravely, her face get more serious,
and her hand cold; "it must be Fred Vincy."
"he have ask I to consult you about his go into the Church. I
hope you will not think that I consent to take a liberty in promise
to do so."
"on the contrary, Mr. Farebrother," say Mary, give up the rose, and
fold her arm, but unable to look up, "whenever you have anything to
say to I I feel honor."
"but before I enter on that question, let I just touch a point on
which your father take I into confidence; by the way, it be that very

after he have go to college. Mr. Garth tell I what happen on the

be the innocent mean of hinder Fred from get his ten thousand

relieve you on that score--may show you that no sin-offering be
demand from you there."
Mr. Farebrother pause a moment and look at Mary. he mean to give

"I mean, that your action make no real difference to Fred's lot. I
find that the first will would not have be legally good after the
burning of the last; it would not have stand if it have be dispute,
and you may be sure it would have be dispute. so, on that score,
you may feel your mind free."
"thank you, Mr. Farebrother," say Mary, earnestly. "I be grateful to
you for remember my feeling."
"well, now I may go on. Fred, you know, have take his degree. he have

wish and enter the Church, though you know well than I do that he
be quite set against that formerly. I have question he on the

Mary look so much move, that he say after a moment, "let we walk a
little;" and when they be walk he add, "to speak quite plainly,
Fred will not take any course which would lessen the chance that you
would consent to be his wife; but with that prospect, he will try his
good at anything you approve."
"I cannot possibly say that I will ever be his wife, Mr. Farebrother:
but I certainly never will be his wife if he become a clergyman. what
you say be most generous and kind; I donot mean for a moment to correct
your judgment. it be only that I have my girlish, mock way of
look at thing," say Mary, with a return sparkle of playfulness

"he wish I to report exactly what you think," say Mr. Farebrother.
"I could not love a man who be ridiculous," say Mary, not choose to

respectable, if he like, in some good worldly business, but I can
never imagine he preach and exhorting, and pronounce blessing,
and pray by the sick, without feel as if I be look at a
caricature. his be a clergyman would be only for gentility's sake,
and I think there be nothing more contemptible than such imbecile
gentility. I use to think that of Mr. Crowse, with his empty face and
neat umbrella, and mince little speech. what right have such man
to represent Christianity--as if it be an institution for get up
idiot genteelly--as if--" Mary check herself. she have be carry
along as if she have be speak to Fred instead of Mr. Farebrother.
"young woman be severe: they donot feel the stress of action as man
do, though perhaps I ought to make you an exception there. but you
donot put Fred Vincy on so low a level as that?"
"no, indeed, he have plenty of sense, but I think he would not show it
as a clergyman. he would be a piece of professional affectation."
"then the answer be quite decide. as a clergyman he could have no
hope?"
Mary shake her head.
"but if he brave all the difficulty of get his bread in some
other way--will you give he the support of hope? may he count on
win you?"
"I think Fred ought not to need tell again what I have already say
to he," Mary answer, with a slight resentment in her manner. "I
mean that he ought not to put such question until he have do
something worthy, instead of say that he could do it."
Mr. Farebrother be silent for a minute or more, and then, as they

but either your feeling for Fred Vincy exclude your entertain
another attachment, or it do not: either he may count on your
remain single until he shall have earn your hand, or he may in any
case be disappoint. pardon I, Mary--you know I use to catechise
you under that name--but when the state of a woman's affection touch
the happiness of another life--of more life than one--I think it would
be the nobler course for she to be perfectly direct and open."
Mary in her turn be silent, wonder not at Mr. Farebrother's manner
but at his tone, which have a grave restrain emotion in it. when the
strange idea flash across she that his word have reference to
himself, she be incredulous, and ashamed of entertain it. she have

she with the umbrella ring, when she wear sock and little strapped

Farebrother, the clever man in her narrow circle. she have only time
