clear and determine--her answer.
"since you think it my duty, Mr. Farebrother, I will tell you that I
have too strong a feeling for Fred to give he up for any one else. I
should never be quite happy if I think he be unhappy for the loss of
I. it have take such deep root in I--my gratitude to he for always
love I good, and mind so much if I hurt myself, from the time
when we be very little. I cannot imagine any new feeling come to
make that weak. I should like well than anything to see he worthy
of every one's respect. but please tell he I will not promise to
marry he till then: I should shame and grieve my father and mother.
he be free to choose some one else."
"then I have fulfil my commission thoroughly," say Mr. Farebrother,
put out his hand to Mary, "and I shall ride back to Middlemarch
forthwith. with this prospect before he, we shall get Fred into the
right niche somehow, and I hope I shall live to join your hand. God
bless you!"
"oh, please stay, and let I give you some tea," say Mary. her eye
fill with tear, for something indefinable, something like the
resolute suppression of a pain in Mr. Farebrother's manner, make she
feel suddenly miserable, as she have once feel when she see her father's
hand tremble in a moment of trouble.
"no, my dear, no. I must get back."
in three minute the Vicar be on horseback again, have go
magnanimously through a duty much hard than the renunciation of
whist, or even than the writing of penitential meditation.
CHAPTER LIII.
it be but a shallow haste which concludeth insincerity from
what outsider call inconsistency--put a dead mechanism
of "if" and "therefore" for the live myriad of hide
sucker whereby the belief and the conduct be work into
mutual sustainment.
Mr. Bulstrode, when he be hop to acquire a new interest in Lowick,
have naturally have an especial wish that the new clergyman should be one
whom he thoroughly approve; and he believe it to be a chastisement
and admonition direct to his own shortcoming and those of the nation
at large, that just about the time when he come in possession of the
deed which make he the proprietor of Stone Court, Mr. Farebrother
"read himself" into the quaint little church and preach his first
sermon to the congregation of farmer, laborer, and village artisan.
it be not that Mr. Bulstrode intend to frequent Lowick Church or to
reside at Stone Court for a good while to come: he have buy the
excellent farm and fine homestead simply as a retreat which he might
gradually enlarge as to the land and beautify as to the dwelling, until
it should be conducive to the divine glory that he should enter on it
as a residence, partially withdraw from his present exertion in the
administration of business, and throw more conspicuously on the side
of Gospel truth the weight of local land proprietorship, which
Providence might increase by unforeseen occasion of purchase. a
strong lead in this direction seem to have be give in the
surprising facility of get Stone Court, when every one have expect
that Mr. Rigg Featherstone would have clung to it as the Garden of
Eden. that be what poor old Peter himself have expect; have often,
in imagination, look up through the sod above he, and, unobstructe
by. perspective, see his frog-face legatee enjoy the fine old
place to the perpetual surprise and disappointment of other survivor.
but how little we know what would make paradise for our neighbor! we
judge from our own desire, and our neighbor themselves be not always
open enough even to throw out a hint of theirs. the cool and judicious
Joshua Rigg have not allow his parent to perceive that Stone Court be
anything less than the chief good in his estimation, and he have
certainly wish to call it his own. but as Warren Hastings look at
gold and thought of buy Daylesford, so Joshua Rigg look at Stone
Court and thought of buy gold. he have a very distinct and intense
vision of his chief good, the vigorous greed which he have inherit
have take a special form by dint of circumstance: and his chief good
be to be a moneychanger. from his early employment as an
errand-boy in a seaport, he have look through the window of the
moneychanger as other boy look through the window of the
pastry-cook; the fascination have work itself gradually into a deep
special passion; he mean, when he have property, to do many thing, one
of they be to marry a genteel young person; but these be all
accident and joy that imagination could dispense with. the one joy
after which his soul thirst be to have a money-changer's shop on a
much-frequent quay, to have lock all round he of which he hold the
key, and to look sublimely cool as he handle the breeding coin of
all nation, while helpless cupidity look at he enviously from the
other side of an iron lattice. the strength of that passion have be a
power enable he to master all the knowledge necessary to gratify it.
and when other be think that he have settle at Stone Court for
life, Joshua himself be think that the moment now be not far off
when he should settle on the North Quay with the good appointment in
safe and lock.
enough. we be concern with look at Joshua Rigg's sale of his
land from Mr. Bulstrode's point of view, and he interpret it as a
cheer dispensation conveying perhaps a sanction to a purpose which
he have for some time entertain without external encouragement; he
interpret it thus, but not too confidently, offer up his
thanksgiving in guarded phraseology. his doubt do not arise from the
possible relation of the event to Joshua Rigg's destiny, which
belong to the unmapped region not take under the providential
government, except perhaps in an imperfect colonial way; but they arise
from reflect that this dispensation too might be a chastisement for
himself, as Mr. Farebrother's induction to the living clearly be.
this be not what Mr. Bulstrode say to any man for the sake of
deceive he: it be what he say to himself--it be as genuinely his
mode of explain event as any theory of yours may be, if you happen
to disagree with he. for the egoism which enter into our theory
do not affect their sincerity; rather, the more our egoism be
satisfied, the more robust be our belief.
however, whether for sanction or for chastisement, Mr. Bulstrode,
hardly fifteen month after the death of Peter Featherstone, have become
the proprietor of Stone Court, and what Peter would say "if he be
worthy to know," have become an inexhaustible and consolatory subject of
conversation to his disappointed relative. the table be now turn
on that dear brother depart, and to contemplate the frustration of
his cunning by the superior cunning of thing in general be a cud of
delight to Solomon. Mrs. Waule have a melancholy triumph in the proof
that it do not answer to make false featherstone and cut off the
genuine; and Sister Martha receive the news in the Chalky Flats say,
"dear, dear! then the Almighty could have be none so pleased with the
almshouse after all."
Affectionate Mrs. Bulstrode be particularly glad of the advantage
which her husband's health be likely to get from the purchase of Stone
Court. few day pass without his rid thither and look over
some part of the farm with the bailiff, and the evening be delicious
in that quiet spot, when the new hay-rick lately set up be send
forth odor to mingle with the breath of the rich old garden. one
evening, while the sun be still above the horizon and burn in
golden lamp among the great walnut bough, Mr. Bulstrode be pause
on horseback outside the front gate wait for Caleb Garth, who have
meet he by appointment to give an opinion on a question of stable
drainage, and be now advise the bailiff in the rick-yard.
Mr. Bulstrode be conscious of be in a good spiritual frame and more
than usually serene, under the influence of his innocent recreation.
he be doctrinally convince that there be a total absence of merit in
himself; but that doctrinal conviction may be hold without pain when
the sense of demerit do not take a distinct shape in memory and
revive the tingling of shame or the pang of remorse. Nay, it may be
hold with intense satisfaction when the depth of our sinning be but a
measure for the depth of forgiveness, and a clench proof that we be
peculiar instrument of the divine intention. the memory have as many
mood as the temper, and shift its scenery like a diorama. at this
moment Mr. Bulstrode feel as if the sunshine be all one with that of
far-off evening when he be a very young man and use to go out
preach beyond Highbury. and he would willingly have have that
service of exhortation in prospect now. the text be there still,
and so be his own facility in expound they. his brief reverie be
interrupt by the return of Caleb Garth, who also be on horseback,
and be just shake his bridle before start, when he exclaimed--
"bless my heart! whatbe this fellow in black come along the lane?
hebe like one of those man one see about after the race."
Mr. Bulstrode turn his horse and look along the lane, but make no
reply. the comer be our slight acquaintance Mr. Raffles, whose
appearance present no other change than such as be due to a suit of
black and a crape hat-band. he be within three yard of the horseman
now, and they could see the flash of recognition in his face as he
whirl his stick upward, look all the while at Mr. Bulstrode, and
at last exclaiming:--
"by Jove, Nick, itbe you! I couldnot be mistake, though the
five-and-twenty year have play old Boguy with we both! how be you,
eh? you donot expect to see _I_ here. come, shake we by the hand."
to say that Mr. Raffles' manner be rather excited would be only one
mode of say that it be evening. Caleb Garth could see that there
be a moment of struggle and hesitation in Mr. Bulstrode, but it end
in his put out his hand coldly to Raffles and saying--
"I do not indeed expect to see you in this remote country place."
"well, it belong to a stepson of mine," say Raffles, adjust
himself in a swagger attitude. "I come to see he here before. Ibe
not so surprised at see you, old fellow, because I pick up a
letter--what you may call a providential thing. itbe uncommonly
fortunate I meet you, though; for I donot care about see my stepson:
hebe not affectionate, and his poor mother's go now. to tell the
truth, I come out of love to you, Nick: I come to get your address,
for--look here!" Raffles draw a crumpled paper from his pocket.
almost any other man than Caleb Garth might have be tempt to linger
on the spot for the sake of hear all he could about a man whose
acquaintance with Bulstrode seem to imply passage in the banker's
life so unlike anything that be know of he in Middlemarch that they
must have the nature of a secret to pique curiosity. but Caleb be
peculiar: certain human tendency which be commonly strong be
almost absent from his mind; and one of these be curiosity about
personal affair. especially if there be anything discreditable to be
find out concern another man, Caleb prefer not to know it; and
if he have to tell anybody under he that his evil doing be
discover, he be more embarrassed than the culprit. he now spur
his horse, and say, "I wish you good evening, Mr. Bulstrode; I must
be get home," set off at a trot.
"you donot put your full address to this letter," Raffles continue.
"that be not like the first-rate man of business you use to be. 'the
shrubs,'--they may be anywhere: you live near at hand, eh?--have cut
the London concern altogether--perhaps turn country squire--have a
rural mansion to invite I to. Lord, how many year it be ago! the
old lady must have be dead a pretty long while--go to glory without
the pain of know how poor her daughter be, eh? but, by Jove!
yoube very pale and pasty, Nick. Come, if yoube go home, Iwill
walk by your side."
Mr. Bulstrode's usual paleness have in fact take an almost deathly hue.
five minute before, the expanse of his life have be submerge in its
evening sunshine which shine backward to its remembered morning: sin
seem to be a question of doctrine and inward penitence, humiliation
an exercise of the closet, the bearing of his deed a matter of private
vision adjust solely by spiritual relation and conception of the
divine purpose. and now, as if by some hideous magic, this loud red
figure have rise before he in unmanageable solidity--an incorporate
past which have not enter into his imagination of chastisement. but
Mr. Bulstrode's thought be busy, and he be not a man to act or speak
rashly.
"I be go home," he say, "but I can defer my ride a little. and
you can, if you please, rest here."
"thank you," say Raffles, make a grimace. "I donot care now about
see my stepson. Iwould rather go home with you."
"your stepson, if Mr. Rigg Featherstone be he, be here no long. I
be master here now."
Raffles open wide eye, and give a long whistle of surprise, before
he say, "well then, Ihave no objection. Ihave have enough walk from
the coach-road. I never be much of a walker, or rider either. what I
like be a smart vehicle and a spirited cob. I be always a little
heavy in the saddle. what a pleasant surprise it must be to you to see
I, old fellow!" he continue, as they turn towards the house. "you
donot say so; but you never take your luck heartily--you be always
think of improve the occasion--you'd such a gift for improve
your luck."
Mr. Raffles seem greatly to enjoy his own wit, and Swung his leg in a
swaggering manner which be rather too much for his companion's
judicious patience.
"if I remember rightly," Mr. Bulstrode observe, with chill anger, "our
acquaintance many year ago have not the sort of intimacy which you be
now assume, Mr. Raffles. any service you desire of I will be the
more readily render if you will avoid a tone of familiarity which do
not lie in our former intercourse, and can hardly be warrant by more
than twenty year of separation."
"you donot like be call Nick? why, I always call you Nick in my
heart, and though lose to sight, to memory dear. by Jove! my feeling
have ripen for you like fine old cognac. I hope youhave get some in
the house now. Josh fill my flask well the last time."
Mr. Bulstrode have not yet fully learn that even the desire for cognac
be not strong in Raffles than the desire to torment, and that a hint
of annoyance always serve he as a fresh cue. but it be at least
clear that further objection be useless, and Mr. Bulstrode, in give
order to the housekeeper for the accommodation of the guest, have a
resolute air of quietude.
there be the comfort of think that this housekeeper have be in the
service of Rigg also, and might accept the idea that Mr. Bulstrode
entertain Raffles merely as a friend of her former master.
when there be food and drink spread before his visitor in the
wainscote parlor, and no witness in the room, Mr. Bulstrode said--
"your habit and mine be so different, Mr. Raffles, that we can hardly
enjoy each other's society. the wise plan for both of we will
therefore be to part as soon as possible. since you say that you
wish to meet I, you probably consider that you have some business
to transact with I. but under the circumstance I will invite you to
remain here for the night, and I will myself ride over here early
to-morrow morning--before breakfast, in fact, when I can receive any
Communication you have to make to I."
"with all my heart," say Raffles; "this be a comfortable place--a
little dull for a continuance; but I can put up with it for a night,
with this good liquor and the prospect of see you again in the
morning. yoube a much well host than my stepson be; but Josh owe
I a bit of a grudge for marry his mother; and between you and I
there be never anything but kindness."
Mr. Bulstrode, hop that the peculiar mixture of joviality and
sneer in Raffles' manner be a good deal the effect of drink, have
determine to wait till he be quite sober before he spend more word
upon he. but he ride home with a terribly lucid vision of the
difficulty there would be in arrange any result that could be
permanently count on with this man. it be inevitable that he should
wish to get rid of John Raffles, though his reappearance could not be
regard as lie outside the divine plan. the spirit of evil might
have send he to threaten Mr. Bulstrode's subversion as an instrument
of good; but the threat must have be permit, and be a
chastisement of a new kind. it be an hour of anguish for he very
different from the hour in which his struggle have be securely
private, and which have end with a sense that his secret misdeed be
pardon and his service accept. those misdeed even when
commit--have they not be half sanctify by the singleness of his
desire to devote himself and all he possess to the furtherance of the
divine scheme? and be he after all to become a mere stone of
stumbling and a rock of offence? for who would understand the work
within he? who would not, when there be the pretext of cast
disgrace upon he, confound his whole life and the truth he have
espouse, in one heap of obloquy?
in his close meditation the life-long habit of Mr. Bulstrode's mind
clothe his most egoistic terror in doctrinal reference to superhuman
end. but even while we be talk and meditate about the earth's
orbit and the solar system, what we feel and adjust our movement to be
the stable earth and the change day. and now within all the
automatic succession of theoretic phrase--distinct and inmost as the
shiver and the ache of oncoming fever when we be discuss abstract
pain, be the forecast of disgrace in the presence of his neighbor and
of his own wife. for the pain, as well as the public estimate of
disgrace, depend on the amount of previous profession. to man who
only aim at escape felony, nothing short of the prisoner's dock be
disgrace. but Mr. Bulstrode have aim at be an eminent Christian.
it be not more than half-past seven in the morning when he again
reach Stone Court. the fine old place never look more like a
delightful home than at that moment; the great white lily be in
flower, the nasturtium, their pretty leaf all silver with dew,
be run away over the low stone wall; the very noise all around
have a heart of peace within they. but everything be spoil for the
owner as he walk on the gravel in front and await the descent of
Mr. Raffles, with whom he be condemn to breakfast.
it be not long before they be seat together in the wainscote
parlor over their tea and toast, which be as much as Raffles care to
take at that early hour. the difference between his morning and
evening self be not so great as his companion have imagine that it
might be; the delight in tormenting be perhaps even the strong
because his spirit be rather less highly pitch. certainly his
manner seem more disagreeable by the morning light.
"as I have little time to spare, Mr. Raffles," say the banker, who
could hardly do more than sip his tea and break his toast without
eat it, "I shall be oblige if you will mention at once the ground
on which you wish to meet with I. I presume that you have a home
elsewhere and will be glad to return to it."
"why, if a man have get any heart, donot he want to see an old friend,
nick?--i must call you Nick--we always do call you young Nick when we
know you mean to marry the old widow. some say you have a handsome
family likeness to old Nick, but that be your mother's fault, call
you Nicholas. benot you glad to see I again? I expect an invite
to stay with you at some pretty place. my own establishment be break
up now my wife's dead. Ihave no particular attachment to any spot; I
would as soon settle hereabout as anywhere."
"may I ask why you return from America? I consider that the strong
wish you express to go there, when an adequate sum be furnish, be
tantamount to an engagement that you would remain there for life."
"never know that a wish to go to a place be the same thing as a wish
to stay. but I do stay a matter of ten year; it donot suit I to
stay any long. and Ibe not go again, Nick." here Mr. Raffles
wink slowly as he look at Mr. Bulstrode.
"do you wish to be settle in any business? what be your call now?"
"thank you, my calling be to enjoy myself as much as I can. I donot
care about work any more. if I do anything it would be a little
travel in the tobacco line--or something of that sort, which take
a man into agreeable company. but not without an independence to fall
back upon. thatbe what I want: Ibe not so strong as I be, Nick,
though Ihave get more color than you. I want an independence."
"that could be supply to you, if you would engage to keep at a
distance," say Mr. Bulstrode, perhaps with a little too much eagerness
in his undertone.
"that must be as it suit my convenience," say Raffles coolly. "I see
no reason why I shouldnot make a few acquaintance hereabout. Ibe not
ashamed of myself as company for anybody. I drop my portmanteau at
the turnpike when I get down--change of linen--genuine--honor bright--more
than front and wristband; and with this suit of mourning, strap
and everything, I should do you credit among the nob here." Mr.
Raffles have push away his chair and look down at himself,
particularly at his strap. his chief intention be to annoy
Bulstrode, but he really think that his appearance now would produce
a good effect, and that he be not only handsome and witty, but clothe in
a mourning style which imply solid connection.
"if you intend to rely on I in any way, Mr. Raffles," say Bulstrode,
after a moment's pause, "you will expect to meet my wish."
"ah, to be sure," say Raffles, with a mocking cordiality. "donot I
always do it? Lord, you make a pretty thing out of I, and I get but
little. Ihave often think since, I might have do well by tell
the old woman that Iwould find her daughter and her grandchild: it would
have suit my feeling well; Ihave get a soft place in my heart. but
youhave bury the old lady by this time, I suppose--itbe all one to she
now. and youhave get your fortune out of that profitable business which
have such a blessing on it. youhave take to be a nob, buy land,
be a country bashaw. still in the dissent line, eh? still
godly? or take to the Church as more genteel?"
this time Mr. Raffles' slow wink and slight protrusion of his tongue
be bad than a nightmare, because it hold the certitude that it be
not a nightmare, but a wake misery. Mr. Bulstrode feel a shuddering
nausea, and do not speak, but be consider diligently whether he
should not leave Raffles to do as he would, and simply defy he as a
slanderer. the man would soon show himself disreputable enough to make
people disbelieve he. "but not when he tell any ugly-look truth
about _you_," say discern consciousness. and again: it seem no
wrong to keep Raffles at a distance, but Mr. Bulstrode shrink from the
direct falsehood of deny true statement. it be one thing to look
back on forgive sin, nay, to explain questionable conformity to lax
custom, and another to enter deliberately on the necessity of
falsehood.
but since Bulstrode do not speak, Raffles run on, by way of use time
to the utmost.
"Ihave not have such fine luck as you, by Jove! thing go confoundedly
with I in New York; those Yankees be cool hand, and a man of
gentlemanly feeling have no chance with they. I marry when I come
back--a nice woman in the tobacco trade--very fond of I--but the
trade be restrict, as we say. she have be settle there a good
many year by a friend; but there be a son too much in the case. Josh
and I never hit it off. however, I make the most of the position, and
Ihave always take my glass in good company. itbe be all on the
square with I; Ibe as open as the day. you willnot take it ill of I
that I donot look you up before. Ihave get a complaint that make I a
little dilatory. I think you be trade and pray away in London
still, and donot find you there. but you see I be send to you,
Nick--perhaps for a blessing to both of we."
Mr. Raffles end with a jocose snuffle: no man feel his intellect more
superior to religious canot. and if the cunning which calculate on the
mean feeling in man could be, call intellect, he have his share,
for under the blurting rallying tone with which he speak to Bulstrode,
there be an evident selection of statement, as if they have be so
many move at chess. meanwhile Bulstrode have determine on his move,
and he say, with gather resolution--
"you will do well to reflect, Mr. Raffles, that it be possible for a
man to overreach himself in the effort to secure undue advantage.
although I be not in any way bind to you, I be willing to supply you
with a regular annuity--in quarterly payment--so long as you fulfil a
promise to remain at a distance from this neighborhood. it be in your
power to choose. if you insist on remain here, even for a short
time, you will get nothing from I. I shall decline to know you."
"ha, ha!" say Raffles, with an affected explosion, "that remind I of
a droll dog of a thief who decline to know the constable."
"your allusion be lose on I sir," say Bulstrode, with white heat;
"the law have no hold on I either through your agency or any other."
"you cannot understand a joke, my good fellow. I only mean that I
should never decline to know you. but let we be serious. your
quarterly payment willnot quite suit I. I like my freedom."
here Raffles rise and stalk once or twice up and down the room,
swing his leg, and assume an air of masterly meditation. at last
he stop opposite Bulstrode, and say, "Iwill tell you what! give we
a couple of hundred--come, thatbe modest--and Iwill go away--honor
bright!--pick up my portmanteau and go away. but I shall not give up
my Liberty for a dirty annuity. I shall come and go where I like.
perhaps it may suit I to stay away, and correspond with a friend;
perhaps not. have you the money with you?"
"no, I have one hundred," say Bulstrode, feel the immediate
riddance too great a relief to be reject on the ground of future
uncertainty. "I will forward you the other if you will mention an
address."
"no, Iwill wait here till you bring it," say Raffles. "Iwill take a
stroll and have a snack, and youwill be back by that time."
Mr. Bulstrode's sickly body, shatter by the agitation he have go
through since the last evening, make he feel abjectly in the power of
this loud invulnerable man. at that moment he snatch at a temporary
repose to be win on any term. he be rise to do what Raffles
suggest, when the latter say, lift up his finger as if with a
sudden recollection--
"I do have another look after Sarah again, though I donot tell you;
Ihave a tender conscience about that pretty young woman. I donot find
she, but I find out her husband's name, and I make a note of it. but
hang it, I lose my pocketbook. however, if I hear it, I should know
it again. Ihave get my faculty as if I be in my prime, but name
wear out, by Jove! sometimes Ibe no well than a confound tax-paper
before the name be fill in. however, if I hear of she and her
family, you shall know, Nick. youwould like to do something for she, now
shebe your step-daughter."
"Doubtless," say Mr. Bulstrode, with the usual steady look of his
light-gray eye; "though that might reduce my power of assist you."
as he walk out of the room, Raffles wink slowly at his back, and
then turn towards the window to watch the banker rid away--virtually
at his command. his lip first curl with a smile and then open
with a short triumphant laugh.
"but what the deuce be the name?" he presently say, half aloud,
scratch his head, and wrinkle his brow horizontally. he have not
really care or think about this point of forgetfulness until it
occur to he in his invention of annoyance for Bulstrode.
"it begin with L; it be almost all l's I fancy," he go on, with a
sense that he be get hold of the slippery name. but the hold be
too slight, and he soon get tired of this mental chase; for few man
be more impatient of private occupation or more in need of make
themselves continually hear than Mr. Raffles. he prefer use his
time in pleasant conversation with the bailiff and the housekeeper,
from whom he gather as much as he want to know about Mr.
Bulstrode's position in Middlemarch.
after all, however, there be a dull space of time which need
relieve with bread and cheese and ale, and when he be seat alone
with these resource in the wainscoted parlor, he suddenly slap his
knee, and exclaim, "Ladislaw!" that action of memory which he have
try to set go, and have abandon in despair, have suddenly
complete itself without conscious effort--a common experience,
agreeable as a complete sneeze, even if the name remember be of no
value. raffle immediately take out his pocket-book, and write down
the name, not because he expect to use it, but merely for the sake of
not be at a loss if he ever do happen to want it. he be not go
to tell Bulstrode: there be no actual good in telling, and to a mind
like that of Mr. Raffles there be always probable good in a secret.
he be satisfied with his present success, and by three o'clock that
day he have take up his portmanteau at the turnpike and mount the
coach, relieve Mr. Bulstrode's eye of an ugly black spot on the
landscape at Stone Court, but not relieve he of the dread that the
black spot might reappear and become inseparable even from the vision
of his hearth.
BOOK VI.
the WIDOW and the wife.
CHAPTER LIV.
"Negli occhi porta la mia donna Amore;
per che si fa gentil eio ch'ella mira:
Ov'ella passa, ogni uom ver lei si gira,
e cui saluta fa tremar lo core.
Sicche, bassando il viso, tutto smore,
E d'ogni suo difetto allor sospira:
Fuggon dinanzi a lei Superbia ed Ira:
Aiutatemi, donne, a farle onore.
Ogni dolcezza, ogni pensiero umile
Nasee nel core a chi parlar la sente;
ond' e beato chi prima la vide.
Quel ch'ella par quand' un poco sorride,
non si pub dicer, ne tener a mente,
Si e nuovo miracolo gentile."
--DANTE: la Vita Nuova.
by that delightful morning when the hay-rick at Stone Court be
scent the air quite impartially, as if Mr. Raffles have be a guest
worthy of fine incense, Dorothea have again take up her abode at
Lowick Manor. after three month Freshitt have become rather
oppressive: to sit like a model for Saint Catherine look rapturously
at Celia's baby would not do for many hour in the day, and to remain
in that momentous babe's presence with persistent disregard be a
course that could not have be tolerate in a childless sister.
Dorothea would have be capable of carry baby joyfully for a mile
if there have be nee, and of love it the more tenderly for that
labor; but to an aunt who do not recognize her infant nephew as
Bouddha, and have nothing to do for he but to admire, his behavior be
apt to appear monotonous, and the interest of watch he exhaustible.
this possibility be quite hide from Celia, who feel that Dorothea's
childless widowhood fall in quite prettily with the birth of little
Arthur (baby be name after Mr. Brooke).
"Dodo be just the creature not to mind about have anything of her
own--child or anything!" say Celia to her husband. "and if she
have have a baby, it never could have be such a dear as Arthur. could
it, James?
"not if it have be like Casaubon," say Sir James, conscious of some
indirectness in his answer, and of hold a strictly private opinion
as to the perfection of his first-bear.
"no! just imagine! really it be a mercy," say Celia; "and I think it
be very nice for Dodo to be a widow. she can be just as fond of our
baby as if it be her own, and she can have as many notion of her own
as she like."
"it be a pity she be not a queen," say the devout Sir James.
"but what should we have be then? we must have be something else,"
say Celia, object to so laborious a flight of imagination. "I like
she well as she be."
hence, when she find that Dorothea be make arrangement for her
final departure to Lowick, Celia raise her eyebrow with

sarcasm.
"what will you do at Lowick, Dodo? you say yourself there be nothing
to be do there: everybody be so clean and well off, it make you
quite melancholy. and here you have be so happy go all about
Tipton with Mr. Garth into the bad backyard. and now uncle be
abroad, you and Mr. Garth can have it all your own way; and I be sure
James do everything you tell he."
"I shall often come here, and I shall see how baby grow all the
well," say Dorothea.
"but you will never see he wash," say Celia; "and that be quite the
good part of the day." she be almost pout: it do seem to her very
hard in Dodo to go away from the baby when she might stay.
"Dear Kitty, I will come and stay all night on purpose," say Dorothea;
"but I want to be alone now, and in my own home. I wish to know the
farebrother well, and to talk to Mr. Farebrother about what there be
to be do in Middlemarch."
Dorothea's native strength of will be no long all convert into
resolute submission. she have a great yearning to be at Lowick, and be
simply determined to go, not feel bound to tell all her reason.
but every one around she disapprove. Sir James be much pained, and
offer that they should all migrate to Cheltenham for a few month

could hardly know what to propose if Cheltenham be reject.
the Dowager Lady Chettam, just return from a visit to her daughter in

invite to accept the office of companion to Mrs. Casaubon: it be not
credible that Dorothea as a young widow would think of live alone in
the house at Lowick. Mrs. Vigo have be reader and secretary to royal
personage, and in point of knowledge and sentiment even Dorothea
could have nothing to object to she.
Mrs. Cadwallader say, privately, "you will certainly go mad in that
house alone, my dear. you will see vision. we have all get to exert
ourselves a little to keep sane, and call thing by the same name as
other people call they by. to be sure, for young son and woman who
have no money, it be a sort of provision to go mad: they be take care
of then. but you must not run into that. I dare say you be a little
bored here with our good dowager; but think what a bore you might
become yourself to your fellow-creature if you be always play
tragedy queen and take thing sublimely. sit alone in that
library at Lowick you may fancy yourself rule the weather; you must
get a few people round you who wouldnot believe you if you tell they.
that be a good lower medicine."
"I never call everything by the same name that all the people about
I do," say Dorothea, stoutly.
"but I suppose you have find out your mistake, my dear," say Mrs.

Dorothea be aware of the sting, but it do not hurt she. "no," she
say, "I still think that the great part of the world be mistaken
about many thing. surely one may be sane and yet think so, since the
great part of the world have often have to come round from its opinion."
Mrs. Cadwallader say no more on that point to Dorothea, but to her
husband she remark, "it will be well for she to marry again as soon
as it be proper, if one could get she among the right people. of
course the Chettams would not wish it. but I see clearly a husband be
the good thing to keep she in order. if we be not so poor I would
invite Lord Triton. he will be marquis some day, and there be no
deny that she would make a good marchioness: she look handsomer
than ever in her mourning."
"my dear Elinor, do let the poor woman alone. such contrivance be of
no use," say the easy Rector.
"no use? how be match make, except by bring man and woman
together? and it be a shame that her uncle should have run away and
shut up the Grange just now. there ought to be plenty of eligible
match invite to Freshitt and the Grange. Lord Triton be precisely
the man: full of plan for make the people happy in a soft-headed
sort of way. that would just suit Mrs. Casaubon."
"let Mrs. Casaubon choose for herself, Elinor."
"that be the nonsense you wise man talk! how can she choose if she have
no variety to choose from? a woman's choice usually mean take the
only man she can get. mark my word, Humphrey. if her friend donot
exert themselves, there will be a bad business than the Casaubon
business yet."
"for heaven's sake donot touch on that topic, Elinor! it be a very sore
point with Sir James he would be deeply offend if you enter on it
to he unnecessarily."
"I have never enter on it," say Mrs Cadwallader, open her hand.
"Celia tell I all about the will at the beginning, without any asking
of mine."
"yes, yes; but they want the thing hush up, and I understand that the
young fellow be go out of the neighborhood."
Mrs. Cadwallader say nothing, but give she husband three significant
nod, with a very sarcastic expression in her dark eye.
Dorothea quietly persist in spite of remonstrance and persuasion. so
by the end of June the shutter be all open at Lowick Manor, and
the morning gaze calmly into the library, shin on the row of
note-book as it shin on the weary waste plant with huge stone,
the mute memorial of a forget faith; and the evening laden with

oftenest to sit. at first she walk into every room, question the

the note-book as she imagine that he would wish to see they, in
orderly sequence. the pity which have be the restrain compelling
motive in her life with he still clung about his image, even while she
remonstrate with he in indignant thought and tell he that he be
unjust. one little act of hers may perhaps be smile at as

she carefully enclose and seal, write within the envelope, "I
could not use it. do you not see now that I could not submit my soul
to your, by work hopelessly at what I have no belief in--Dorothea?"
then she deposit the paper in her own desk.
that silent colloquy be perhaps only the more earnest because
underneath and through it all there be always the deep longing which
have really determine she to come to Lowick. the longing be to see
Will Ladislaw. she do not know any good that could come of their
meeting: she be helpless; her hand have be tie from make up to
he for any unfairness in his lot. but her soul thirst to see he.
how could it be otherwise? if a princess in the day of enchantment
have see a four-footed creature from among those which live in herd

choice and beseech, what would she think of in her journeying, what
would she look for when the herd pass she? surely for the gaze
which have find she, and which she would know again. life would be no
well than candle-light tinsel and daylight rubbish if our spirit
be not touch by what have be, to issue of longing and constancy.
it be true that Dorothea want to know the farebrother well, and
especially to talk to the new rector, but also true that remember

she count on Willbe come to Lowick to see the Farebrother family.
the very first Sunday, _before_ she enter the church, she see he as

"probably some of Mr. Farebrother's Middlemarch hearer may follow he

"if they be wise they will, Mrs. Casaubon," say the old lady. "I see

stand up; the dress be an experiment in the utmost lay on of

her reverie be break by Tantripp, who come to say that Mr. Ladislaw
be below, and beg permission to see Madam if it be not too early.

the drawing-room be the most neutral room in the house to she--the
one least associate with the trial of her married life: the damask
match the wood-work, which be all white and gold; there be two
tall mirror and table with nothing on they--in brief, it be a room
where you have no reason for sit in one place rather than in

"be you go away immediately?"

Rome, I think you only care for poetry and art, and the thing that
adorn life for we who be well off. but now I know you think about the
rest of the world."

try hard to reconcile the utmost pride with the utmost effort to get

she be not aware how long it be before she answer. she have turn

will speak at random: he be merely vent his petulance; it be a

the word cut Dorothea to the heart, and make her relent. she answer

after shake hand with Dorothea, he bow as slightly as possible to

one day that she go to Freshitt to fulfil her promise of stay all

be go on a fishing excursion. it be a warm evening, and even in

"Dear Dodo, do throw off that cap. I be sure your dress must make you
feel ill."
"I be so used to the cap--it have become a sort of shell," say

"I must see you without it; it make we all warm," say Celia, throw

"my dear Celia," say Lady Chettam, "a widow must wear her mourning at

they say Captain Beevor drag she about by the hair, and hold up

"oh, if she take the wrong man!" say Mrs. Cadwallader, who be in a

priority be a poor recommendation in a husband if he have get no other.
I would rather have a good second husband than an indifferent first."
"my dear, your clever tongue run away with you," say Lady Chettam.
"I be sure you would be the last woman to marry again prematurely, if

"oh, I make no vow; it might be a necessary economy. it be lawful to
marry again, I suppose; else we might as well be Hindoos instead of

the consequence, and one who do it twice over deserve her fate.
but if she can marry blood, beauty, and bravery--the soon the

later in the evening, after Mrs. Cadwallader be go, Celia say

remark. to his secret feeling there be something repulsive in a

woman of one-and-twenty; the practice of "the world" be to treat of
a young widow's second marriage as certain and probably near, and to
smile with meaning if the widow act accordingly. but if Dorothea do
choose to espouse her solitude, he feel that the resolution would well
become she.
CHAPTER LVI.
"how happy be he bear and teach
that serveth not another's will;

and have nothing yet hath all."
--sir HENRY WOTTON.
Dorothea's confidence in Caleb Garth's knowledge, which have begin on
her hearing that he approve of her cottage, have grow fast during she
stay at Freshitt, Sir James have induce she to take ride over the
two estate in company with himself and Caleb, who quite return she

business most uncommon in a woman. it must be remember that by
"business" Caleb never mean money transaction, but the skilful
application of labor.
"most uncommon!" repeat Caleb. "she say a thing I often use to
think myself when I be a lad:--'mr. Garth, I should like to feel, if I
live to be old, that I have improve a great piece of land and build a
great many good cottage, because the work be of a healthy kind while
it be be do, and after it be do, man be the well for it.'
those be the very word: she see into thing in that way."
"but womanly, I hope," say Mrs. Garth, half suspect that Mrs.
Casaubon might not hold the true principle of subordination.
"oh, you cannot think!" say Caleb, shake his head. "you would like

like music. bless I! it remind I of bit in the 'messiah'--'and
straightway there appear a multitude of the heavenly host, praise
God and say;' it have a tone with it that satisfy your ear."
Caleb be very fond of music, and when he could afford it go to hear
an oratorio that come within his reach, return from it with a
profound reverence for this mighty structure of tone, which make he
sit meditatively, look on the floor and throw much unutterable
language into his outstretche hand.
with this good understanding between they, it be natural that Dorothea
ask Mr. Garth to undertake any business connect with the three
farm and the numerous tenement attach to Lowick Manor; indeed, his
expectation of get work for two be be fast fulfil. as he
say, "business breed." and one form of business which be begin
to breed just then be the construction of railway. a project line
be to run through Lowick parish where the cattle have hitherto graze
in a peace unbroken by astonishment; and thus it happen that the
infant struggle of the railway system enter into the affair of
Caleb Garth, and determine the course of this history with regard to
two person who be dear to he. the submarine railway may have its
difficulty; but the bed of the sea be not divide among various
land proprietor with claim for damage not only measurable but
sentimental. in the hundred to which Middlemarch belong railway
be as exciting a topic as the Reform Bill or the imminent horror of
Cholera, and those who hold the most decide view on the subject be
woman and landholder. woman both old and young regard travel by
steam as presumptuous and dangerous, and argue against it by say
that nothing should induce they to get into a railway carriage; while
proprietor, differ from each other in their argument as much as
Mr. Solomon Featherstone differ from Lord Medlicote, be yet
unanimous in the opinion that in sell land, whether to the Enemy of
mankind or to a company oblige to purchase, these pernicious agency
must be make to pay a very high price to landowner for permission to
injure mankind.
but the slow wit, such as Mr. Solomon and Mrs. Waule, who both
occupy land of their own, take a long time to arrive at this
conclusion, their mind halt at the vivid conception of what it
would be to cut the Big Pasture in two, and turn it into three-cornered
bit, which would be "nohow;" while accommodation-bridge and high
payment be remote and incredible.
"the cow will all cast their calf, brother," say Mrs. Waule, in a
tone of deep melancholy, "if the railway come across the Near Close;
and I shouldnot wonder at the mare too, if she be in foal. itbe a
poor tale if a widow's property be to be spade away, and the law say
nothing to it. whatbe to hinder them from cut right and leave if
they begin? itbe well know, _i_ cannot fight."
"the good way would be to say nothing, and set somebody on to send them
away with a flea in their ear, when they come spy and measure,"
say Solomon. "folk do that about Brassing, by what I can
understand. itbe all a pretence, if the truth be know, about their
be force to take one way. let them go cut in another parish.
and I donot believe in any pay to make amend for bring a lot of
ruffian to trample your crop. wherebe a company's pocket?"
"Brother Peter, God forgive he, get money out of a company," say Mrs.
Waule. "but that be for the manganese. that benot for railway to
blow you to piece right and left."
"well, therebe this to be say, Jane," Mr. Solomon conclude, lower
his voice in a cautious manner--"the more spoke we put in their wheel,
the more theywill pay we to let them go on, if they must come whether or
not."
this reasoning of Mr. Solomon's be perhaps less thorough than he
imagine, his cunning bearing about the same relation to the course of
railway as the cunning of a diplomatist bear to the general chill or
catarrh of the solar system. but he set about act on his view in a
thoroughly diplomatic manner, by stimulate suspicion. his side of
Lowick be the most remote from the village, and the house of the
labor people be either lone cottage or be collect in a hamlet
call Frick, where a water-mill and some stone-pit make a little
centre of slow, heavy-shoulder industry.
in the absence of any precise idea as to what railway be, public
opinion in Frick be against they; for the human mind in that grassy
corner have not the proverbial tendency to admire the unknown, hold
rather that it be likely to be against the poor man, and that
suspicion be the only wise attitude with regard to it. even the rumor
of Reform have not yet excite any millennial expectation in Frick,
there be no definite promise in it, as of gratuitous grain to
fatten Hiram Ford's pig, or of a publican at the "Weights and Scales"
who would brew beer for nothing, or of an offer on the part of the
three neighbor farmer to raise wage during winter. and without
distinct good of this kind in its promise, Reform seem on a footing
with the bragging of pedler, which be a hint for distrust to every
know person. the man of Frick be not ill-fed, and be less give
to fanaticism than to a strong muscular suspicion; less inclined to
believe that they be peculiarly care for by heaven, than to regard
heaven itself as rather disposed to take they in--a disposition
observable in the weather.
thus the mind of Frick be exactly of the sort for Mr. Solomon
Featherstone to work upon, he have more plenteous idea of the same
order, with a suspicion of heaven and earth which be well feed and
more entirely at leisure. Solomon be overseer of the road at that
time, and on his slow-pace cob often take his round by Frick to look
at the workman get the stone there, pause with a mysterious
deliberation, which might have mislead you into suppose that he have
some other reason for stay than the mere want of impulse to move.
after look for a long while at any work that be go on, he would
raise his eye a little and look at the horizon; finally he would shake
his bridle, touch his horse with the whip, and get it to move slowly
onward. the hour-hand of a clock be quick by comparison with Mr.
Solomon, who have an agreeable sense that he could afford to be slow.
he be in the habit of pausing for a cautious, vaguely design chat
with every hedger or ditcher on his way, and be especially willing to
listen even to news which he have hear before, feel himself at an
advantage over all narrator in partially disbelieve they. one day,
however, he get into a dialogue with Hiram Ford, a wagoner, in which he
himself contribute information. he wish to know whether Hiram have
see fellow with stave and instrument spy about: they call
themselves railroad people, but there be no tell what they be or
what they mean to do. the least they pretend be that they be
go to cut Lowick Parish into six and seven.
"why, therewill be no stirrin' from one pla-ace to another," say Hiram,
thinking of his wagon and horse.
"not a bit," say Mr. Solomon. "and cut up fine land such as this
parish! let them go into Tipton, say I. but therebe no knowing what
there be at the bottom of it. traffic be what they put for'ard; but
itbe to do harm to the land and the poor man in the long-run."
"why, theybe Lunnon chap, I reckon," say Hiram, who have a dim notion
of London as a centre of hostility to the country.
"ay, to be sure. and in some part against Brassing, by what Ihave
hear say, the folk fall on them when they be spy, and break their
peep-hole as they carry, and drive them away, so as they know well
than come again."
"it war good foon, Iwould be bind," say Hiram, whose fun be much
restrict by circumstance.
"well, I wouldnot meddle with them myself," say Solomon. "but some say
this country's see its good day, and the sign be, as itbe be

crack."
"Iwill crack _my_ whip about their ear'n, afore they bring it to that,
though," say Hiram, while Mr. Solomon, shake his bridle, move
onward.
nettle-seed need no digging. the ruin of this countryside by

"yo're a coward, yo be. Yo git off your horse, young measter, and

at that time the opinion exist that it be beneath a gentleman to

"for there can live no hatred in thine eye,

--SHAKESPEARE: sonnet.
at the time when Mr. Vincy utter that presentiment about Rosamond,

hinder your share in my disgrace--if there be disgrace."
Rosamond do not answer immediately, but at last she say, "very well,
I will stay at home."
"I shall not touch these jewel, Rosy. take they away again. but I

pack up and send at once."
"the servant will know _that_," say Rosamond, with the slight
touch of sarcasm.
"well, we must meet some disagreeable as necessity. where be the
ink, I wonder?" say Lydgate, rise, and throw the account on the
large table where he mean to write.
Rosamond go to reach the inkstand, and after set it on the table

his arm round she and draw she towards he, saying--
"Come, darling, let we make the good of thing. it will only be for a
time, I hope, that we shall have to be stingy and particular. kiss I."
his native warm-heartedness take a great deal of quenching, and it be a
part of manliness for a husband to feel keenly the fact that an

accord be recover for the time. but Lydgate could not help look

expenditure and the necessity for a complete change in their way of
live.

CHAPTER LXII.
"he be a squyer of lowe degre,
that love the king's daughter of Hungrie.
--Old Romance.
will Ladislaw's mind be now wholly bent on see Dorothea again, and

Ladislaw feel the awkwardness of ask for more last word. his

CHAPTER LXIII.
these little thing be great to little man.--goldsmith.
"have you see much of your scientific phoenix, Lydgate, lately?" say
Mr. Toller at one of his Christmas dinner-party, speak to Mr.
Farebrother on his right hand.
"not much, I be sorry to say," answer the Vicar, accustom to parry
Mr. Toller's banter about his belief in the new medical light. "I be
out of the way and he be too busy."
"be he? I be glad to hear it," say Dr. Minchin, with mingled suavity
and surprise.
"he give a great deal of time to the New Hospital," say Mr.
Farebrother, who have his reason for continue the subject: "I hear of
that from my neighbor, Mrs. Casaubon, who go there often. she say
Lydgate be indefatigable, and be make a fine thing of Bulstrode's
institution. he be prepare a new ward in case of the cholera come
to we."
"and prepare theory of treatment to try on the patient, I
suppose," say Mr. Toller.
"Come, Toller, be candid," say Mr. Farebrother. "you be too clever
not to see the good of a bold fresh mind in medicine, as well as in
everything else; and as to cholera, I fancy, none of you be very sure
what you ought to do. if a man go a little too far along a new road,
it be usually himself that he harm more than any one else."
"I be sure you and Wrench ought to be oblige to he," say Dr.
Minchin, look towards Toller, "for he have send you the cream of
Peacock's patient."
"lydgate have be live at a great rate for a young beginner," say
Mr. Harry Toller, the brewer. "I suppose his relation in the North
back he up."
"I hope so," say Mr. Chichely, "else he ought not to have marry that
nice girl we be all so fond of. hang it, one have a grudge against a
man who carry off the prettiest girl in the town."
"ay, by God! and the good too," say Mr. Standish.
"my friend Vincy donot half like the marriage, I know that," say Mr.
Chichely. "_he_ wouldnot do much. how the relation on the other side
may have come down I cannot say." there be an emphatic kind of
reticence in Mr. Chichely's manner of speak.
"oh, I shouldnot think Lydgate ever look to practice for a living,"
say Mr. Toller, with a slight touch of sarcasm, and there the subject
be drop.
this be not the first time that Mr. Farebrother have hear hint of
Lydgate's expense be obviously too great to be meet by his practice,
but he think it not unlikely that there be resource or
expectation which excuse the large outlay at the time of Lydgate's
marriage, and which might hinder any bad consequence from the
disappointment in his practice. one evening, when he take the pain to
go to Middlemarch on purpose to have a chat with Lydgate as of old, he
notice in he an air of excited effort quite unlike his usual easy way
of keep silence or break it with abrupt energy whenever he have
anything to say. Lydgate talk persistently when they be in his
work-room, put argument for and against the probability of certain
biological view; but he have none of those definite thing to say or to
show which give the waymark of a patient uninterrupted pursuit, such
as he use himself to insist on, say that "there must be a systole
and diastole in all inquiry," and that "a man's mind must be
continually expand and shrink between the whole human horizon and
the horizon of an object-glass." that evening he seem to be talk
widely for the sake of resist any personal bearing; and before long
they go into the drawing room, where Lydgate, have ask Rosamond
to give they music, sink back in his chair in silence, but with a
strange light in his eye. "he may have be take an opiate," be a
thought that cross Mr. Farebrother's mind--"tic-douloureux
perhaps--or medical worry."
it do not occur to he that Lydgate's marriage be not delightful: he
believe, as the rest do, that Rosamond be an amiable, docile
creature, though he have always think she rather uninteresting--a
little too much the pattern-card of the finishing-school; and his
mother could not forgive Rosamond because she never seem to see that
Henrietta Noble be in the room. "however, Lydgate fall in love with
she," say the Vicar to himself, "and she must be to his taste."
Mr. Farebrother be aware that Lydgate be a proud man, but have very
little corresponding fibre in himself, and perhaps too little care
about personal dignity, except the dignity of not be mean or
foolish, he could hardly allow enough for the way in which lydgate
shrank, as from a burn, from the utterance of any word about his
private affair. and soon after that conversation at Mr. Toller's, the
Vicar learn something which make he watch the more eagerly for an
opportunity of indirectly let Lydgate know that if he want to
open himself about any difficulty there be a friendly ear ready.
the opportunity come at Mr. Vincy's, where, on New Year's day, there
be a party, to which Mr. Farebrother be irresistibly invite, on the
plea that he must not forsake his old friend on the first new year of
his be a great man, and Rector as well as Vicar. and this party
be thoroughly friendly: all the lady of the Farebrother family be
present; the Vincy child all din at the table, and Fred have
persuade his mother that if she do not invite Mary Garth, the
Farebrothers would regard it as a slight to themselves, Mary be
their particular friend. Mary come, and Fred be in high spirit,
though his enjoyment be of a checker kind--triumph that his mother
should see Mary's importance with the chief personage in the party

Fred use to be much more easy about his own accomplishment in the

unsuccessfully to fancy herself care about Mary's appearance in

particularly bright; be glad, for Fred's sake, that his friend be
get kind to she, and be also quite willing that they should
see how much she be value by other whom they must admit to be judge.

Lydgate certainly have good reason to reflect on the service his
practice do he in counteract his personal care. he have no long

sake of pass the time. that evening he have the peculiar light in

in the room, where there be a good deal of Middlemarch company; and
several looker-on, as well as some of the player, be bet with
animation. Lydgate be play well, and feel confident; the bet be

gain which might double the sum he be save from his horse, he begin

Brassing, where there be gamble on a grander scale to be have, and
where, by one powerful snatch at the devil's bait, he might carry it
off without the hook, and buy his rescue from his daily soliciting.
he be still win when two new visitor enter. one of they be a
young Hawley, just come from his law study in town, and the other be

his. Young Hawley, an accomplished billiard-player, bring a cool
fresh hand to the cue. but Fred Vincy, startle at see Lydgate, and
astonish to see he bet with an excited air, stand aside, and
keep out of the circle round the table.
Fred have be rewarding resolution by a little laxity of late. he have
be work heartily for six month at all outdoor occupation under

defect of his handwriting, this practice be, perhaps, a little the

have not be out hunt once this season, have have no horse of his own

the heroic project of save almost all of the eighty pound that Mr.
Garth offer he, and return it, which he could easily do by give

clothe, and no expense in his board. in that way he could, in one

than she do now. nevertheless, it must be acknowledge that on this

bet. why? well, when sovereign be fly about, why shouldnot he
catch a few? he would never go far along that road again; but a man
like to assure himself, and man of pleasure generally, what he could
do in the way of mischief if he choose, and that if he abstain from
make himself ill, or beggar himself, or talk with the utmost

often begin.
but the last thing likely to have enter Fred's expectation be that

Lydgate, by bet on his own stroke, have win sixteen pound; but

Fred be surprised, not quite comfortably, but send word that he

"Farebrother have just send up a message to say that he want to speak

Fred have simply snatch up this pretext for speak, because he could

"Bambridge be over there, but he be make a row--I donot think hebe

Lydgate feel shame, but could not bear to act as if he feel it, by

"I think Lydgate never go to the Green Dragon?"

"he be not play, then?"
Fred have not mean to tell this, but he be oblige now to say, "yes,

"you have be go often yourself, then, lately?"
"oh, about five or six time."
"I think you have some good reason for give up the habit of go

"glad to hear it. but I say, my prompting be to look on and see you

there be a pause. Mr. Farebrother seem to wait for a recognition of

there be a drop in the Vicar's voice when he speak the last word he

right, other thing will keep right."

"thatbe enough. good-by, and God bless you."
in that way they part. but both of they walk about a long while
before they go out of the starlight. much of Fred's rumination might
be sum up in the word, "it certainly would have be a fine thing

husband?"
perhaps Mr. Farebrother's might be concentrate into a single shrug and
one little speech. "to think of the part one little woman can play in
the life of a man, so that to renounce she may be a very good imitation
of heroism, and to win she may be a discipline!"
CHAPTER LXVII.
now be there civil war within the soul:
Resolve be thrust from off the sacred throne
by clamorous need, and Pride the grand-vizier
make humble compact, play the supple part
of envoy and deft-tongue apologist
for hungry rebel.
Happily Lydgate have end by lose in the billiard-room, and bring
away no encouragement to make a raid on luck. on the contrary, he feel
unmixed disgust with himself the next day when he have to pay four or
five pound over and above his gain, and he carry about with he a
most unpleasant vision of the figure he have make, not only rub
elbow with the man at the Green Dragon but behave just as they do.
a philosopher fall to bet be hardly distinguishable from a
Philistine under the same circumstance: the difference will chiefly be
find in his subsequent reflection, and Lydgate chew a very
disagreeable cud in that way. his reason tell he how the affair might
have be magnify into ruin by a slight change of scenery--if it have
be a gambling-house that he have turn into, where chance could be
clutch with both hand instead of be pick up with thumb and
fore-finger. nevertheless, though reason strangle the desire to
gamble, there remain the feeling that, with an assurance of luck to
the needful amount, he would have like to gamble, rather than take the
alternative which be begin to urge itself as inevitable.
that alternative be to apply to Mr. Bulstrode. Lydgate have so many
time boast both to himself and other that he be totally
independent of Bulstrode, to whose plan he have lend himself solely
because they enable he to carry out his own idea of professional
work and public benefit--he have so constantly in their personal
intercourse have his pride sustain by the sense that he be make a
good social use of this predominate banker, whose opinion he think
contemptible and whose motif often seem to he an absurd mixture of
contradictory impression--that he have be create for himself
strong ideal obstacle to the proffering of any considerable request to
he on his own account.
still, early in March his affair be at that pass in which man begin
to say that their oath be deliver in ignorance, and to perceive
that the act which they have call impossible to they be become
manifestly possible. with Dover's ugly security soon to be put in
force, with the proceed of his practice immediately absorb in pay
back debt, and with the chance, if the bad be know, of daily
supply be refuse on credit, above all with the vision of
Rosamond's hopeless discontent continually haunt he, Lydgate have
begin to see that he should inevitably bend himself to ask help from
somebody or other. at first he have consider whether he should write
to Mr. Vincy; but on question Rosamond he find that, as he have
suspect, she have already apply twice to her father, the last time
be since the disappointment from Sir Godwin; and papa have say that
Lydgate must look out for himself. "Papa say he have come, with one
bad year after another, to trade more and more on borrow capital, and
have have to give up many indulgence; he could not spare a single
hundred from the charge of his family. he say, let Lydgate ask
Bulstrode: they have always be hand and glove."
indeed, Lydgate himself have come to the conclusion that if he must end
by ask for a free loan, his relation with Bulstrode, more at least
than with any other man, might take the shape of a claim which be not
purely personal. Bulstrode have indirectly help to cause the failure
of his practice, and have also be highly gratified by get a
medical partner in his plans:--but who among we ever reduce himself
to the sort of dependence in which Lydgate now stand, without try to
believe that he have claim which diminish the humiliation of ask?
it be true that of late there have seem to be a new languor of
interest in Bulstrode about the Hospital; but his health have get bad,
and show sign of a deep-seat nervous affection. in other respect
he do not appear to be change: he have always be highly polite, but
Lydgate have observe in he from the first a marked coldness about his
marriage and other private circumstance, a coldness which he have
hitherto prefer to any warmth of familiarity between they. he
defer the intention from day to day, his habit of act on his
conclusion be make infirm by his repugnance to every possible
conclusion and its consequent act. he see Mr. Bulstrode often, but he
do not try to use any occasion for his private purpose. at one moment
he think, "I will write a letter: I prefer that to any circuitous
talk;" at another he think, "no; if I be talk to he, I could
make a retreat before any sign of disinclination."
still the day pass and no letter be write, no special interview
seek. in his shrinking from the humiliation of a dependent attitude
towards Bulstrode, he begin to familiarize his imagination with another
step even more unlike his remembered self. he begin spontaneously to
consider whether it would be possible to carry out that puerile notion
of Rosamond's which have often make he angry, namely, that they should
quit Middlemarch without see anything beyond that preface. the
question came--"would any man buy the practice of I even now, for as
little as it be worth? then the sale might happen as a necessary
preparation for go away."
but against his take this step, which he still feel to be a
contemptible relinquishment of present work, a guilty turning aside
from what be a real and might be a widen channel for worthy
activity, to start again without any justified destination, there be
this obstacle, that the purchaser, if procurable at all, might not be
quickly forthcoming. and afterwards? rosamond in a poor lodging,
though in the large city or most distant town, would not find the
life that could save she from gloom, and save he from the reproach of
have plunge she into it. for when a man be at the foot of the hill
in his fortune, he may stay a long while there in spite of
professional accomplishment. in the british climate there be no
incompatibility between scientific insight and furnish lodging: the
incompatibility be chiefly between scientific ambition and a wife who
object to that kind of residence.
but in the midst of his hesitation, opportunity come to decide he. a
note from Mr. Bulstrode request Lydgate to call on he at the Bank.
a hypochondriacal tendency have show itself in the banker's
constitution of late; and a lack of sleep, which be really only a
slight exaggeration of an habitual dyspeptic symptom, have be dwelt on
by he as a sign of threaten insanity. he want to consult lydgate
without delay on that particular morning, although he have nothing to
tell beyond what he have tell before. he listen eagerly to what

only repetition; and this moment in which Bulstrode be receive a
medical opinion with a sense of comfort, seem to make the
communication of a personal need to he easy than it have be in

would be well for Mr. Bulstrode to relax his attention to business.
"one see how any mental strain, however slight, may affect a delicate
frame," say Lydgate at that stage of the consultation when the remark
tend to pass from the personal to the general, "by the deep stamp which
anxiety will make for a time even on the young and vigorous. I be
naturally very strong; yet I have be thoroughly shake lately by an
accumulation of trouble."
"I presume that a constitution in the susceptible state in which mine
at present be, would be especially liable to fall a victim to cholera,
if it visit our district. and since its appearance near London, we
may well besiege the mercy-seat for our protection," say Mr.
Bulstrode, not intend to evade Lydgate's allusion, but really
preoccupy with alarm about himself.
"you have at all event take your share in use good practical
precaution for the town, and that be the good mode of ask for
protection," say Lydgate, with a strong distaste for the break

the apparent deafness of his sympathy. but his mind have take up its
long-prepare movement towards get help, and be not yet arrest.
he add, "the town have do well in the way of cleansing, and find

enemy will admit that the arrangement in the Hospital be a public
good."
"truly," say Mr. Bulstrode, with some coldness. "with regard to what
you say, Mr. Lydgate, about the relaxation of my mental labor, I have

a very decide character. I contemplate at least a temporary
withdrawal from the management of much business, whether benevolent or
commercial. also I think of change my residence for a time: probably
I shall close or let 'the Shrubs,' and take some place near the
coast--under advice of course as to salubrity. that would be a measure
which you would recommend?"
"oh yes," say Lydgate, fall backward in his chair, with
ill-repressed impatience under the banker's pale earnest eye and
intense preoccupation with himself.
"I have for some time feel that I should open this subject with you in
relation to our hospital," continue Bulstrode. "under the
circumstance I have indicate, of course I must cease to have any
personal share in the management, and it be contrary to my view of
responsibility to continue a large application of mean to an
institution which I cannot watch over and to some extent regulate. I
shall therefore, in case of my ultimate decision to leave Middlemarch,
consider that I withdraw other support to the New Hospital than that
which will subsist in the fact that I chiefly supply the expense of
build it, and have contribute far large sum to its successful
working."
Lydgate's thought, when Bulstrode pause accord to his willnot, be,
"he have perhaps be lose a good deal of money." this be the most
plausible explanation of a speech which have cause rather a startling
change in his expectation. he say in reply--
"the loss to the Hospital can hardly be make up, I fear."
"hardly," return Bulstrode, in the same deliberate, silvery tone;
"except by some change of plan. the only person who may be certainly
count on as willing to increase her contribution be Mrs. Casaubon.
I have have an interview with she on the subject, and I have point out
to she, as I be about to do to you, that it will be desirable to win a
more general support to the New Hospital by a change of system."
another pause, but Lydgate do not speak.
"the change I mean be an amalgamation with the Infirmary, so that the
New Hospital shall be regard as a special addition to the elder
institution, have the same directing board. it will be necessary,
also, that the medical management of the two shall be combine. in
this way any difficulty as to the adequate maintenance of our new
establishment will be remove; the benevolent interest of the town
will cease to be divide."
Mr. Bulstrode have lower his eye from Lydgate's face to the button
of his coat as he again pause.
"no doubt that be a good device as to way and mean," say Lydgate,
with an edge of irony in his tone. "but I cannot be expect to rejoice
in it at once, since one of the first result will be that the other
medical man will upset or interrupt my method, if it be only because
they be mine."
"I myself, as you know, Mr. Lydgate, highly value the opportunity of
new and independent procedure which you have diligently employ: the
original plan, I confess, be one which I have much at heart, under
submission to the Divine Will. but since providential indication
demand a renunciation from I, I renounce."
Bulstrode show a rather exasperating ability in this conversation.
the broken metaphor and bad logic of motive which have stir his
hearer's contempt be quite consistent with a mode of put the
fact which make it difficult for Lydgate to vent his own indignation
and disappointment. after some rapid reflection, he only asked--
"what do Mrs. Casaubon say?"
"that be the further statement which I wish to make to you," say
Bulstrode, who have thoroughly prepare his ministerial explanation.
"she be, you be aware, a woman of most munificent disposition, and
happily in possession--not I presume of great wealth, but of fund
which she can well spare. she have inform I that though she have
destine the chief part of those fund to another purpose, she be
willing to consider whether she cannot fully take my place in relation

Lydgate be ready to say, "if Mrs. Casaubon would take your place,
there would be gain, instead of loss." but there be still a weight on

then, that I may enter into the subject with Mrs. Casaubon."
"precisely; that be what she expressly desire. her decision, she
say, will much depend on what you can tell she. but not at present:
she be, I believe, just set out on a journey. I have her letter

immediately otherwise engage,' she say. 'I be go into Yorkshire

some land which I be to see there may affect my power of contribute

this matter; but I wish to apprise you beforehand of what may
possibly occur."
Mr. Bulstrode return the letter to his side-pocket, and change his
attitude as if his business be closed. lydgate, whose renew hope
about the Hospital only make he more conscious of the fact which
poison his hope, feel that his effort after help, if make at all,
must be make now and vigorously.
"I be much oblige to you for give I full notice," he say, with a
firm intention in his tone, yet with an interruptedness in his delivery
which show that he speak unwillingly. "the high object to I be

can at present make of my profession. but the good use be not always

have nobody to pay on my own side." Lydgate wait a little, but

same interrupted enunciation--as if he be bite an objectional leek.
"I have slip into money difficulty which I can see no way out of,
unless some one who trust I and my future will advance I a sum
without other security. I have very little fortune leave when I come
here. I have no prospect of money from my own family. my expense,
in consequence of my marriage, have be very much great than I have
expect. the result at this moment be that it would take a thousand
pound to clear I. I mean, to free I from the risk of have all my
good sell in security of my large debt--as well as to pay my other
debt--and leave anything to keep we a little beforehand with our small
income. I find that it be out of the question that my wife's father
should make such an advance. that be why I mention my position to--to
the only other man who may be hold to have some personal connection
with my prosperity or ruin."
Lydgate hat to hear himself. but he have speak now, and have speak
with unmistakable directness. Mr. Bulstrode reply without haste, but
also without hesitation.
"I be grieve, though, I confess, not surprise by this information,
Mr. Lydgate. for my own part, I regret your alliance with my
brother-in-law's family, which have always be of prodigal habit, and
which have already be much indebted to I for sustainment in its
present position. my advice to you, Mr. Lydgate, would be, that
instead of involve yourself in further obligation, and continue a
doubtful struggle, you should simply become a bankrupt."
"that would not improve my prospect," say Lydgate, rise and speak
bitterly, "even if it be a more agreeable thing in itself."
"it be always a trial," say Mr. Bulstrode; "but trial, my dear sir, be
our portion here, and be a need corrective. I recommend you to weigh
the advice I have give."
"thank you," say Lydgate, not quite know what he say. "I have
occupy you too long. good-day."

"what suit of grace hath Virtue to put on
if Vice shall wear as good, and do as well?
if wrong, if Craft, if Indiscretion
Act as fair part with end as laudable?
which all this mighty volume of event
the world, the universal map of deed,

that the direct course still good succeed.

CHAPTER LXIX.
"if thou hast hear a word, let it die with thee."
--Ecclesiasticus.
Mr. Bulstrode be still seat in his manager's room at the Bank, about

be carry by Mr. Spicer, shoemaker and parish-clerk, who have it from
