evening when Lydgate have come down from the billiard room with Fred
Vincy, Mr. Farebrother's thought about he have be rather gloomy.
play at the Green Dragon once or oftener might have be a trifle in
another man; but in Lydgate it be one of several sign that he be
get unlike his former self. he be begin to do thing for
which he have formerly even an excessive scorn. whatever certain
dissatisfaction in marriage, which some silly tinkling of gossip have
give he hint of, might have to do with this change, Mr. Farebrother
feel sure that it be chiefly connect with the debt which be be
more and more distinctly report, and he begin to fear that any notion
of Lydgate's have resource or friend in the background must be
quite illusory. the rebuff he have meet with in his first attempt to win
Lydgate's confidence, disincline he to a second; but this news of the
execution be actually in the house, determine the Vicar to overcome
his reluctance.
Lydgate have just dismiss a poor patient, in whom he be much
interested, and he come forward to put out his hand--with an open
cheerfulness which surprise Mr. Farebrother. could this too be a
proud rejection of sympathy and help? never mind; the sympathy and
help should be offer.
"how be you, Lydgate? I come to see you because I have hear something
which make I anxious about you," say the Vicar, in the tone of a good
brother, only that there be no reproach in it. they be both seat
by this time, and Lydgate answer immediately--
"I think I know what you mean. you have hear that there be an
execution in the house?"
"yes; be it true?"
"it be true," say Lydgate, with an air of freedom, as if he do not
mind talk about the affair now. "but the danger be over; the debt
be pay. I be out of my difficulty now: I shall be free from debt,
and able, I hope, to start afresh on a well plan."
"I be very thankful to hear it," say the Vicar, fall back in his
chair, and speak with that low-toned quickness which often follow
the removal of a load. "I like that well than all the news in the
'Times.' I confess I come to you with a heavy heart."
"thank you for come," say Lydgate, cordially. "I can enjoy the
kindness all the more because I be happy. I have certainly be a
good deal crush. Ibe afraid I shall find the bruise still painful
by-and by," he add, smile rather sadly; "but just now I can only
feel that the torture-screw be off."
Mr. Farebrother be silent for a moment, and then say earnestly, "my
dear fellow, let I ask you one question. forgive I if I take a
liberty."
"I donot believe you will ask anything that ought to offend I."
"then--this be necessary to set my heart quite at rest--you have
not--have you?--in order to pay your debt, incur another debt which
may harass you worse hereafter?"
"no," say Lydgate, color slightly. "there be no reason why I
should not tell you--since the fact be so--that the person to whom I be
indebted be Bulstrode. he have make I a very handsome advance--a
thousand pound--and he can afford to wait for repayment."
"well, that be generous," say Mr. Farebrother, compel himself to
approve of the man whom he dislike. his delicate feeling shrank from
dwell even in his thought on the fact that he have always urge
Lydgate to avoid any personal entanglement with Bulstrode. he add
immediately, "and Bulstrode must naturally feel an interest in your
welfare, after you have work with he in a way which have probably
reduce your income instead of add to it. I be glad to think that
he have act accordingly."
Lydgate feel uncomfortable under these kindly supposition. they make
more distinct within he the uneasy consciousness which have show its
first dim stirring only a few hour before, that Bulstrode's motif
for his sudden beneficence follow close upon the chillest
indifference might be merely selfish. he let the kindly supposition
pass. he could not tell the history of the loan, but it be more
vividly present with he than ever, as well as the fact which the Vicar
delicately ignore--that this relation of personal indebtedness to
Bulstrode be what he have once be most resolve to avoid.
he begin, instead of answer, to speak of his project economy,
and of his have come to look at his life from a different point of
view.
"I shall set up a surgery," he say. "I really think I make a mistaken
effort in that respect. and if Rosamond will not mind, I shall take an
apprentice. I donot like these thing, but if one carry they out
faithfully they be not really lower. I have have a severe galling
to begin with: that will make the small rub seem easy."
Poor Lydgate! the "if Rosamond will not mind," which have fall from
he involuntarily as part of his thought, be a significant mark of the
yoke he bear. but Mr. Farebrother, whose hope enter strongly into
the same current with Lydgate's, and who know nothing about he that
could now raise a melancholy presentiment, leave he with affectionate
congratulation.

Clown. . . . 'twa in the Bunch of Grapes, where, indeed,

Froth. I have so: because it be an open room, and good for winter.

five day after the death of Raffles, Mr. Bambridge be stand at his
leisure under the large archway lead into the yard of the Green
Dragon. he be not fond of solitary contemplation, but he have only
just come out of the house, and any human figure stand at ease under
the archway in the early afternoon be as certain to attract
companionship as a pigeon which have find something worth peek at.
in this case there be no material object to feed upon, but the eye of
reason see a probability of mental sustenance in the shape of gossip.
Mr. Hopkins, the meek-mannered draper opposite, be the first to act on
this inward vision, be the more ambitious of a little masculine talk
because his customer be chiefly woman. Mr. Bambridge be rather
curt to the draper, feel that Hopkins be of course glad to talk to
_he_, but that he be not go to waste much of his talk on Hopkins.
soon, however, there be a small cluster of more important listener,
who be either deposit from the passer-by, or have saunter to the

guineas, and which Faulkner have sell for a hundred and sixty two month

privilege of call Mr. Bambridge by a very ugly name until the
exercise make his throat dry.
when the discourse be at this point of animation, come up Mr. Frank

be the highest conceivable unlikelihood. Mr. Hawley, stand with his
back to the street, be fix a time for look at the gray and
see it try, when a horseman pass slowly by.
"Bulstrode!" say two or three voice at once in a low tone, one of

nobody have more intention in this interjectural naming than if they

but as Bambridge's eye follow it he make a sarcastic grimace.
"by jingo! that remind I," he begin, lower his voice a little, "I

I pick up a fine story about Bulstrode. do you know how he come by
his fortune? any gentleman want a bit of curious information, I can
give it he free of expense. if everybody get their desert, Bulstrode
might have have to say his prayer at Botany Bay."
"what do you mean?" say Mr. Hawley, thrust his hand into his
pocket, and push a little forward under the archway. if Bulstrode
should turn out to be a rascal, Frank Hawley have a prophetic soul.
"I have it from a party who be an old chum of Bulstrode's. Iwill tell
you where I first pick he up," say Bambridge, with a sudden gesture
of his fore-finger. "he be at Larcher's sale, but I know nothing of
he then--he slip through my finger--be after Bulstrode, no
doubt. he tell I he can tap Bulstrode to any amount, know all his
secret. however, he blab to I at Bilkley: he take a stiff glass.
Damme if I think he mean to turn king's evidence; but hebe that sort
of brag fellow, the bragging run over hedge and ditch with he,
till hewould brag of a spavin as if it 'ud fetch money. a man should know
when to pull up." Mr. Bambridge make this remark with an air of
disgust, satisfy that his own bragging show a fine sense of the
marketable.
"whatbe the man's name? where can he be find?" say Mr. Hawley.
"as to where he be to be find, I leave he to it at the saracen's Head;
but his name be Raffles."
"Raffles!" exclaim Mr. Hopkins. "I furnish his funeral yesterday.
he be bury at Lowick. Mr. Bulstrode follow he. a very decent
funeral." there be a strong sensation among the listener. Mr.
Bambridge give an ejaculation in which "brimstone" be the mild

"at Stone Court," say the draper. "the housekeeper say he be a
relation of the master's. he come there ill on Friday."
"why, it be on Wednesday I take a glass with he," interpose

"do any doctor attend he?" say Mr. Hawley
"yes. Mr. Lydgate. Mr. Bulstrode sit up with he one night. he die
the third morning."
"go on, Bambridge," say Mr. Hawley, insistently. "what do this
fellow say about Bulstrode?"
the group have already become large, the town-clerk's presence be a

but this gossip about Bulstrode spread through Middlemarch like the

could not be win from the question whether the Lords would throw out

for hardly anybody doubt that some scandalous reason or other be at

but this vague conviction of indeterminable guilt, which be enough to

this be the tone of thought chiefly sanction by Mrs. Dollop, the

"thatbe odd," say Mr. Limp, a meditative shoemaker, with weak eye and

of Wellington say when he turn his coat and go over to the Romans."
"very like," say Mrs. Dollop. "if one raskill say it, itbe more

thing with that high hand, as there be no parson I' the country good

Old Harry's be too many for he."
"ay, ay, hebe a 'complice you cannot send out o' the country," say Mr.

the head of this discussion at "Dollop's" have be the common theme
among all class in the town, have be carry to Lowick Parsonage on

of the Vincy family, and have be discuss with sad reference to "poor

be accustom to very cordial relation with his neighbor, and hence
he could not miss the sign of cordiality; moreover, he have be take

he need not quit Middlemarch, and feel able consequently to
determine on matter which he have before leave in suspense.
"we will make a journey to Cheltenham in the course of a month or two,"
he have say to his wife. "there be great spiritual advantage to be
have in that town along with the air and the water, and six week there
will be eminently refreshing to we."
he really believe in the spiritual advantage, and mean that his life
henceforth should be the more devoted because of those later sin which
he represent to himself as hypothetic, pray hypothetically for
their pardon:--"if I have herein transgress."
as to the Hospital, he avoid say anything further to lydgate,
fear to manifest a too sudden change of plan immediately on the
death of Raffles. in his secret soul he believe that Lydgate
suspect his order to have be intentionally disobey, and
suspect this he must also suspect a motive. but nothing have be
betray to he as to the history of Raffles, and Bulstrode be anxious
not to do anything which would give emphasis to his undefined
suspicion. as to any certainty that a particular method of treatment
would either save or kill, Lydgate himself be constantly argue
against such dogmatism; he have no right to speak, and he have every
motive for be silent. hence Bulstrode feel himself providentially
secure. the only incident he have strongly wince under have be an
occasional encounter with Caleb Garth, who, however, have raise his hat
with mild gravity.
meanwhile, on the part of the principal townsman a strong determination
be grow against he.
a meeting be to be hold in the Town-Hall on a sanitary question which
have rise into press importance by the occurrence of a cholera case
in the town. since the Act of Parliament, which have be hurriedly
pass, authorize assessment for sanitary measure, there have be a
Board for the superintendence of such measure appoint in
Middlemarch, and much cleansing and preparation have be concur in
by whig and Tories. the question now be, whether a piece of ground
outside the town should be secure as a burial-ground by mean of
assessment or by private subscription. the meeting be to be open, and
almost everybody of importance in the town be expect to be there.
Mr. Bulstrode be a member of the Board, and just before twelve o'clock
he start from the Bank with the intention of urge the plan of
private subscription. under the hesitation of his project, he have for
some time keep himself in the background, and he feel that he should
this morning resume his old position as a man of action and influence
in the public affair of the town where he expect to end his day.
among the various person go in the same direction, he see Lydgate;
they join, talk over the object of the meeting, and enter it
together.
it seem that everybody of mark have be early than they. but there
be still space leave near the head of the large central table, and
they make their way thither. Mr. Farebrother sit opposite, not far
from Mr. Hawley; all the medical man be there; Mr. Thesiger be in
the chair, and Mr. Brooke of Tipton be on his right hand.
Lydgate notice a peculiar interchange of glance when he and Bulstrode
take their seat.
after the business have be fully open by the chairman, who point
out the advantage of purchase by subscription a piece of ground

Mr. Hawley's mode of speech, even when public decorum repress his

in Middlemarch a wife could not long remain ignorant that the town hold

CHAPTER LXXV.
"Le sentiment de la faussete des plaisirs present, et
l'ignorance de la vanite des plaisir absent causent
l'inconstance."--pascal.
Rosamond have a gleam of return cheerfulness when the house be free

that be a bright bit of morning. but soon the sky become black over

he think, "I be a fool. havenot I give up expect anything? I

"Rosamond, have you hear anything that distress you?"
"yes," she answer, lay down her work, which she have be carry
on with a languid semi-consciousness, most unlike her usual self.
"what have you hear?"
"everything, I suppose. Papa tell I."
"that people think I disgrace?"
"yes," say Rosamond, faintly, begin to sew again automatically.

this silence of hers bring a new rush of gall to that bitter mood in

apart, do what she object to. he start from his chair with an

"surely, Tertius--"
"well?"
"surely now at last you have give up the idea of stay in

issue. if his energy could have bear down that check, he might still

the next day Lydgate have to go to Brassing, and tell Rosamond that he

CHAPTER LXXXII.
"my grief lie onward and my joy behind."
--SHAKESPEARE: sonnet.
Exiles notoriously feed much on hope, and be unlikely to stay in
banishment unless they be oblige. when will Ladislaw exile himself
from Middlemarch he have place no strong obstacle to his return than
his own resolve, which be by no mean an iron barrier, but simply a

be do. but I be not listen to."
"you want what be impossible, you know, Chettam," say Mr. Brooke.
"you want he ship off. I tell you Ladislaw be not to be do as
we like with: he have his idea. he be a remarkable fellow--I always
say he be a remarkable fellow."
"yes," say Sir James, unable to repress a retort, "it be rather a pity
you form that high opinion of he. we be indebted to that for his
be lodge in this neighborhood. we be indebted to that for see
a woman like Dorothea degrade herself by marry he." Sir James
make little stoppage between his clause, the word not come easily.
"a man so mark out by her husband's will, that delicacy ought to have
forbid she from see he again--who take she out of her proper
rank--into poverty--have the meanness to accept such a sacrifice--have
always have an objectionable position--a bad origin--and, I _believe_,
be a man of little principle and light character. that be my opinion."
Sir James end emphatically, turn aside and cross his leg.
"I point everything out to she," say Mr. Brooke, apologetically--"i
mean the poverty, and abandon her position. I say, 'my dear, you
donot know what it be to live on seven hundred a-year, and have no
carriage, and that kind of thing, and go amongst people who donot know
who you be.' I put it strongly to she. but I advise you to talk to
Dorothea herself. the fact be, she have a dislike to Casaubon's
property. you will hear what she say, you know."
"no--excuse I--I shall not," say Sir James, with more coolness. "I
cannot bear to see she again; it be too painful. it hurt I too much
that a woman like Dorothea should have do what be wrong."
"be just, Chettam," say the easy, large-lipped Rector, who object to
all this unnecessary discomfort. "Mrs. Casaubon may be act
imprudently: she be give up a fortune for the sake of a man, and we
man have so poor an opinion of each other that we can hardly call a
woman wise who do that. but I think you should not condemn it as a
wrong action, in the strict sense of the word."
"yes, I do," answer Sir James. "I think that Dorothea commit a
wrong action in marry Ladislaw."
"my dear fellow, we be rather apt to consider an act wrong because it
be unpleasant to we," say the Rector, quietly. like many man who take
life easily, he have the knack of say a home truth occasionally to
those who feel themselves virtuously out of temper. Sir James take out
his handkerchief and begin to bite the corner.
"it be very dreadful of Dodo, though," say Celia, wish to justify
her husband. "she say she _never would_ marry again--not anybody at
all."
"I hear she say the same thing myself," say Lady Chettam,
majestically, as if this be royal evidence.
"oh, there be usually a silent exception in such case," say Mrs.
Cadwallader. "the only wonder to I be, that any of you be surprised.
you do nothing to hinder it. if you would have have Lord Triton down
here to woo she with his philanthropy, he might have carry she off
before the year be over. there be no safety in anything else. Mr.
Casaubon have prepare all this as beautifully as possible. he make
himself disagreeable--or it please God to make he so--and then he
dare she to contradict he. itbe the way to make any trumpery
tempting, to ticket it at a high price in that way."
"I donot know what you mean by wrong, Cadwallader," say Sir James,
still feel a little stung, and turn round in his chair towards
the Rector. "hebe not a man we can take into the family. at least, I
must speak for myself," he continue, carefully keep his eye off
Mr. Brooke. "I suppose other will find his society too pleasant to
care about the propriety of the thing."
"well, you know, Chettam," say Mr. Brooke, good-humoredly, nurse his
leg, "I cannot turn my back on Dorothea. I must be a father to she up
to a certain point. I say, 'my dear, I willnot refuse to give you
away.' I have speak strongly before. but I can cut off the entail,
you know. it will cost money and be troublesome; but I can do it, you
know."
Mr. Brooke nod at Sir James, and feel that he be both show his
own force of resolution and propitiate what be just in the Baronet's
vexation. he have hit on a more ingenious mode of parry than he be
aware of. he have touch a motive of which Sir James be ashamed. the
mass of his feeling about Dorothea's marriage to Ladislaw be due
partly to excusable prejudice, or even justifiable opinion, partly to a
jealous repugnance hardly less in Ladislaw's case than in Casaubon's.
he be convince that the marriage be a fatal one for Dorothea. but
amid that mass run a vein of which he be too good and honorable a man
to like the avowal even to himself: it be undeniable that the union of
the two estate--Tipton and Freshitt--lie charmingly within a
ring-fence, be a prospect that flatter he for his son and heir.
hence when Mr. Brooke noddingly appeal to that motive, Sir James feel
a sudden embarrassment; there be a stoppage in his throat; he even
blush. he have find more word than usual in the first jet of his
anger, but Mr. Brooke's propitiation be more clog to his tongue
than Mr. Cadwallader's caustic hint.
but Celia be glad to have room for speech after her uncle's suggestion
of the marriage ceremony, and she say, though with as little eagerness
of manner as if the question have turn on an invitation to dinner, "do
you mean that Dodo be go to be marry directly, uncle?"
"in three week, you know," say Mr. Brooke, helplessly. "I can do
nothing to hinder it, Cadwallader," he add, turn for a little
countenance toward the Rector, who said--
"--I--should not make any fuss about it. if she like to be poor, that
be her affair. nobody would have say anything if she have marry the
young fellow because he be rich. plenty of benefice clergy be
poor than they will be. here be Elinor," continue the provoking
husband; "she vex her friend by I: I have hardly a thousand
a-year--I be a lout--nobody could see anything in I--my shoe be
not the right cut--all the man wonder how a woman could like I.
upon my word, I must take Ladislaw's part until I hear more harm of
he."
"Humphrey, that be all sophistry, and you know it," say his wife.
"everything be all one--that be the beginning and end with you. as if
you have not be a Cadwallader! do any one suppose that I would have
take such a monster as you by any other name?"
"and a clergyman too," observe Lady Chettam with approbation. "elinor
cannot be say to have descend below her rank. it be difficult to
say what Mr. Ladislaw be, eh, James?"
Sir James give a small grunt, which be less respectful than his usual
mode of answer his mother. Celia look up at he like a thoughtful
kitten.
"it must be admit that his blood be a frightful mixture!" say Mrs.
Cadwallader. "the Casaubon cuttle-fish fluid to begin with, and then a
rebellious polish fiddler or dancing-master, be it?--and then an old
clo--"
"Nonsense, Elinor," say the Rector, rise. "it be time for we to go."
"after all, he be a pretty sprig," say Mrs. Cadwallader, rise too,
and wish to make amend. "he be like the fine old Crichley
portrait before the idiot come in."
"Iwill go with you," say Mr. Brooke, start up with alacrity. "you
must all come and dine with I to-morrow, you know--eh, Celia, my dear?"
"you will, James--won't you?" say Celia, take her husband's hand.
"oh, of course, if you like," say Sir James, pull down his
waistcoat, but unable yet to adjust his face good-humoredly. "that be
to say, if it be not to meet anybody else.':
"no, no, no," say Mr. Brooke, understand the condition. "Dorothea
would not come, you know, unless you have be to see she."
when Sir James and Celia be alone, she say, "do you mind about my
have the carriage to go to, Lowick, James?"
"what, now, directly?" he answer, with some surprise.
"yes, it be very important," say Celia.
"remember, Celia, I cannot see she," say Sir James.
"not if she give up marry?"
"what be the use of say that?--however, Ibe go to the stable.
Iwill tell Briggs to bring the carriage round."
Celia think it be of great use, if not to say that, at least to take
a journey to Lowick in order to influence Dorothea's mind. all through
their girlhood she have feel that she could act on her sister by a word
judiciously place--by open a little window for the daylight of her
own understanding to enter among the strange colored lamp by which
Dodo habitually see. and Celia the matron naturally feel more able to
advise her childless sister. how could any one understand Dodo so well
as Celia do or love she so tenderly?
Dorothea, busy in her boudoir, feel a glow of pleasure at the sight of
her sister so soon after the revelation of her intended marriage. she
have prefigure to herself, even with exaggeration, the disgust of her
friend, and she have even fear that Celia might be keep aloof from
she.
"o Kitty, I be delighted to see you!" say Dorothea, put her hand
on Celia's shoulder, and beam on she. "I almost think you would
not come to I."
"I have not bring Arthur, because I be in a hurry," say Celia, and
they sit down on two small chair opposite each other, with their knee
touching.
"you know, Dodo, it be very bad," say Celia, in her placid guttural,
look as prettily free from humor as possible. "you have
disappoint we all so. and I cannot think that it ever _will_ be--you
never can go and live in that way. and then there be all your plan!
you never can have think of that. James would have take any trouble
for you, and you might have go on all your life do what you like."
"on the contrary, dear," say Dorothea, "I never could do anything that
I like. I have never carry out any plan yet."
"because you always want thing that wouldnot do. but other plan
would have come. and how can you marry Mr. Ladislaw, that we none of
we ever think you _could_ marry? it shock James so dreadfully. and
then it be all so different from what you have always be. you would
have Mr. Casaubon because he have such a great soul, and be so and
dismal and learn; and now, to think of marry Mr. Ladislaw, who have
get no estate or anything. I suppose it be because you must be make
yourself uncomfortable in some way or other."
Dorothea laugh.
"well, it be very serious, Dodo," say Celia, become more impressive.
"how will you live? and you will go away among queer people. and I
shall never see you--and you willnot mind about little Arthur--and I
think you always would--"
Celia's rare tear have get into her eye, and the corner of her mouth
be agitate.
"Dear Celia," say Dorothea, with tender gravity, "if you donot ever
see I, it will not be my fault."
"yes, it will," say Celia, with the same touching distortion of her
small feature. "how can I come to you or have you with I when James
cannot bear it?--that be because he think it be not right--he think
you be so wrong, Dodo. but you always be wrong: only I cannot help
love you. and nobody can think where you will live: where can you
go?"
"I be go to London," say Dorothea.
"how can you always live in a street? and you will be so poor. I
could give you half my thing, only how can I, when I never see you?"
"bless you, Kitty," say Dorothea, with gentle warmth. "take comfort:
perhaps James will forgive I some time."
"but it would be much well if you would not be marry," say Celia,
dry her eye, and return to her argument; "then there would be
nothing uncomfortable. and you would not do what nobody think you
could do. James always say you ought to be a queen; but this be not
at all be like a queen. you know what mistake you have always be
make, Dodo, and this be another. nobody think Mr. Ladislaw a proper
husband for you. and you _say you_ would never be marry again."
"it be quite true that I might be a wise person, Celia," say
Dorothea, "and that I might have do something well, if I have be
well. but this be what I be go to do. I have promise to marry
Mr. Ladislaw; and I be go to marry he."
the tone in which Dorothea say this be a note that Celia have long
learn to recognize. she be silent a few moment, and then say, as
if she have dismiss all contest, "be he very fond of you, Dodo?"
"I hope so. I be very fond of he."
"that be nice," say Celia, comfortably. "only I rather you have such a
sort of husband as James be, with a place very near, that I could drive
to."
Dorothea smile, and Celia look rather meditative. presently she
say, "I cannot think how it all come about." Celia think it would be
pleasant to hear the story.
"I dare say not," say-Dorothea, pinch her sister's chin. "if you
know how it come about, it would not seem wonderful to you."
"cannot you tell I?" say Celia, settle her arm cozily.
"no, dear, you would have to feel with I, else you would never know."
CHAPTER LIXXV.
"then go the jury out whose name be Mr. Blindman, Mr.
no-good, Mr. Malice, Mr. Love-lust, Mr. Live-loose, Mr.
Heady, Mr. high-mind, Mr. Enmity, Mr. Liar, Mr. Cruelty, Mr.
Hate-light, Mr. Implacable, who every one give in his
private verdict against he among themselves, and afterwards
unanimously conclude to bring he in guilty before the
judge. and first among themselves, Mr. Blindman, the
foreman, say, I see clearly that this man be a heretic.
then say Mr. no-good, away with such a fellow from the
earth! ay, say Mr. Malice, for I hate the very look of he.
then say Mr. Love-lust, I could never endure he. nor I,
say Mr. Live-loose; for he would be always condemn my
way. hang he, hang he, say Mr. Heady. a sorry scrub, say
Mr. high-mind. my heart riseth against he, say Mr. Enmity.
he be a rogue, say Mr. Liar. hanging be too good for he,
say Mr. Cruelty. let we despatch he out of the way say
Mr. Hate-light. then say Mr. Implacable, might I have all
the world give I, I could not be reconcile to he;
therefore let we forthwith bring he in guilty of death."
--Pilgrim's Progress.
when immortal Bunyan make his picture of the persecuting passion
bring in their verdict of guilty, who pity faithful? that be a
rare and blessed lot which some great man have not attain, to know
ourselves guiltless before a condemn crowd--to be sure that what we
be denounce for be solely the good in we. the pitiable lot be that
of the man who could not call himself a martyr even though he be to
persuade himself that the man who stone he be but ugly passion
incarnate--who know that he be stone, not for profess the right,
but for not be the man he profess to be.
this be the consciousness that Bulstrode be wither under while he
make his preparation for depart from Middlemarch, and go to end
his stricken life in that sad refuge, the indifference of new face.
the duteous merciful constancy of his wife have deliver he from one
dread, but it could not hinder her presence from be still a tribunal
before which he shrink from confession and desire advocacy. his
equivocation with himself about the death of Raffles have sustain the
conception of an omniscience whom he pray to, yet he have a terror
upon he which would not let he expose they to judgment by a full
confession to his wife: the act which he have wash and dilute with
inward argument and motive, and for which it seem comparatively easy
to win invisible pardon--what name would she call they by? that she
should ever silently call his act murder be what he could not bear.
he feel shroud by her doubt: he get strength to face she from the
sense that she could not yet feel warrant in pronounce that bad
condemnation on he. some time, perhaps--when he be die--he would
tell she all: in the deep shadow of that time, when she hold his hand
in the gathering darkness, she might listen without recoil from his
touch. perhaps: but concealment have be the habit of his life, and
the impulse to confession have no power against the dread of a deep
humiliation.
he be full of timid care for his wife, not only because he deprecate
any harshness of judgment from she, but because he feel a deep distress
at the sight of her suffering. she have send her daughter away to
board at a school on the coast, that this crisis might be hide from
they as far as possible. Set free by their absence from the
intolerable necessity of account for her grief or of behold their
frightened wonder, she could live unconstrainedly with the sorrow that
be every day streak her hair with whiteness and make her eyelid
languid.
"tell I anything that you would like to have I do, Harriet,"
Bulstrode have say to she; "I mean with regard to arrangement of
property. it be my intention not to sell the land I possess in this
neighborhood, but to leave it to you as a safe provision. if you have
any wish on such subject, do not conceal it from I."
a few day afterwards, when she have return from a visit to her
brother's, she begin to speak to her husband on a subject which have for
some time be in her mind.
"I _should_ like to do something for my brother's family, Nicholas; and
I think we be bind to make some amend to Rosamond and her husband.
Walter say Mr. Lydgate must leave the town, and his practice be almost
good for nothing, and they have very little leave to settle anywhere
with. I would rather do without something for ourselves, to make some
amend to my poor brother's family."
Mrs. Bulstrode do not wish to go near to the fact than in the
phrase "make some amend;" know that her husband must understand
she. he have a particular reason, which she be not aware of, for
wince under her suggestion. he hesitate before he said--
"it be not possible to carry out your wish in the way you propose, my
dear. Mr. Lydgate have virtually reject any further service from I.
he have return the thousand pound which I lend he. Mrs. Casaubon
advance he the sum for that purpose. here be his letter."
the letter seem to cut Mrs. Bulstrode severely. the mention of Mrs.
Casaubon's loan seem a reflection of that public feeling which hold
it a matter of course that every one would avoid a connection with her
husband. she be silent for some time; and the tear fall one after
the other, her chin trembling as she wipe they away. Bulstrode,
sit opposite to she, ache at the sight of that grief-wear face,
which two month before have be bright and blooming. it have age to
keep sad company with his own withered feature. urge into some
effort at comfort she, he said--
"there be another mean, Harriet, by which I might do a service to your
brother's family, if you like to act in it. and it would, I think, be
beneficial to you: it would be an advantageous way of manage the land
which I mean to be yours."
she look attentive.
"Garth once think of undertake the management of Stone Court in
order to place your nephew Fred there. the stock be to remain as it
be, and they be to pay a certain share of the profit instead of an
ordinary rent. that would be a desirable beginning for the young man,
in conjunction with his employment under Garth. would it be a
satisfaction to you?"
"yes, it would," say Mrs. Bulstrode, with some return of energy.
"Poor Walter be so cast down; I would try anything in my power to do
he some good before I go away. we have always be brother and
sister."
"you must make the proposal to Garth yourself, Harriet," say Mr.
Bulstrode, not like what he have to say, but desire the end he have
in view, for other reason besides the consolation of his wife. "you
must state to he that the land be virtually your, and that he need
have no transaction with I. communication can be make through
Standish. I mention this, because Garth give up be my agent. I can
put into your hand a paper which he himself draw up, state
condition; and you can propose his renew acceptance of they. I
think it be not unlikely that he will accept when you propose the thing
for the sake of your nephew."
CHAPTER LXXXVI.
"Le coeur se sature d'amour comme d'un sel divin qui le
conserve; de la l'incorruptible adherence de ceux qui se
sont aim des l'aube de la vie, et la fraicheur des vielle
amour prolonge. Il existe un embaumement d'amour. C'est de
Daphnis et Chloe que sont fait Philemon et Baucis. Cette
vieillesse la, ressemblance du soir avec l'aurore."
--VICTOR HUGO: l'homme qui rit.
Mrs. Garth, hear Caleb enter the passage about tea-time, open the
parlor-door and say, "there you be, Caleb. have you have your
dinner?" (Mr. Garth's meal be much subordinate to "business.")
"oh yes, a good dinner--cold mutton and I donot know what. where be
Mary?"
"in the garden with Letty, I think."
"Fred be not come yet?"
"no. be you go out again without take tea, Caleb?" say Mrs.
Garth, see that her absent-minded husband be put on again the
hat which he have just take off.
"no, no; Ibe only go to Mary a minute."
Mary be in a grassy corner of the garden, where there be a swing
loftily hang between two pear-tree. she have a pink kerchief tie over
her head, make a little poke to shade her eye from the level
sunbeam, while she be give a glorious swing to Letty, who laugh
and scream wildly.
see her father, Mary leave the swing and go to meet he, push
back the pink kerchief and smile afar off at he with the involuntary
smile of love pleasure.
"I come to look for you, Mary," say Mr. Garth. "let we walk about a
bit."
Mary know quite well that her father have something particular to say:
his eyebrow make their pathetic angle, and there be a tender gravity
in his voice: these thing have be sign to she when she be Letty's
age. she put her arm within his, and they turn by the row of
nut-tree.
"it will be a sad while before you can be marry, Mary," say she
father, not look at she, but at the end of the stick which he hold
in his other hand.
"not a sad while, father--I mean to be merry," say Mary, laughingly.
"I have be single and merry for four-and-twenty year and more: I
suppose it will not be quite as long again as that." then, after a
little pause, she say, more gravely, bend her face before her
father's, "if you be content with Fred?"
Caleb screw up his mouth and turn his head aside wisely.
"now, father, you do praise he last Wednesday. you say he have an
uncommon notion of stock, and a good eye for thing."
"do I?" say Caleb, rather slyly.
"yes, I put it all down, and the date, anno Domini, and everything,"
say Mary. "you like thing to be neatly book. and then his
behavior to you, father, be really good; he have a deep respect for you;
and it be impossible to have a well temper than Fred have."
"ay, ay; you want to coax I into think he a fine match."
"no, indeed, father. I donot love he because he be a fine match."
"what for, then?"
"oh, dear, because I have always love he. I should never like
scold any one else so well; and that be a point to be think of in
a husband."
"your mind be quite settle, then, Mary?" say Caleb, return to his
first tone. "therebe no other wish come into it since thing have be
go on as they have be of late?" (Caleb mean a great deal in that
vague phrase;) "because, well late than never. a woman must not
force her heart--she'll do a man no good by that."
"my feeling have not change, father," say Mary, calmly. "I shall be
constant to Fred as long as he be constant to I. I donot think either
of we could spare the other, or like any one else well, however much
we might admire they. it would make too great a difference to we--like
see all the old place alter, and change the name for
everything. we must wait for each other a long while; but Fred know
that."
instead of speak immediately, Caleb stand still and screw his
stick on the grassy walk. then he say, with emotion in his voice,
"well, Ihave get a bit of news. what do you think of Fred go to live
at Stone Court, and manage the land there?"
"how can that ever be, father?" say Mary, wonderingly.
"he would manage it for his aunt Bulstrode. the poor woman have be to
I beg and pray. she want to do the lad good, and it might be
a fine thing for he. with save, he might gradually buy the stock,
and he have a turn for farming."
"oh, Fred would be so happy! it be too good to believe."
"ah, but mind you," say Caleb, turn his head warningly, "I must
take it on _my_ shoulder, and be responsible, and see after
everything; and that will grieve your mother a bit, though she maynot
say so. Fred have need be careful."
"perhaps it be too much, father," say Mary, check in her joy.
"there would be no happiness in bring you any fresh trouble."
"Nay, nay; work be my delight, child, when it donot vex your mother.
and then, if you and Fred get marry," here Caleb's voice shake just
perceptibly, "hewill be steady and saving; and youhave get your mother's
cleverness, and mine too, in a woman's sort of way; and youwill keep he
in order. hewill be come by-and-by, so I want to tell you first,
because I think youwould like to tell _he_ by yourself. after that, I
could talk it well over with he, and we could go into business and the
nature of thing."
"oh, you dear good father!" cry Mary, put her hand round her
father's neck, while he bend his head placidly, willing to be caress.
"I wonder if any other girl think her father the good man in the
world!"
"nonsense, child; youwill think your husband well."
"impossible," say Mary, relapse into her usual tone; "husband be
an inferior class of man, who require keep in order."
when they be enter the house with Letty, who have run to join they,
Mary see Fred at the orchard-gate, and go to meet he.
"what fine clothe you wear, you extravagant youth!" say Mary, as Fred
stand still and raise his hat to she with playful formality. "you be
not learn economy."
"now that be too bad, Mary," say Fred. "just look at the edge of
these coat-cuff! it be only by dint of good brushing that I look
respectable. I be save up three suit--one for a wedding-suit."
"how very droll you will look!--like a gentleman in an old
fashion-book."
"oh no, they will keep two year."
"two year! be reasonable, Fred," say Mary, turn to walk. "donot
encourage flattering expectation."
"why not? one live on they well than on unflattering one. if we
cannot be marry in two year, the truth will be quite bad enough when
it come."
"I have hear a story of a young gentleman who once encourage
flattering expectation, and they do he harm."
"Mary, if youhave get something discouraging to tell I, I shall bolt; I
shall go into the house to Mr. Garth. I be out of spirit. my father
be so cut up--home be not like itself. I cannot bear any more bad news."
"should you call it bad news to be tell that you be to live at Stone
Court, and manage the farm, and be remarkably prudent, and save money
every year till all the stock and furniture be your own, and you be
a distinguished agricultural character, as Mr. Borthrop Trumbull
say--rather stout, I fear, and with the greek and latin sadly
weather-worn?"
"you donot mean anything except nonsense, Mary?" say Fred, color
slightly nevertheless.
"that be what my father have just tell I of as what may happen, and he
never talk nonsense," say Mary, look up at Fred now, while he
grasp her hand as they walk, till it rather hurt she; but she would
not complain.
"oh, I could be a tremendously good fellow then, Mary, and we could be
marry directly."
"not so fast, sir; how do you know that I would not rather defer our
marriage for some year? that would leave you time to misbehave, and
then if I like some one else well, I should have an excuse for
jilt you."
"pray donot joke, Mary," say Fred, with strong feeling. "tell I
seriously that all this be true, and that you be happy because of
it--because you love I well."
"it be all true, Fred, and I be happy because of it--because I love you
well," say Mary, in a tone of obedient recitation.
they linger on the door-step under the steep-roofed porch, and Fred
almost in a whisper said--
"when we be first engage, with the umbrella-ring, Mary, you use
to--"
the spirit of joy begin to laugh more decidedly in Mary's eye, but the
fatal Ben come run to the door with Brownie yap behind he,
and, bounce against they, said--
"Fred and Mary! be you ever come in?--or may I eat your cake?"
FINALE.
every limit be a beginning as well as an ending. who can quit young
life after be long in company with they, and not desire to know
what befell they in their after-year? for the fragment of a life,
however typical, be not the sample of an even web: promise may not be
keep, and an ardent outset may be follow by declension; latent power
may find their long-wait opportunity; a past error may urge a grand
retrieval.
marriage, which have be the bourne of so many narrative, be still a
great beginning, as it be to Adam and Eve, who keep their honeymoon in
Eden, but have their first little one among the thorn and thistle of
the wilderness. it be still the beginning of the home epic--the
gradual conquest or irremediable loss of that complete union which
make the advance year a climax, and age the harvest of sweet
memory in common.
some set out, like Crusaders of old, with a glorious equipment of hope
and enthusiasm and get break by the way, want patience with each
other and the world.
all who have care for Fred Vincy and Mary Garth will like to know that
these two make no such failure, but achieve a solid mutual happiness.
Fred surprise his neighbor in various way. he become rather
distinguish in his side of the county as a theoretic and practical
farmer, and produce a work on the "Cultivation of Green Crops and the
Economy of Cattle-Feeding" which win he high congratulation at
agricultural meeting. in Middlemarch admiration be more reserved:
most person there be inclined to believe that the merit of Fred's
authorship be due to his wife, since they have never expect Fred
Vincy to write on turnip and mangel-wurzel.
but when Mary write a little book for her boy, call "story of
Great Men, take from Plutarch," and have it print and publish by
Gripp & Co., Middlemarch, every one in the town be willing to give the
credit of this work to Fred, observe that he have be to the
University, "where the ancient be study," and might have be a
clergyman if he have choose.
in this way it be make clear that Middlemarch have never be deceive,
and that there be no need to praise anybody for write a book, since
it be always do by somebody else.
moreover, Fred remain unswervingly steady. some year after his
marriage he tell Mary that his happiness be half owe to Farebrother,
who give he a strong pull-up at the right moment. I cannot say that
he be never again mislead by his hopefulness: the yield of crop or the
profit of a cattle sale usually fall below his estimate; and he be
always prone to believe that he could make money by the purchase of a
horse which turn out badly--though this, Mary observe, be of
course the fault of the horse, not of Fred's judgment. he keep his
love of horsemanship, but he rarely allow himself a day's hunting;
and when he do so, it be remarkable that he submit to be laugh
at for cowardliness at the fence, seem to see Mary and the boy
sit on the five-bar gate, or show their curly head between
hedge and ditch.
there be three boy: Mary be not discontent that she bring forth
man-child only; and when Fred wish to have a girl like she, she
say, laughingly, "that would be too great a trial to your mother."
Mrs. Vincy in her decline year, and in the diminished lustre of her
housekeeping, be much comfort by her perception that two at least of
Fred's boy be real Vincys, and do not "feature the Garths." but
Mary secretly rejoice that the young of the three be very much
what her father must have be when he wear a round jacket, and show
a marvellous nicety of aim in play at marble, or in throw stone
to bring down the mellow pear.
Ben and Letty Garth, who be uncle and aunt before they be well in
their teen, dispute much as to whether nephew or niece be more
desirable; Ben contend that it be clear girl be good for less
than boy, else they would not be always in petticoat, which show
how little they be mean for; whereupon Letty, who argue much from
book, get angry in reply that God make coat of skin for both Adam
and Eve alike--also it occur to she that in the East the man too
wear petticoat. but this latter argument, obscure the majesty of
the former, be one too many, for Ben answer contemptuously, "the
more spooney they!" and immediately appeal to his mother whether
boy be not well than girl. Mrs. Garth pronounce that both be
alike naughty, but that boy be undoubtedly strong, could run
fast, and throw with more precision to a great distance. with this
oracular sentence Ben be well satisfied, not mind the naughtiness;
but Letty take it ill, her feeling of superiority be strong than
her muscle.
Fred never become rich--his hopefulness have not lead he to expect that;
but he gradually save enough to become owner of the stock and
furniture at Stone Court, and the work which Mr. Garth put into his
hand carry he in plenty through those "bad time" which be always
present with farmer. Mary, in her matronly day, become as solid in
figure as her mother; but, unlike she, give the boy little formal
teaching, so that Mrs. Garth be alarmed lest they should never be well
ground in grammar and geography. nevertheless, they be find quite
forward enough when they go to school; perhaps, because they have
like nothing so well as be with their mother. when Fred be rid
home on winter evening he have a pleasant vision beforehand of the
bright hearth in the wainscoted parlor, and be sorry for other man who
could not have Mary for their wife; especially for Mr. Farebrother.
"he be ten time worthy of you than I be," Fred could now say to
she, magnanimously. "to be sure he be," Mary answer; "and for that
reason he could do well without I. but you--I shudder to think what
you would have be--a curate in debt for horse-hire and cambric
pocket-handkerchief!"
on inquiry it might possibly be find that Fred and Mary still inhabit
Stone Court--that the creep plant still cast the foam of their
blossom over the fine stone-wall into the field where the walnut-trees
stand in stately row--and that on sunny day the two lover who be
first engage with the umbrella-ring may be see in white-haired
placidity at the open window from which Mary Garth, in the day of old
Peter Featherstone, have often be order to look out for Mr. Lydgate.
lydgate's hair never become white. he die when he be only fifty,
leave his wife and child provide for by a heavy insurance on his
life. he have gain an excellent practice, alternate, accord to
the season, between London and a Continental bathing-place; have
write a treatise on Gout, a disease which have a good deal of wealth
on its side. his skill be rely on by many pay patient, but he
always regard himself as a failure: he have not do what he once
mean to do. his acquaintance think he enviable to have so
charm a wife, and nothing happen to shake their opinion. Rosamond
never commit a second compromise indiscretion. she simply
continue to be mild in her temper, inflexible in her judgment,
dispose to admonish her husband, and able to frustrate he by
stratagem. as the year go on he oppose she less and less, whence
Rosamond conclude that he have learn the value of her opinion; on the
other hand, she have a more thorough conviction of his talent now that
he gain a good income, and instead of the threaten cage in Bride
Street provide one all flower and gilding, fit for the bird of
paradise that she resemble. in brief, Lydgate be what be call a
successful man. but he die prematurely of diphtheria, and Rosamond
afterwards marry an elderly and wealthy physician, who take kindly to
her four child. she make a very pretty show with her daughter,
drive out in her carriage, and often speak of her happiness as "a
reward"--she do not say for what, but probably she mean that it be a
reward for her patience with Tertius, whose temper never become
faultless, and to the last occasionally let slip a bitter speech which
be more memorable than the sign he make of his repentance. he once
call she his basil plant; and when she ask for an explanation, say
that basil be a plant which have flourish wonderfully on a murder
man's brain. Rosamond have a placid but strong answer to such
speech. why then have he choose she? it be a pity he have not have
Mrs. Ladislaw, whom he be always praise and place above she. and
thus the conversation end with the advantage on Rosamond's side. but
it would be unjust not to tell, that she never utter a word in
depreciation of Dorothea, keep in religious remembrance the
generosity which have come to her aid in the sharp crisis of her life.
Dorothea herself have no dream of be praise above other woman,
feel that there be always something well which she might have
do, if she have only be well and know well. still, she never
repent that she have give up position and fortune to marry will
Ladislaw, and he would have hold it the great shame as well as
sorrow to he if she have repent. they be bind to each other by a
love strong than any impulse which could have mar it. no life
would have be possible to Dorothea which be not fill with emotion,
and she have now a life fill also with a beneficent activity which she
have not the doubtful pain of discover and mark out for herself.
will become an ardent public man, work well in those time when
reform be begin with a young hopefulness of immediate good which have
be much check in our day, and get at last return to
Parliament by a constituency who pay his expense. Dorothea could
have like nothing well, since wrong exist, than that her husband
should be in the thick of a struggle against they, and that she should
give he wifely help. many who know she, think it a pity that so
substantive and rare a creature should have be absorb into the life
of another, and be only know in a certain circle as a wife and mother.
but no one state exactly what else that be in her power she ought
rather to have do--not even Sir James Chettam, who go no far
than the negative prescription that she ought not to have marry Will
Ladislaw.
but this opinion of his do not cause a lasting alienation; and the way
in which the family be make whole again be characteristic of all
concern. Mr. Brooke could not resist the pleasure of correspond
with Will and Dorothea; and one morning when his pen have be
remarkably fluent on the prospect of Municipal Reform, it run off into
an invitation to the Grange, which, once write, could not be do
away with at less cost than the sacrifice (hardly to be conceive) of
the whole valuable letter. during the month of this correspondence
Mr. Brooke have continually, in his talk with Sir James Chettam, be
presuppose or hint that the intention of cut off the entail
be still maintain; and the day on which his pen give the daring
invitation, he go to Freshitt expressly to intimate that he have a
strong sense than ever of the reason for take that energetic step
as a precaution against any mixture of low blood in the heir of the
Brookes.
but that morning something exciting have happen at the Hall. a letter
have come to Celia which make her cry silently as she read it; and when
Sir James, unused to see she in tear, ask anxiously what be the
matter, she burst out in a wail such as he have never hear from she
before.
"Dorothea have a little boy. and you will not let I go and see she.
and I be sure she want to see I. and she will not know what to do
with the baby--she will do wrong thing with it. and they think she
would die. it be very dreadful! suppose it have be I and little
Arthur, and Dodo have be hinder from come to see I! I wish you
would be less unkind, James!"
"good heaven, Celia!" say Sir James, much work upon, "what do you
wish? I will do anything you like. I will take you to town to-morrow
if you wish it." and Celia do wish it.
it be after this that Mr. Brooke come, and meet the Baronet in the
ground, begin to chat with he in ignorance of the news, which Sir
James for some reason do not care to tell he immediately. but when
the entail be touch on in the usual way, he say, "my dear sir, it
be not for I to dictate to you, but for my part I would let that
alone. I would let thing remain as they be."
Mr. Brooke feel so much surprised that he do not at once find out how
much he be relieve by the sense that he be not expect to do
anything in particular.
such be the bent of Celia's heart, it be inevitable that Sir James
should consent to a reconciliation with Dorothea and her husband.
where woman love each other, man learn to smother their mutual dislike.
Sir James never like Ladislaw, and will always prefer to have Sir
James's company mix with another kind: they be on a footing of
reciprocal tolerance which be make quite easy only when Dorothea and
Celia be present.
it become an understand thing that Mr. and Mrs. Ladislaw should pay at
least two visit during the year to the Grange, and there come
gradually a small row of cousin at Freshitt who enjoy play with
the two cousin visit Tipton as much as if the blood of these
cousin have be less dubiously mixed.
Mr. Brooke live to a good old age, and his estate be inherit by
Dorothea's son, who might have represent Middlemarch, but decline,
think that his opinion have less chance of be stifle if he
remain out of door.
Sir James never cease to regard Dorothea's second marriage as a
mistake; and indeed this remain the tradition concern it in
Middlemarch, where she be speak of to a young generation as a fine
girl who marry a sickly clergyman, old enough to be her father, and
in little more than a year after his death give up her estate to marry
his cousin--young enough to have be his son, with no property, and
not well-bear. those who have not see anything of Dorothea usually
observe that she could not have be "a nice woman," else she would
not have marry either the one or the other.
certainly those determine act of her life be not ideally
beautiful. they be the mixed result of young and noble impulse
struggle amidst the condition of an imperfect social state, in which
great feeling will often take the aspect of error, and great faith the
aspect of illusion. for there be no creature whose inward be be so
strong that it be not greatly determine by what lie outside it. a
new Theresa will hardly have the opportunity of reform a conventual
life, any more than a new Antigone will spend her heroic piety in
dare all for the sake of a brother's burial: the medium in which
their ardent deed take shape be forever go. but we insignificant
people with our daily word and act be prepare the life of many
dorothea, some of which may present a far sad sacrifice than that
of the Dorothea whose story we know.
she finely touch spirit have still its fine issue, though they be
not widely visible. her full nature, like that river of which Cyrus
break the strength, spend itself in channel which have no great name on
the earth. but the effect of she be on those around she be
incalculably diffusive: for the grow good of the world be partly
dependent on unhistoric act; and that thing be not so ill with you
and I as they might have be, be half owe to the number who live
faithfully a hidden life, and rest in unvisited tomb.
